topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Annealing Schedule

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Foundations of Simulated Annealing (SA)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview — The Annealing Schedule

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Annealing Schedule (Cooling Schedule):** In Simulated Annealing, the *annealing schedule* is the finite sequence of values that governs the controlled reduction of the control parameter $T$ (temperature) from an initial value $T_0$ to a final value $T_f$. It is the mathematical function $T(k)$ (or $T(t)$) that determines **how fast**, **how slowly**, and **at what rate** the system is allowed to "cool" while searching for the global optimum of an optimization problem.

Mathematically, the annealing schedule is a mapping:

$$T : \mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{R}^+ \quad \text{such that} \quad T(k+1) < T(k)$$

where $k$ is the iteration counter and $T(k) \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ (for asymptotic convergence).

The annealing schedule is one of the **four design pillars** of the SA algorithm along with:
1. **Solution Representation**
2. **Neighborhood Function** ($N(s)$)
3. **Acceptance Probability** (Metropolis criterion: $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$)
4. **Annealing Schedule** $T(k)$ ← *our focus*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bead Maker's Furnace"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a goldsmith melting gold in a furnace. If he **quenches** the molten metal by dunking it into cold water instantly, the atoms freeze in a disordered, brittle, high-energy state — the metal cracks and is worthless. But if he **cools it very slowly and patiently** (true annealing), the atoms have enough time to settle into a perfect, low-energy crystalline lattice — strong, beautiful, and optimal.

In Simulated Annealing, the "gold" is the candidate solution, the "energy" is the cost function $E(s)$, and the **annealing schedule is the goldsmith's thermometer dial setting the cooling rate**. Cool too fast → trapped in local optima. Cool too slow → wastes enormous computation. The schedule is the **art of balance**.

### 1.3 The Four Components of a Complete Annealing Schedule

| # | Component | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|
| 1 | **Initial Temperature** | $T_0$ | Sets the starting "exploration" energy. Must be hot enough to accept virtually all moves. |
| 2 | **Final Temperature** | $T_f$ | The "freeze" point. Should be near zero so only improving moves are accepted. |
| 3 | **Cooling Function** | $T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k)$ | The mathematical law of temperature decay. |
| 4 | **Inner Loop Count** (Epoch Length) | $L_k$ or $N_k$ | Number of iterations spent at temperature $T(k)$ to reach thermal equilibrium. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A complete annealing schedule is **not just** the cooling function. You must specify *all four* components for a valid SA design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Annealing Schedule

### 2.1.1 Initial Temperature ($T_0$)

The starting temperature must satisfy:

$$T_0 \geq \frac{\Delta E_{max}}{\ln(\chi_0)}$$

where:
- $\Delta E_{max}$ = the **maximum expected cost difference** between two neighboring states in the search space.
- $\chi_0 \in (0, 1]$ = the **initial acceptance probability** (typically $\chi_0 \approx 0.8$ to $0.99$).

**Intuition:** A high $T_0$ makes the Boltzmann factor $e^{-\Delta E / T}$ approach $1$, so almost every uphill move is accepted — the algorithm behaves like a random walk, *exploring* the entire landscape.

### 2.1.2 Final Temperature ($T_f$)

A common stopping rule is:

$$T_f \ll \Delta E_{min} \quad \text{or simply} \quad T_f \approx 0.01$$

where $\Delta E_{min}$ is the smallest non-zero cost difference. At $T_f$, the algorithm behaves like **pure hill-climbing** — *exploiting* only improving moves.

### 2.1.3 The Cooling Function (Core of the Schedule)

Below are the **KTU-favorite** cooling laws, ranked by popularity in board exams:

| # | Schedule Type | Mathematical Law $T(k)$ | Parameters | Use Case |
|---|---------------|--------------------------|------------|----------|
| 1 | **Geometric (Exponential)** | $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$ | $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$ | **Most widely used** in practice |
| 2 | **Linear** | $T(k) = T_0 - \beta k$ | $\beta = (T_0 - T_f) / K_{max}$ | Simple, predictable |
| 3 | **Logarithmic (Classical)** | $T(k) = T_0 / \log(k + k_0)$ | $k_0 > 0$ | Asymptotic convergence proof |
| 4 | **Cauchy-like (Fast Annealing)** | $T(k) = T_0 / k$ | — | Slower than geometric |
| 5 | **Adaptive** | $T(k+1) = T(k) \cdot (1 + \alpha \cdot \sigma / T(k))^{-1}$ | $\sigma$ = std. dev. of $\Delta E$ | Self-tuning, robust |

### 2.1.4 Inner Loop Length (Equilibrium Epoch $L_k$)

At each fixed temperature $T(k)$, the Markov chain of states must reach a **quasi-equilibrium** distribution:

$$\pi_k(s) = \frac{e^{-E(s)/T(k)}}{\sum_{j \in S} e^{-E(j)/T(k)}}$$

A practical rule:

$$L_k = \max\{L_{min}, \; n \cdot |\mathcal{N}(s)|\}$$

where $n$ is the problem dimension and $\vert\mathcal{N}(s)\vert$ is the neighborhood size.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Boards:** Memorize the four primary cooling laws and the Metropolis acceptance probability. Ninety percent of annealing-schedule questions test these directly.

| # | Formula | Description | Constraints |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $P(\Delta E, T) = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ | Metropolis acceptance probability | $T > 0$, $\Delta E > 0$ for worsening |
| 2 | $T_0 = \frac{\Delta E_{max}}{\ln(\chi_0^{-1})}$ | Initial temperature for acceptance ratio $\chi_0$ | $\chi_0 \in (0,1)$ |
| 3 | $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$ | Geometric cooling | $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ |
| 4 | $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \frac{\log(k_0)}{\log(k_0 + k)}$ | Logarithmic cooling (Geman & Geman, 1984) | Asymptotic convergence |
| 5 | $T(k) = T_0 - k\beta$ | Linear cooling | $\beta = T_0 / K_{max}$ |
| 6 | $L_k = n \cdot N$ | Epoch length | $n$ = dimension, $N$ = neighborhood size |
| 7 | $T_f = \epsilon \cdot \Delta E_{avg}$ | Stopping condition | $\epsilon \approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| 8 | $T_{final} = T_0 \cdot \alpha^{K-1}$ | Temperature at iteration $K$ | Geometric |

> **Important Notation Note:** In the above formulas, $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is rendered as `\vert x \vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always write it the same way in your KTU answer sheets.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The annealing schedule is the **heart** of practical SA solvers used in:
- **VLSI Circuit Placement & Routing** (e.g., TimberWolf, 1980s)
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — logistics & supply chain
- **Job-Shop Scheduling** in manufacturing
- **Image Segmentation** in medical imaging
- **Neural Network Weight Optimization** (Boltzmann machines)
- **Antenna Array Design** in RF engineering
- **Protein Folding** simulation in bioinformatics

In every application, the choice of schedule trades off **solution quality** vs. **CPU time** — a slow schedule (logarithmic) yields near-optimal solutions but takes forever; a fast schedule (linear) is quick but may miss the global optimum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Computing the Initial Temperature $T_0$

**Problem:** Given a maximum expected cost difference $\Delta E_{max} = 10$ and a desired initial acceptance ratio $\chi_0 = 0.90$, find $T_0$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Metropolis acceptance formula:**

$$P(\text{accept worsening move}) = e^{-\Delta E / T}$$

**Step 2 — At $T = T_0$, set the acceptance probability equal to $\chi_0$:**

$$\chi_0 = e^{-\Delta E_{max} / T_0}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln(\chi_0) = -\frac{\Delta E_{max}}{T_0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $T_0$:**

$$T_0 = -\frac{\Delta E_{max}}{\ln(\chi_0)} = \frac{\Delta E_{max}}{\ln(1/\chi_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$T_0 = \frac{10}{\ln(1/0.90)} = \frac{10}{\ln(1.1111)}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the logarithm numerically:**

$$\ln(1.1111) \approx 0.10536$$

**Step 7 — Final numerical answer:**

$$T_0 = \frac{10}{0.10536} \approx 94.92$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Starting at $T_0 \approx 95$ ensures that the algorithm will accept a worsening move of size $10$ about 90% of the time — giving a healthy, exploratory "hot start."

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Number of Iterations Under Geometric Cooling

**Given:** $T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k$ and a fixed epoch length $L$ at each temperature.

**Step 1 — Total iterations after $K$ temperature drops:**

$$N_{total} = K \times L$$

**Step 2 — We want the temperature to reach $T_f$ at iteration $K$:**

$$T_f = T_0 \cdot \alpha^K$$

**Step 3 — Take logarithm base $\alpha$ of both sides:**

$$K = \log_\alpha\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right) = \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Total iterations become:**

$$N_{total} = L \cdot \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example:** Let $T_0 = 100$, $T_f = 0.01$, $\alpha = 0.95$, $L = 50$.

$$K = \frac{\ln(0.01/100)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{\ln(0.0001)}{\ln(0.95)} = \frac{-9.2103}{-0.0513} \approx 179.5$$

**Step 6 — Rounding up, $K = 180$ temperature levels, so:**

$$N_{total} = 180 \times 50 = 9{,}000 \text{ total iterations}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acceptance Probability vs. Temperature Trade-off (Worked Table)

Using $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$, we tabulate how acceptance changes for a fixed worsening $\Delta E = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \rightarrow \infty &: \quad P = e^0 = 1.000 \quad \text{(accept all)} \\
T = 100 &: \quad P = e^{-5/100} = e^{-0.05} \approx 0.9512 \\
T = 50 &: \quad P = e^{-5/50} = e^{-0.10} \approx 0.9048 \\
T = 25 &: \quad P = e^{-5/25} = e^{-0.20} \approx 0.8187 \\
T = 10 &: \quad P = e^{-5/10} = e^{-0.50} \approx 0.6065 \\
T = 5  &: \quad P = e^{-5/5}  = e^{-1.00} \approx 0.3679 \\
T = 1  &: \quad P = e^{-5/1}  = e^{-5.00} \approx 0.00674 \\
T \to 0 &: \quad P \to 0 \quad \text{(accept only improving moves)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This table shows precisely *why* the annealing schedule matters. At $T = 100$, the algorithm is exploratory; at $T = 1$, it is exploitative. A good schedule smoothly traverses this transition.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Convergence Rate of Geometric Schedule

**Step 1 — Start with the recursive cooling law:**

$$T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k), \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$

**Step 2 — Unroll the recursion:**

$$T(1) = \alpha T(0), \quad T(2) = \alpha^2 T(0), \quad \ldots \quad T(k) = \alpha^k T_0$$

**Step 3 — The "half-life" $k_{1/2}$ (iterations to halve $T$) satisfies:**

$$\alpha^{k_{1/2}} = \frac{1}{2} \implies k_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(0.5)}{\ln(\alpha)} = -\frac{\ln 2}{\ln \alpha}$$

**Step 4 — For $\alpha = 0.95$:**

$$k_{1/2} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.0513} \approx 13.5 \text{ iterations to halve temperature}$$

**Step 5 — For $\alpha = 0.99$ (slow cooling):**

$$k_{1/2} = \frac{-0.6931}{-0.01005} \approx 68.97 \text{ iterations to halve temperature}$$

> **Trade-off:** $\alpha = 0.99$ gives **5x more exploration per halving** than $\alpha = 0.95$, but consumes **5x more CPU time**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Annealing Schedule Execution Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize T equals T0] --> B[Set k equals 0]
    B --> C[Generate random initial solution s]
    C --> D[Inner Loop: L_k iterations at current T]
    D --> E[Generate neighbor s_new from s]
    E --> F[Compute delta E equals E_s_new minus E_s]
    F --> G{delta E less than 0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Accept s_new as new s]
    G -->|No| I[Generate random r in 0 to 1]
    I --> J{r less than exp minus delta E over T?}
    J -->|Yes| H
    J -->|No| K[Reject s_new, keep s]
    H --> L{Inner loop done?}
    K --> L
    L -->|No| D
    L -->|Yes| M[Cool: T equals T0 times alpha to power k]
    M --> N{T greater than Tf?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Increment k]
    O --> D
    N -->|No| P[Output best s and Stop]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of the Schedule

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph input[Input Parameters]
        A1[T0 Initial Temp]
        A2[Alpha Cooling Rate]
        A3[Tf Final Temp]
        A4[L Epoch Length]
    end

    subgraph core[Schedule Engine]
        B1[Cooling Function T of k]
        B2[Equilibrium Checker]
        B3[Stopping Criterion]
    end

    subgraph output[Schedule Output]
        C1[Temporal Temperature Profile]
        C2[Acceptance Probability Curve]
        C3[Convergence Diagnostic]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B3
    A4 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison — Cooling Laws Visual Summary

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cooling Schedules Family] --> B[Geometric Exponential]
    A --> C[Linear]
    A --> D[Logarithmic]
    A --> E[Adaptive]
    B --> B1[Fast convergence, simple]
    C --> C1[Predictable, may freeze early]
    D --> D1[Provably convergent, very slow]
    E --> E1[Self tuning, robust to landscape]
```

## 4.4 State-Transition Diagram — Temperature States in SA

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Hot
    Hot --> Warm : T decays geometrically
    Warm --> Tepid : T decays geometrically
    Tepid --> Cool : T decays geometrically
    Cool --> Cold : T decays geometrically
    Cold --> Frozen : T less than Tf
    Frozen --> [*] : Solution reported
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** The cooling schedule traces a deterministic path from the *Hot* state (pure exploration) through *Warm*, *Tepid*, *Cool* states (mixed exploration + exploitation) to *Frozen* (pure exploitation / hill climbing).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** Define *annealing schedule* in Simulated Annealing. List its four essential components.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** An annealing schedule (or cooling schedule) is a finite sequence of control parameter (temperature) values $\{T(k)\}_{k=0}^{K}$ that governs how the temperature is reduced from an initial value $T_0$ to a final value $T_f$ during the execution of the SA algorithm.
>
> **Four Components:**
> 1. **Initial Temperature** ($T_0$) — starting value of the control parameter.
> 2. **Final Temperature / Stopping Criterion** ($T_f$) — value at which the algorithm halts.
> 3. **Cooling Function / Decay Rule** — the mathematical law $T(k+1) = f(T(k))$ for reducing temperature.
> 4. **Inner Loop Length / Epoch** ($L_k$) — number of iterations at each temperature to approach equilibrium.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Write the geometric and logarithmic cooling laws used in Simulated Annealing and state one advantage of each.

**Model Answer:**

> **Geometric (Exponential) Cooling Law:**
>
> $$T(k) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^k, \quad 0 < \alpha < 1$$
>
> **Advantage:** Simple, fast, requires only one parameter $\alpha$, and is the most widely used in practice.
>
> **Logarithmic Cooling Law (Geman & Geman):**
>
> $$T(k) = \frac{T_0}{\log(k + k_0)}, \quad k_0 > 1$$
>
> **Advantage:** Provably guarantees asymptotic convergence to the global optimum, making it the mathematically preferred choice for theoretical analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q:** With neat functional block diagram, explain the role of the **Annealing Schedule** in Simulated Annealing. Discuss the geometric cooling law in detail and derive an expression for the number of temperature reductions required to reach a given final temperature $T_f$ starting from $T_0$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Role and Block Diagram of Annealing Schedule

**Model Answer:**

The **annealing schedule** is the heart of the Simulated Annealing algorithm — it controls the *exploration vs. exploitation* trade-off over the course of the run. In the early stages (high $T$), the schedule allows the algorithm to accept worsening moves with high probability, escaping local optima. In the later stages (low $T$), the schedule forces the algorithm to behave like a hill-climber, refining the solution near the global optimum.

**Functional Block Diagram:**

```
+--------------------+        +-------------------+
|  Initial State s0  | -----> |  Evaluate E(s0)   |
+--------------------+        +-------------------+
                                       |
                                       v
+--------------------+        +-------------------+
|  Set T = T0        | -----> |  Inner Loop (Lk)  |
+--------------------+        +-------------------+
                                       |
                                       v
                          +------------------------+
                          |  Generate s_new, ΔE     |
                          +------------------------+
                                       |
                                       v
                          +------------------------+
                          |  Accept? (Metropolis)  |
                          +------------------------+
                                       |
                                       v
                          +------------------------+
                          |  Cool: T = α·T         |
                          |  T < Tf? -> STOP       |
                          +------------------------+
```

The annealing schedule drives the **temperature update block** (T = α·T) and the **stopping criterion** (T < Tf), linking the inner Metropolis loop to the outer cooling loop.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Geometric Cooling and Derivation of $K$

The geometric cooling law is given by:

$$T(k+1) = \alpha \cdot T(k), \quad 0 < \alpha < 1, \quad T(0) = T_0$$

Unrolling the recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(1) &= \alpha \cdot T_0 \\
T(2) &= \alpha^2 \cdot T_0 \\
\vdots \\
T(k) &= \alpha^k \cdot T_0
\end{aligned}
$$

To find the number of reductions $K$ such that $T(K) = T_f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= T_0 \cdot \alpha^K \\
\frac{T_f}{T_0} &= \alpha^K \\
\ln\left(\frac{T_f}{T_0}\right) &= K \cdot \ln(\alpha) \\
K &= \frac{\ln(T_f / T_0)}{\ln(\alpha)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical check:** For $T_0 = 100$, $T_f = 0.1$, $\alpha = 0.9$:

$$K = \frac{\ln(0.001)}{\ln(0.9)} = \frac{-6.9078}{-0.10536} \approx 65.5 \implies K = 66$$

So 66 temperature reductions are required.

> **Why Geometric?** It is parameter-sparse (only $\alpha$), computationally cheap, and provides a controlled exponential decay that has been shown empirically to outperform linear and many adaptive schedules on combinatorial problems.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Compare the **linear, geometric, and logarithmic** cooling schedules of Simulated Annealing. Derive the formula for the initial temperature $T_0$ such that the initial acceptance probability of a worsening move of magnitude $\Delta E_{max}$ is $\chi_0$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Comparative Study of Cooling Schedules

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Linear | Geometric | Logarithmic |
|----------|--------|-----------|-------------|
| **Formula** | $T(k) = T_0 - \beta k$ | $T(k) = T_0 \alpha^k$ | $T(k) = T_0 / \ln(k + k_0)$ |
| **Convergence Speed** | Fast | Medium | Very Slow |
| **Solution Quality** | Moderate | Good | Best (asymptotic) |
| **Theoretical Guarantee** | No | No | **Yes (Geman & Geman, 1984)** |
| **Practical Use** | Rare | **Most Common** | Rare (too slow) |
| **Parameter Count** | 2 ($T_0, \beta$) | 2 ($T_0, \alpha$) | 2 ($T_0, k_0$) |
| **Behavior Near $T_f$** | Linear tail-off | Exponential tail-off | Long, slow descent |

The **logarithmic** schedule is theoretically optimal — it ensures the SA algorithm converges in probability to the global optimum as $k \to \infty$. However, it is impractically slow for real engineering problems. The **geometric** schedule is the de-facto industry standard because it strikes the best balance between quality and runtime. The **linear** schedule is rarely used because it tends to "freeze" the algorithm prematurely when $\beta$ is too large.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Initial Temperature $T_0$

**Setup:** Let the maximum expected energy difference (worsening) be $\Delta E_{max} > 0$, and let the desired acceptance probability at $T_0$ be $\chi_0 \in (0, 1)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Metropolis criterion:**

$$P(\text{accept}) = e^{-\Delta E / T_0}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to desired probability $\chi_0$ for the worst-case worsening move:**

$$\chi_0 = e^{-\Delta E_{max} / T_0}$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln \chi_0 = -\frac{\Delta E_{max}}{T_0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T_0$:**

$$T_0 = -\frac{\Delta E_{max}}{\ln \chi_0} = \frac{\Delta E_{max}}{\ln(1/\chi_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the units and limits:**

- If $\chi_0 \to 1$ (accept everything), $\ln(1/\chi_0) \to 0$, so $T_0 \to \infty$. ✓
- If $\chi_0 \to 0$ (accept nothing), $\ln(1/\chi_0) \to \infty$, so $T_0 \to 0$. ✓

**Step 6 — Numerical example:** $\Delta E_{max} = 50$, $\chi_0 = 0.8$:

$$T_0 = \frac{50}{\ln(1/0.8)} = \frac{50}{\ln(1.25)} = \frac{50}{0.2231} \approx 224.1$$

> **Interpretation:** Starting at $T_0 \approx 224$ guarantees that the algorithm will accept any single worsening move (no matter how bad) with probability $0.8$ — a high-exploration initialization.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Annealing Schedule Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Annealing Schedule" with "Cooling Function" alone.** A schedule requires *all four* components — listing only the decay law costs at least 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the constraint** $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ for geometric cooling. Writing $\alpha > 1$ means the system *heats up*, not cools.
> 3. **Mixing up** $T_0 = -\Delta E_{max} / \ln \chi_0$ with the *reverse* (sign error). Always re-derive to be safe.
> 4. **In derivation questions,** skipping the "take natural log" step is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Examiners *want* to see the log step explicitly.
> 5. **Not specifying the inner loop length** $L_k$ when asked to "design a complete annealing schedule." Partial answers = partial marks.
> 6. **Confusing** *annealing* (slow cooling) with *quenching* (instant freezing) — these are opposite processes in metallurgy and in SA.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Annealing Schedule = the 4-tuple** $(T_0, T_f, \text{Decay Law}, L_k)$.
- [x] **Metropolis criterion:** $P = e^{-\Delta E / T}$ — drives stochastic acceptance.
- [x] **Geometric cooling** $T(k) = T_0 \alpha^k$ with $\alpha \in (0.8, 0.99)$ is the **practical default**.
- [x] **Logarithmic cooling** is the **theoretical gold standard** for convergence guarantees.
- [x] **Initial temperature formula:** $T_0 = \Delta E_{max} / \ln(1/\chi_0)$.
- [x] **Number of temperature drops (geometric):** $K = \ln(T_f / T_0) / \ln(\alpha)$.
- [x] **Inner loop** must be long enough to reach **quasi-equilibrium** at each $T(k)$.
- [x] **Annealing** ≠ **quenching** — slow is good, fast is bad.
- [x] **Cool too fast** → trapped in local optima; **cool too slow** → wasted computation.
- [x] **Final temperature** $T_f$ should be much smaller than $\Delta E_{min}$ for the solution to be effectively "frozen."
- [x] **Adapt the schedule** to the problem: TSP, VLSI, scheduling, and image-processing problems each benefit from different $\alpha$ and $L_k$ values.
- [x] **Logarithmic schedule** proven convergent (Geman & Geman, 1984) — remember the year for KTU context questions.
- [x] **Geometric schedule** is *not* proven convergent but works brilliantly in practice.

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Annealing Schedule | PECNT869 — Evolutionary Computing | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parameter Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parameter Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In Evolutionary Computing (EC), **Parameter Selection** (also called **Parameter Tuning** or **Control Parameter Setting**) is defined as the systematic process of identifying suitable values for the numerical and structural control parameters of an Evolutionary Algorithm (EA) — such as population size, mutation rate, crossover probability, selection pressure, and elitism ratio — such that the algorithm exhibits robust convergence behaviour, high solution quality, and reliable performance across a defined class of optimisation problems.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme classification, parameter settings in EC are categorised into two primary paradigms:

| Paradigm | Description | When Applied |
|---|---|---|
| **Parameter Tuning** | Setting parameters *a priori* (before the run) and keeping them fixed | Offline, off-line configuration |
| **Parameter Control** | Modifying parameters *during* the run using rules, feedback, or encoded adaptation | Online, run-time adaptation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Parameter Selection is a *foundational* topic of Module 1. It directly underpins the design of Genetic Algorithms (GAs), Evolution Strategies (ES), Genetic Programming (GP), and Differential Evolution (DE). Questions are routinely asked in Part A (3 marks) and as sub-parts of Part B (14 marks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are baking a cake (the **evolutionary algorithm**), and you have ingredients like flour, sugar, eggs, and oven temperature (the **parameters**). 

- If you use too much sugar, the cake is sickly sweet. 
- Too little flour, and it collapses. 
- Wrong oven temperature, and the cake burns or stays raw.

Before you start baking, you must **select** the right amounts of each ingredient. Sometimes you do this by trial-and-error (basic **tuning**), sometimes you taste the batter halfway and adjust (**adaptive control**), and sometimes you hire an AI baker that watches the oven and dynamically alters everything (**self-adaptive control**). 

This is precisely what parameter selection means in EC: choosing — and sometimes dynamically adjusting — the "ingredients" (parameters) of an EA so that it consistently produces the "best cake" (optimal/near-optimal solution).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of EA Parameters

Evolutionary Algorithms typically contain three parameter categories:

1. **Categorical (Qualitative) Parameters** — e.g., type of crossover (single-point, two-point, uniform), type of selection (roulette-wheel, tournament, rank-based).
2. **Numerical (Quantitative) Parameters** — e.g., population size $N$, mutation rate $p_m$, crossover rate $p_c$, selection pressure $s$, scaling window $\sigma$.
3. **Structural (Topological) Parameters** — e.g., representation (binary, real-valued, permutation), number of sub-populations (islands), neighbourhood topology in cellular EAs.

> [!NOTE]
> Standard reference metrics used in KTU problems: **Population Size $N$**, **Mutation Probability $p_m$**, **Crossover Probability $p_c$**, **Selection Pressure $s$**, **Generational Gap $G$**, and **Scaling Factor $F$** (specific to DE).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parameter Setting Problem (Formal Statement)

Let an Evolutionary Algorithm $\mathcal{A}$ be characterised by a vector of parameters:

$$\vec{\theta} = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_k) \in \Theta$$

where $\Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^k$ denotes the parameter space. For a given problem instance $f$ drawn from a problem class $\mathcal{F}$, the *expected performance* of $\mathcal{A}$ over $R$ independent runs is:

$$\bar{P}(\vec{\theta}, f) = \frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R} P_r(\vec{\theta}, f)$$

The **Parameter Selection Problem** is the optimisation problem:

$$\vec{\theta}^* = \arg\min_{\vec{\theta} \in \Theta} \mathbb{E}_{f \in \mathcal{F}}\big[\bar{P}(\vec{\theta}, f)\big]$$

That is, we seek the parameter vector $\vec{\theta}^*$ that minimises the *expected* performance (e.g., error to optimum, number of function evaluations) over the distribution of problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **meta-optimisation** problem: an outer-level optimisation *over* an inner-level optimisation (the EA itself). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly notes that this hierarchical structure is the reason parameter selection is computationally expensive.

## 2.2 Classification of Parameter Control Strategies

According to Eiben, Hinterding, and Michalewicz (the standard KTU reference), parameter control is classified along three axes:

### (a) By the *type* of change:
- **Deterministic Control** — parameter update follows a fixed, pre-scheduled rule with no feedback.
- **Adaptive Control** — parameter is updated based on feedback from the search (e.g., current population diversity).
- **Self-Adaptive Control** — parameter values are encoded into the chromosomes and evolve themselves.

### (b) By the *scope* of change:
- **Global vs. Local** (parameter is the same for all individuals or varies per individual).
- **Static vs. Dynamic** (whether the parameter can change during the run).

### (c) By the *evidence* used:
- **Absolute evidence** (current fitness values)
- **Relative evidence** (relative ranking, progress indicators)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **tested KTU high-yield items** — practise substituting numerical values for each.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Meaning | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Expected EA performance | $\bar{P}(\vec{\theta}, f) = \frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R} P_r(\vec{\theta}, f)$ | $R$ = no. of runs | Basis of meta-optimisation |
| 2 | Mutation rate (Bäck's classical GA) | $p_m = \frac{1}{L}$ | $L$ = chromosome length | 1-bit-per-chromosome rule |
| 3 | Crossover rate (typical) | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$ | Empirical range | For real-coded GAs |
| 4 | Population size (Gambler's Ruin model) | $N = k \cdot \frac{\sigma_f \cdot \sqrt{\ln n}}{d}$ | $k$ = confidence constant, $\sigma_f$ = fitness std-dev, $n$ = problem dim, $d$ = distance to optimum | De Jong & Spears bound |
| 5 | Boltzmann acceptance (deterministic cooling) | $T(t) = T_0 \cdot \alpha^t$ | $T_0$ = initial temp, $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | Geometric schedule |
| 6 | Logarithmic cooling | $T(t) = \frac{T_0}{\log(t+1)}$ | — | Slower cooling |
| 7 | Self-adaptive mutation (ES) | $\sigma' = \sigma \cdot \exp(\tau \cdot N(0,1))$ | $\tau = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Schwefel's 1/5-success rule variant |
| 8 | 1/5-success rule (Rechenberg) | $p_s = \frac{1}{5}$ | $p_s$ = success prob. | Rechenberg's classical ES |
| 9 | Selection intensity (rank-based) | $I = \frac{1}{\mu}\sum_{i=1}^{\mu} E[Z_{(i)}]$ | $Z_{(i)}$ = ordered standard normal | $I \approx 1.16$ for tournament size 2 |
| 10 | Takeover time (selection pressure) | $T^* = \frac{\ln(\mu)}{\ln(s)}$ | $\mu$ = pop. size, $s$ = selection pressure | Bäck's growth model |
| 11 | Selection pressure $s$ (tournament size $k$) | $s = k^{\frac{1}{k}}$ | $k$ = tournament size | Empirical approx. |
| 12 | Diversity loss rate | $\Delta H(t) \approx -I \cdot \sqrt{V_t}$ | $V_t$ = fitness variance | Goldberg's model |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Antenna design (NASA ST5 mission):** EA parameters were tuned using Taguchi methods to produce a quasi-periodic antenna design — a flagship KTU reference example.
- **Hyper-parameter tuning of neural networks** is mathematically identical to EA parameter selection.
- **Robotics gait optimisation:** Self-adaptive $\sigma$ values in Evolution Strategies allow robots to adapt their gait to unknown terrains.
- **VLSI placement and routing:** Fixed parameters chosen via F-Race or REVAC lead to 10–30% reduction in wire-length.
- **Antenna & aerospace structures:** Taguchi orthogonal arrays reduce tuning cost by an order of magnitude.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Optimal Population Size (Gambler's Ruin / De Jong Bound)

**Statement:** For a GA operating on a binary string of length $L$, the minimum population size $N$ required to ensure that the algorithm reliably contains a schema of order $k$ and defining length $\ell$ with probability at least $1-\delta$ is given by the schema theorem.

**Step 1 — Probability that a specific schema $H$ survives selection.**

In a binary GA with selection probability proportional to fitness, the probability that a single individual contains schema $H$ is its fitness $f(H)$ divided by the mean fitness $\bar{f}$. The expected number of copies after selection is:

$$m(H, t+1) = m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$$

**Step 2 — Propagation over $t$ generations.**

Iterating, the expected number of copies after $t$ generations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(H,t) &= m(H,0) \cdot \left(\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}\right)^{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Probability that at least one copy exists in the population.**

Using the Poisson approximation with mean $\lambda = m(H,t)$, the probability of *zero* copies is $e^{-\lambda}$. For survival with probability $\geq 1 - \delta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{-\lambda} &\leq \delta \\
\lambda &\geq -\ln \delta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for population size $N$.**

If $m(H,0) = N \cdot p_s$ (where $p_s$ is the probability of generating $H$ in the initial random population, equal to $2^{-k}$ for a schema of order $k$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \cdot 2^{-k} \cdot \left(\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}\right)^{t} &\geq -\ln \delta \\
N &\geq -\ln \delta \cdot 2^{k} \cdot \left(\frac{\bar{f}}{f(H)}\right)^{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **De Jong population-sizing bound** used in KTU derivations.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Self-Adaptive Mutation Step-Size (Schwefel's ES)

**Statement:** In Evolution Strategies, the step-size $\sigma$ of a mutation operator can be encoded in the chromosome and co-evolved. Schwefel proposed the log-normal update.

**Step 1 — Sample a global learning rate.**

Draw $N(0,1)$, a standard normal random variable, **once per individual**:

$$
\tau_0 = \frac{c}{\sqrt{n}}, \quad c \text{ a constant (often } c=1\text{)}
$$

**Step 2 — Update the strategy parameter $\sigma$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma'_i &= \sigma_i \cdot \exp\bigl(\tau_0 \cdot N_i(0,1)\bigr) \quad \text{for } i = 1, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Update the object variables $x_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x'_i &= x_i + \sigma'_i \cdot N_i(0,1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Explanation of the multiplicative form.**

A *multiplicative* update guarantees $\sigma > 0$ and is invariant under rescaling of the search space — a property essential for performance on badly-scaled fitness landscapes. The expected value of $\sigma'$ is approximately $\sigma$, giving **unbiased** adaptation.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Deterministic Cooling Schedule (Boltzmann Selection)

**Step 1 — Define the initial temperature.**

Set $T_0$ such that the acceptance probability of a worse neighbour is close to 1:

$$
T_0 = c \cdot \sigma_f, \quad c \in [3, 10]
$$

where $\sigma_f$ is the standard deviation of the fitness values in the initial population.

**Step 2 — Geometric (exponential) cooling.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(t) &= T_0 \cdot \alpha^{t}, \quad \alpha \in (0.9, 0.99)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Acceptance probability (Metropolis criterion).**

For a candidate with worse fitness by $\Delta f > 0$:

$$
P_{\text{accept}} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T(t)}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Show that acceptance probability decays.**

Substituting $T(t) = T_0 \alpha^t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{accept}}(t) &= \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Delta f}{T_0 \alpha^t}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

As $t \to \infty$, $\alpha^t \to 0$, so $\frac{\Delta f}{T_0 \alpha^t} \to \infty$, and $P_{\text{accept}} \to 0$. This proves that the algorithm converges to a hill-climber (only improvements accepted) — a standard KTU result.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Tuning Methods

| Method | Type | Cost (EA-runs) | Robustness | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual / Trial-and-error** | Tuning | Low | Very low | Mentioned in KTU as baseline |
| **Full Factorial Design** | Tuning | $p^k$ (explodes) | High | Statistically rigorous |
| **Taguchi OA** | Tuning | $\sim p^{2}$ (L_4, L_8, L_9) | High | **Very High** (NASA case study) |
| **F-Race** | Tuning | Variable (race-style) | High | **High** |
| **REVAC** | Tuning | Iterative | High | **High** |
| **CALIBRA** | Tuning | Iterative | High | Moderate |
| **Deterministic control** | Control | Zero extra cost | Medium | **High** |
| **Adaptive control** | Control | Zero extra cost | High | **High** |
| **Self-adaptive** | Control | Encoded overhead | Very high | **Very High** |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A GA is run on a 50-bit OneMax problem. Population size $N=100$, tournament size $k=3$. Compute the selection pressure $s$ and the takeover time $T^*$.

**Solution:**

Selection pressure (tournament):

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= k^{1/k} = 3^{1/3} \approx 1.442
\end{aligned}
$$

Takeover time (Bäck's growth model):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T^* &= \frac{\ln \mu}{\ln s} = \frac{\ln 100}{\ln 1.442} = \frac{4.605}{0.366} \approx 12.58
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, the best individual takes over the population in approximately **12.58 generations** under tournament selection of size 3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Taxonomy of Parameter Setting Strategies (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Parameter Setting in EAs] --> B[Parameter Tuning]
    A --> C[Parameter Control]
    B --> B1[Manual]
    B --> B2[Statistical DOE]
    B --> B3[Meta-EA]
    B --> B4[Sequential Methods]
    B2 --> B2a[Full Factorial]
    B2 --> B2b[Taguchi OA]
    B2 --> B2c[Latin Hypercube]
    B3 --> B3a[REVAC]
    B3 --> B3b[Meta-GA]
    B4 --> B4a[F-Race]
    B4 --> B4b[CALIBRA]
    C --> C1[Deterministic]
    C --> C2[Adaptive]
    C --> C3[Self-Adaptive]
    C1 --> C1a[Boltzmann Cooling]
    C1 --> C1b[Time-Dependent p_m]
    C2 --> C2a[1/5 Success Rule]
    C2 --> C2b[Fitness Feedback]
    C3 --> C3a[Encoded Sigma ES]
    C3 --> C3b[Evolutionary p_m]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Self-Adaptive EA

```mermaid
graph TD
    popInit[Initialize Population with Encoded Parameters] --> eval[Evaluate Fitness]
    eval --> select[Selection Operator]
    select --> recombine[Recombination Crossover]
    recombine --> mutate[Mutation with Self-Adapted Step Size]
    mutate --> decode[Decode Object and Strategy Variables]
    decode --> monitor[Monitor Population Diversity and Success Rate]
    monitor --> adapt[Adapt Strategy Parameters in Next Generation]
    adapt --> eval
    adapt --> stopCheck{Stopping Criterion Met}
    stopCheck -->|Yes| output[Output Best Solution]
    stopCheck -->|No| select
```

## 4.3 Taguchi Orthogonal Array Tuning Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Identify Parameters and Levels] --> B[Select Orthogonal Array L_n]
    B --> C[Run EA for Each Row of OA]
    C --> D[Compute S/N Ratio per Row]
    D --> E[ANOVA on Factor Effects]
    E --> F[Determine Optimal Parameter Combination]
    F --> G[Confirmation Run with Optimal Settings]
    G --> H[Final Tuned EA Configuration]
```

## 4.4 Meta-Optimisation Loop for Parameter Tuning

```mermaid
graph TD
    outer[Outer Loop: Meta-Optimiser] --> inner[Inner Loop: EA on Target Problem]
    inner --> perf[Performance Metric]
    perf --> outer
    outer --> decision{Stopping Criterion}
    decision -->|No| nextParam[Sample New Parameter Vector]
    nextParam --> inner
    decision -->|Yes| bestParam[Output Best Parameter Vector theta star]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]
**Q:** Differentiate between **Parameter Tuning** and **Parameter Control** in Evolutionary Algorithms. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

- **Parameter Tuning** refers to the *offline* process of setting parameter values *before* the EA is executed, with the values held constant throughout the run. It is a meta-optimisation problem where the goal is to find a parameter vector $\vec{\theta}^*$ that minimises expected EA performance. *Example:* Using Taguchi orthogonal arrays to choose $N=100$, $p_c=0.9$, $p_m=0.01$ before running a GA on TSP.
- **Parameter Control** refers to the *online* modification of parameter values *during* the run based on a rule (deterministic), feedback (adaptive), or by encoding parameters into the chromosome (self-adaptive). *Example:* Decreasing mutation rate $p_m$ linearly from $0.1$ to $0.001$ over 500 generations (deterministic control), or self-adapting the step-size $\sigma$ in a $(\mu+\lambda)$-ES.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]
**Q:** What is the **1/5-success rule** in Evolution Strategies? State the formula.

**Model Answer:**

The 1/5-success rule, proposed by Rechenberg, is a deterministic-adaptive rule for controlling the mutation step-size $\sigma$ in a $(1+1)$-ES. The rule states:

$$
\text{If } p_s > \frac{1}{5}, \text{ then } \sigma \leftarrow \sigma \cdot a; \quad \text{If } p_s < \frac{1}{5}, \text{ then } \sigma \leftarrow \sigma / a
$$

where $p_s$ is the ratio of successful mutations (those that improved fitness) over the last $G$ generations, and $a \in (0,1)$ (typically $a = 0.817$). The rule aims to maintain a success rate of $1/5$, which is optimal for the sphere model and a good heuristic for many real problems.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three categories of parameter control** strategies in Evolutionary Algorithms. Discuss the merits and demerits of each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **optimal population size** using the Schema Theorem for a binary GA. State clearly the assumptions and the meaning of each variable. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The three categories of parameter control are:

**1. Deterministic Control:** Parameters are changed according to a pre-determined schedule, with no feedback from the search. *Example:* The mutation rate schedule $p_m(t) = 0.1 - 0.0002 \cdot t$ for $t = 0, 1, \ldots, 500$. 
- *Merits:* Simple, zero computational overhead, reproducible.
- *Demerits:* Blind to the current state of the search; may over- or under-explore.

**2. Adaptive Control:** Parameter values are updated using feedback from the search process (e.g., population diversity, success rate, current best fitness). 
- *Example:* The 1/5-success rule of Rechenberg.
- *Merits:* Responds to landscape characteristics; usually outperforms deterministic.
- *Demerits:* Requires design of the feedback mechanism; risk of oscillation.

**3. Self-Adaptive Control:** Parameter values are encoded into the individual's chromosome and evolve through the same genetic operators as the object variables.
- *Example:* Schwefel's log-normal $\sigma$-self-adaptation in $(\mu,\lambda)$-ES.
- *Merits:* Most robust; parameters "discover" good values via evolution.
- *Demerits:* Adds chromosomes; extends the search space; slower per generation.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The optimal population size derivation follows the steps shown in Section 3.1 of these notes. The final result is:

$$
N \geq -\ln(\delta) \cdot 2^{k} \cdot \left(\frac{\bar{f}}{f(H)}\right)^{t}
$$

where:
- $N$ = population size
- $\delta$ = acceptable failure probability
- $k$ = schema order (number of fixed positions)
- $\bar{f}$ = average fitness of the population
- $f(H)$ = mean fitness of schema $H$
- $t$ = number of generations

**Assumptions:** (i) Selection is fitness-proportional. (ii) Mutation and crossover effects on schema survival are ignored. (iii) Schema $H$ has fitness $f(H)$ significantly above $\bar{f}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **Taguchi method of parameter tuning** for Evolutionary Algorithms. Describe the steps and the role of the **S/N (Signal-to-Noise) ratio**. Show with a small example how a $L_4$ orthogonal array is used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss **self-adaptive mutation in Evolution Strategies**. Derive the log-normal update rule for the strategy parameter $\sigma$ and explain why a *multiplicative* update is used instead of an additive one. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

The Taguchi method, developed by Genichi Taguchi, is a statistical design-of-experiments (DoE) technique for efficiently exploring the parameter space of an EA. It uses **orthogonal arrays (OA)** to sample a small but representative subset of the full factorial design.

**Steps:**
1. Identify the EA parameters (e.g., $N$, $p_c$, $p_m$) and their candidate levels (typically 2–4 levels each).
2. Choose an appropriate orthogonal array $L_n(m^k)$, where $n$ = number of runs, $m$ = number of levels, $k$ = max number of factors.
3. For each row (configuration) of the OA, run the EA multiple times (say 30) on the target problem(s).
4. Compute the **S/N ratio** for each row. For minimisation problems:
   $$\text{S/N} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{R}\sum_{r=1}^{R} y_r^{2}\right)$$
   where $y_r$ is the objective value of run $r$.
5. Compute the **average effect** of each factor level using ANOVA.
6. Determine the **optimal parameter combination** as the level with the highest average S/N ratio for each factor.
7. Perform a **confirmation run** with the optimal combination.

**Example: $L_4(2^3)$ array for 2 parameters $A$ (population size) and $B$ (mutation rate) with 2 levels each:**

| Run | A (Level) | B (Level) | S/N Ratio |
|-----|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 1   | 1         | 1         | $\eta_1$  |
| 2   | 1         | 2         | $\eta_2$  |
| 3   | 2         | 1         | $\eta_3$  |
| 4   | 2         | 2         | $\eta_4$  |

Average S/N for $A_1$ = $(\eta_1 + \eta_2)/2$, for $A_2$ = $(\eta_3 + \eta_4)/2$. Choose the higher. Similarly for $B$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

Refer to Section 3.2 of these notes for the full derivation. The log-normal update rule is:

$$
\sigma'_i = \sigma_i \cdot \exp\bigl(\tau_0 \cdot N_i(0,1)\bigr), \quad \tau_0 = \frac{c}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

**Why multiplicative (not additive)?**

1. **Scale invariance:** A multiplicative update of the form $\sigma' = \sigma \cdot e^{\tau N(0,1)}$ is invariant under rescaling of the search space. If the object variables $x_i$ are scaled by a constant $c$, the optimal $\sigma$ is also scaled by $c$, and the rule preserves this relationship. An additive rule $\sigma' = \sigma + \tau N(0,1)$ would not.

2. **Positivity guarantee:** Multiplicative update with positive exponent ensures $\sigma' > 0$ always, which is essential since $\sigma$ is a standard deviation and must be positive.

3. **Log-normal distribution:** Taking the logarithm gives a normal distribution:
   $$\ln \sigma' = \ln \sigma + \tau N(0,1)$$
   This makes the variance of $\ln \sigma$ equal to $\tau^2$ — a clean, controllable parameter.

4. **Unbiased expectation:** $\mathbb{E}[\sigma'] \approx \sigma$ to first order, so the rule neither systematically grows nor shrinks $\sigma$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing tuning with control** — examiners deduct 2–3 marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 2. **Omitting assumptions** in derivations (e.g., Schema Theorem derivation requires stating the *proportional selection* assumption).
> 3. **Forgetting to define variables** like $L$, $k$, $\ell$ in schema-theorem based answers.
> 4. **Using additive mutation update** in ES self-adaptation — this is **wrong** and loses full marks for part (b).
> 5. **Taguchi without S/N ratio** — the S/N ratio is the *core* of Taguchi's method; omitting it scores <50% on part (a).
> 6. **Mixing up "selection pressure" $s$ and tournament size $k$** — they are related by $s = k^{1/k}$ but are not the same quantity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**

- ☐ Parameter Setting = **Tuning** (offline) ∪ **Control** (online).
- ☐ Three control types: **Deterministic**, **Adaptive**, **Self-Adaptive** — know one example each.
- ☐ **1/5-success rule** of Rechenberg controls $\sigma$ in $(1+1)$-ES using $a \approx 0.817$.
- ☐ **Log-normal self-adaptation** uses $\sigma' = \sigma \cdot e^{\tau N(0,1)}$; multiplicative = scale-invariant.
- ☐ **Boltzmann cooling:** geometric $T(t) = T_0 \alpha^t$ (fast) or logarithmic $T(t) = T_0/\log(t+1)$ (slow).
- ☐ **Selection pressure $s$** for tournament size $k$: $s = k^{1/k}$.
- ☐ **Takeover time** $T^* = \ln(\mu) / \ln(s)$ — measures selection-induced convergence speed.
- ☐ **Schema Theorem** yields the population size bound: $N \geq -\ln \delta \cdot 2^k (\bar{f}/f(H))^t$.
- ☐ **Taguchi method** uses orthogonal arrays (e.g., $L_4, L_8, L_9, L_{16}, L_{27}$) to reduce tuning runs.
- ☐ **F-Race, REVAC, CALIBRA** are sequential/iterative tuning methods; preferred over full-factorial.
- ☐ **Self-adaptive > Adaptive > Deterministic** in robustness, but with added complexity.
- ☐ The KTU 2024 syllabus groups **parameter selection** under the **"Design of EAs"** theme of Module 1.
- ☐ Be ready to write the **block diagram** of self-adaptive EA (chromosome = object + strategy parts).
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** before any derivation; examiners reward this explicitly.
- ☐ For numerical problems, **memorise** the formulas for $s$, $T^*$, $T(t)$, and the population-size bound.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Evolutionary Computing (EC) Applications** refer to the deployment of population-based, stochastic search and optimization meta-heuristics — inspired by the mechanisms of natural evolution (selection, variation, inheritance) — to solve complex, real-world problems across engineering, science, and business domains where classical deterministic methods fail, become intractable, or yield suboptimal solutions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term **"Applications"** under Module 1 specifically maps to the study of *problem domains* where EC paradigms — such as **Genetic Algorithms (GA)**, **Genetic Programming (GP)**, **Evolution Strategies (ES)**, **Evolutionary Programming (EP)**, **Differential Evolution (DE)**, **Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO)**, and **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** — are applied to find near-optimal or satisfactory solutions within reasonable computational budgets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECNT869 / Module 1):** The 2024 scheme positions "Applications" as a *survey and analytical* topic, demanding that the student (a) **enumerate** the major application domains, (b) **classify** the problem types (e.g., NP-hard, multi-modal, constrained, multi-objective), and (c) **justify** the suitability of EC over classical approaches for a given problem class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Survival" of Solutions

Imagine you have a **population of 1,000 candidate solutions** — each is a guess at solving your problem (e.g., the shortest delivery route, the best wing-shape for an aircraft, the optimal neural-network weights). Initially, these guesses are mostly random and poor. Now, apply three "natural" rules repeatedly:

1. **Survival of the Fittest (Selection):** Better solutions are kept; worse ones are discarded — analogous to how cheetahs survive because they run faster than their prey.
2. **Mating & Combination (Crossover):** Two good solutions are merged to produce offspring that *inherit* their strengths — like breeding a fast horse with a strong horse to get a faster, stronger one.
3. **Random Tweaking (Mutation):** Occasionally, random small changes are introduced — analogous to genetic mutations that may (or may not) improve an organism.

After many generations (iterations), the population *evolves* toward excellent solutions. This is the heart of every EC application.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** EC does not require calculus, gradients, or convexity assumptions. It only needs a way to *score* a solution (fitness function). This single property is what makes EC applicable to almost any domain.

### 1.3 The Core Property That Unlocks All Applications

The single property enabling every EC application is:

> **A computable Fitness / Objective Function $f: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ exists for the candidate solution space $\mathcal{S}$.**

If you can score a candidate, you can evolve it.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in EC Applications

Although EC is largely algorithm-driven, application-specific metrics are universal:

- **Computational Budget (in evaluations / generations):** Standard metric is **NFE** (Number of Fitness Evaluations). Typical engineering problems use $10^3$ to $10^7$ NFEs.
- **Convergence Tolerance $\epsilon$:** Default for most applications is $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **Population Size $N$:** Heuristic rule of thumb is $N \in [20, 200]$ for most engineering applications, with $N = 50$ being a common default.
- **Mutation Rate $p_m$:** Typically $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ (per gene).
- **Crossover Rate $p_c$:** Typically $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$.
- **Maximum Generations $G_{max}$:** Problem-dependent; often $100$ to $5{,}000$.

> [!NOTE]
> These values are *application-tuning parameters*, not physical constants. KTU expects students to justify specific values in case-study questions.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of EC Application Domains

EC applications are classified by **problem characteristics**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize the following taxonomy:

#### 2.1.1 By Optimization Type

| Problem Class | Description | Typical EC Algorithm | Real-World Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Continuous / Numerical Optimization** | Real-valued variables, smooth or rugged landscape | GA (real-coded), DE, ES, PSO | Aerodynamic shape design, hyperparameter tuning |
| **Combinatorial Optimization** | Discrete variables, often NP-hard | GA (binary), ACO, GP | TSP, knapsack, scheduling |
| **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO)** | Multiple conflicting objectives, Pareto front | NSGA-II, SPEA2, MOEA/D | Engineering trade-off (cost vs. performance) |
| **Constrained Optimization** | Feasibility constraints present | Penalty-function GA, $\epsilon$-constrained EC | Structural design with load limits |
| **Dynamic Optimization** | Objective function changes over time | Adaptive GA, immigrants-based GA | Stock trading, online routing |
| **Multi-Modal Optimization** | Multiple global optima | Niching GA, Crowding, Fitness Sharing | Clustering, design of multimodal systems |

#### 2.1.2 By Application Sector

| Sector | Representative Problems | Key References in KTU Module 1 |
|---|---|---|
| **Engineering Design** | Truss optimization, antenna design, VLSI floorplanning | Goldberg (1989), Koza (1992) |
| **Machine Learning** | Feature selection, neural-net weight optimization, rule induction | Holland (1975), Whitley (1994) |
| **Scheduling & Planning** | Job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, timetabling | Davis (1985), Fang (1994) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein structure prediction, phylogenetics, gene regulatory networks | Fogel (2006) |
| **Robotics & Control** | Path planning, gait optimization, controller synthesis | Cliff et al. (1993) |
| **Finance** | Portfolio optimization, credit scoring, algorithmic trading | Quah (2008) |
| **Image & Signal Processing** | Image segmentation, filter design, compression | Andrey (1999) |
| **Games & Adversarial Search** | Game-tree search, strategy evolution, NPC behavior | Fogel (2001) |

### 2.2 Theoretical Justification — Why EC Works on These Problems

The following bulleted logic explains the *why* of EC applicability:

- **Step 1: Problem Decomposition.** A real-world problem is encoded as a *chromosome* (a string of decision variables). For a numerical problem, this may be a real-valued vector; for a combinatorial problem, a permutation or binary string.
- **Step 2: Population Initialization.** A population $\mathcal{P} = \{x^{(1)}, x^{(2)}, \ldots, x^{(N)}\}$ is sampled from the search space $\mathcal{S}$ either uniformly or via Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) for better coverage.
- **Step 3: Fitness Evaluation.** Each individual is scored via $f(x^{(i)})$. This is the *only* point of contact with the problem — the EC algorithm is *problem-agnostic*.
- **Step 4: Stochastic Variation.** New offspring are produced via crossover and mutation. Theoretically, the **Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975)** guarantees that short, low-order, above-average schemas grow exponentially across generations.
- **Step 5: Selection Pressure.** Higher-quality individuals are preferentially chosen, ensuring the *building blocks* propagate.
- **Step 6: Iteration & Convergence.** After $G$ generations, the best individual approximates the global optimum, often proven via **Markov Chain convergence theorems** for elitist GAs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Building Block Hypothesis (BBH):** GAs work by combining short, high-fitness sub-solutions (building blocks) to form complete high-fitness solutions — a foundational theoretical justification for EC's success.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fitness Function | $f(x), \; x \in \mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ | Domain-specific |
| 2 | Roulette-Wheel Selection Probability | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | For maximization; $f_i > 0$ |
| 3 | Rank-Based Selection Probability | $P_i = \dfrac{1}{N} \left( \eta^{-} + (\eta^{+} - \eta^{-}) \cdot \dfrac{N - r_i}{N - 1} \right)$ | $\eta^{+} = 2$, $\eta^{-} = 1$, $r_i$ = rank |
| 4 | Single-Point Crossover | Two parents $p_1, p_2$ split at index $k$ to produce two children $c_1, c_2$ | $k \sim U(1, L-1)$ |
| 5 | Uniform Mutation (Real-Coded) | $x_i' = x_i + \sigma \cdot N(0, 1)$ | $\sigma$ = step size (ES) |
| 6 | Bit-Flip Mutation Probability | $P(\text{flip}) = p_m$ per bit | $p_m \approx 1/L$ standard |
| 7 | De Jong's 5 Functions | Sphere, Rosenbrock, Step, Quartic, Shekel's Foxholes | Standard test suite |
| 8 | Schema Theorem (Holland) | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right)$ | $m(H,t)$ = instances, $\delta(H)$ = defining length, $o(H)$ = order |
| 9 | DE/rand/1/bin Mutation | $v_i = x_{r_1} + F \cdot (x_{r_2} - x_{r_3})$ | $F \in [0.4, 1.0]$ |
| 10 | PSO Velocity Update | $v_i(t+1) = w v_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i)$ | $w$: inertia, $c_1, c_2$: accel. constants |
| 11 | ACO Pheromone Update | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}$ | $\rho \in (0,1)$: evaporation |
| 12 | No-Free-Lunch (NFL) Theorem | $\sum_{f} (P(\text{error} \mid f, a_1) - P(\text{error} \mid f, a_2)) = 0$ | No algorithm universally best |
| 13 | NSGA-II Crowding Distance | $d_i = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \dfrac{f_m^{i+1} - f_m^{i-1}}{f_m^{max} - f_m^{min}}$ | For Pareto sorting |
| 14 | Penalty Function Method | $\Phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda \cdot \sum_{j} \max(0, g_j(x))^2$ | $\lambda$ = penalty coefficient |
| 15 | Convergence Indicator | $\Delta f = \vert f_{best}(t) - f_{best}(t-1) \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Stopping criterion |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Cell Rule:** In the formulas above, I have used `\vert` (rendered as a vertical bar) inside expressions like $f(H)/\bar{f}$ and $\vert f_{best}(t) - f_{best}(t-1) \vert$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do NOT use the raw `|` character inside table cells.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of EC Applications

EC has solved problems where classical methods fail:

- **NP-Hardness Bypass:** TSP with 1,000 cities is intractable for exact solvers ($>10^{2500}$ years); EC provides near-optimal solutions in minutes.
- **Black-Box Optimization:** When the objective function has no analytical form (e.g., simulation-based design), EC only needs the output value, not the gradient.
- **Robustness to Noise:** Stochastic EC methods handle noisy fitness evaluations (e.g., experimental measurements) gracefully via implicit averaging.
- **Parallelism:** EC populations are naturally parallelizable on GPUs, clusters, and FPGAs — ideal for high-throughput industrial optimization.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975)

The **Schema Theorem** is the central theoretical justification for *why* EC works in any application. Below is its complete, KTU-exam-ready derivation.

**Setup (Model Assumptions):**
- Population size: $N$
- Chromosome length: $\ell$ (binary alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$ where $*$ is a wildcard)
- A schema $H$ is a template with fixed bits and wildcard bits.
- $\delta(H)$ = *defining length* = distance between first and last fixed bit.
- $o(H)$ = *order* = number of fixed bits.
- $f(H, t)$ = average fitness of schema $H$ at generation $t$.
- $\bar{f}(t)$ = average fitness of population at generation $t$.

**Step 1: Number of schema instances at time $t$:**

$$
m(H, t) = \#\{x \in \mathcal{P}(t) \mid x \text{ matches } H\}
$$

**Step 2: Selection contribution (Roulette Wheel).**

After proportional selection, the expected number of instances becomes:

$$
E[m(H, t+1)]_{\text{select}} = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}(t)}
$$

*Explanation:* Each individual in $H$ is replicated with probability proportional to its fitness, scaled by the mean population fitness.

**Step 3: Crossover disruption.**

A schema $H$ is destroyed by single-point crossover with probability at most:

$$
P_{\text{disrupt}} \leq p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}
$$

*Explanation:* The crossover point must fall inside the defining length $\delta(H)$ to split the fixed bits. There are $\ell - 1$ possible split points.

So the survival probability is at least $1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}$.

**Step 4: Mutation disruption.**

Each fixed bit flips with probability $p_m$. Since there are $o(H)$ fixed bits, the probability that *at least one* flips is at most $p_m \cdot o(H)$ (union bound for small $p_m$).

Survival probability: $1 - p_m \cdot o(H)$.

**Step 5: Combine all effects.**

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right)
$$

**Step 6: Simplified asymptotic form (when $f(H) \gg \bar{f}$ and $\delta, o$ are small).**

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)
$$

for some small $\epsilon > 0$.

**Step 7: Iterating the recurrence.**

$$
m(H, t) \geq m(H, 0) \cdot \left(\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)\right)^{t}
$$

*Conclusion:* Short, low-order, above-average schemas grow **exponentially** across generations — the formal *building-block justification* for EC's applicability to all problem classes.

### 3.2 Derivation: Roulette-Wheel Selection Probability

Given population $\{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$ with fitness $\{f_1, \ldots, f_N\}$, probability of selecting $x_i$ is:

$$
P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}
$$

**Verification of probability axiom:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} \\
&= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j} \\
&= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Applying GA to a Knapsack Problem

**Problem:** Maximize $\sum_{i=1}^{4} v_i x_i$ subject to $\sum_{i=1}^{4} w_i x_i \leq W$, where items have $(v_i, w_i) = (10, 2), (20, 3), (15, 4), (25, 5)$ and $W = 8$.

**Step 1: Encoding.** Use binary chromosome of length 4: $x_1 x_2 x_3 x_4$.

**Step 2: Population initialization.** $N = 4$ individuals:
- $A = 1010$ (items 1, 3)
- $B = 0110$ (items 2, 3)
- $C = 1100$ (items 1, 2)
- $D = 0011$ (items 3, 4)

**Step 3: Fitness evaluation.**

| Individual | Weight | Feasible? | Value = Fitness |
|---|---|---|---|
| A = 1010 | 2+4=6 | Yes | 25 |
| B = 0110 | 3+4=7 | Yes | 35 |
| C = 1100 | 2+3=5 | Yes | 30 |
| D = 0011 | 4+5=9 | No | Penalty → 0 |

**Step 4: Selection probability (Roulette Wheel).**

Total fitness = 25 + 35 + 30 + 0 = 90.

$$
P_A = \frac{25}{90} = 0.278, \quad P_B = \frac{35}{90} = 0.389, \quad P_C = \frac{30}{90} = 0.333, \quad P_D = 0
$$

**Step 5: Selection outcome (expected count).** A: 1.11, B: 1.56, C: 1.33, D: 0. **Best individual is B = 0110 (value 35).**

**Step 6: Crossover (e.g., A and C at point 2).**

$$
A = 10 \vert 10 \to 10 \vert 00 = A' = 1000 \; (\text{value} = 10)
$$

$$
C = 11 \vert 00 \to 11 \vert 10 = C' = 1110 \; (\text{value} = 45)
$$

**Step 7: Verification of constraint for $C'$.** Weight = 2+3+4 = 9 > 8. **Infeasible.** Apply penalty: $f(C') = 45 - \lambda \cdot (9-8)$.

**Step 8: Iteration.** Over generations, the population converges toward feasible high-value individuals. The known optimal is $x^* = 1101$ or $0110$ with value 35–40.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm-to-Application Mapping

| Application Domain | Problem Class | Recommended EC Algorithm | Encoding | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aerospace Wing Design | Continuous, constrained | ES / DE / CMA-ES | Real-valued vector | Gradient-free, handles rugged landscape |
| Travelling Salesman (TSP) | Combinatorial, NP-hard | ACO / GA with OX crossover | Permutation | Pheromone trails, edge-recombination |
| Neural Net Training | Continuous, high-dim | DE / PSO / GA | Real-valued weight vector | Avoids local minima, parallelizable |
| Job-Shop Scheduling | Combinatorial, constrained | GA with precedence-preserving crossover | Permutation + priority rules | Constraint handling via repair operators |
| Portfolio Optimization | Continuous, MOO | NSGA-II | Real-valued weight vector | Pareto front of risk-return trade-offs |
| Protein Folding | Continuous, NP-hard | GA + simulated annealing hybrid | Real-valued dihedral angles | Hybrid escapes local optima |
| Antenna Design | Continuous, multi-modal | Niching GA / crowding DE | Real-valued parameters | Multiple optima preservation |
| Game Strategy Evolution | Mixed, adaptive | GP / co-evolution | Tree or rule-based | Symbolic strategy representation |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three major application domains of Evolutionary Computing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three major application domains of Evolutionary Computing are:

1. **Engineering Design Optimization (1 mark):** EC is used to optimize aerodynamic shapes, truss structures, VLSI layouts, and antenna configurations where the objective function is non-convex, multi-modal, or non-differentiable. Algorithms like **Evolution Strategies (ES)** and **Differential Evolution (DE)** are preferred for their efficiency on continuous domains.

2. **Combinatorial Optimization — Scheduling and Routing (1 mark):** Problems like the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP), job-shop scheduling, and vehicle routing are NP-hard. **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** and **Genetic Algorithms with specialized crossovers (e.g., Order Crossover)** provide near-optimal solutions in polynomial time.

3. **Machine Learning — Feature Selection and Model Training (1 mark):** EC is used to evolve neural-network weights, select optimal feature subsets, and induce rule-based classifiers. **Genetic Programming (GP)** and **DE-trained neural networks** offer gradient-free alternatives to backpropagation, avoiding local minima.

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the fitness function in any EC application. Why is it called the only "point of contact" with the problem?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **fitness function** $f(x)$ evaluates the quality of a candidate solution $x$ and guides the evolutionary search (1 mark). In EC, the algorithm is **problem-agnostic** — it knows nothing about the application domain. The only information it receives about the problem is the scalar fitness value returned by $f(x)$ (1 mark). This decouples the algorithm from the application: the same GA code can optimize antenna shapes, financial portfolios, or neural-net weights simply by changing the fitness function (1 mark). This property is the **key enabler** of EC's broad applicability.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the major application domains of Evolutionary Computing. For each domain, identify the problem characteristics that make EC preferable over classical optimization methods. Also, describe the Schema Theorem and explain how it theoretically justifies EC's success in diverse applications.**

**Model Solution (14 marks, split into two 7-mark parts):**

**Part (a) — Major Application Domains of EC (7 marks):**

**(i) Engineering Design Optimization (2 marks):**
EC is extensively used in **structural design** (truss weight minimization), **aerodynamic shape optimization** (airfoil design, drag reduction), **VLSI floorplanning**, and **electromagnetic antenna design**. The objective function is typically:
- **Non-convex** (multiple local optima)
- **Non-differentiable** (based on CFD/FEA simulations)
- **Black-box** (no analytical gradient available)

Classical gradient-based methods (Newton's, BFGS) require differentiability and convexity assumptions. EC methods (DE, ES, CMA-ES) are **gradient-free** and **global**, making them superior for these problems.

**(ii) Machine Learning (1.5 marks):**
EC is used for **feature selection** (binary GA), **neural-network weight optimization** (DE/PSO replaces backpropagation), **hyperparameter tuning** (GA on learning rate, depth, regularization), and **symbolic regression** (GP discovers equations from data). EC handles the **multi-modal, high-dimensional loss landscape** of ML models.

**(iii) Combinatorial Optimization (1.5 marks):**
**TSP, knapsack, graph coloring, job-shop scheduling** are NP-hard. Exact methods (branch-and-bound) are infeasible for large instances. **ACO** uses pheromone trails to construct solutions, while **GA with OX/PMX crossover** evolves permutations. Both achieve near-optimal solutions in seconds.

**(iv) Bioinformatics (1 mark):**
**Protein structure prediction** (HP-model folding), **DNA sequence alignment**, **phylogenetic tree construction**, and **gene regulatory network inference** are EC applications. The problems have complex, rugged landscapes and large search spaces.

**(v) Robotics, Finance, and Other Domains (1 mark):**
EC is used for **robot path planning** (obstacle avoidance), **portfolio optimization** (NSGA-II for risk-return trade-offs), **game strategy evolution** (co-evolutionary GA), and **image segmentation** (GA optimizes thresholds).

**Part (b) — Schema Theorem and Theoretical Justification (7 marks):**

**Statement of the Schema Theorem (3 marks):**

Let $H$ be a schema with defining length $\delta(H)$ and order $o(H)$. Then the expected number of instances of $H$ in generation $t+1$ satisfies:

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right)
$$

**Derivation Outline (2.5 marks):**
- **Selection** multiplies instances by $f(H)/\bar{f}$ (fitter schemas survive).
- **Crossover** disrupts schemas with probability at most $p_c \cdot \delta(H)/(\ell-1)$.
- **Mutation** flips at least one fixed bit with probability at most $p_m \cdot o(H)$.
- Combining these three multiplicative effects gives the lower bound above.

**Asymptotic Form (1 mark):**
For short, low-order, above-average schemas ($f(H) \gg \bar{f}$, $\delta, o$ small):

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot (1 - \epsilon)
$$

**Implications for EC Applications (0.5 mark):**
The schema theorem proves that EC preferentially combines **building blocks** (short, high-fitness sub-solutions) — explaining why EC works for *any* problem where such building blocks exist, which is virtually all practical engineering and scientific problems.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Apply Evolutionary Computing to (a) the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and (b) Neural Network weight optimization. For each, describe the encoding scheme, fitness function, genetic operators, and justify why EC outperforms classical methods.**

**Model Solution (14 marks, split into two 7-mark parts):**

**Part (a) — EC for Travelling Salesman Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Problem Statement (0.5 mark):**
Given $n$ cities with distances $d(c_i, c_j)$, find a permutation $\pi$ of cities that **minimizes** the total tour length:

$$
L(\pi) = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} d(\pi_i, \pi_{i+1}) + d(\pi_n, \pi_1)
$$

TSP is **NP-hard** for $n \geq 50$; exact solvers become infeasible.

**(ii) Encoding (1 mark):**
Use a **permutation chromosome** of length $n$, e.g., $[3, 1, 4, 2, 5]$ for a 5-city TSP. Each city appears exactly once.

**(iii) Fitness Function (0.5 mark):**
$$
f(\pi) = \frac{1}{L(\pi)} \quad \text{(or } -L(\pi)\text{ if minimization)}
$$
Shorter tours have higher fitness.

**(iv) Genetic Operators (2 marks):**
- **Selection:** Tournament selection (size 2–4) — robust to noise and fast.
- **Crossover:** **Order Crossover (OX)** or **Partially Mapped Crossover (PMX)** preserve permutation validity. OX copies a substring from one parent and fills remaining positions in the order they appear in the other parent.
- **Mutation:** **Inversion mutation** (reverse a sub-segment) or **swap mutation** (swap two cities). Both maintain valid permutations.

**(v) Algorithm Flow (1 mark):**
1. Initialize population of $N$ random permutations.
2. Evaluate fitness $f(\pi)$ for each.
3. Apply selection → crossover → mutation to produce offspring.
4. Replace (with elitism, e.g., keep top 10%).
5. Repeat for $G_{max}$ generations.

**(vi) Why EC Outperforms Classical Methods (2 marks):**
- **Exact methods** (Held-Karp dynamic programming) are $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ — infeasible for $n > 50$.
- **Heuristics** (nearest neighbor, 2-opt) are fast but stuck in local optima.
- **EC** explores the permutation space globally, escapes local optima via mutation, and produces near-optimal solutions (within 1–5% of optimum) in $O(N \cdot G \cdot n)$ time — polynomial and practical for $n = 1{,}000+$.

**Part (b) — EC for Neural Network Weight Optimization (7 marks):**

**(i) Problem Statement (0.5 mark):**
Given training data $\{(x_k, y_k)\}_{k=1}^{K}$ and a neural network with weight vector $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$, find $w$ that **minimizes** the mean-squared error:

$$
\text{MSE}(w) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K} (y_k - \hat{y}_k(w))^2
$$

**(ii) Encoding (1 mark):**
**Real-valued chromosome** of length $d$ (the total number of weights and biases). E.g., for a 3-4-2 network: $d = (3 \times 4) + 4 + (4 \times 2) + 2 = 26$.

**(iii) Fitness Function (0.5 mark):**
$$
f(w) = \frac{1}{1 + \text{MSE}(w)} \quad \text{or} \quad f(w) = -\text{MSE}(w)
$$

**(iv) Genetic Operators (2 marks):**
- **Selection:** Tournament or rank-based.
- **Crossover:** **Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX)** or **BLX-$\alpha$** for real-valued genes.
- **Mutation:** **Gaussian mutation** $w_i' = w_i + \sigma \cdot N(0, 1)$, or **polynomial mutation**.

**(v) Recommended Algorithms (1 mark):**
**Differential Evolution (DE/rand/1/bin)** and **PSO** are particularly effective for NN training due to their real-valued, continuous-domain nature and self-adaptive step sizes.

**(vi) Why EC Outperforms Backpropagation (2 marks):**
- **Backpropagation** is gradient-based; it gets stuck in **local minima** of the non-convex MSE landscape, especially for deep or recurrent networks.
- **EC methods** are **global**, exploring multiple regions of the weight space in parallel.
- EC is **robust to noisy gradients**, **handles non-differentiable activation functions** (e.g., ReLU, sign), and **avoids vanishing-gradient issues** common in deep networks.
- Empirically, **DE-trained networks** match or exceed backpropagation accuracy on benchmarks (MNIST, UCI datasets), and **PSO** is widely used in evolving RBF networks and ANFIS systems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Applications" Questions:**
> 1. **Listing without justification (–2 marks):** Merely enumerating domains (e.g., "EC is used in engineering, finance, biology") without explaining *why* EC is suitable for each (problem class, algorithm choice, encoding) is incomplete. The 2024 scheme demands **critical reasoning**, not recall.
> 2. **Ignoring the fitness function (–2 marks):** A common error is to discuss algorithms without specifying the fitness function. The fitness function is the *contract* between the application and the algorithm; omitting it loses substantial marks.
> 3. **Confusing EC algorithms (–1 to 3 marks):** Students frequently mix up GA, GP, ES, and EP. **Remember:** GA uses binary/real coding with crossover; **GP** evolves *trees* (programs); **ES** emphasizes mutation (Gaussian) over crossover; **EP** evolves finite-state machines. Misattribution costs marks.
> 4. **Skipping the Schema Theorem derivation (–3 to 4 marks):** In 14-mark questions, the schema theorem is often a required sub-part. A memorized statement without the derivation of selection, crossover, and mutation factors is considered incomplete.
> 5. **No real-world numerical example (–2 marks):** A worked example (knapsack, TSP, NN) with actual fitness values and one or two generations of evolution is highly valued. Always include a small numerical illustration.
> 6. **Ignoring constraints (–1 mark):** In real applications, constraints are universal. Forgetting penalty functions or constraint-handling techniques is a common omission.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Definition:** EC Applications = use of population-based meta-heuristics (GA, GP, ES, EP, DE, PSO, ACO) to solve complex, real-world optimization problems where classical methods fail.
- **Enabling Property:** A computable **fitness function** is the only interface between EC and the problem.
- **Six Major Application Domains:** Engineering Design, Machine Learning, Combinatorial Optimization (scheduling/routing), Bioinformatics, Robotics/Control, Finance/Economics.
- **Six Problem Classes:** Continuous, Combinatorial, Multi-Objective, Constrained, Dynamic, Multi-Modal.
- **Algorithm–Problem Mapping:**
  * Continuous → DE, ES, PSO
  * Combinatorial → ACO, Binary GA, GP
  * Multi-Objective → NSGA-II, SPEA2
  * Multi-Modal → Niching GA, Crowding
- **Standard Parameters:** $N \in [20, 200]$, $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$, $p_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$, $\epsilon \in [10^{-6}, 10^{-3}]$.
