- **Schema Theorem (Holland 1975):** $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right)$.
- **Building Block Hypothesis:** EC works by combining short, high-fitness sub-solutions into complete high-fitness solutions.
- **No-Free-Lunch Theorem (Wolpert 1997):** No EC algorithm is universally superior; algorithm choice is problem-dependent.
- **Roulette-Wheel Selection:** $P_i = f_i / \sum_j f_j$; requires $f_i > 0$.
- **DE/rand/1/bin:** $v_i = x_{r_1} + F \cdot (x_{r_2} - x_{r_3})$, $F \in [0.4, 1.0]$.
- **PSO Update:** $v_i(t+1) = w v_i(t) + c_1 r_1 (pbest_i - x_i) + c_2 r_2 (gbest - x_i)$.
- **ACO Pheromone Update:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_k \Delta \tau_{ij}^k$.
- **Real-World Justifications:** EC handles NP-hardness, black-box functions, noise, rugged landscapes, and parallelizable evaluation.
- **Penalty Function for Constraints:** $\Phi(x) = f(x) + \lambda \cdot \sum_j \max(0, g_j(x))^2$.
- **Stopping Criterion:** $\Delta f = \vert f_{best}(t) - f_{best}(t-1) \vert \leq \epsilon$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Tip:** For a 14-mark answer, the ideal structure is **(1) domain enumeration (3 marks) → (2) problem-class justification (3 marks) → (3) algorithm–domain mapping (3 marks) → (4) theoretical support via Schema Theorem (3 marks) → (5) worked numerical example (2 marks)**. Memorize this 5-block structure to maximize marks under exam pressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hill Climbing: Mathematical Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hill Climbing: Mathematical Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hill Climbing** is a deterministic (or stochastic) **iterative local search algorithm** that traverses the solution space of an optimization problem by repeatedly moving from the current state to an adjacent state that improves (or at least does not worsen) the value of an **objective function** $f : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, until no such improving neighbor exists in the local neighborhood.

In the language of the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome CO1, Module 1), Hill Climbing is positioned as the *foundational local-search heuristic* upon which more sophisticated Evolutionary Algorithms (Genetic Algorithms, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search) are conceptually layered. It belongs to the family of **Generate-and-Test** algorithms and operates on a **state-space landscape** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{N}, f \rangle$, where $\mathcal{S}$ is the search space, $\mathcal{N}: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow 2^{\mathcal{S}}$ is a neighborhood structure, and $f$ is the evaluation (fitness) function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In PECNT869, "Hill Climbing" is treated as a *baseline* metaheuristic. The mathematical description focuses on (a) the *move operator*, (b) the *neighborhood relation*, (c) the *acceptance criterion*, and (d) *convergence properties* under discrete and continuous domains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "A Blindfolded Hiker on a Foggy Mountain":**
> Imagine you are placed blindfolded on a hilly terrain during a dense fog. You cannot see the global topography, but you can feel the slope of the ground directly beneath your feet and immediately around you. To reach the highest peak, the only strategy you can use is: **probe one step in each of the four cardinal directions (North, South, East, West)**, measure which step takes you to a higher elevation, and take that step. You repeat this process until **every neighboring step either descends or stays flat** — at which point you declare, "I have reached the summit." This summit may or may not be the tallest mountain (Everest); it is merely the highest point reachable from where you started, given your limited vision.
>
> - **You (the searcher)** = the current candidate solution $x_t$.
> - **The terrain elevation map** = the objective function $f$.
> - **The 4 directions you can step** = the neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x_t)$.
> - **Reaching a local summit** = convergence to a **local optimum** $x^{*}_{local}$.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Terms You Must Memorize for KTU:**
> - **State / Solution Vector $x$** — a single point in the candidate space $\mathcal{S}$.
> - **Objective / Fitness Function $f(x)$** — a scalar measure of solution quality.
> - **Neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$** — the set of all solutions reachable by a single elementary *move* (perturbation) from $x$.
> - **Move Operator $\mathcal{M}$** — the rule that generates $\mathcal{N}(x)$ from $x$.
> - **Local Optimum $x^{*}_{local}$** — a state $x$ such that $\forall x' \in \mathcal{N}(x) : f(x') \le f(x)$.
> - **Global Optimum $x^{*}_{global}$** — $\forall x \in \mathcal{S} : f(x) \le f(x^{*}_{global})$.
> - **Plateau** — a region of $\mathcal{S}$ where $f(x)$ is constant for $x$ and all its neighbors.
> - **Ridge** — a sequence of local optima from which a non-improving lateral move is required to make further progress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical State-Space Landscape

A hill-climbing problem is completely specified by a **triple** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{N}, f \rangle$:

1. **Search Space $\mathcal{S}$** — May be discrete (e.g., $\mathcal{S} = \{0,1\}^n$ binary strings) or continuous ($\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$). The cardinality $\vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ is assumed to be finite or compact.
2. **Neighborhood Function $\mathcal{N} : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow 2^{\mathcal{S}}$** — Maps each state to a finite (or compact) subset of related states. The standard **1-bit flip neighborhood** for binary strings of length $n$ is:
$$\mathcal{N}(x) = \{ x' \in \mathcal{S} \mid x' \text{ differs from } x \text{ in exactly one bit} \}$$
This implies $\vert \mathcal{N}(x) \vert = n$ for binary spaces.
3. **Objective Function $f : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$** — A real-valued quality measure. For *maximization* we seek to maximize $f$; for *minimization* we may equivalently maximize $-f$.

### 2.2 The Canonical Update Rule

At iteration $t$, given current state $x_t$, the algorithm performs the following mathematical operation:

**Steepest-Ascent (Discrete) Hill Climbing:**
$$x_{t+1} = \underset{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}\; f(x')$$

The algorithm **stops** when:
$$f(x_{t+1}) \le f(x_t) \quad \text{AND} \quad \forall x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t) : f(x') \le f(x_t)$$

**Continuous-Domain Gradient Ascent (a.k.a. Gradient Hill Climbing):**
$$x_{t+1} = x_t + \eta_t \cdot \nabla f(x_t)$$
where $\eta_t > 0$ is the **step size (learning rate)** and $\nabla f$ is the gradient vector. This converges to a stationary point satisfying $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in Evolutionary Computing:** Hill Climbing defines the *core learning signal* — *greedy improvement via local information* — that all evolutionary algorithms attempt to approximate. GA crossover/mutation operators are essentially generalized *move operators* operating on populations rather than single solutions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Domain / Notes |
|---|---------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Canonical update (discrete) | $x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)} f(x')$ | Maximization, discrete $\mathcal{S}$ |
| 2 | Canonical update (minimization) | $x_{t+1} = \arg\min_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)} f(x')$ | Equivalent via $-f$ |
| 3 | Gradient-ascent update | $x_{t+1} = x_t + \eta_t \nabla f(x_t)$ | Continuous $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| 4 | Neighborhood (binary, 1-flip) | $\mathcal{N}(x) = \{x' : d_H(x,x') = 1\}$ | $d_H$ = Hamming distance |
| 5 | Termination (local max) | $\forall x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t) : f(x') \le f(x_t)$ | Plateau handled separately |
| 6 | Acceptance prob. (stochastic) | $P(\text{accept } x') = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x') \ge f(x_t) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Greedy; no sideways moves |
| 7 | Random-restart expected cost | $E[\text{restarts}] = 1 / p$ | $p$ = success probability per run |
| 8 | Convergence (asymptotic) | $\lim_{t \to \infty} f(x_t) = f(x^{*}_{local})$ | Under greedy acceptance |
| 9 | Continuous optimality (necessary) | $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$ | First-order condition |
| 10 | Continuous optimality (sufficient) | $H_f(x^{*}) \succ 0$ (max) or $H_f(x^{*}) \prec 0$ (min) | $H_f$ = Hessian matrix |

> **Notation Reminder:** In the table, $\vert \cdot \vert$ has been written as `vert` or `mid` to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. In your answer sheet, always use proper LaTeX notation.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Hill Climbing underpins practical applications in **VLSI circuit partitioning**, **neural network weight initialization (pre-training)**, **job-shop scheduling**, **antenna design parameter tuning**, and **robotic path planning**. In all of these, the *objective function* $f$ is the system performance metric, and the *neighborhood* is defined by allowable hardware/software parameter perturbations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Greedy Heuristic to Fixed-Point Equation

The mathematical essence of Hill Climbing is the **fixed-point characterization** of a local optimum. We derive it below in full KTU-valuation rigor.

**Given:**
- Search space $\mathcal{S}$
- Objective $f : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ (assume $f$ is *bounded* above, i.e., $\sup_{x \in \mathcal{S}} f(x) = f^{*} < \infty$).
- Neighborhood $\mathcal{N} : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow 2^{\mathcal{S}}$, assumed non-empty for all $x$.

**Step 1 — Define the Local-Successor Function.**
Let $G : \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathcal{S}$ be the *greedy successor map*:
$$G(x) = \underset{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x)}{\operatorname{arg\,max}}\; f(x')$$

> *Why this step?* It encapsulates the *entire* decision-making of hill climbing into a single deterministic function. From this, the algorithm is just *iterated application* of $G$.

**Step 2 — Express the Algorithm as an Iteration.**
$$x_{t+1} = G(x_t), \quad x_0 = x_{\text{init}}$$

> *Why?* Standard fixed-point iteration. Convergence is guaranteed by the *Monotone Sequence Theorem* (Step 4).

**Step 3 — Show Monotonicity of the Objective.**
By construction, $f(G(x)) \ge f(x)$ for all $x \in \mathcal{S}$ (since $G(x)$ is chosen to maximize $f$ over $\mathcal{N}(x)$, and $x \in \mathcal{N}(x)$ is allowed for the case $\mathcal{N}(x) \supseteq \{x\}$). Hence:
$$f(x_{t+1}) = f(G(x_t)) \ge f(x_t)$$

**Step 4 — Show Convergence to a Local Optimum.**
The sequence $\{f(x_t)\}_{t=0}^{\infty}$ is non-decreasing and bounded above by $f^{*}$. Therefore, it converges. Let:
$$L = \lim_{t \to \infty} f(x_t)$$

By the Bolzano–Weierstrass theorem on the (assumed finite) set of possible $f$-values, there exists $T$ large enough such that $f(x_{T+1}) = f(x_T)$. At this point:
$$f(x_T) = f(G(x_T)) = \max_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_T)} f(x')$$

This is precisely the local optimality condition, so $x_T = x^{*}_{local}$.

**Step 5 — The Fixed-Point Equation.**
Combining, a hill-climbing local optimum $x^{*}_{local}$ satisfies:
$$x^{*}_{local} = G(x^{*}_{local}) \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad f(x^{*}_{local}) = \max_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x^{*}_{local})} f(x')$$

This is the *defining fixed-point equation* of hill climbing.

### 3.2 Derivation: Expected Number of Restarts (Random-Restart Hill Climbing)

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU boards.

**Given:**
- Each independent hill-climbing run has probability $p \in (0,1]$ of finding the global optimum.
- The number of restarts $R$ needed follows a geometric distribution.

**Derivation:**
A run succeeds on the $k$-th attempt if the first $k-1$ runs fail and the $k$-th run succeeds. The probability of failure of one run is $(1-p)$. Therefore:
$$P(R = k) = (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

The expected number of runs (initial run + restarts) is:
$$E[R] = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} p = \frac{1}{p}$$

**Final result (boxed for KTU answer sheet):**
$$E[R] = \frac{1}{p}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If each run has a 20% chance of finding the global optimum, we expect $1/0.2 = 5$ restarts on average. This justifies *Random-Restart Hill Climbing* as a powerful extension that converts a local optimizer into a global one (in expectation), provided $p$ is non-negligible.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Variants of Hill Climbing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison Table**

| Variant | Neighbor Selection | Acceptance Rule | Pros | Cons |
|---------|-------------------|-----------------|------|------|
| **Simple (Next-Ascent)** | Pick a random neighbor $x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)$ | Accept if $f(x') > f(x_t)$ | Cheap, simple | Slow; random ordering bias |
| **Steepest-Ascent** | Evaluate *all* neighbors; pick best $x' = \arg\max f$ | Accept if $f(x') > f(x_t)$ | Fastest local progress | Most expensive per iteration |
| **Stochastic (First-Choice)** | Sample one random $x'$ | Accept if $f(x') \ge f(x_t)$ | Handles large $\mathcal{N}$ | High variance in trajectory |
| **Random-Restart** | Any of the above | Any of the above; **restart from new $x_0$ on convergence** | Approximates global optimum | Costly if $p$ is small |
| **Gradient Ascent (continuous)** | Move along $\nabla f$ | Always accept (or with line search) | Smooth, fast convergence | Requires differentiable $f$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Initialize x0 randomly]
    B --> C[Set t = 0]
    C --> D[Evaluate all neighbors in Nxt]
    D --> E[Find x_best = argmax f xprime in Nxt]
    E --> F{f x_best greater than f x_t}
    F -- YES --> G[Set x_t+1 = x_best]
    G --> H[Increment t]
    H --> D
    F -- NO --> I([Stop: Local Optimum Reached])
    I --> J[Return x_t as solution]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Random-Restart Hill Climbing

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize --> Climb: Select random x0
    Climb --> Climb: Improve via N x and accept
    Climb --> CheckTerm: No improving neighbor
    CheckTerm --> Restart: Random x0 new start
    CheckTerm --> Accept: Global optimum found
    Restart --> Climb
    Accept --> [*]: Output x_star
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Hill-Climbing Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        R[Random Number Generator]
    end
    R --> INIT[Initialization Module]
    INIT -->|x0| EVAL[Fitness Evaluator f x]
    EVAL -->|f x_t| COMP[Neighbor Generator N x_t]
    COMP -->|candidates| SEL[Selection Operator]
    SEL -->|x_best| DEC{Acceptance Test}
    DEC -->|Yes| UPD[Update x_t+1 = x_best]
    DEC -->|No| TERM[Termination Module]
    UPD --> EVAL
    TERM -->|x_star| OUT[(Output: Best Solution)]
```

### 4.4 Landscape Schematic (Conceptual Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0((x0)) -- ascent --> L1((x1))
    L1 -- ascent --> L2((x2))
    L2 -- ascent --> L3((x3 local peak))
    L3 -. no ascent .-> L3
    L3 == restart ==> R0((x0 new))
    R0 -- ascent --> R1((x1 alt))
    R1 -- ascent --> R2((x2 alt))
    R2 -- ascent --> R3((x_star global peak))
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = improving moves. Dotted arrow = attempted move that fails the acceptance test. Double arrow = random restart. The hill-climber is stuck at $x_3$ (local peak) but a restart to a new initial point eventually reaches $x^{*}_{global}$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECNT869]**
> *Define Hill Climbing. State its key components in terms of the triple $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{N}, f \rangle$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hill Climbing is an iterative local search algorithm that moves from a current solution $x_t$ to a neighboring solution $x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)$ if and only if $f(x') \ge f(x_t)$, terminating when no such improvement is possible. The three key components are:
- **$\mathcal{S}$**: the search (solution) space containing all feasible candidates.
- **$\mathcal{N}$**: the neighborhood function mapping each $x$ to a set of related solutions.
- **$f$**: the objective/fitness function $f: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies solution quality.

*(Distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the triple.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper PECNT869]**
> *Differentiate between Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing and Stochastic (First-Choice) Hill Climbing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | Steepest-Ascent | Stochastic (First-Choice) |
|--------|----------------|---------------------------|
| Neighbor selection | Evaluates **all** of $\mathcal{N}(x_t)$ and picks the best | Samples one random $x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)$ |
| Computational cost per iteration | $O(\vert \mathcal{N}(x_t) \vert)$ evaluations | $O(1)$ evaluation per probe (but may take many probes) |
| Determinism | Deterministic given $x_t$ and $f$ | Stochastic; trajectories vary between runs |
| Best suited for | Small neighborhoods | Large / black-box neighborhoods |

*(Distribution: 1 mark for selection rule + 1 mark for cost + 1 mark for determinism.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECNT869]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*Consider the optimization problem: maximize $f(x_1, x_2) = -(x_1^2 + x_2^2) + 4x_1 + 6x_2$ over $\mathcal{S} = \mathbb{R}^2$ using Gradient-Ascent Hill Climbing. Start from $x_0 = (0, 0)^T$ with a constant step size $\eta = 0.1$.*
*(a)* *Derive the gradient $\nabla f$ and the update rule.* *(7 marks)*
*(b)* *Perform two iterations of the algorithm. Show the trajectory $(x_0, x_1, x_2)$ and the corresponding $f$-values. Does the algorithm converge, and to what point?* *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Gradient and Update Rule (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute partial derivatives:
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = -2x_1 + 4, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -2x_2 + 6$$

**Step 2:** Assemble the gradient vector:
$$\nabla f(x) = \begin{pmatrix} -2x_1 + 4 \\ -2x_2 + 6 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** State the update rule for gradient-ascent hill climbing:
$$x_{t+1} = x_t + \eta \cdot \nabla f(x_t)$$

**Step 4:** Verify the function is concave: The Hessian is
$$H_f = \begin{pmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{pmatrix}$$
which is negative-definite. Hence $f$ has a unique maximum (the problem is concave maximization).

**Step 5:** Compute the global maximum analytically by setting $\nabla f = 0$:
$$-2x_1 + 4 = 0 \implies x_1 = 2$$
$$-2x_2 + 6 = 0 \implies x_2 = 3$$
$$x^{*}_{global} = (2, 3)^T, \quad f(x^{*}_{global}) = -(4+9)+8+18 = 13$$

**Part (b) — Two Iterations (7 marks):**

**Iteration 0 → 1:** $x_0 = (0,0)^T$, $f(x_0) = 0$.
$$\nabla f(x_0) = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix}$$
$$x_1 = x_0 + 0.1 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$$
$$f(x_1) = -(0.16+0.36)+1.6+3.6 = -0.52 + 5.2 = 4.68$$

**Iteration 1 → 2:** $x_1 = (0.4, 0.6)^T$.
$$\nabla f(x_1) = \begin{pmatrix} -2(0.4)+4 \\ -2(0.6)+6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 3.2 \\ 4.8 \end{pmatrix}$$
$$x_2 = x_1 + 0.1 \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 3.2 \\ 4.8 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.72 \\ 1.08 \end{pmatrix}$$
$$f(x_2) = -(0.5184+1.1664)+2.88+6.48 = -1.6848 + 9.36 = 7.6752$$

**Convergence Analysis:** The sequence $f(x_0)=0, f(x_1)=4.68, f(x_2)=7.6752$ is monotonically increasing. The sequence $\{x_t\}$ converges to $x^{*}_{global}=(2,3)^T$ as $t \to \infty$ because $f$ is strictly concave. Closed-form solution to the iteration gives:
$$x_t = (2,3)^T - (1-\eta \cdot 2)^t \cdot (2,3)^T = (2,3)^T \cdot \left[1 - 0.8^t\right]$$
which converges geometrically with ratio $0.8$ per step.

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
*(a)* *Explain the limitations of Hill Climbing with reference to local optima, plateaus, and ridges. Use diagrams in your description.* *(7 marks)*
*(b)* *Describe the Random-Restart Hill Climbing algorithm. Prove that the expected number of restarts required to find the global optimum is $E[R] = 1/p$ where $p$ is the per-run success probability.* *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Limitations (7 marks):**
- **Local Optima:** The algorithm terminates at a peak that is not the global maximum. Reference: schematic showing $x_0$ climbing to $x^{*}_{local}$ instead of $x^{*}_{global}$.
- **Plateaus:** A region where $f(x) = f(x')$ for all neighbors. The algorithm may stall indefinitely (assuming no sideways moves allowed). Mitigation: allow sideways moves with a fixed bound.
- **Ridges:** A sequence of steep maxima separated by narrow descents. Greedy moves cannot traverse ridges efficiently because the required direction is perpendicular to the local gradient.

**Part (b) — Random-Restart Algorithm & Derivation (7 marks):**

**Algorithm:**
1. Generate random $x_0 \in \mathcal{S}$.
2. Run hill climbing from $x_0$ until local optimum $x^{*}_{local}$ is reached.
3. If $x^{*}_{local}$ satisfies the global optimality condition, halt. Else, return to step 1 with a fresh random $x_0$.

**Derivation of $E[R] = 1/p$:** *(See Section 3.2 above for the full geometric-distribution proof.)*
The number of runs $R$ needed to first reach global success follows a geometric distribution with success probability $p$, giving $E[R] = 1/p$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students write the update rule as $x_{t+1} = \arg\max f(x_t)$ — **WRONG**. The argmax must be over $\mathcal{N}(x_t)$, not over $x_t$ itself. Always write $x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)} f(x')$. **(–1 mark deduction)**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *local* and *global* optima. Always state the *fixed-point condition* explicitly when claiming convergence.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In the random-restart proof, students often forget to define $p$ as the *probability of success per run*. Without this, the proof is incomplete. **(–2 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting that Hill Climbing *cannot* escape a local optimum in a single trajectory — only randomization (restarts) or non-greedy acceptance (simulated annealing) can.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam**

- **Definition (must-memorize):** Hill Climbing is a Generate-and-Test local search with a *greedy* acceptance rule.
- **The Triple:** $\langle \mathcal{S}, \mathcal{N}, f \rangle$ — Search space, Neighborhood, Objective.
- **Update Rule:** $x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_t)} f(x')$ (maximization) or its continuous analog $x_{t+1} = x_t + \eta \nabla f(x_t)$.
- **Termination:** $f(x_{t+1}) \le f(x_t)$ — equivalent to the fixed-point condition $x = G(x)$.
- **Five Variants to Know:** Simple, Steepest-Ascent, Stochastic (First-Choice), Random-Restart, Gradient Ascent.
- **Three Classic Limitations:** Local optima, Plateaus, Ridges.
- **Three Solutions to Limitations:** Random restarts, sideways moves, non-greedy acceptance (link to Simulated Annealing in later modules).
- **Key Result:** $E[\text{restarts for global success}] = 1/p$.
- **Mathematical Connection:** Hill Climbing is a special case of fixed-point iteration and a continuous analog of gradient ascent; it is the conceptual *parent* of all population-based evolutionary algorithms.
- **Engineering Examples:** VLSI partitioning, antenna tuning, neural-net pre-training, scheduling.
- **Exam Tip:** Always state the *fixed-point condition* explicitly when discussing convergence. Always define $p$ explicitly when proving the $1/p$ restart formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Hill Climbing Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Hill Climbing Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hill Climbing** is a mathematical optimization technique belonging to the family of **local search algorithms**. It iteratively moves from the current state (solution) to a neighboring state that improves the value of an **objective function** $f(x)$, terminating when no neighbor offers a better value or a **local optimum** / **global optimum** is reached.

Mathematically, a hill climbing search can be expressed as a discrete iterative mapping:

$$x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x \in N(x_t)} f(x)$$

where $N(x_t)$ is the neighborhood set of the current state $x_t$, and $f(x)$ is the **heuristic evaluation function** to be maximized.

A **Hill Climbing Algorithm** is therefore any member of a class of search procedures that, given a current node in a state-space graph, selects a successor node such that the value of the heuristic is *maximally increased* (steepest ascent), *increased by any positive amount* (simple ascent), or chosen *probabilistically* (stochastic ascent), until a peak/plateau is encountered.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blindfolded Mountain Climber"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a climber, blindfolded, dropped at a random point on a rugged, hilly terrain in thick fog. The climber can only feel the ground slope immediately beneath his feet. To reach the highest peak, he takes a step in the direction where the ground slopes **steepest upward**. He keeps doing this until every direction around him either slopes **downward** or is **flat**. At that moment, he concludes he is on a peak and stops.

This analogy maps directly to the algorithm:

| Real-World Element | Algorithm Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Mountain terrain | The search space (solution space) |
| Ground slope | Gradient of the objective function $f(x)$ |
| Climber's position | Current candidate solution $x_t$ |
| Step taken | Move to neighboring state $x_{t+1}$ |
| Reached peak | Local (or global) optimum |
| Thick fog / blindfold | No knowledge of the *global* landscape |
| Foot-step options | The **neighborhood** $N(x_t)$ |

## 1.3 Why Multiple Types?

Different terrains have different properties — sharp ridges, gentle slopes, broad plateaus, many false peaks. A single climbing strategy cannot handle *all* terrains efficiently. Hence, KTU classifies Hill Climbing into distinct **types**, each varying the rule by which the next neighbor is selected.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification typically covers **Simple Hill Climbing**, **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing**, **Stochastic Hill Climbing**, and **Random-Restart Hill Climbing**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Skeleton of *Any* Hill Climbing Procedure

Regardless of the variant, every hill climbing algorithm follows this universal 3-stage structure:

1. **Initialization:** Pick or generate an initial state $x_0 \in S$ (the solution space $S$).
2. **Neighbor Generation & Evaluation:** Define a neighborhood operator $N: S \rightarrow 2^S$ that maps each state to a set of "nearby" states. Evaluate $f(x')$ for each $x' \in N(x_t)$.
3. **Selection & Move:** Apply the **variant-specific rule** to pick the next state $x_{t+1}$ from $N(x_t)$. If no better state exists, **terminate** (peak reached).

> [!NOTE]
> The *only* difference between variants is **Step 3 — the rule used to pick the next state**.

## 2.2 Type 1 — Simple Hill Climbing (First-Choice / Naïve)

**Operational Rule:** Examine the neighborhood in some fixed order. Move to the **first** neighbor that improves $f$. Do not look at the rest of the neighbors.

**Decision Rule:**

$$x_{t+1} = \text{first } x' \in N(x_t) \text{ such that } f(x') > f(x_t)$$

If no such $x'$ exists, stop.

- ✅ **Strength:** Fastest in time-per-iteration; minimal memory.
- ❌ **Weakness:** Order-biased; can get trapped on a local optimum; may miss the steepest ascent.

## 2.3 Type 2 — Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing (Gradient Ascent)

**Operational Rule:** Evaluate **every** neighbor in $N(x_t)$ and move to the one with the **maximum** improvement. This is the closest discrete analogue of gradient ascent.

**Decision Rule:**

$$x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x' \in N(x_t)} f(x')$$

**Difference Formulation:** Let the discrete gradient at $x_t$ be:

$$\nabla_d f(x_t) = \max_{x' \in N(x_t)} \left[ f(x') - f(x_t) \right]$$

The step is taken only if $\nabla_d f(x_t) > 0$; otherwise terminate.

- ✅ **Strength:** Largest improvement per move; often reaches a peak in fewer iterations.
- ❌ **Weakness:** Expensive when $\vert N(x_t) \vert$ is large (e.g., exponential neighborhoods in TSP).

## 2.4 Type 3 — Stochastic Hill Climbing

**Operational Rule:** Select a neighbor **at random** from $N(x_t)$. Move to it if (and only if) it is *better* than the current state.

**Decision Rule:**

$$x_{t+1} = \begin{cases} x' & \text{if } f(x') > f(x_t) \text{ and } x' \sim U(N(x_t)) \\ x_t & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The probability of acceptance of a worse state is **0** (in pure stochastic hill climbing). The randomness is in *which* neighbor is examined, not *whether* to accept it.

- ✅ **Strength:** Avoids the ordering bias of simple hill climbing; simple to implement.
- ❌ **Weakness:** May examine many non-improving neighbors before finding a good one; slower convergence per evaluation.

> [!TIP]
> **Variants students confuse with Stochastic HC:**
> - *Random-Mutation Hill Climbing* (a.k.a. RMHC) — generates **one** random neighbor per iteration.
> - *Random-Restart HC* (next section) — restarts the whole climb from a new random point.

## 2.5 Type 4 — Random-Restart Hill Climbing (Meta-Algorithm)

**Operational Rule:** This is a *meta-heuristic* built on top of any base hill climbing method. Run the base hill climber until it gets stuck. Then **restart** from a *new randomly generated* initial state. Repeat for a fixed number of restarts $R$, returning the **best** solution found across all runs.

**Decision Rule:**

$$x^* = \arg\max_{r \in \{1, 2, \dots, R\}} f(x^{(r)}_{\text{final}})$$

where $x^{(r)}_{\text{final}}$ is the local optimum reached on the $r$-th restart.

- ✅ **Strength:** Provably converges to a **global optimum with probability 1** as $R \to \infty$ (if the problem has finite solutions and each restart has non-zero probability of hitting the global basin).
- ❌ **Weakness:** Computationally expensive if the basin of attraction of the global optimum is small.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Variant | Selection Rule (KTU Notation) | Time / Iteration | Memory | Local Optimum Trap Risk |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple HC** | First $x' \in N(x_t)$ with $f(x') > f(x_t)$ | Lowest | $O(1)$ | High (order-sensitive) |
| 2 | **Steepest-Ascent HC** | $\arg\max_{x' \in N(x_t)} f(x')$ | Highest (scans all) | $O(\vert N(x_t) \vert)$ | High (deterministic) |
| 3 | **Stochastic HC** | Random $x' \sim U(N(x_t))$, accept if better | Medium | $O(1)$ | Medium-High |
| 4 | **Random-Restart HC** | Run base HC $\to$ restart $R$ times $\to$ return best | Depends on $R$ | $O(1)$ per run | **Low** (with large $R$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** "**F, S, R, R**" — **F**irst-choice, **S**teepest, **R**andom (stochastic), **R**estart.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **VLSI Design** | Placement & routing optimization (steepest-ascent used in simulated-annealing hybrids) |
| **Robotics & Path Planning** | Local trajectory refinement (stochastic HC) |
| **Machine Learning** | Hyperparameter tuning, weight optimization in shallow NNs (random-restart HC) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein structure prediction (stochastic + restart) |
| **Operations Research** | Combinatorial scheduling, TSP, knapsack variants |
| **Game AI** | Game-tree evaluation in adversarial search |

## 2.8 Common Failure Modes (Critical for KTU Answers)

> [!WARNING]
> Three classic failure modes of *all* hill climbing variants:
> 1. **Local Optima** — Not the global maximum, but locally best.
> 2. **Plateaus** — A flat region where neighboring states have $f(x') = f(x_t)$. Algorithm gets "lost."
> 3. **Ridges** — Sequence of local maxima; greedy moves along a ridge point *sideways* rather than *upward* (problematic for steepest-ascent).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Convergence Analysis of Random-Restart Hill Climbing

Let $p$ = probability that a single run of the base hill climber reaches the **global optimum** (i.e., the random initial state lies in the basin of attraction of the global optimum). For a finite problem with a non-degenerate search space:

$$0 < p \leq 1$$

The probability that **none** of the $R$ independent restarts hits the global optimum is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{fail in } R \text{ restarts}) &= (1 - p)^R
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the probability of **success** (finding the global optimum at least once across $R$ restarts) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success}) &= 1 - (1 - p)^R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering implication:** This expression lets us *quantify* how many restarts are needed to reach a target reliability level. For example, if we want $P(\text{success}) \geq 0.99$ and $p = 0.1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - (1 - 0.1)^R &\geq 0.99 \\
(0.9)^R &\leq 0.01 \\
R \cdot \ln(0.9) &\leq \ln(0.01) \\
R &\geq \frac{\ln(0.01)}{\ln(0.9)} \\
R &\geq \frac{-4.6052}{-0.10536} \\
R &\geq 43.7 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; R_{\min} = 44 \text{ restarts}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **canonical KTU 2024 numerical** — students are often asked to compute minimum restarts for a given success probability.

## 3.2 Derivation: Expected Number of Evaluations — Simple vs. Steepest-Ascent

Let $\vert N \vert$ = size of the neighborhood (assume constant). Suppose the *best* neighbor is at position $k$ in the ordering (uniformly distributed over $\{1, 2, \dots, \vert N \vert\}$).

**For Simple Hill Climbing (first-choice):**
Expected evaluations per move = expected position of the *first improving* neighbor. In the worst case where only one neighbor improves:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{evals}_{\text{simple}}] &= \frac{\vert N \vert + 1}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing:**
Every neighbor *must* be evaluated to find the best:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[\text{evals}_{\text{steepest}}] &= \vert N \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ratio (steepest vs simple):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{ratio}} &= \frac{E[\text{evals}_{\text{steepest}}]}{E[\text{evals}_{\text{simple}}]} = \frac{2 \vert N \vert}{\vert N \vert + 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

For large $\vert N \vert$ (typical in combinatorial problems), $R_{\text{ratio}} \to 2$, meaning steepest-ascent is roughly **twice as expensive per move** as simple hill climbing. However, steepest-ascent often reaches a peak in *fewer moves*, so the trade-off is non-trivial — a frequent KTU conceptual question.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Variant

| Scenario | Recommended Variant | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Neighborhood is small ($\vert N \vert \leq 20$) | **Steepest-Ascent HC** | Marginal cost of scanning all neighbors is low; better quality per move. |
| Neighborhood is large (e.g., exponential) | **Simple HC** or **Stochastic HC** | Scanning all neighbors infeasible; first/best-improving trade-off favours cheap variants. |
| Function is noisy / has many shallow local peaks | **Stochastic HC** | Randomness helps escape shallow traps more easily than deterministic moves. |
| Global optimum is critical; compute is cheap | **Random-Restart HC** with $R \to \infty$ | Provably converges to global optimum with $P=1$ in the limit. |
| Real-time / embedded system | **Simple HC** | Predictable, low-latency, $O(1)$ memory. |
| State-space has ridges | **Stochastic HC** with sideways moves allowed | Deterministic variants get stuck on ridges. |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem:** Consider a 1-D optimization with objective $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$. Start at $x_0 = 0$. Neighborhood is $N(x) = \{x - 0.5, x + 0.5\}$. Apply **Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing** for 3 iterations and report $x_3$ and $f(x_3)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Evaluate at $x_0 = 0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0) &= -(0)^2 + 4(0) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Neighbors: $f(-0.5) = -0.25 - 2 = -2.25$, $f(0.5) = -0.25 + 2 = 1.75$.

Best neighbor: $x' = 0.5$ with $f = 1.75$.

**Step 2 — Move to $x_1 = 0.5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(0.5) &= 1.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Neighbors: $f(0) = 0$, $f(1.0) = -1 + 4 = 3$. Best: $x' = 1.0$, $f = 3$.

**Step 3 — Move to $x_2 = 1.0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.0) &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Neighbors: $f(0.5) = 1.75$, $f(1.5) = -2.25 + 6 = 3.75$. Best: $x' = 1.5$, $f = 3.75$.

**Step 4 — Move to $x_3 = 1.5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.5) &= -2.25 + 6 = 3.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification — analytical global maximum** of $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(x) &= -2x + 4 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x^* = 2, \quad f(2) = -4 + 8 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

The algorithm is *approaching* the true global max at $x^* = 2$ but has not reached it (limited by step size $0.5$). The next iteration would attempt $x = 2.0$ (if allowed) and $f(2) = 4$, the global maximum. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The choice of **step size** (granularity of neighborhood) is critical — too coarse, and the algorithm "steps over" the peak; too fine, and the algorithm becomes slow. This concept reappears in KTU Module 2 (Genetic Algorithms — mutation step size).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Hill Climbing Algorithms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["HILL CLIMBING ALGORITHMS"]:::root
    A --> B1["Simple Hill Climbing\nFirst-Choice Method"]
    A --> B2["Steepest-Ascent HC\nBest-Choice Method"]
    A --> B3["Stochastic Hill Climbing\nRandom-Choice Method"]
    A --> B4["Random-Restart HC\nMeta-Heuristic Wrapper"]

    B1 --> C1["Pick first neighbor\nwith f greater"]
    B2 --> C2["Evaluate all neighbors\nPick best one"]
    B3 --> C3["Pick neighbor at random\nAccept if better"]
    B4 --> C4["Run base HC\nRestart on new random x\nReturn global best"]

    C4 --> D1["Base = Simple HC"]
    C4 --> D2["Base = Steepest HC"]
    C4 --> D3["Base = Stochastic HC"]

    classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef branch fill:#d9e7f5,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2a47
    classDef leaf fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#a37500,color:#3a2a00

    class A root
    class B1,B2,B3,B4 branch
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,D1,D2,D3 leaf
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Generic Hill Climbing Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["START: Initialize x_t"]:::step
    S2["Generate Neighborhood N of x_t"]:::step
    S3["Apply Variant-Specific Selection Rule"]:::step
    S4["x' better than x_t?"]:::decision
    S5["Set x_t+1 = x'"]:::step
    S6["REPORT LOCAL OPTIMUM\nTERMINATE"]:::term
    S7["t = t + 1\nLOOP BACK"]:::loop

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
    S4 -- Yes --> S5 --> S7
    S4 -- No --> S6

    classDef step fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2a47
    classDef decision fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc6600,color:#5a2d00
    classDef term fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#7a1f4d,color:#ffffff
    classDef loop fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#3a6b1f,color:#1a3309

    class S1,S2,S3,S5 step
    class S4 decision
    class S6 term
    class S7 loop
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Random-Restart Hill Climbing as a Wrapper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I["Random Initial x"]:::input --> H["Base Hill Climber\nSimple/Steepest/Stochastic"]:::core
    H --> O["Local Optimum x_local_r"]:::output
    O --> Q{"R restarts\ncompleted?"}:::decision
    Q -- No --> I
    Q -- Yes --> B["Select best among\nall x_local_r"]:::best
    B --> F["Final Global-Best Solution"]:::final

    classDef input fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#3a6b1f,color:#1a3309
    classDef core fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#330000
    classDef output fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a37500,color:#3a2a00
    classDef decision fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#cc6600,color:#5a2d00
    classDef best fill:#c9daf8,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2a47
    classDef final fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#7a1f4d,color:#ffffff

    class I input
    class H core
    class O output
    class Q decision
    class B best
    class F final
```

## 4.4 State-Space Topology — Failure Modes Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph A["Global Landscape Profile"]
        direction LR
        P1["Start Point"]:::start --> P2["Ascending Slope"]:::up
        P2 --> P3["LOCAL MAX - A\nfalse peak"]:::local
        P3 --> P4["DESCENT - dip"]:::down
        P4 --> P5["Ascending Slope"]:::up
        P5 --> P6["PLATEAU - flat region"]:::plateau
        P6 --> P7["Steep Ridge"]:::ridge
        P7 --> P8["GLOBAL MAX - G\ntrue peak"]:::global
    end

    classDef start fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#3a6b1f,color:#1a3309
    classDef up fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#0b2a47
    classDef local fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#330000
    classDef down fill:#ead1dc,stroke:#7a1f4d,color:#330020
    classDef plateau fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#5a3d8a,color:#1f0d3a
    classDef ridge fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#a37500,color:#3a2a00
    classDef global fill:#d5a6bd,stroke:#7a1f4d,color:#ffffff

    class P1 start
    class P2,P5 up
    class P3 local
    class P4 down
    class P6 plateau
    class P7 ridge
    class P8 global
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Simple Hill Climbing and Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing. Mention one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simple HC | Steepest-Ascent HC |
|---|---|---|
| Neighbor scanned | Only **first improving** neighbor | **All** neighbors |
| Selection | First-fit | Best-fit |
| Cost per move | Low | High |
| Move quality | Sub-optimal | Largest possible improvement |

**Advantage of Simple HC:** Lower time complexity per move; suitable for large neighborhoods. *(1 mark)*

**Advantage of Steepest-Ascent HC:** Each move gives the maximum possible gain, often reaching a peak in fewer iterations; better quality trajectories. *(1 mark)*

*(1 mark reserved for clear differentiation in tabular form.)*

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a local optimum in the context of Hill Climbing? Why is it considered a limitation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **local optimum** is a state $x^*$ such that every neighbor $x' \in N(x^*)$ satisfies $f(x') \leq f(x^*)$, even though there exists some other state $x^{**}$ in the search space with $f(x^{**}) > f(x^*)$. *(2 marks)*

**Limitation:** A standard hill climber has no information about regions of the state space beyond its immediate neighborhood, so once it reaches a local optimum, it cannot distinguish it from the global optimum and will **terminate prematurely** without finding the true best solution. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**a) Explain the Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing algorithm in detail with a suitable example. Compare it with Simple Hill Climbing.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Steepest-Ascent Hill Climbing is a variant of local search in which, at every iteration, **all** neighbors of the current state are evaluated and the algorithm moves to the neighbor with the **highest objective function value**. The mathematical selection rule is:

$$x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x' \in N(x_t)} f(x')$$

If no neighbor improves on the current state (i.e., $f(x') \leq f(x_t)$ for all $x' \in N(x_t)$), the algorithm terminates at a local optimum.

**Algorithmic Procedure (2 marks):**

1. Initialize $x_0$ randomly in the search space $S$.
2. Repeat:
   a. Generate the neighborhood $N(x_t)$.
   b. For every $x' \in N(x_t)$, compute $f(x')$.
   c. Identify $x_{\text{best}} = \arg\max_{x' \in N(x_t)} f(x')$.
   d. If $f(x_{\text{best}}) \leq f(x_t)$, **STOP** (local optimum reached).
   e. Else, set $x_{t+1} = x_{\text{best}}$ and $t = t + 1$.

**Worked Example (2 marks):** *(Same as the numerical in Section 3.4 — $f(x) = -x^2 + 4x$, step $0.5$, three iterations reaching $x_3 = 1.5$, $f(1.5) = 3.75$)*.

**Comparison with Simple Hill Climbing (1 mark):**

| Criterion | Steepest-Ascent | Simple |
|---|---|---|
| Neighbors checked | All | Until first improvement |
| Per-move cost | High | Low |
| Per-move gain | Maximum | Any positive |
| Trajectory quality | Higher | Lower |

---

**b) Discuss Random-Restart Hill Climbing. Derive the expression for the number of restarts required to achieve a given success probability.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Random-Restart Hill Climbing is a **meta-algorithm** that wraps any base hill climbing method. After the base method converges to a local optimum, the algorithm *discards* that solution, generates a **new random initial state**, and re-runs the base method. After $R$ restarts, the algorithm returns the **best** solution found across all runs.

This strategy addresses the central weakness of plain hill climbing — its tendency to get trapped in shallow local optima — by giving the search *multiple* chances to begin in the basin of attraction of the global optimum.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let $p$ be the probability that any single run of the base hill climber succeeds (finds the global optimum). For a finite search space, $0 < p \leq 1$.

The probability that a *single* run **fails** is $(1 - p)$.

Since the $R$ restarts are **statistically independent** (each uses a freshly drawn random initial state), the probability that **all** $R$ restarts fail is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{all fail}) &= (1 - p)^R
\end{aligned}
$$

The probability that **at least one** restart succeeds (i.e., the overall algorithm finds the global optimum) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{success}) &= 1 - (1 - p)^R
\end{aligned}
$$

To find the minimum $R$ required to achieve a target success probability $P_{\text{target}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - (1 - p)^R &\geq P_{\text{target}} \\
(1 - p)^R &\leq 1 - P_{\text{target}} \\
R \cdot \ln(1 - p) &\leq \ln(1 - P_{\text{target}}) \\
R &\geq \frac{\ln(1 - P_{\text{target}})}{\ln(1 - p)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for final formula and numerical interpretation.)*

**Numerical Example:** For $p = 0.1$, $P_{\text{target}} = 0.95$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &\geq \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.9)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-0.10536} \approx 28.4
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $R_{\min} = 29$ restarts.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Random-restart hill climbing trades *redundant computation* (multiple independent climbs) for a *probabilistic guarantee* of finding the global optimum, with the guarantee sharpening exponentially in $R$.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1 - Alternative Choice]`

**a) Explain Stochastic Hill Climbing with its mathematical formulation. In what situations is it preferred over deterministic variants?** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Stochastic Hill Climbing is a probabilistic variant in which, at every iteration, a single neighbor is **chosen uniformly at random** from the neighborhood $N(x_t)$. The move is accepted **only if** the randomly chosen neighbor has a strictly higher objective function value than the current state. Otherwise, the current state is retained and a fresh random neighbor is drawn.

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks):**

Let $U(N(x_t))$ denote the uniform distribution over the neighborhood. The selection is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &\sim U(N(x_t)) \\
x_{t+1} &= \begin{cases} x' & \text{if } f(x') > f(x_t) \\ x_t & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

The probability of *accepting* a worse state is **zero** — what is randomized is *which* neighbor is examined, not the *accept/reject* decision. This distinguishes it from Simulated Annealing, which can probabilistically accept worse states.

**Preferred Scenarios (2 marks):**

1. **Large or unstructured neighborhoods** (e.g., continuous high-dimensional spaces): deterministic variants would need to evaluate all neighbors, which is infeasible.
2. **Noisy objective functions** (e.g., evaluations from a physical sensor with measurement error): stochastic sampling acts as a built-in low-pass filter against noise.
3. **Avoiding ordering bias** of simple hill climbing without the cost of steepest-ascent.

---

**b) List and briefly explain the three classical failure modes of Hill Climbing. With a labeled diagram, describe how Random-Restart Hill Climbing overcomes the "local maximum" problem.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Three Failure Modes (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Local Maximum:** A state that is higher than all its neighbors but lower than the global maximum. The algorithm halts prematurely.
2. **Plateau:** A region of the state space where the objective function is *flat* — $f(x') = f(x_t)$ for all $x'$ in the neighborhood. The algorithm wanders aimlessly and may time out.
3. **Ridge:** A narrow, sloped region where the algorithm can only move *sideways* (because greedy moves point off the ridge into a valley). Steepest-ascent is especially vulnerable.

**How Random-Restart HC Solves the Local-Maximum Problem (3 marks):** When the base hill climber gets stuck at a local maximum, Random-Restart HC **abandons that point** and begins a fresh climb from a new randomly chosen starting state. If the new start lies in the basin of attraction of the global maximum, the next run will reach it. The algorithm is run $R$ times, and the **best** of all local maxima found is returned. As $R \to \infty$, the probability of having explored the basin of the global maximum approaches 1.

**Diagram (1 mark):** *(Refer to Section 4.4 — the State-Space Topology diagram explicitly labels the local max, plateau, ridge, and global max, illustrating the failure modes.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Stochastic HC with Simulated Annealing** — Stochastic HC **never** accepts a worse state; Simulated Annealing *can* (with a temperature-dependent probability). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Wrong formula for $R$** — Many students write $R \geq \frac{\ln(P_{\text{target}})}{\ln(p)}$ instead of $R \geq \frac{\ln(1 - P_{\text{target}})}{\ln(1 - p)}$. Both **numerator and denominator** must be of the *failure* event.
> 3. **Omitting the "argmax" or "first-fit" qualifier** in the selection rule — the answer must state *which* neighbor is selected, not just that *a* neighbor is.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify "improving neighbor only"** — hill climbing by definition never *worsens* (in its basic forms), so a model answer that says "move to any neighbor" is incomplete.
> 5. **Not distinguishing between *variant* and *meta-algorithm*** — Random-Restart HC is a meta-heuristic that uses another variant as its base; do not list it as a peer of the others without this distinction.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Types of Hill Climbing Algorithms**

- ☐ **Hill Climbing** is a local-search optimization method; moves to a better neighbor until no better neighbor exists.
- ☐ **Four canonical types** (KTU 2024): **Simple, Steepest-Ascent, Stochastic, Random-Restart**.
- ☐ **Simple HC** = first-fit; **Steepest-Ascent HC** = best-fit; **Stochastic HC** = random-fit with strict improvement; **Random-Restart HC** = meta-algorithm over any of the above.
- ☐ The **selection rule** is the *only* operational difference between the three "primary" variants.
- ☐ All basic variants (excluding Random-Restart) suffer from **local optima, plateaus, and ridges**.
- ☐ **Random-Restart HC** provably converges to the global optimum with $P = 1$ as $R \to \infty$.
- ☐ The success probability formula is: $P(\text{success}) = 1 - (1 - p)^R$.
- ☐ The minimum restart formula is: $R \geq \dfrac{\ln(1 - P_{\text{target}})}{\ln(1 - p)}$.
- ☐ **Stochastic HC ≠ Simulated Annealing** — SA can accept *worse* states; Stochastic HC cannot.
- ☐ **Steepest-ascent is roughly 2× more expensive per move** than Simple HC in large neighborhoods but converges in *fewer* moves.
- ☐ The **basin of attraction** concept is central — a Random-Restart succeeds if its random start lies in the *correct* basin.
- ☐ For KTU numericals, always show: **(i)** $R$ derivation with $\ln$ algebra, **(ii)** one full worked example iteration, and **(iii)** a comparison table.
- ☐ Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are **never** valid as standalone node IDs in exam diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local and Global Maxima
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Local and Global Maxima

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Evolutionary Computing** and numerical optimization, the optimization of a continuous objective function $f : \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ fundamentally revolves around locating its extremal values. A **Local Maximum** and a **Global Maximum** represent two distinct hierarchical levels of optimality that an Evolutionary Algorithm (EA) attempts to discover within the search space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECNT869):**
> A point $x^{*} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is a **Local Maximum** of the function $f(x)$ if there exists an $\epsilon$-neighborhood (a small radius around $x^{*}$) such that for all points $x$ within that neighborhood, $f(x^{*}) \geq f(x)$. If this inequality holds for *every* point $x$ across the entire feasible domain $S$, then $x^{*}$ is a **Global Maximum**.

### 1.2 Mathematical Statement of Optimality

A point $x^{*} \in S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is classified as a **strict local maximum** if:

$$f(x^{*}) > f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in S \cap B(x^{*}, \epsilon), \, x \neq x^{*}$$

where $B(x^{*}, \epsilon)$ denotes the open ball of radius $\epsilon > 0$ centered at $x^{*}$. When the inequality is non-strict ($f(x^{*}) \geq f(x)$), the point is termed a **weak local maximum** or simply a **local maximum**.

A point $x^{**} \in S$ is a **global maximum** if:

$$f(x^{**}) \geq f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in S$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In evolutionary algorithms such as Genetic Algorithms (GA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), and Differential Evolution (DE), the search agents (individuals/particles) explicitly converge toward maxima (or minima, depending on sign convention). The distinction between local and global maxima is the central reason why techniques like **mutation**, **crossover**, and **migration** are introduced into EAs — to escape *local* maxima and pursue the *global* maximum.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are a mountaineer in the **Western Ghats of Kerala** at night, and you are trying to locate the single highest peak in the entire range using only a flashlight:

- **Local Maximum:** The flashlight beam reveals a small hill right in front of you. You climb it and reach its top — for as far as you can see with your limited flashlight (the $\epsilon$-neighborhood), no point is higher. You mistakenly conclude you have found the highest peak. This is a *local maximum* — optimal *within your immediate vicinity*, but not the tallest mountain overall.

- **Global Maximum:** Far away, beyond your flashlight's reach, stands **Anamudi Peak** ($2,695$ m), the highest point in South India. That is the *global maximum* — the absolute best point in the entire landscape.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Evolutionary Computing

Evolutionary algorithms maintain a *population* of candidate solutions that evolve over generations. The fitness landscape induced by the objective function is typically **multimodal** — containing many peaks. The fundamental challenge is:

| Problem | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Premature Convergence** | All hikers cluster on the same small hilltop, never exploring further |
| **Local Maxima Trapping** | The population's average fitness stagnates at a sub-optimal peak |
| **Global Maxima Discovery** | Diversity-preservation mechanisms allow exploration of the entire mountain range |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The phenomenon of a population converging to a local maximum rather than the global maximum is called **premature convergence** — it is the single most critical failure mode in evolutionary computing and is heavily examined in KTU assessments.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Logic Steps for Identifying Maxima

The theoretical machinery behind classifying a stationary point as a local or global maximum proceeds in three rigorously defined stages:

**Step 1 — Stationarity Condition (First-Order Necessary Condition):**
If $x^{*}$ is an interior local maximum of a differentiable function $f(x)$ and $f$ is differentiable at $x^{*}$, then the gradient must vanish:

$$\nabla f(x^{*}) = \mathbf{0}$$

Equivalently, for a single-variable function $f(x): \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$:

$$f'(x^{*}) = 0$$

This is a *necessary* but not *sufficient* condition — $f'(x^{*}) = 0$ also holds at minima and saddle points.

**Step 2 — Second-Order Sufficient Condition (Hessian Test):**
For a twice-differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$, define the **Hessian matrix** $H(x)$:

$$H_{ij}(x) = \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{i} \partial x_{j}}(x)$$

A point $x^{*}$ satisfying $\nabla f(x^{*}) = \mathbf{0}$ is a **strict local maximum** if the Hessian is **negative definite** at $x^{*}$:

$$H(x^{*}) \prec 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mathbf{v}^{T} H(x^{*}) \mathbf{v} < 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$$

For the scalar case ($n=1$), this reduces to the well-known condition:

$$f''(x^{*}) < 0$$

**Step 3 — Global Optimality via Concavity:**
A local maximum is also the global maximum if the function is **strictly concave** over the entire feasible region $S$:

$$f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) y) > \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y) \quad \forall \, x, y \in S, \, x \neq y, \, \lambda \in (0,1)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in EAs:** Most real-world fitness landscapes in evolutionary computing are **non-convex** — they violate the strict concavity condition. Hence, a local maximum found by the algorithm is *not* automatically the global maximum. This non-convexity is precisely why we need stochastic, population-based EAs rather than deterministic gradient ascent.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes every formula a KTU 2024 examinee must memorize for this topic. Pay close attention — column notation uses `\vert` instead of the vertical pipe character to maintain markdown table integrity.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Class |
|---|---|---|
| Local Maximum (Pointwise) | $f(x^{*}) \geq f(x), \quad \forall \, x \in B(x^{*}, \epsilon)$ | $\epsilon > 0$ small radius |
| Strict Local Maximum | $f(x^{*}) > f(x), \quad \forall \, x \in B(x^{*}, \epsilon) \setminus \{x^{*}\}$ | Interior strict optimum |
| Global Maximum | $f(x^{**}) \geq f(x), \quad \forall \, x \in S$ | Holds over entire domain |
| Gradient Vanishing (Necessary) | $\nabla f(x^{*}) = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order stationarity |
| Second Derivative Test | $f''(x^{*}) < 0$ | Sufficient for univariate strict local max |
| Hessian Test (Multivariate) | $H(x^{*}) \prec 0$ (negative definite) | Sufficient for multivariate strict local max |
| Strict Concavity | $f(\lambda x + (1-\lambda) y) > \lambda f(x) + (1-\lambda) f(y)$ | Implies local max is global max |
| Quadratic Form Test | $\mathbf{v}^{T} H(x^{*}) \mathbf{v} < 0, \quad \forall \, \mathbf{v} \neq \mathbf{0}$ | Negative definiteness check |
| Neighborhood Distance | $\vert x - x^{*} \vert < \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$-neighborhood definition |
| Population Convergence Indicator | $\sigma_{\text{population}}(t) \rightarrow 0$ as $t \rightarrow \infty$ | Trapping at local max signal |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The local-vs-global maximum distinction drives engineering decisions across multiple domains:

- **Neural Network Training (Deep Learning):** Loss landscapes of deep networks contain millions of local minima/maxima. Techniques like **stochastic gradient descent with momentum**, **learning rate scheduling**, and **simulated annealing** are explicitly designed to escape local maxima and locate better generalizing solutions.

- **Antenna Array Design:** Beam-pattern optimization seeks the global maximum of the radiated power pattern. Local maxima correspond to undesirable sidelobes — genetic algorithms are routinely deployed to suppress these.

- **VLSI Circuit Routing:** Wire-length minimization involves navigating a discrete multimodal cost landscape where local minima represent dead-end routing configurations.

- **Structural Engineering (Truss Optimization):** Weight minimization of truss structures under stress constraints produces a non-convex search space where global maxima of stiffness (or minima of weight) require evolutionary search.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** When answering KTU questions on this topic, always explicitly state whether the function is **convex/concave**, **differentiable**, and whether the maximum is **strict** or **weak**. These three qualifiers differentiate full-mark answers from partial-credit ones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Unimodal vs Multimodal Function Classification

**Problem Statement:** Given the scalar function $f(x) = x^{3} - 3x^{2} - 9x + 5$, determine all local and global maxima over the domain $S = [-3, 6]$.

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative:**

$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(x^{3} - 3x^{2} - 9x + 5\right)$$

$$f'(x) = 3x^{2} - 6x - 9$$

This step applies the standard power rule: $\frac{d}{dx}(x^{n}) = nx^{n-1}$.

**Step 2 — Set the first derivative to zero to find stationary points:**

$$3x^{2} - 6x - 9 = 0$$

Divide through by 3:

$$x^{2} - 2x - 3 = 0$$

Factor using the identity $(x-3)(x+1) = x^{2} - 2x - 3$:

$$(x - 3)(x + 1) = 0$$

Therefore the stationary points are:

$$x_{1}^{*} = -1, \quad x_{2}^{*} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative:**

$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(3x^{2} - 6x - 9\right)$$

$$f''(x) = 6x - 6$$

**Step 4 — Apply the second derivative test at each stationary point:**

At $x_{1}^{*} = -1$:

$$f''(-1) = 6(-1) - 6 = -12 < 0$$

Since $f''(-1) < 0$, by the second derivative sufficient condition, $x_{1}^{*} = -1$ is a **strict local maximum**.

At $x_{2}^{*} = 3$:

$$f''(3) = 6(3) - 6 = 12 > 0$$

Since $f''(3) > 0$, by the analogous test, $x_{2}^{*} = 3$ is a **strict local minimum**.

**Step 5 — Evaluate $f(x)$ at the local maximum and at the boundary points to identify the global maximum:**

$$f(-1) = (-1)^{3} - 3(-1)^{2} - 9(-1) + 5 = -1 - 3 + 9 + 5 = 10$$

$$f(3) = (3)^{3} - 3(3)^{2} - 9(3) + 5 = 27 - 27 - 27 + 5 = -22$$

$$f(-3) = (-3)^{3} - 3(-3)^{2} - 9(-3) + 5 = -27 - 27 + 27 + 5 = -22$$

$$f(6) = (6)^{3} - 3(6)^{2} - 9(6) + 5 = 216 - 108 - 54 + 5 = 59$$

**Step 6 — Compare all candidate values:**

$$\max\left\{f(-1), f(3), f(-3), f(6)\right\} = \max\left\{10, -22, -22, 59\right\} = 59$$

Since $59$ occurs at the boundary point $x = 6 \notin \{x_{1}^{*}, x_{2}^{*}\}$, we conclude:

- **Local Maximum** at $x = -1$ with $f(-1) = 10$
- **Global Maximum** at $x = 6$ with $f(6) = 59$ (attained on the boundary of $S$)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This problem demonstrates that the *global* maximum need not occur at a stationary point — it may lie on the boundary of the feasible region. Evolutionary algorithms must therefore evaluate both interior candidates *and* boundary solutions.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Multivariate Case with Hessian Eigenvalue Test

**Problem Statement:** For the bivariate function $f(x_{1}, x_{2}) = 4 - x_{1}^{2} - 2x_{2}^{2} + x_{1}x_{2}$, determine whether the origin $(0, 0)$ is a local maximum, local minimum, or saddle point.

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{1}} = -2x_{1} + x_{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{2}} = -4x_{2} + x_{1}$$

The gradient vector is:

$$\nabla f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} -2x_{1} + x_{2} \\ x_{1} - 4x_{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Set the gradient to zero:**

$$-2x_{1} + x_{2} = 0 \implies x_{2} = 2x_{1}$$

$$x_{1} - 4x_{2} = 0 \implies x_{1} = 4x_{2}$$

Substituting $x_{2} = 2x_{1}$ into the second equation:

$$x_{1} = 4(2x_{1}) = 8x_{1}$$

$$-7x_{1} = 0 \implies x_{1} = 0, \, x_{2} = 0$$

So the only stationary point is $(0, 0)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Hessian matrix:**

$$H(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1}^{2}} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{1} \partial x_{2}} \\ \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{2} \partial x_{1}} & \dfrac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x_{2}^{2}} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 1 \\ 1 & -4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Note that the Hessian is **constant** (independent of $x$) — this function is quadratic.

**Step 4 — Test negative definiteness using leading principal minors:**

A symmetric matrix is negative definite if and only if its leading principal minors alternate in sign, starting with a negative value:

$$\Delta_{1} = -2 < 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\Delta_{2} = \det(H) = (-2)(-4) - (1)(1) = 8 - 1 = 7 > 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Both conditions hold, therefore $H \prec 0$ (negative definite).

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

By the second-order sufficient condition, the point $(0, 0)$ is a **strict local maximum**. The function value is:

$$f(0, 0) = 4 - 0 - 0 + 0 = 4$$

Since the function is strictly concave everywhere (the Hessian is globally negative definite), this local maximum is in fact the **global maximum**, attained as $x_{1}, x_{2} \rightarrow \infty$ does not apply (the function is unbounded below for large $|x|$), but restricted to $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, $f(0,0) = 4$ is the unique global maximum for any bounded feasible region.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Function Classification Table

The table below classifies common optimization functions and their maximum structures. This is a high-yield KTU reference.

| Function | Expression | Local Maxima Count | Global Maximum | Concave? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quadratic (Negative Definite) | $f(x) = 4 - x^{2}$ | $1$ | $f(0) = 4$ | Yes (strictly) |
| Cubic Polynomial | $f(x) = x^{3} - 3x^{2} - 9x + 5$ | $1$ | Boundary-dependent | No |
| Trigonometric | $f(x) = \sin(x)$ | Infinitely many | $f(\pi/2) = 1$ (local only) | No |
| Gaussian (Bounded) | $f(x) = e^{-x^{2}}$ | $1$ | $f(0) = 1$ | Locally, no (globally no) |
| Rosenbrock (Flipped) | $f(x) = -(1-x)^{2} - 100(x^{2}-x)^{2}$ | $1$ | $f(1) = 0$ | No |
| Two-Peak Gaussian Mixture | $f(x) = e^{-(x-2)^{2}} + 0.8 e^{-(x+3)^{2}}$ | $2$ | $f(2) = 1$ | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fitness Landscape Diagram — Local vs Global Maxima

The diagram below illustrates a one-dimensional multimodal fitness landscape with multiple local maxima and a single global maximum, together with the convergence behavior of an evolutionary algorithm's population.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Landscape Domain Start] --> B[Local Max 1 - Height 0.6]
    B --> C[Valley 1 - Height 0.1]
    C --> D[Local Max 2 - Height 0.8]
    D --> E[Valley 2 - Height 0.2]
    E --> F[GLOBAL MAXIMUM - Height 1.0]
    F --> G[Valley 3 - Height 0.3]
    G --> H[Local Max 3 - Height 0.7]
    H --> I[Landscape Domain End]

    J[EA Population t=0 - Scattered] --> K[EA Population t=50 - Near Local Max 2]
    K --> L[EA Population t=200 - TRAPPED at Local Max 2]

    M[Diversity Mechanism Mutation] --> N[EA Population Re-dispersed]
    N --> O[EA Population t=300 - Approaching GLOBAL MAX]

    L -. Without diversity .-> P[Premature Convergence - Suboptimal Solution]
    O -. With diversity .-> Q[Global Optimum Discovered]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of an Evolutionary Algorithm with Local Maxima Escape Mechanisms

The following block diagram shows the architectural flow of an EA and the points at which diversity-preserving operators intervene to escape local maxima.

```mermaid
graph TD
    initP[Initialize Random Population] --> evalF[Evaluate Fitness of Each Individual]
    evalF --> select[Selection Operator - Roulette / Tournament]
    select --> crossover[Crossover Operator - Recombination]
    crossover --> mutation[Mutation Operator - Diversity Injection]
    mutation --> newPop[Generate New Population]
    newPop --> checkConv{Check Convergence}
    checkConv -->|Not Converged| evalF
    checkConv -->|Converged| output[Output Best Individual as Solution]
    checkConv -->|Stagnation Detected| restart[Restart with New Seeds]
    restart --> initP

    style initP fill:#e1f5ff
    style mutation fill:#ffe1e1
    style restart fill:#ffe1e1
    style output fill:#e1ffe1
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Flow — From Local Maximum Identification to Global Maximum Discovery

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Given Function f over Domain S] --> smooth[Check Differentiability of f]
    smooth --> computeGrad[Compute Gradient nabla f]
    computeGrad --> stationary[Solve nabla f equals 0 to find Stationary Points]
    stationary --> classify{Apply Hessian Test}
    classify -->|H negative definite| localMax[Classify as Local Maximum]
    classify -->|H positive definite| localMin[Classify as Local Minimum]
    classify -->|H indefinite| saddle[Classify as Saddle Point]
    localMax --> compareEval[Compare f values at all candidates]
    saddle --> compareEval
    localMin --> compareEval
    compareEval --> boundary[Evaluate f on Boundary of S]
    boundary --> selectMax[Select Point with Maximum f value]
    selectMax --> globalMax[Global Maximum Identified]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Hierarchy of Optimality

```mermaid
graph TD
    opt[Optimum of Function f] --> local[Local Optimum]
    opt --> global[Global Optimum]
    local --> strictL[Strict Local Maxima]
    local --> weakL[Weak Local Maxima]
    global --> strictG[Strict Global Maxima]
    global --> weakG[Weak Global Maxima]
    strictG -.includes.-> strictL
    weakG -.includes.-> weakL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "explain with a diagram," KTU examiners expect both a *graph of the function* (showing peaks visually) *and* a *flowchart of the optimization algorithm*. Always include both — partial marks are awarded for each.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]:**
Define a *local maximum* and a *global maximum* of a real-valued function $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. Use a fitness landscape diagram to illustrate why an evolutionary algorithm might get trapped at a local maximum.

**Model Answer:**
A point $x^{*} \in S$ is a **local maximum** of $f$ if there exists an $\epsilon > 0$ such that $f(x^{*}) \geq f(x)$ for all $x \in S$ with $\vert x - x^{*} \vert < \epsilon$. A point $x^{**} \in S$ is a **global maximum** if $f(x^{**}) \geq f(x)$ for *all* $x \in S$.

In an EA, the population of candidate solutions evolves via selection pressure toward higher-fitness regions. If the population converges to a local maximum, **selection** favors the best individuals, **crossover** combines their traits without introducing new genetic material, and without sufficient **mutation**, the population loses diversity and becomes trapped. This is **premature convergence** — the EA reports a sub-optimal solution because it failed to explore beyond the local peak.

---

**Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]:**
State the first-order necessary condition and the second-order sufficient condition for a point $x^{*}$ to be a local maximum of a twice-differentiable function $f: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$.

**Model Answer:**

**First-Order Necessary Condition:** If $x^{*}$ is an interior local maximum of $f$ and $f$ is differentiable at $x^{*}$, then:

$$f'(x^{*}) = 0$$

This is necessary (but not sufficient), because it also holds at minima and saddle points.

**Second-Order Sufficient Condition:** If $f'(x^{*}) = 0$ and $f''(x^{*}) < 0$, then $x^{*}$ is a **strict local maximum** of $f$. The negative second derivative indicates the function curves downward at $x^{*}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Consider the function $f(x) = -x^{4} + 8x^{3} - 18x^{2} + 10$ defined on $S = [0, 5]$.
   - (i) Find all stationary points of $f$ in $S$. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Classify each stationary point as a local maximum, local minimum, or saddle point using the second derivative test. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss with a fitness landscape diagram how an evolutionary algorithm can escape local maxima to discover the global maximum. Mention at least two mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Stationary Points:**

Computing the first derivative:

$$f'(x) = -4x^{3} + 24x^{2} - 36x$$

Setting $f'(x) = 0$:

$$-4x(x^{2} - 6x + 9) = 0$$

$$-4x(x - 3)^{2} = 0$$

Therefore the stationary points are $x = 0$ and $x = 3$ (the latter is a repeated root).

**(ii) Classification:**

Computing the second derivative:

$$f''(x) = -12x^{2} + 48x - 36 = -12(x^{2} - 4x + 3) = -12(x-1)(x-3)$$

At $x = 0$:

$$f''(0) = -12(0-1)(0-3) = -12(-1)(-3) = -36 < 0$$

So $x = 0$ is a **strict local maximum** with $f(0) = 10$.

At $x = 3$:

$$f''(3) = -12(3-1)(3-3) = -12(2)(0) = 0$$

The second derivative test is **inconclusive** here. We use the first derivative sign test: for $x \in (2.9, 3.1)$, $f'(x) = -4x(x-3)^{2} \leq 0$, meaning $f$ is non-increasing on both sides. Hence $x = 3$ is a **point of inflection** (saddle), not a local extremum.

We must also check boundary points: $f(5) = -625 + 1000 - 450 + 10 = -65$. Comparing $f(0) = 10$, $f(3) = -81 + 216 - 162 + 10 = -17$, and $f(5) = -65$, the **global maximum** is at $x = 0$ with value $f(0) = 10$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

An evolutionary algorithm (EA) can escape local maxima and discover the global maximum through the following mechanisms:

1. **Mutation Operator:** Mutation introduces random perturbations into individuals' genetic representations (e.g., bit-flips in binary GAs or Gaussian noise in Evolution Strategies). Even after the population has converged near a local maximum, mutation can produce offspring that lie outside the current peak's neighborhood, enabling exploration of new regions of the fitness landscape.

2. **Crowding / Fitness Sharing:** These niching techniques subdivide the population into sub-populations that occupy different peaks simultaneously. By penalizing individuals that are too close to one another (sharing), the EA maintains diversity across multiple local maxima, increasing the probability that the global maximum is located.

3. **Restart Strategies:** When stagnation is detected (e.g., best fitness unchanged for $k$ generations), the EA restarts with a fresh random population, providing another opportunity to locate the global maximum.

4. **Migration in Island Models:** Multiple sub-populations evolve independently on different "islands," periodically exchanging individuals. This spatial structure preserves diversity and prevents all islands from converging to the same local maximum.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternate]**

**(a)** For the function $f(x_{1}, x_{2}) = 5 - 2x_{1}^{2} - 3x_{2}^{2} + x_{1}x_{2}$:
   - (i) Compute the gradient $\nabla f$ and the Hessian matrix $H$. **(3 marks)**
   - (ii) Find the stationary point and classify it using the Hessian definiteness test. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with mathematical justification why *strict concavity* of the objective function guarantees that every local maximum is also a global maximum. Hence state why most real-world EA fitness landscapes violate this property. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**(i) Gradient and Hessian:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{1}} = -4x_{1} + x_{2}, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_{2}} = -6x_{2} + x_{1}$$

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} -4x_{1} + x_{2} \\ x_{1} - 6x_{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -4 & 1 \\ 1 & -6 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(ii) Stationary Point and Classification:**

Setting $\nabla f = 0$:

$$-4x_{1} + x_{2} = 0 \implies x_{2} = 4x_{1}$$

$$x_{1} - 6x_{2} = 0 \implies x_{1} = 6x_{2} = 24x_{1} \implies -23x_{1} = 0 \implies x_{1} = 0, x_{2} = 0$$

So the unique stationary point is $(0, 0)$. The Hessian is constant, so test its definiteness via leading principal minors:

$$\Delta_{1} = -4 < 0, \quad \Delta_{2} = (-4)(-6) - (1)(1) = 24 - 1 = 23 > 0$$

Since $\Delta_{1} < 0$ and $\Delta_{2} > 0$, the Hessian is **negative definite**, so $(0, 0)$ is a **strict local maximum**. The function value is $f(0, 0) = 5$. Because the Hessian is globally negative definite, the function is strictly concave on $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, so this is also the **global maximum** for any bounded region containing the origin.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Mathematical Justification of Strict Concavity Implying Global Maxima:**

Suppose $f: S \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ is strictly concave, and let $x^{*}$ be a local maximum. We prove $x^{*}$ is global. By contradiction, assume there exists $y \in S$ with $f(y) > f(x^{*})$. Let $\lambda \in (0, 1)$ and define:

$$z_{\lambda} = \lambda y + (1 - \lambda) x^{*}$$

By strict concavity:

$$f(z_{\lambda}) > \lambda f(y) + (1 - \lambda) f(x^{*})$$

$$f(z_{\lambda}) > \lambda f(x^{*}) + \lambda (f(y) - f(x^{*})) + (1 - \lambda) f(x^{*})$$

$$f(z_{\lambda}) > f(x^{*}) + \lambda(f(y) - f(x^{*}))$$

Since $f(y) > f(x^{*})$ and $\lambda > 0$:

$$f(z_{\lambda}) > f(x^{*})$$

But as $\lambda \rightarrow 0$, $z_{\lambda} \rightarrow x^{*}$, so $z_{\lambda}$ lies in any neighborhood of $x^{*}$. This contradicts the assumption that $x^{*}$ is a local maximum. Therefore no such $y$ exists, and $x^{*}$ is the global maximum.

**Why Real-World EA Landscapes Violate Strict Concavity:**

Real-world fitness landscapes in evolutionary computing are almost never strictly concave because:

1. **Multi-modality:** Engineering optimization problems (e.g., antenna design, neural architecture search) have multiple peaks corresponding to different valid configurations.
