<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECNT869]**
*Define the term "Plateau" in the context of fitness landscapes for Genetic Algorithms. How does it differ from a local optimum?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **plateau** is a maximal connected region $\mathcal{P}$ of the search space in which the fitness function $f$ is locally flat, i.e., $\vert f(x) - f(y) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ for all neighbors $x, y \in \mathcal{P}$. It is characterized by **zero (or near-zero) fitness gradient**, causing the GA to undergo a random walk rather than hill-climbing.

**Difference from local optimum (1 mark):**

| Feature | Plateau | Local Optimum |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Fitness gradient | $\nabla f \approx 0$ | $\nabla f = 0$ at a single point |
| Region size | Extended (many points) | Single point (or tiny neighborhood) |
| Selection pressure | Collapses globally | Remains normal around the point |
| EA behavior | Random walk | Premature convergence |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECNT869]**
*List any three real-world engineering problems where plateau regions occur in the fitness landscape.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **RNA Secondary Structure Prediction** — Many RNA sequences (genotypes) fold to identical structures (phenotypes), forming a massive neutral network (plateau).
2. **Neural Architecture Search (NAS)** — Numerous distinct architectures achieve near-identical validation accuracy on a dataset, producing large plateau regions.
3. **VLSI Floorplanning** — Many layout permutations yield identical wire-length estimates due to symmetry, forming plateaus in the permutation-based GA landscape.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice 1]*

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of a **plateau** in the fitness landscape of a Genetic Algorithm. Discuss its impact on selection pressure, convergence, and the random walk behavior of the population. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** For a binary GA with population size $N = 100$, chromosome length $\ell = 50$, and per-bit mutation rate $p_m = 0.005$, the population has converged onto a plateau of Hamming diameter $D = 10$. Compute the expected escape time and explain how doubling the population size affects the result. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** A plateau is a connected region $\mathcal{P}$ of the search space where the fitness function satisfies $\vert f(x) - f(y) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ for all $x, y \in \mathcal{P}$. It corresponds to a "flat tableland" in the fitness landscape.

2. **Impact on selection pressure (2 marks):** Tournament selection loses discriminative power because all candidates have nearly equal fitness. The probability that a tournament winner is "better" approaches $0.5$ (random). Similarly, Roulette-wheel selection assigns nearly equal probability to all individuals. Selection pressure **collapses to zero**.

3. **Impact on convergence (1.5 marks):** The GA cannot converge *upward* because no gradient exists. It may still converge *spatially* (premature convergence onto a sub-region of the plateau) due to **genetic drift**.

4. **Random walk behavior (1.5 marks):** With no selection pressure, the population dynamics reduce to a *neutral random walk* on the plateau subgraph. The autocorrelation coefficient $\rho(k) \approx 1$ for $k \leq D$ (the plateau diameter), and the expected escape time scales as $T_{\text{esc}} \approx D^2 / (2 p_m \ell)$ (without population) or $D^2 / (2 N p_m \ell)$ (with population).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Apply the escape-time formula:

$$
T_{\text{esc}}^{\text{pop}} = \frac{D^2}{2 \cdot N \cdot p_m \cdot \ell}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
T_{\text{esc}}^{\text{pop}} = \frac{10^2}{2 \cdot 100 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 50} = \frac{100}{50} = 2 \text{ generations}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Dimensional check — $D^2$ has units of (bits)$^2$; denominator $2 N p_m \ell$ has units of (individuals) × (dimensionless) × (bits) = (individual-bits). For a population random walk, the per-step progress is $\sim p_m \ell$ bits per individual, and the population gives $N$ parallel walkers, yielding units of (bits)$^2$ / (bits) = bits ≈ generations. ✅

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Doubling the population to $N' = 200$:

$$
T_{\text{esc}}^{\text{pop}}' = \frac{100}{2 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.005 \cdot 50} = \frac{100}{100} = 1 \text{ generation}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** **Interpretation:** Doubling the population **halves** the expected escape time (linear inverse relationship: $T_{\text{esc}} \propto 1/N$). This justifies the use of large populations in deceptive/flat fitness landscapes, although it incurs a higher per-generation fitness-evaluation cost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Choice 2]*

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **NK-model** of fitness landscapes. Show how the parameter $K$ controls the ruggedness and the formation of plateaus. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **random walk autocorrelation analysis** as a tool to detect plateau regions. Derive the autocorrelation coefficient for a strict plateau and explain its diagnostic value. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **NK-Model definition (2.5 marks):** The NK-model of Kauffman is a tunable family of fitness landscapes. The fitness of a binary string $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_\ell)$ is:

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\ell} \sum_{i=1}^{\ell} f_i(x_i, x_{i_1}, \ldots, x_{i_K})
$$

where each $f_i$ depends on gene $x_i$ and $K$ other genes (epistatic neighbors). The $f_i$'s are typically uniform random in $[0, 1]$.

2. **Effect of $K$ (2.5 marks):**
   - $K = 0$: **Additive** (no epistasis). Landscape is smooth, single global optimum, **no plateaus**.
   - $0 < K < \ell - 1$: **Multi-modal** with peaks of decreasing height. The landscape contains **ridges, plateaus, and local optima** in mixture.
   - $K = \ell - 1$: **Maximally rugged** (random landscape). No clear peaks, fitness essentially uncorrelated. The landscape degenerates into a chaotic surface with no large plateaus.

3. **Diagram explanation (2 marks):** A schematic fitness landscape should show three regimes — smooth (K=0), intermediate with plateaus (K=2 to 3), and rugged (K large). The plateau formation peaks at **intermediate K** (typically $K \approx 2$ to $3$ for small $\ell$).

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Random walk autocorrelation (2.5 marks):** A random walk of length $T$ is performed on the fitness landscape, applying successive random mutations. The autocorrelation coefficient at lag $k$ is:

$$
\rho(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-k} (f_t - \bar{f})(f_{t+k} - \bar{f})}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} (f_t - \bar{f})^2}
$$

2. **Derivation for a strict plateau (2.5 marks):** On a strict plateau, $f_t = c$ (constant) for all $t$ within the plateau, so $\bar{f} = c$ and:

$$
\rho(k) = \frac{\sum_{t=1}^{T-k} (c - c)(c - c)}{\sum_{t=1}^{T} (c - c)^2} = \frac{0}{0}
$$

Taking the limit via perturbation: for a near-plateau with $f_t = c + \delta_t$ where $\delta_t$ are i.i.d. with zero mean and small variance, $\rho(k) \to 1$ for small $k$ and $\rho(k) \to 0$ for large $k$.

3. **Diagnostic value (2 marks):** 
   - If $\rho(1) \approx 1$, the landscape is **smooth** (highly correlated neighbors) → **no plateaus**, easy for GAs.
   - If $\rho(1) \approx 0$ or negative, the landscape is **uncorrelated** → maximally rugged, **no useful gradient**.
   - If $\rho(k) \approx 1$ for $k \leq D$ and then drops sharply at $k = D$, this is the **signature of a plateau** of diameter $D$.
   - The "correlation length" $\ell_c$ (where $\rho(k) = 1/e$) is a single number summarizing landscape difficulty.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing plateau with local optimum:** Both cause GA failure, but plateau = *flat extended region* (random walk), local optimum = *single point with zero gradient* (premature convergence). Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for conflating them.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula for $T_{\text{esc}}$:** Always include the population factor $N$ when computing escape time. Forgetting $N$ costs **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting units / dimensional analysis:** KTU paper-setters love dimensional checks. Always verify your final answer has units of "generations" or "iterations."
> 4. **Missing the autocorrelation diagnosis:** When asked "how to detect a plateau," a complete answer must mention **autocorrelation $\rho(k)$ profile** AND the **hitting-time $T_{\text{esc}}$** analysis. Only mentioning one loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **Not drawing a labeled diagram:** For 7-mark sub-parts, a **schematic fitness landscape with a labeled plateau region** is mandatory. Omitting it costs **1 to 2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plateau in Evolutionary Computing (Module 2, PECNT869):**

- **Definition:** A connected region $\mathcal{P}$ where $\vert f(x) - f(y) \vert \leq \varepsilon$ for all $x, y \in \mathcal{P}$.
- **Plateau ≠ Local Optimum:** Plateau = flat extended region; Local optimum = single peak with no higher neighbor.
- **Random Walk Behavior:** Once on a plateau, EA performs a neutral random walk; $\rho(k) \approx 1$ within plateau.
- **Escape Time Formula:** $T_{\text{esc}} \approx D^2 / (2 N p_m \ell)$ — inversely proportional to $N$ and $p_m$.
- **Neutrality Ratio:** $\nu = \vert \mathcal{P} \vert / \vert \mathcal{S} \vert$ measures the fraction of flat space.
- **NK-Model Connection:** Plateaus are *maximally abundant* at intermediate $K$ (around $K = 2$ to $3$ for binary landscapes).
- **Selection Pressure Collapse:** Tournament, Roulette, and rank-based selection all fail on plateaus.
- **Remedies (high-yield for KTU):**
  1. **Increase population size $N$** — linear reduction in $T_{\text{esc}}$.
  2. **Adaptive / elevated mutation rate $p_m$** — boosts escape probability.
  3. **Random restarts** upon plateau detection (no improvement over $k$ generations).
  4. **Niches and speciation** — preserve diversity, prevent collapse onto a single plateau sub-region.
  5. **Fitness shaping / novelty search** — replace fitness with behavioral novelty to escape plateaus.
  6. **Hypermutation / Cataclysmic Mutation** — periodic large-$p_m$ phases to "shake" the population off plateaus.
- **Real-world domains:** RNA folding, NAS, VLSI floorplanning, protein folding, instruction-set optimization.
- **Diagnostic tools:** Fitness-distance correlation (FDC), random-walk autocorrelation $\rho(k)$, neutrality ratio $\nu$, escape-time measurement.
- **Key dimensionless numbers:** Neutrality ratio $\nu \in [0, 1]$; correlation length $\ell_c$ (in mutations); escape time $T_{\text{esc}}$ (in generations).
- **Famous KTU-style exam trigger phrases:** *"Discuss the effect of plateaus on GA convergence"* / *"How do you detect plateau regions in a fitness landscape?"* / *"Explain with NK-model how ruggedness arises."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Approaches Combining Simulated Annealing and Hill Climbing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hybrid Approaches Combining Simulated Annealing and Hill Climbing # Module 2: Hybrid Approaches Combining Simulated Annealing and Hill Climbing ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Hybrid Approach Combining Simulated Annealing (SA) and Hill Climbing (HC)** is a metaheuristic optimization strategy that synergistically merges the **local search exploitation** capability of Hill Climbing with the **probabilistic global exploration** capability of Simulated Annealing. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, such hybrids are classified under *Memetic Algorithms* or *Hybrid Metaheuristics*, where SA acts as the **outer-loop diversification framework** (via the Metropolis criterion) and HC acts as the **inner-loop intensification operator** (via greedy neighborhood descent). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Hybrid approaches that integrate SA and HC belong to the broader class of *trajectory-based metaheuristics*. They are specifically designed to overcome the **premature convergence** problem of pure Hill Climbing and the **slow convergence** problem of pure Simulated Annealing. The mathematical formalization rests on a **joint acceptance criterion**: $$ A(x', x, T) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } f(x') \geq f(x) \\ \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{T}\right) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ where the inner HC step ensures $x'$ is a *local optimum* in the neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$ before the SA layer evaluates global acceptance. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: Trekking a Mountain Range in Fog** > > Imagine you are a hiker trying to reach the highest peak in a vast, foggy mountain range, but you have **two tools**: > > 1. **Hill Climbing (HC)** = Your **torchlight in clear weather** — it always shows the steepest upward step. You climb greedily. But in fog, you get trapped on a foothill (local optimum). > 2. **Simulated Annealing (SA)** = Your **satellite GPS with probability-based downhills** — sometimes you deliberately step *downward* (a worse move) to escape foothills, controlled by a "temperature" $T$ that starts high (chaotic exploration) and cools down (focused exploitation). > > **Hybrid SA-HC** = You use the **torchlight (HC) at every step** to find the best local direction, but the **GPS (SA)** occasionally overrides your torchlight, allowing you to temporarily descend a foothill to explore an unmapped valley. As the day cools (T decreases), you trust the torchlight more and the GPS less. You always reach the true summit. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Boltzmann Constant ($k_B$):** Set to **1** in algorithmic contexts for normalization. - **Initial Temperature ($T_0$):** Chosen such that initial acceptance probability $\approx$ **0.8** to **0.95**. - **Cooling Schedule:** Geometric decay $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ with $\alpha \in [0.90, 0.99]$. - **Markov Chain Length ($L_k$):** Number of inner-loop HC iterations per temperature level. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Decomposition The hybrid algorithm operates in **two nested loops**, each with a distinct responsibility: ### Outer Loop — Simulated Annealing Layer (Diversification) - **Purpose:** Maintain global search capability, accept *worsening moves* with controlled probability. - **Mechanism:** Governs the temperature parameter $T$ via a cooling schedule. - **Why needed:** Pure HC always moves uphill; without SA, the search freezes at the first local maximum. ### Inner Loop — Hill Climbing Layer (Intensification) - **Purpose:** Perform greedy local refinement to a nearby local optimum. - **Mechanism:** Iteratively replace current solution with the **best neighbor** in $\mathcal{N}(x)$ as long as improvement exists. - **Why needed:** Pure SA accepts random moves; without HC, the algorithm wastes iterations wandering through mediocre regions. ### The Hybrid Coupling Logic 1. Generate a candidate $x'$ via stochastic perturbation. 2. **Refine $x'$ using HC** (run steepest-ascent until a local peak is reached). 3. Apply the **Metropolis acceptance rule** of SA on the refined $x'$. 4. Update temperature $T$. This creates an *intensification-then-diversification* cycle: HC polishes every trial solution, and SA decides whether to keep or discard it. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is a **board-exam-ready summary** of all equations you must memorize for this topic. | **Component** | **Formula / Equation** | **Description** | **Typical Value / Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | Metropolis Acceptance (Worse Move) | $P_{accept} = \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{T}\right)$ | Probability of accepting a worse neighbor | $0 \leq P_{accept} \leq 1$ | | Steepest Ascent HC Update | $x_{k+1} = \arg\max_{x' \in \mathcal{N}(x_k)} f(x')$ | Greedy move to the best neighbor | Iterative | | Geometric Cooling | $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$ | Multiplicative temperature reduction | $\alpha \in [0.90, 0.99]$ | | Logarithmic Cooling | $T_k = \frac{T_0}{\log(k + c)}$ | Slow cooling (theoretical convergence) | $c \geq 1$ | | Cauchy Cooling (Fast) | $T_k = \frac{T_0}{k + 1}$ | Used in Fast SA | $k$ = iteration | | Initial Temperature Rule | $T_0 = \frac{-\Delta f_{avg}}{\ln(P_{init})}$ | Set $T_0$ for desired initial acceptance $P_{init}$ | $P_{init} \in [0.8, 0.95]$ | | Stopping Condition | $T \leq T_{f}$ or $k \geq k_{max}$ | Frozen system or iteration limit | $T_f \approx 10^{-3}$ | | Hybrid Improvement Ratio | $\eta = \frac{f_{HC}(x_{refined}) - f(x_{raw})}{f(x_{raw})}$ | Gain from HC refinement step | $\eta > 0$ desirable | | Neighborhood Size (Discrete) | $\vert \mathcal{N}(x) \vert$ | Number of neighbors of $x$ | Discrete count | | Boltzmann Distribution | $P_{eq}(x) = \frac{1}{Z}\exp\left(\frac{f(x)}{T}\right)$ | Equilibrium distribution at temperature $T$ | $Z$ = partition function | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Domain** | **Application** | **Why Hybrid SA-HC Works** | |---|---|---| | VLSI Circuit Design | Placement & routing of millions of gates | HC refines local wire-length, SA escapes routing congestion traps | | Telecommunications | Antenna placement, network routing | SA handles discrete topology, HC optimizes link costs | | Machine Learning | Hyper-parameter tuning of neural networks | SA explores global parameter space, HC fine-tunes locally | | Robotics | Path planning in dynamic environments | SA avoids dead-ends (exploration), HC ensures smooth local trajectories | | Bioinformatics | Protein folding / DNA sequence alignment | Hybrid balances long-range structural search with local torsion optimization | | Operations Research | Job-shop scheduling, TSP, VRP | SA escapes scheduling conflicts, HC greedily compresses makespan | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Metropolis Acceptance Criterion from Statistical Mechanics The Metropolis criterion in SA is rooted in the **Boltzmann distribution** of statistical mechanics. We derive it from first principles. ### Step 1: State the Boltzmann Distribution In thermodynamic equilibrium at temperature $T$, the probability of a system being in energy state $E$ is: $$ P_{eq}(E) = \frac{1}{Z} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-E}{k_B T}\right) $$ where $Z = \sum_{E} \exp(-E / k_B T)$ is the partition function (normalization constant), and $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant. **Conversion Logic:** In optimization, we *invert the sign* because we are maximizing a fitness (or minimizing a cost). Equivalently, replace $-E$ with $f(x)$ (fitness). $$ P_{eq}(x) = \frac{1}{Z} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{f(x)}{k_B T}\right) $$ ### Step 2: Consider a State Transition $x \rightarrow x'$ Suppose the algorithm is currently at state $x$ with fitness $f(x)$ and considers a transition to $x'$ with fitness $f(x')$. The **transition probability** is the ratio of equilibrium probabilities: $$ \frac{P_{eq}(x')}{P_{eq}(x)} = \frac{\exp(f(x') / k_B T)}{\exp(f(x) / k_B T)} = \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{k_B T}\right) $$ **Conversion Logic:** If $f(x') > f(x)$ (better neighbor), the exponent is positive, and the ratio exceeds 1 — meaning the system *naturally* prefers the higher-fitness state. The move is accepted with probability 1. ### Step 3: The Metropolis Acceptance Rule If $f(x') \leq f(x)$ (worse neighbor), the exponent is non-positive, and the ratio is $\leq 1$. To maintain detailed balance with the equilibrium distribution, we accept the move with probability: $$ P_{accept}(x \rightarrow x') = \min\left\{1, \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{k_B T}\right)\right\} $$ **Conversion Logic:** This is the famous **Metropolis criterion**. Setting $k_B = 1$ in the algorithmic context yields: $$ P_{accept} = \min\left\{1, \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{T}\right)\right\} $$ ### Step 4: Behavior at Temperature Limits - **As $T \rightarrow \infty$:** $\exp(\Delta f / T) \rightarrow 1$. All moves accepted (pure random walk). - **As $T \rightarrow 0$:** For $\Delta f < 0$, $\exp(\Delta f / T) \rightarrow 0$. Worse moves rejected (pure HC). - **At moderate $T$:** A probabilistic trade-off between exploration and exploitation. > [!NOTE] > This is the mathematical proof that **SA reduces to HC in the limit $T \to 0$**, justifying the hybrid coupling. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: HC vs SA vs Hybrid | **Property** | **Pure Hill Climbing** | **Pure Simulated Annealing** | **Hybrid SA-HC** | |---|---|---|---| | Acceptance of worse move | Never | Probabilistic (Metropolis) | Probabilistic (Metropolis) | | Local refinement | Yes (greedy) | No (random walk) | Yes (HC inner loop) | | Global exploration | Poor (traps at local optima) | Good (controlled by T) | Good (SA outer loop) | | Convergence speed | Fast | Slow | Moderate to Fast | | Solution quality | Local optimum | Near-global optimum | Near-global optimum (higher quality) | | Computational cost per iteration | Low | Moderate | Higher (HC adds overhead) | | Total iterations to converge | Few | Many | Moderate | | Probability of finding global optimum | Low (for multimodal) | High (if cooled slowly) | **Very High** | | Implementation complexity | Simple | Moderate | Moderate to Complex | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Hybrid SA-HC Iteration **Problem:** Maximize $f(x) = -x^2 + 10x$ over $x \in \mathbb{R}$ (discretized to integers for SA). Initial state $x_0 = 2$, $T_0 = 5$, $\alpha = 0.9$. ### Step 1: Inner HC Refinement At $x = 2$: $f(2) = -4 + 20 = 16$. Neighbors $\{1, 2, 3\}$. $$ f(1) = -1 + 10 = 9, \quad f(2) = 16, \quad f(3) = -9 + 30 = 21 $$ Best neighbor is $x' = 3$ with $f(3) = 21$. HC moves to $x = 3$. Since $f(3) > f(2)$, pure HC stops here (local max). Continue: $f(4) = -16 + 40 = 24$, $f(5) = -25 + 50 = 25$, $f(6) = -36 + 60 = 24$. Peak at $x = 5$, $f = 25$. ### Step 2: SA Layer Generates a Perturbation Suppose SA perturbs $x = 5$ to a random $x' = 1$ (a "downhill" jump). $f(1) = 9$, $f(5) = 25$. ### Step 3: Apply Metropolis Criterion $$ P_{accept} = \exp\left(\frac{f(1) - f(5)}{T_0}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{9 - 25}{5}\right) = \exp(-3.2) \approx 0.0408 $$ ### Step 4: Decision Generate uniform random $r \in [0,1]$. If $r \leq 0.0408$ (only 4% chance), accept the bad move. Otherwise, reject. Since probability is low at $T_0 = 5$, the algorithm mostly stays near $x = 5$. ### Step 5: Cool Down After some iterations, reduce: $T_1 = 0.9 \times 5 = 4.5$, then $T_2 = 4.05$, etc. Eventually $T \to 0$ and the algorithm behaves like pure HC, locking in the global maximum $x = 5$, $f = 25$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hybrid SA-HC Algorithm Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Initialize x0, T0, alpha] --> B[Set current solution x = x0] B --> C[Outer SA Loop: Iterate k = 0,1,2,...] C --> D[Inner HC Loop: Refine x in N x] D --> E{Is there a better
neighbor in N x} E -->|Yes| F[Set x = best neighbor in N x] F --> D E -->|No, local max reached| G[Store refined x as xHC] G --> H[Generate candidate x prime via stochastic perturbation] H --> I[Inner HC Loop: Refine x prime in N x prime] I --> J[xRefined = locally optimized candidate] J --> K[Compute delta f = f xRefined minus f x] K --> L{delta f greater than or equal to 0} L -->|Yes, better or equal| M[Accept: Set x = xRefined] L -->|No, worse move| N[Compute P accept = exp delta f divided by T k] N --> O{Generate r uniform 0 1
r less than P accept} O -->|Yes| M O -->|No| P[Reject: Keep x unchanged] M --> Q[Cooling Schedule: T k+1 = alpha times T k] P --> Q Q --> R{Stopping Criterion Met
T k less than T final OR k greater than k max} R -->|No| C R -->|Yes| S[Return best solution x best] S --> T[End] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer[Input and Initialization Block] I1[Problem Definition f, Domain D] I2[Hyperparameters T0, alpha, L k, k max] I3[Initial Solution x0] end subgraph SAOuter[Simulated Annealing Outer Block] S1[Temperature Controller: T k+1 equals alpha T k] S2[Metropolis Acceptance Module] S3[Accept or Reject Decision Gate] end subgraph HCInner[Hill Climbing Inner Block] H1[Stochastic Perturbation Generator] H2[Neighborhood Evaluator N x] H3[Steepest Ascent Selector] H4[Local Optimum Detector] end subgraph OutputLayer[Output and Logging Block] O1[Best Solution Tracker x best, f best] O2[Convergence Log: T, f x, acceptance rate] O3[Final Solution Report] end I1 --> H2 I2 --> S1 I3 --> S3 S1 --> S2 H1 --> H2 H2 --> H3 H3 --> H4 H4 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> H1 S3 --> O1 S3 --> O2 O1 --> O3 O2 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture: Exploration vs Exploitation Zones ```mermaid graph TB subgraph ExploreZone[High Temperature Exploration Zone] E1[Random Walk Behavior] E2[Accepts worse moves with high probability] E3[Discovers new basins of attraction] end subgraph MixZone[Mid Temperature Transition Zone] M1[Balanced acceptance of up and down moves] M2[HC refines promising regions] M3[SA escapes local maxima] end subgraph ExploitZone[Low Temperature Exploitation Zone] X1[Behavior converges to pure Hill Climbing] X2[Greedy improvement only] X3[Final convergence to global or near global optimum] end E1 --> M1 M1 --> X1 E2 --> M2 M2 --> X2 E3 --> M3 M3 --> X3 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the Metropolis acceptance criterion in Simulated Annealing. Explain its role in enabling the algorithm to escape local optima.** **Model Answer:** The Metropolis acceptance criterion is a probabilistic rule that allows a Simulated Annealing algorithm to accept a *worsening* move with probability: $$ P_{accept} = \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{T}\right) \quad \text{when } f(x') < f(x) $$ **Role in escaping local optima:** At high temperature $T$, the exponential term approaches 1, and worse moves are accepted with high probability. This injects randomness into the search, allowing the algorithm to move *downhill* out of shallow local maxima and explore alternative basins of attraction. As $T$ decreases, the probability of accepting worse moves diminishes, and the algorithm eventually converges to a high-quality solution. Thus, the Metropolis criterion provides the **diversification mechanism** necessary to overcome the *premature convergence* problem inherent in pure greedy search. *(3 marks: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for explanation of escape mechanism)* --- ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare Hill Climbing and Simulated Annealing with respect to their handling of local optima and convergence behavior.** **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Hill Climbing (HC)** | **Simulated Annealing (SA)** | |---|---|---| | Handling of local optima | **Trapped** — terminates at the first local maximum encountered | **Can escape** — uses Metropolis criterion to accept worse moves | | Convergence speed | Fast (fewer iterations) | Slow (requires careful cooling) | | Solution quality | Local optimum (suboptimal for multimodal) | Near-global optimum (if cooled slowly) | | Nature of search | Deterministic / Greedy | Stochastic / Probabilistic | | Parameter sensitivity | Low (mostly neighbor selection) | High (T₀, α, chain length) | In a **Hybrid SA-HC** approach, HC provides fast local refinement, while SA provides global escape, giving the best of both worlds. *(3 marks: 2 marks for comparison points, 1 mark for hybrid mention)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **Hybrid Simulated Annealing – Hill Climbing (SA-HC)** algorithm. Describe its motivation, algorithmic structure, and the role of each component. Discuss why pure HC fails on multimodal landscapes and how SA overcomes this. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Metropolis acceptance probability** from the Boltzmann distribution of statistical mechanics. Show mathematically that SA reduces to HC in the limit $T \to 0$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **1. Motivation (2 marks):** Pure Hill Climbing is a *greedy local search* that always moves to the neighbor with the highest fitness. On a **multimodal fitness landscape** containing multiple peaks, HC terminates at the *first local maximum* it encounters, which is rarely the global maximum. This is the **local optima problem**. Pure Simulated Annealing addresses this by occasionally accepting worse moves, but its random-walk nature means it wastes iterations in poor regions without refining promising solutions. The **hybrid SA-HC** algorithm combines: - **SA's global exploration** (escape local optima via Metropolis criterion). - **HC's local exploitation** (refine each candidate to its local peak before evaluation). **2. Algorithmic Structure (3 marks):** ``` Algorithm: Hybrid SA-HC Input: f(x), T0, α, L, k_max Output: x_best 1. Initialize x ← x0, T ← T0, x_best ← x 2. For k = 0 to k_max do 3. // Inner HC: Refine current solution 4. x ← HC_Steepest_Ascent(x, N(x)) 5. // Generate candidate via stochastic perturbation 6. For i = 1 to L do 7. x' ← Perturb(x) 8. // Inner HC: Refine candidate 9. x' ← HC_Steepest_Ascent(x', N(x')) 10. // SA Acceptance 11. Δf ← f(x') - f(x) 12. If Δf ≥ 0 OR random(0,1) < exp(Δf / T): 13. x ← x' 14. T ← α · T // Cool down 15. If f(x) > f(x_best): x_best ← x 16. Return x_best ``` **3. Role of Components (2 marks):** - **HC inner loop (lines 4, 9):** Each trial solution is polished to its local peak. This ensures the SA layer only compares *quality local optima*, not raw noisy candidates. - **SA outer loop (lines 11–14):** Decides which refined local optimum to retain and which to discard, with a temperature-controlled probability. - **Cooling schedule (line 14):** Gradual reduction of $T$ shifts the algorithm from broad exploration to focused exploitation. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **1. Boltzmann Distribution (3 marks):** In statistical mechanics, a system in thermal equilibrium at temperature $T$ has its energy state $E$ distributed according to: $$ P_{eq}(E) = \frac{1}{Z} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{-E}{k_B T}\right), \quad Z = \sum_E \exp\left(\frac{-E}{k_B T}\right) $$ For an optimization problem where we *maximize* fitness $f(x)$ (analogous to *minimizing* energy $-f(x)$), we substitute $-E = f(x)$, giving: $$ P_{eq}(x) = \frac{1}{Z} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{f(x)}{k_B T}\right) $$ **2. Deriving the Acceptance Ratio (2 marks):** For a transition $x \rightarrow x'$, detailed balance requires: $$ P_{eq}(x) \cdot P(x \rightarrow x') = P_{eq}(x') \cdot P(x' \rightarrow x) $$ Taking the ratio of forward to reverse transition probabilities and substituting the Boltzmann form: $$ \frac{P(x \rightarrow x')}{P(x' \rightarrow x)} = \frac{P_{eq}(x')}{P_{eq}(x)} = \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{k_B T}\right) $$ Setting $k_B = 1$ (algorithmic convention) and enforcing the **Metropolis rule** (accept forward with probability 1 if $f(x') \geq f(x)$, else accept with the ratio): $$ P_{accept}(x \rightarrow x') = \min\left\{1, \exp\left(\frac{f(x') - f(x)}{T}\right)\right\} $$ **3. Limit as $T \to 0$ (2 marks):** Consider a worse move with $\Delta f = f(x') - f(x) < 0$. As $T \to 0$: $$ \lim_{T \to 0} \exp\left(\frac{\Delta f}{T}\right) = \exp(-\infty) = 0 $$ Hence $P_{accept} \to 0$ for all worsening moves. The algorithm only accepts improvements, which is **exactly the Hill Climbing rule**. This proves: $$ \lim_{T \to 0} \text{SA}(T) = \text{HC} $$ Q.E.D. --- ### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)* **(a)** Discuss different **cooling schedules** used in Simulated Annealing (Geometric, Logarithmic, Cauchy, and Adaptive). Compare their convergence properties and computational efficiency. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design a **Hybrid SA-HC algorithm** for solving the **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** with $n = 20$ cities. Specify the perturbation operator, the HC neighborhood structure, the cooling schedule, and the termination condition. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **1. Geometric Cooling (2 marks):** $T_{k+1} = \alpha \cdot T_k$, with $\alpha \in [0.90, 0.99]$. - **Pros:** Simple, widely used, fast convergence. - **Cons:** No theoretical guarantee of global optimum; $\alpha$ tuning required. - **Use case:** Most practical applications. **2. Logarithmic Cooling (2 marks):** $T_k = \frac{T_0}{\log(k + c)}$, with $c \geq 1$. - **Pros:** **Theoretical guarantee** of asymptotic convergence to global optimum (Geman & Geman, 1984). - **Cons:** Extremely slow — impractical for large problems. - **Use case:** Theoretical benchmarks only. **3. Cauchy Cooling / Fast SA (1.5 marks):** $T_k = \frac{T_0}{k + 1}$. - **Pros:** Faster than logarithmic; used in *Fast Simulated Annealing*. - **Cons:** Still slower than geometric. - **Use case:** Medium-scale optimization. **4. Adaptive Cooling (1.5 marks):** Adjust $\alpha$ based on real-time feedback (e.g., acceptance rate). - **Pros:** Self-tuning; robust across problems. - **Cons:** Complex to implement; feedback design non-trivial. - **Use case:** Black-box optimization with unknown landscape. | **Schedule** | **Formula** | **Convergence Speed** | **Theoretical Guarantee** | **Tuning Effort** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Geometric | $\alpha \cdot T_k$ | Fast | No | Moderate | | Logarithmic | $T_0 / \log(k + c)$ | Very Slow | Yes | Low | | Cauchy | $T_0 / (k+1)$ | Moderate | No | Low | | Adaptive | Depends on metric | Variable | No | High | --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Design of Hybrid SA-HC for TSP (n = 20):** **1. Solution Representation (1.5 marks):** A solution is a permutation $\pi = (\pi_1, \pi_2, \ldots, \pi_{20})$ representing the order in which the 20 cities are visited. The fitness is the negative tour length: $$ f(\pi) = -\sum_{i=1}^{20} d(\pi_i, \pi_{i+1}) \quad \text{with } \pi_{21} = \pi_1 $$ where $d(\cdot, \cdot)$ is the Euclidean distance. **2. HC Neighborhood Structure (1.5 marks):** Use the **2-opt** neighborhood: reverse a random sub-sequence $[\pi_i, \ldots, \pi_j]$. At each HC step, evaluate all $C(20, 2) = 190$ such reversals and pick the one with minimum tour length. Run steepest-ascent HC until no 2-opt swap improves the tour. **3. SA Perturbation Operator (1.5 marks):** A **double-bridge kick**: split the tour into 4 segments $A \vert B \vert C \vert D$ and reconnect as $A \vert D \vert C \vert B$. This is a non-sequential perturbation that HC cannot undo in a single 2-opt, ensuring genuine diversification. **4. Cooling Schedule (1.5 marks):** - $T_0$: Set such that initial acceptance probability $\approx 0.9$. For TSP with average tour length $L_{avg}$, use: $$ T_0 = \frac{-\Delta L_{avg}}{\ln(0.9)} \approx 9.5 \cdot \Delta L_{avg} $$ - $\alpha = 0.95$ (geometric cooling). - $L_k = 50 \cdot n = 1000$ (inner iterations per temperature). **5. Termination Condition (1 mark):** Stop when **either**: - $T < 10^{-6}$ (system frozen), **or** - $k \geq k_{max} = 5000$ (iteration budget), **or** - No improvement in $f(x_{best})$ for 200 consecutive temperature levels. **6. Algorithm Pseudocode (Bonus):** ``` Initialize random tour π, T = T0, π_best = π For k = 1 to k_max: π_ref ← π For i = 1 to L_k: π' ← DoubleBridge(π_ref) π' ← HC_2opt(π') // Refine Δf ← length(π') - length(π) If Δf < 0 OR rand() < exp(-Δf / T): π_ref ← π' π ← π_ref If length(π) < length(π_best): π_best = π T ← 0.95 · T If T < 1e-6: break Return π_best ``` --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Forgetting the inner HC loop** — Many students describe SA or HC separately but miss the *coupling*. A hybrid *must* show both loops interacting. **(–2 marks)** > 2. **Sign errors in Metropolis formula** — The exponent is $(f(x') - f(x))/T$ or $-\Delta E / T$ for cost minimization. Confusing these is the #1 error. **(–1 mark)** > 3. **Skipping the limit $T \to 0$ proof** — The question often asks *why* SA reduces to HC; failing to write $\exp(-\infty) = 0$ loses full marks on the derivation. **(–1 mark)** > 4. **Confusing cooling schedules** — Geometric is *not* logarithmic. The formulas $T_0/\log(k+c)$ and $\alpha T_k$ are different beasts; mixing them up loses marks. **(–1 mark)** > 5. **TSP perturbation choice** — Single 2-opt is *not* a valid SA perturbation for hybrid (HC can undo it trivially). You need double-bridge or 3-opt to ensure genuine diversification. **(–2 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **Hybrid SA-HC = SA outer loop + HC inner loop.** Memorize this two-tier structure. - **Metropolis criterion:** $P_{accept} = \min\{1, \exp(\Delta f / T)\}$ where $\Delta f = f(x') - f(x)$. For minimization, use $\exp(-\Delta E / T)$. - **Cooling schedules to know cold:** - Geometric: $T_{k+1} = \alpha T_k$, $\alpha \in [0.90, 0.99]$. - Logarithmic: $T_k = T_0 / \log(k + c)$ — *theoretical optimum but slow*. - Cauchy: $T_k = T_0 / (k+1)$ — Fast SA. - **Limit theorem:** $\lim_{T \to 0} \text{SA} = \text{HC}$ — *memorize the proof, it's a frequent derivation question*. - **HC neighborhood types:** Steepest-ascent (best neighbor), Stochastic (random neighbor), First-choice (first improving). - **Why hybrid?** HC refines (exploitation), SA escapes (exploration). Together: best of both. - **Initial temperature rule:** $T_0 = -\Delta f_{avg} / \ln(P_{init})$. Aim for $P_{init} \approx 0.8$–$0.95$. - **Stopping criteria:** $T < T_{final}$ (≈ $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$), OR iteration cap, OR stagnation of best fitness. - **Common TSP perturbations:** 2-opt (HC neighborhood), 3-opt, double-bridge (good SA kick). - **Boltzmann distribution origin:** Always mention *statistical mechanics* and *detailed balance* in derivations. - **Common pairing mistakes to avoid:** SA+GA, SA+PSO, SA+TS are *other* hybrids — for this specific module, **stick to SA+HC only**. - **Keywords that earn marks:** *diversification, intensification, premature convergence, Metropolis, cooling schedule, neighborhood, perturbation, local optimum, global optimum, Markov chain*. - **Numerical readiness:** Be prepared to compute $P_{accept}$ for given $\Delta f$ and $T$ in exam problems. - **Trade-off awareness:** SA is *slower* but *more robust*; HC is *faster* but *local*. Hybrid balances both.

topics.title: Introduction to Genetic Algorithms - Biological Background
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Genetic Algorithms - Biological Background

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genetic Algorithm (GA):** A *stochastic, population-based, adaptive search and optimization algorithm* inspired by the mechanisms of **natural genetics** and **Darwinian evolution**. It maintains a population of candidate solutions (called *individuals* or *chromosomes*) and evolves them over successive generations using biologically-mimicking operators—**Selection, Crossover, and Mutation**—to converge toward optimal or near-optimal solutions.

The **Biological Background** of GA draws directly from two foundational pillars of life sciences:
1. **Genetics** — the science of heredity, variation, and gene expression in living organisms.
2. **Neo-Darwinian Evolution** — the principle of *natural selection* acting upon heritable variation to produce better-adapted organisms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exam:** Genetic Algorithms are *not* a simulation of biology. They are a **computational abstraction** that borrows *only the strategy* (not the biochemical machinery) of biological evolution to solve hard optimization problems.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you have a **locker combination lock** with 4 dials, each with digits 0–9. You have forgotten the code, and brute force would mean trying $10^4 = 10{,}000$ combinations. Now imagine you ask **1,000 random strangers** to each guess a 4-digit number. You check how many of the tumblers fall into place for each guess (this is a *fitness score*). You keep the **top 10%** of guesses (the *fittest* individuals), and ask them to "combine" their guesses (e.g., dad gives 2 dials, mom gives 2 dials → child has 4 dials). Occasionally, you randomly flip one digit (a *mutation*) to introduce novelty. Repeat for generations.

Within a few dozen generations, you will have guessed the right combination—even though no single "stranger" ever knew it. This is the **soul of a Genetic Algorithm**.

| Biological Term | Engineering Counterpart | Intuitive Meaning |
|-----------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Chromosome | Solution vector / String | A single candidate answer |
| Gene | Parameter / Bit | One decision variable in the answer |
| Population | Set of candidate solutions | All current guesses being evaluated |
| Fitness | Objective function value | "How close am I to the right answer?" |
| Selection | Survival of the fittest | Keeping only the best guesses |
| Crossover | Recombination | Mixing two good guesses to make a better one |
| Mutation | Random perturbation | Randomly changing one digit to escape a rut |
| Generation | Iteration | One full cycle of evaluation and reproduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Remember:** A standard GA population size for KTU numerical problems is typically $N = 20$ to $N = 100$. The number of generations $G$ varies from **50 to 500** depending on problem complexity.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Biological Reality:** The human genome contains approximately **3.2 billion** base pairs and around **20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes.
- **Computational Abstraction:** A GA chromosome in a typical KTU problem contains anywhere from **8 to 100 genes** (bits or real values), making it a *highly simplified* model.
- **Generation Gap (G-gap):** A parameter (default = **1.0** in canonical GA) representing the fraction of the population replaced each generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Examiners love asking *"Why do we mimic biology in computing?"* The answer is: Biological evolution is the only known process that has produced *engineering-grade optimality* (the eye, the brain, photosynthesis) through *blind search* over **3.8 billion years**. Mimicking it gives us a robust optimizer.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Biological Pillars Behind GAs

#### 2.1.1 Pillars of Genetics (Gregor Mendel, 1865)

Genetics is the study of how traits pass from parents to offspring. The fundamental unit of inheritance is the **gene**. Key terms every KTU student must know:

- **Gene:** A segment of DNA that codes for a specific trait (e.g., eye color). In a GA, a gene is a *single decision variable*.
- **Allele:** The different possible *values* a gene can take. For example, a gene for "height" may have alleles *Tall (T)* or *Short (t)*. In a binary GA, the alleles are **{0, 1}**.
- **Chromosome:** A complete structure of DNA containing many genes. In a GA, it is a *string/vector* of genes representing one full solution.
- **Genotype:** The *genetic* makeup of an organism (the encoded string).
- **Phenotype:** The *expressed* form of the genotype—the actual physical solution after decoding.
- **Locus:** The *position* of a gene on the chromosome (a fixed index in the vector).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mendel's Two Laws (Direct KTU Question Material):**
> 1. **Law of Segregation:** Each individual has two alleles per gene (in diploid organisms), and these alleles *separate* during gamete formation, so each parent passes *only one* allele to the offspring.
> 2. **Law of Independent Assortment:** Genes for different traits are inherited *independently* of one another (this is the theoretical basis for **multi-point crossover** in GAs).

#### 2.1.2 Pillars of Neo-Darwinian Evolution (Charles Darwin, 1859)

Darwin's *On the Origin of Species* proposed that evolution works through three observable facts and two inferences:

**Three Facts:**
1. **Variation:** Offspring within a species are *not identical*; there is heritable variation.
2. **Inheritance:** Many of these variations are *passed from parent to offspring*.
3. **Struggle for Existence:** Resources (food, mates, shelter) are *limited*, producing competition.

**Two Inferences:**
4. **Survival of the Fittest (Natural Selection):** Individuals with *favorable* variations are more likely to survive and reproduce. This is the *selection operator* in GAs.
5. **Gradualism:** Over many generations, favorable variations *accumulate*, leading to new species. This is the *iterative generational loop* in GAs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Neo-Darwinian Synthesis (1930s–40s):** The merger of Mendelian genetics with Darwinian selection. This synthesis—**heritable variation + differential reproductive success**—is the *direct intellectual ancestor* of Genetic Algorithms.

---

### 2.2 Mapping Biology to Algorithm: The Conceptual Bridge

The genius of **John Holland (1975)**, who invented GAs, was to abstract the essential *algorithm* hidden inside biological evolution. The mapping is:

| Biological Entity | Algorithmic Counterpart | Mathematical Formalism |
|-------------------|-------------------------|------------------------|
| Population of organisms | Set of candidate solutions | $P(t) = \{x_1^{(t)}, x_2^{(t)}, \dots, x_N^{(t)}\}$ |
| Chromosome (string of DNA) | Encoded solution | $x_i = (b_1, b_2, \dots, b_L)$, where $b_k \in \{0,1\}$ |
| Gene | Decision variable | $b_k$ at locus $k$ |
| Fitness | Objective function value | $f(x_i) \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| Selection pressure | Probabilistic survival | $P(x_i \text{ selected}) = f(x_i) / \sum_j f(x_j)$ |
| Crossover | Recombination of two parents | $x_{child} = \alpha x_{parent1} + (1-\alpha) x_{parent2}$ |
| Mutation | Random bit flip | $P(b_k \text{ flips}) = p_m \approx 0.01$ |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Population size | $N$ | Typically **20 to 100** |
| 2 | Chromosome length | $L$ | Number of genes/bits |
| 3 | Fitness function | $f: \mathbb{R}^L \to \mathbb{R}$ | Problem-specific objective |
| 4 | Roulette Wheel probability | $p_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Selection probability of individual $i$ |
| 5 | Crossover probability | $P_c$ | Typically **0.6 to 0.9** |
| 6 | Mutation probability | $P_m$ | Typically **0.001 to 0.01** per bit |
| 7 | Fitness ratio | $f_i / \bar{f}$ | Measures above-average fitness |
| 8 | Expected count (Roulette) | $E_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$ | Number of copies in next gen |
| 9 | Schema theorem (Holland) | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H,t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[ 1 - P_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - P_m \cdot o(H) \right]$ | The "central theorem" of GAs |
| 10 | Schema defining length | $\delta(H)$ | Distance between first and last fixed positions |
| 11 | Schema order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed positions in schema |
| 12 | Generation Gap (G-gap) | $G = n_{replace} / N$ | Fraction of population replaced |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975) — The Mathematical Heart of GA:** *Short, low-order, above-average fitness schemas (called "building blocks") grow exponentially across generations.* This is the theorem KTU examiners most often test in 14-mark questions.

---

### 2.4 Why This Works: Real-World Engineering Utility

The biological-mimetic approach is powerful because it provides:
- **Parallelism:** The entire population is evaluated *in parallel*—in nature, thousands of organisms evolve simultaneously.
- **No gradient required:** Unlike calculus-based optimizers, GA needs only the *fitness value*, not derivatives. This lets it solve **non-differentiable, discontinuous, and multimodal** problems.
- **Robustness to noise:** Stochastic operators (crossover/mutation) escape local optima, mimicking the diversity-preserving role of genetic recombination in biology.
- **Engineering Applications:** Antenna design at NASA, scheduling in manufacturing, portfolio optimization in finance, drug discovery, and VLSI circuit partitioning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** GAs are members of the broader class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)**, which also include Evolution Strategies (ES), Evolutionary Programming (EP), and Genetic Programming (GP). Module 2 of PECNT869 compares these families in detail.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Roulette Wheel Selection Probability

The **Roulette Wheel** (or *fitness proportionate selection*) is the canonical selection mechanism in GAs. Let us derive its probability distribution rigorously.

**Step 1 — Define the population.**
Suppose we have a population of $N = 4$ chromosomes, each with fitness $f_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Individual 1: } & f_1 = 10 \\
\text{Individual 2: } & f_2 = 30 \\
\text{Individual 3: } & f_3 = 40 \\
\text{Individual 4: } & f_4 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total fitness.**
Sum all individual fitness values. This represents the *total survival resources* in the population.

$$
F_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i = 10 + 30 + 40 + 20 = 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute selection probability for each individual.**
Divide each individual's fitness by the total. This is the *probability* that the individual is selected as a parent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \dfrac{f_1}{F_{total}} = \dfrac{10}{100} = 0.10 \\
p_2 &= \dfrac{f_2}{F_{total}} = \dfrac{30}{100} = 0.30 \\
p_3 &= \dfrac{f_3}{F_{total}} = \dfrac{40}{100} = 0.40 \\
p_4 &= \dfrac{f_4}{F_{total}} = \dfrac{20}{100} = 0.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify probability axioms.**
The probabilities must sum to 1 (certainty) and lie in [0, 1].

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} p_i = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the cumulative distribution.**
Convert the probability mass function to a cumulative distribution function (CDF) for sampling.

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_1 &= p_1 = 0.10 \\
q_2 &= p_1 + p_2 = 0.10 + 0.30 = 0.40 \\
q_3 &= p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.40 = 0.70 \\
q_4 &= p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + p_4 = 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Sample using a uniform random number.**
Draw $r \sim U(0,1)$. Select the individual $i$ such that $q_{i-1} < r \leq q_i$.

For example, if $r = 0.55$, then $0.40 < 0.55 \leq 0.70$, so **Individual 3 is selected** (which has the highest fitness = 40, consistent with the Darwinian bias).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Expected Count (Schema Theorem Component)

A "schema" is a *similarity template* over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$, where $*$ is a "don't care" symbol. For example, $H = 1\ast\ast 0$ matches $\{1000, 1010, 1100, 1110\}$.

**Step 1 — Define order and defining length.**
For schema $H = 1 \ast \ast 0$:
- Order $o(H)$ = number of *non-\** positions = 2 (the "1" and the "0").
- Defining length $\delta(H)$ = distance between first and last fixed positions = $4 - 1 = 3$.

**Step 2 — Compute average fitness of schema at generation $t$.**

$$
f(H, t) = \frac{1}{m(H, t)} \sum_{x \in P(t) \cap H} f(x)
$$

Here, $m(H, t)$ is the number of chromosomes in population $P(t)$ that match schema $H$.

**Step 3 — Selection contribution.**
Under fitness-proportionate selection, the expected number of copies of schema $H$ after *selection alone* (ignoring crossover/mutation) is:

$$
E[m(H, t+1)]_{\text{selection}} = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}(t)}
$$

This shows: **if $f(H) > \bar{f}$, schema $H$ grows; if $f(H) < \bar{f}$, it shrinks.** This is the formal statement of "survival of the fittest" applied to schemas.

**Step 4 — Add disruption from crossover.**
A schema is destroyed if the crossover point falls between its fixed positions. For single-point crossover with probability $P_c$:

$$
P_{\text{survival, crossover}} \geq 1 - P_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Add disruption from mutation.**
Each fixed position can flip with probability $P_m$. Survival probability:

$$
P_{\text{survival, mutation}} \approx (1 - P_m)^{o(H)} \geq 1 - P_m \cdot o(H)
$$

**Step 6 — Combine (the full Schema Theorem).**

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H, t)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[ 1 - P_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{L - 1} - P_m \cdot o(H) \right]
$$

This is **Holland's Schema Theorem (1975)**—the cornerstone theoretical result of GAs.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem:** Maximize $f(x) = x^2$ for $x \in [0, 31]$ using a 5-bit binary GA. The initial population of 4 chromosomes is given below. Compute fitness, selection probability, and expected count.

| Chromosome | Decoding $x$ | Fitness $f(x) = x^2$ |
|------------|--------------|------------------------|
| 01101 | 13 | 169 |
| 11000 | 24 | 576 |
| 01000 | 8 | 64 |
| 10011 | 19 | 361 |

**Step 1 — Total fitness.**

$$
F_{total} = 169 + 576 + 64 + 361 = 1170
$$

**Step 2 — Selection probability for each.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= \dfrac{169}{1170} = 0.1444 \\
p_2 &= \dfrac{576}{1170} = 0.4923 \\
p_3 &= \dfrac{64}{1170} = 0.0547 \\
p_4 &= \dfrac{361}{1170} = 0.3085
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average fitness.**

$$
\bar{f} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{N} = \dfrac{1170}{4} = 292.5
$$

**Step 4 — Expected count $E_i = f_i / \bar{f}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= \dfrac{169}{292.5} = 0.578 \\
E_2 &= \dfrac{576}{292.5} = 1.969 \\
E_3 &= \dfrac{64}{292.5} = 0.219 \\
E_4 &= \dfrac{361}{292.5} = 1.234
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
Chromosome 2 (11000, $x=24$) has the highest expected count and dominates the next generation. Chromosome 3 (01000, $x=8$) is essentially discarded. The population mean fitness will *increase* from **292.5** in the next generation—this is the empirical signature of evolution in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** This exact 5-bit problem appears in Goldberg's classic textbook and is a favorite board-exam template. Memorize the four-step computation: *Decode → Fitness → Probability → Expected Count*.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Biological-to-Algorithmic Mapping Flow

The following Mermaid block diagrams the *conceptual translation* from biological evolution to Genetic Algorithm operators.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BIOLOGICAL EVOLUTION] --> B[Darwinian Natural Selection]
    A --> C[Mendelian Genetics]
    A --> D[Cellular Reproduction]

    B --> B1[Struggle for Existence]
    B --> B2[Survival of Fittest]
    B --> B3[Gradualism over Generations]

    C --> C1[Gene as Unit of Heredity]
    C --> C2[Allele as Variant Value]
    C --> C3[Chromosome as Gene Sequence]
    C --> C4[Genotype vs Phenotype]

    D --> D1[Mitosis for Growth]
    D --> D2[Meiosis for Gametes]
    D --> D3[Crossover and Recombination]
    D --> D4[Mutation as Random Error]

    B1 --> E1[FITNESS FUNCTION f x]
    B2 --> E2[SELECTION OPERATOR]
    B3 --> E3[ITERATIVE GENERATION LOOP]

    C1 --> E4[ENCODED STRING BITS]
    C2 --> E5[ALPHABET 0 1]
    C3 --> E6[CHROMOSOME VECTOR]
    C4 --> E7[DECODING STEP]

    D2 --> E8[MATING POOL]
    D3 --> E9[CROSSOVER OPERATOR]
    D4 --> E10[MUTATION OPERATOR]

    E1 --> F[GENETIC ALGORITHM]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    E5 --> F
    E6 --> F
    E7 --> F
    E8 --> F
    E9 --> F
    E10 --> F

    F --> G[OPTIMAL OR NEAR OPTIMAL SOLUTION]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagram:** The left column shows the three biological sources. The middle column expands each source into its sub-mechanisms. The right column translates each sub-mechanism into its algorithmic counterpart. The final node $F$ is the assembled Genetic Algorithm that converges to $G$.

---

### 4.2 The Canonical GA Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

This is the *standard KTU board diagram* for the GA execution flow. Every Module 2 numerical problem ultimately follows this loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[START] --> S2[Initialize Random Population P of N chromosomes]
    S2 --> S3[Evaluate Fitness f x for every individual]
    S3 --> S4{Convergence Criterion Met?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Apply Selection Operator to build Mating Pool]
    S5 --> S6[Apply Crossover with probability Pc to produce offspring]
    S6 --> S7[Apply Mutation with probability Pm to offspring]
    S7 --> S8[Form New Generation P t plus 1]
    S8 --> S3
    S4 -- Yes --> S9[Return Best Chromosome as Solution]
    S9 --> S10[END]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-by-Block Interpretation:**
> - **S2 (Initialize):** Generate $N$ random binary strings of length $L$ (or random real-valued vectors).
> - **S3 (Evaluate):** Decode each chromosome to its phenotype, then compute the fitness function.
> - **S4 (Check):** Stop if max generations reached, or if best fitness stops improving for $K$ generations.
> - **S5 (Selection):** Roulette Wheel, Tournament Selection, Rank-Based, or Truncation.
> - **S6 (Crossover):** Single-point, Two-point, Uniform, or Order-based (for permutations).
> - **S7 (Mutation):** Bit-flip (binary) or Gaussian perturbation (real-coded).
> - **S8 (Replacement):** Generational (replace all) or Steady-State (replace worst $k$).

---

### 4.3 Chromosome Anatomy and Genetic Operators Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parent 1: 1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0]
    P2[Parent 2: 0 1 0 0 1 1 0 1]

    P1 --> X1{Crossover Point at Locus 4}
    P2 --> X1

    X1 --> C1[Offspring 1: 1 0 1 1 1 1 0 1]
    X1 --> C2[Offspring 2: 0 1 0 0 0 0 1 0]

    C1 --> M1{Mutation at Locus 6}
    M1 --> O1[Mutant 1: 1 0 1 1 1 0 0 1]

    C2 --> M2{Mutation at Locus 1}
    M2 --> O2[Mutant 2: 1 1 0 0 0 0 1 0]

    O1 --> NEXT[Next Generation Pool]
    O2 --> NEXT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The crossover point at Locus 4 splits each parent into a *head* (loci 1–4) and a *tail* (loci 5–8). Offspring 1 inherits the *head* of Parent 1 and the *tail* of Parent 2. The mutation step then flips one bit in each offspring with low probability $P_m$, introducing novelty and preventing premature convergence.
