# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Adaptive Genetic Algorithm. How does it differ from a standard Genetic Algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**
An **Adaptive Genetic Algorithm (AGA)** is a variant of GA in which the **operator probabilities** ($P_c$ and $P_m$) are **dynamically varied** during the run based on the **fitness statistics** of the current population, rather than being held constant. In a standard GA, $P_c$ and $P_m$ are fixed for the entire run, which often leads to **premature convergence** (when too low) or **loss of good solutions** (when too high). In contrast, an AGA continuously **senses** $f_{max}$ and $f_{avg}$, and **adapts** the operators so that high-fitness individuals are protected while low-fitness ones are aggressively recombined.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the role of $f_{max}$ and $f_{avg}$ in the Srinivas–Patnaik Adaptive GA?**

**Model Answer:**
$f_{max}$ and $f_{avg}$ are the **two control statistics** that drive the adaptation. $f_{max}$ represents the **best fitness** currently in the population, while $f_{avg}$ represents the **mean fitness**. The difference $(f_{max} - f_{avg})$ acts as a **normalization denominator** that **automatically inflates** $P_c$ and $P_m$ when the population converges (small denominator) and **automatically deflates** them when the population is diverse (large denominator). Thus $f_{max}$ and $f_{avg}$ together form the **feedback signal** of the self-adaptive control loop.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Derive the adaptive crossover probability formula proposed by Srinivas and Patnaik. Explain how it self-regulates during the evolutionary process.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** We model $P_c$ as a linear function of parent fitness $f'$:
$$P_c = a - b \cdot f'$$

**Step 2:** Apply boundary conditions:
- $P_c = k_1$ when $f' = f_{avg}$
- $P_c = k_3$ when $f' = f_{max}$

This gives:
$$k_1 = a - b \cdot f_{avg}$$
$$k_3 = a - b \cdot f_{max}$$

**Step 3:** Solving:
$$b = \frac{k_1 - k_3}{f_{max} - f_{avg}}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back and simplifying for $f' \ge f_{avg}$:
$$P_c = \frac{k_1 (f_{max} - f')}{f_{max} - f_{avg}}$$

For $f' < f_{avg}$:
$$P_c = k_3$$

**Self-Regulation Explanation:**
- When the population converges ($f_{max} \approx f_{avg}$), the denominator shrinks, automatically **inflating $P_c$** to inject diversity.
- When the population is diverse ($f_{max} \gg f_{avg}$), the denominator is large, keeping $P_c$ moderate and allowing convergence.
- High-fitness parents ($f'$ near $f_{max}$) get **low $P_c$**, preserving their genetic material.
- Low-fitness parents ($f' < f_{avg}$) get the **fixed floor $k_3$**, allowing aggressive recombination with elite solutions.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**A population has fitness values $\{ 15, 25, 35, 45, 55, 65 \}$. Given $k_1 = 1.0, k_2 = 0.04, k_3 = 0.6, k_4 = 0.005$, compute the adaptive $P_c$ and $P_m$ for:**
- **(i) A parent pair with $f' = 55$**
- **(ii) An individual with $f = 25$**
- **(iii) An individual with $f = 65$**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute statistics:**
$$f_{max} = 65, \quad f_{avg} = \frac{15+25+35+45+55+65}{6} = \frac{240}{6} = 40$$
$$f_{max} - f_{avg} = 65 - 40 = 25$$

**Step 2 — (i) $P_c$ for $f' = 55$** (above average):
$$P_c = \frac{1.0 \cdot (65 - 55)}{25} = \frac{10}{25} = 0.40$$

**Step 3 — (ii) $P_m$ for $f = 25$** (below average, $f < 40$):
$$P_m = k_4 = 0.005$$

**Step 4 — (iii) $P_m$ for $f = 65$** (above average, the elite):
$$P_m = \frac{0.04 \cdot (65 - 65)}{25} = \frac{0}{25} = 0.000$$

**Interpretation:**
- The elite ($f=65$) gets **zero** mutation — perfectly preserved.
- The above-average parent ($f'=55$) gets a moderate crossover of $40\%$.
- The weak individual ($f=25$) gets the floor mutation of $0.5\%$, but it can still be recombined via a higher $P_c$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Explain the Adaptive Genetic Algorithm with a neat block diagram. Discuss how it overcomes the premature convergence problem.**

**Model Solution:**

An **AGA** introduces a **feedback loop** between the population's fitness statistics and the operator probabilities. The block diagram has the following components:

1. **Population Initialization** — generate random chromosomes.
2. **Fitness Evaluation** — compute $f_i$ for every chromosome.
3. **Statistics Aggregator** — extract $f_{max}$ and $f_{avg}$.
4. **Adaptive Controller** — computes dynamic $P_c$ and $P_m$ using F-1, F-2, F-3, F-4.
5. **Selection, Adaptive Crossover, Adaptive Mutation** — applied with the dynamic probabilities.
6. **Elitism & Replacement** — best chromosome copied unchanged.
7. **Convergence Check** — loop or terminate.

**Overcoming Premature Convergence:**

Premature convergence occurs when the population loses diversity and gets stuck in a local optimum. AGA overcomes this by:

- **Auto-inflating $P_m$** when $f_{max} - f_{avg} \to 0$ (population has collapsed). The denominator in F-3 becomes very small, so $P_m$ shoots up, injecting new genetic material.
- **Protecting elites** via the formula's design — high-fitness individuals have $P_m$ and $P_c$ close to zero, so the best solution is **never destroyed** even during diversity-injection phases.
- **Providing consistent exploration** for below-average individuals, ensuring they are continually remixed with the gene pool.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Compare fixed-parameter GA and Adaptive GA on the following parameters: (i) operator probabilities, (ii) population diversity handling, (iii) computational overhead, (iv) convergence reliability.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Fixed-Parameter GA | Adaptive GA |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Operator probabilities | Constant $P_c, P_m$ throughout the run | Dynamic $P_c, P_m$ — function of $f_{max}, f_{avg}$ |
| (ii) Population diversity handling | Cannot self-correct; collapses into local optima | Self-corrects via denominator blow-up in F-1, F-3 |
| (iii) Computational overhead | Low (one parameter setting) | Slightly higher (must recompute $f_{max}, f_{avg}$ each generation) |
| (iv) Convergence reliability | Low to moderate — sensitive to parameter tuning | High — robust across problem instances |
| Best solution preservation | At risk if $P_m$ is too high | Guaranteed via elitism + scaled probabilities |
| Tuning effort | Manual tuning per problem | Self-tuning — minimal user intervention |
| Suitable for dynamic environments | Poor | Excellent — adapts in real time |

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Swapping $k_1$ and $k_3$ roles** — students often confuse the upper and lower bounds. Recall: $k_1$ is the **upper-bound scaling factor** used when $f' \ge f_{avg}$, and $k_3$ is the **floor** when $f' < f_{avg}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the floor clause** — the formula is *piecewise*. Writing only the scaled version without the $k_3$ fallback loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Incorrectly using $f$ vs. $f'$** — remember: $P_c$ uses $f'$ (the **larger** of two parents' fitness), $P_m$ uses $f$ (the fitness of the **single** individual being mutated). Confusing these costs 3 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the denominator boundary** — if $f_{max} = f_{avg}$, the denominator is zero. In practice, a small $\epsilon$ is added to avoid division by zero. Mention this in your answer for full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the feedback explanation** — KTU examiners reward explanations of *why* adaptation works, not just formula writing. Always state the role of the **denominator as a diversity sensor**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Adaptive Genetic Algorithms**
> 
> - AGA = **GA + adaptive operator control** using fitness statistics.
> - Key statistics: $f_{max}$ (best) and $f_{avg}$ (mean) per generation.
> - **Four canonical formulas (Srinivas–Patnaik 1994):**
>   - $P_c = k_1 (f_{max} - f')/(f_{max} - f_{avg})$ if $f' \ge f_{avg}$, else $k_3$.
>   - $P_m = k_2 (f_{max} - f)/(f_{max} - f_{avg})$ if $f \ge f_{avg}$, else $k_4$.
> - $P_c$ uses the **larger parent's fitness $f'$**; $P_m$ uses the **individual's fitness $f$**.
> - Typical coefficient values: $k_1 = 1.0$, $k_2 = 0.05$, $k_3 = 0.5$, $k_4 = 0.005$.
> - The denominator $(f_{max} - f_{avg})$ acts as a **diversity sensor** — small denominator → high operator probability → diversity injection.
> - **Elitism is mandatory** in AGA to protect the global best from adaptive mutation.
> - **Advantages**: self-tuning, robust, escapes premature convergence, less manual parameter calibration.
> - **Disadvantages**: slightly higher per-generation cost, requires careful $\epsilon$-guard against $f_{max} = f_{avg}$.
> - **Applications**: aerospace, VLSI design, neural architecture search, power dispatch, robotics.
> - **Examiner's favorite trick**: ask for a numerical example with 5–6 fitness values — practice computing $P_c$ and $P_m$ for at least 3 cases (above-avg, below-avg, and elite).
> - Remember to **mention the piecewise nature** of the formula — KTU rewards completeness.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Genetic Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Genetic Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Hybrid Genetic Algorithm (HGA)** is an advanced metaheuristic optimization technique that integrates the global exploration capability of a standard Genetic Algorithm (GA) with the local exploitation strength of a deterministic or stochastic refinement procedure. Formally, it is defined as a population-based stochastic search algorithm of the form:

$$
HGA = (GA \oplus LS) \cup \mathcal{F}_{refine}
$$

where $GA$ is the canonical evolutionary loop (selection–crossover–mutation), $LS$ denotes a local search (or neighborhood search) operator, and $\mathcal{F}_{refine}$ is the refinement function applied either to every offspring, a fraction of the population, or to the elite individuals only.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** Hybridization is the *principal mechanism* used to overcome the two classical weaknesses of pure GAs: (1) **premature convergence** due to weak local tuning, and (2) **slow finishing** because the discrete genetic operators cannot fine-tune solutions near an optimum.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are searching for the **highest peak in a vast, fog-covered mountain range** (the global optimum):

- A **pure Genetic Algorithm** is like releasing a fleet of hot-air balloons at random points. They drift around, share their altitude readings, and slowly cluster around the tallest mountains — but because they move in large, random jumps, they can never precisely measure the **exact summit elevation**.
- A **Hybrid Genetic Algorithm** adds a **team of mountain climbers** (local search operators) to each balloon. Once a balloon lands near a promising peak, the climbers descend and do a meticulous, step-by-step climb to the **true peak** (the refined local optimum).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The balloons (GA) provide the *exploration* (searching broadly), while the climbers (local search) provide the *exploitation* (searching intensively around a promising area). This is a classic **exploration–exploitation tradeoff** resolution.

### 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

- **Memetic Algorithm (MA):** The most widely used HGA subtype — coined by **Pablo Moscato** (1989).
- **Lamarckian HGA:** Offspring are *replaced* by locally improved versions (genotype = improved phenotype).
- **Baldwinian HGA:** Local search only *updates fitness*, not the chromosome (genotype preserved).
- **Adaptation Rate ($p_{LS}$):** The fraction of individuals undergoing local improvement per generation; typically **0.1 to 0.3**.
- **Computational Budget Split:** **70% GA, 30% local search** is a commonly cited industry heuristic.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** The word "Hybrid" in KTU Module 2 always implies the *combination of two distinct search paradigms* — never a simple parameter tweak of a single GA.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Hybrid GA

The HGA operational pipeline is decomposed into the following structured stages:

1. **Initialization Phase** — Generate initial population $\mathcal{P}_0 = \{x_1^{(0)}, x_2^{(0)}, \dots, x_N^{(0)}\}$ using Latin Hypercube Sampling (LHS) for better space-filling.
2. **Fitness Evaluation** — Compute $f(x_i)$ for each individual.
3. **Genetic Operators (Exploration)** — Apply selection (tournament/roulette), crossover (SBX, BLX-$\alpha$), and mutation (polynomial/Gaussian).
4. **Local Search Trigger Condition** — Check the adaptation policy:
   - **Random Trigger:** Apply LS to a random $p_{LS} \cdot N$ individuals.
   - **Best-Trigger:** Apply LS only to top-$k$ elitists.
   - **Improvement-Trigger:** Apply LS only if fitness $f(x) > f_{threshold}$.
5. **Local Refinement (Exploitation)** — Apply gradient descent, hill-climbing, or Nelder–Mead simplex to selected individuals.
6. **Replacement Strategy** — Update population using $(\mu + \lambda)$ or steady-state elitism.
7. **Termination Check** — Stop when $f_{best}^{(t)} - f_{opt} \leq \epsilon$ OR $t \geq T_{max}$.

### 2.2 Why Hybridization Works — The Theoretical "Why"

> The **No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem** (Wolpert & Macready, 1997) states that no single search algorithm outperforms all others across *all* problem classes. Therefore, **combining complementary algorithms** (GA + LS) reduces the average-case error on specific problem distributions.

The **Schema Theorem** tells us GA efficiently samples building blocks. Local search then **completes** the building blocks that GA cannot precisely optimize. The synergy yields:

$$
E[\text{fitness improvement}] = \underbrace{\Delta f_{GA}}_{\text{Exploration}} + \underbrace{\Delta f_{LS}}_{\text{Exploitation}} > \max(\Delta f_{GA}, \Delta f_{LS})
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** No vertical pipe `|` used in math expressions below — LaTeX commands `\vert` and `\mid` are used instead to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Value | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Population size | $N \in [50, 200]$ (typical) | Diversity control |
| $p_c$ | Crossover probability | $p_c \in [0.6, 0.95]$ | Exploration driver |
| $p_m$ | Mutation probability | $p_m \in [0.001, 0.05]$ | Diversity injection |
| $p_{LS}$ | Local search probability | $p_{LS} \in [0.05, 0.5]$ | Exploitation rate |
| $L_{LS}$ | Local search iterations | $L_{LS} \in [5, 50]$ | Computational budget |
| $\alpha$ | BLX-$\alpha$ mixing ratio | $\alpha = 0.5$ standard | Crossover spread |
| $\eta$ | SBX distribution index | $\eta \in [2, 20]$ | Crossover granularity |
| $T$ | Tournament size | $T = 2$ or $3$ | Selection pressure |
| $f_{LS}$ | Local search objective | $\min_{x \in \mathcal{N}(x_0)} f(x)$ | Refinement metric |
| $E_{total}$ | Total computational cost | $E_{total} = E_{GA} + N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot L_{LS} \cdot c_{LS}$ | Resource planning |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industrial Relevance:** Hybrid GAs are the **de facto standard** in modern engineering design optimization. Examples include:
> - **Aerospace:** Aircraft wing-shape optimization (GA + CFD gradient solver)
> - **VLSI:** Circuit placement (GA + simulated annealing)
> - **Robotics:** Path planning (GA + A* heuristic)
> - **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction (GA + molecular dynamics)
> - **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch (GA + lambda-iteration method)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Expected Fitness Gain of HGA

We rigorously derive the expected fitness improvement per generation in a hybrid framework.

**Step 1: Pure GA Expected Improvement**
From the Schema Theorem, the expected number of copies of a schema $H$ at generation $t+1$ is:

$$
E[m(H, t+1)] = m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \delta(H) - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
$$

where $\delta(H)$ is the defining length and $o(H)$ is the order of schema $H$.

**Step 2: Local Search Improvement**
When local search is applied with probability $p_{LS}$ to a population subset of size $N$, the expected number of improved individuals is:

$$
E[\text{improved}] = N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot P(\text{LS finds better } x \vert x \in \mathcal{N})
$$

**Step 3: Total HGA Fitness Gain**
The total expected fitness gain $\Delta f_{HGA}$ per generation is the sum of genetic improvement and local refinement:

$$
\Delta f_{HGA} = \underbrace{m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \delta(H) - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]}_{\text{GA Component}} + \underbrace{N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot \mathbb{E}[\Delta f_{LS}]}_{\text{LS Component}}
$$

**Step 4: Net Improvement Bound**
Rearranging to a closed form for convergence analysis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{best}^{(t+1)} - f_{best}^{(t)} &= \Delta f_{HGA}^{(t)} \\
&= \alpha_{GA} \cdot \left(f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(t)}\right) + \alpha_{LS} \cdot \left(f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(t)}\right) \\
&= (\alpha_{GA} + \alpha_{LS}) \cdot \left(f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(t)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha_{GA}$ and $\alpha_{LS}$ are the convergence rates of the GA and LS components respectively.

**Step 5: Convergence Rate Comparison**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GA only:} \quad f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(t)} &\approx (1 - \alpha_{GA})^{t} \cdot \left(f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(0)}\right) \\
\text{HGA:} \quad f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(t)} &\approx (1 - \alpha_{GA} - \alpha_{LS})^{t} \cdot \left(f_{opt} - f_{best}^{(0)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\alpha_{GA} + \alpha_{LS} > \alpha_{GA}$ and $0 < 1 - \alpha_{GA} - \alpha_{LS} < 1 - \alpha_{GA}$, the HGA converges **strictly faster** to the optimum. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Numerical Evaluation: Computational Cost Trade-off

Let us quantify the cost-benefit of hybridization with a worked example.

**Given:**
- Population size: $N = 100$
- GA cost per evaluation: $c_{GA} = 1$ ms
- LS cost per iteration: $c_{LS} = 5$ ms
- Local search length: $L_{LS} = 10$ iterations
- Local search probability: $p_{LS} = 0.2$

**Step 1: Cost of Pure GA per Generation**

$$
C_{GA} = N \cdot c_{GA} = 100 \times 1 = 100 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2: Additional Cost of Local Search**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{LS} &= N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot L_{LS} \cdot c_{LS} \\
&= 100 \times 0.2 \times 10 \times 5 \\
&= 1000 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total HGA Cost**

$$
C_{HGA} = C_{GA} + C_{LS} = 100 + 1000 = 1100 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4: Cost Increase Ratio**

$$
R = \frac{C_{HGA}}{C_{GA}} = \frac{1100}{100} = 11\times
$$

**Step 5: Acceptable Trade-off Interpretation**
If HGA reaches the same quality solution in **11× fewer generations** (achievable in practice), the **wall-clock time is reduced**. This is the standard justification for hybridization in industry.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Exam:** HGA is *cost-effective* when $L_{LS} \leq 5\%$ of the GA generation cost AND $\alpha_{LS} \geq 5 \cdot \alpha_{GA}$.

### 3.3 Lamarckian vs Baldwinian Evolution: A Mathematical Distinction

Let $x$ be a chromosome and $x'$ be its locally improved version.

**Lamarckian Evolution (direct encoding):**

$$
x^{(t+1)} = x' \quad \text{(genotype is REPLACED by the improved phenotype)}
$$

$$
f^{(t+1)}(x) = f(x') \quad \text{(fitness is updated to the new improved value)}
$$

**Baldwinian Evolution (indirect encoding):**

$$
x^{(t+1)} = x \quad \text{(genotype is PRESERVED)}
$$

$$
f^{(t+1)}(x) = f(x') \quad \text{(only FITNESS is updated, gene pool unchanged)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Distinction:** Baldwinian HGA is biologically more accurate (Lamarckian inheritance is not supported by modern genetics), but Lamarckian HGA is *computationally more aggressive* and often converges faster.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Hybrid GA Operational Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start HGA] --> B[Initialize Population P_0]
    B --> C[Evaluate Fitness f_x for all x]
    C --> D[Apply Selection Tournament T]
    D --> E[Apply Crossover SBX with p_c]
    E --> F[Apply Mutation Polynomial with p_m]
    F --> G{LS Trigger Condition p_LS}
    G -- Yes --> H[Apply Local Search Hill Climbing]
    G -- No --> I[Skip Refinement]
    H --> J[Update Population mu plus lambda]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Termination f_best converges}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Return Best Solution x_star]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[GA Engine] --> B[Exploration Module]
    B --> C[Selection Operator]
    B --> D[Crossover Operator]
    B --> E[Mutation Operator]
    F[Local Search Engine] --> G[Exploitation Module]
    G --> H[Neighborhood Search]
    G --> I[Gradient Refinement]
    A <--> F
    A --> J[Hybrid Coordinator]
    F --> J
    J --> K[Population Archive P_t]
    K --> A
    K --> F
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Lamarckian vs Baldwinian Branching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Chromosome x] --> B{Local Search Type}
    B -- Lamarckian --> C[Generate x prime via LS]
    C --> D[Replace genotype x with x prime]
    D --> E[Update fitness to f_x prime]
    E --> F[Inject into next generation]
    B -- Baldwinian --> G[Generate x prime via LS]
    G --> H[Keep genotype x unchanged]
    H --> I[Update fitness only to f_x prime]
    I --> F
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Architecture: The Hybridization Spectrum

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Pure Genetic Algorithm] -->|Add weak LS| B[Light Hybrid GA]
    B -->|Increase p_LS| C[Memetic Algorithm]
    C -->|Full LS on all| D[Heavy Memetic]
    D -->|Replace GA by LS| E[Pure Local Search]
    A -->|Remove operators| F[Pure Random Search]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The spectrum shows that HGA is not a binary switch but a **continuous design space**. The optimal position depends on the problem's landscape ruggedness.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define Hybrid Genetic Algorithm. List any two advantages over a standard GA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Hybrid Genetic Algorithm (HGA)** is a metaheuristic that combines a Genetic Algorithm with one or more local search techniques to balance global exploration and local exploitation.

**Advantages (any two, 1.5 marks each):**
1. **Faster Convergence:** Local search refines promising solutions, reducing generations needed to reach optimum.
2. **Higher Solution Quality:** Combines GA's broad search with LS's fine-tuning, yielding near-optimal accuracy.
3. **Reduced Premature Convergence:** Local search diversifies the search by introducing high-fitness perturbations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Lamarckian and Baldwinian learning in the context of Memetic Algorithms.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Lamarckian HGA | Baldwinian HGA |
|---|---|---|
| Genotype Update | **Replaced** by improved version | **Preserved** |
| Fitness Update | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Biological Basis | Discredited (not in nature) | Biologically accurate |
| Convergence Speed | **Faster** | Slower but stable |
| Diversity Loss | **Higher** | Lower |

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a Memetic Algorithm. Describe how the local search component is integrated into the GA loop with a suitable flowchart. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the optimization problem $f(x) = (x-5)^2 + 2\sin(3x)$ on the domain $x \in [0, 10]$. A pure GA is observed to converge to $f = 1.42$ in **200 generations**, while an HGA with $p_{LS} = 0.2$ and $L_{LS} = 10$ reaches $f = 0.001$ in **25 generations**. Compute the wall-clock time and prove HGA is more efficient given $c_{GA} = 1$ ms, $c_{LS} = 4$ ms, $N = 50$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Architecture of Memetic Algorithm (7 marks):**

A **Memetic Algorithm (MA)** is the canonical HGA. Its architecture consists of:

1. **Global Search Layer (GA) — 2 marks**
   - Population initialization
   - Fitness evaluation
   - Tournament selection with size $T=2$
   - Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX) with $p_c = 0.9$
   - Polynomial mutation with $p_m = 0.02$

2. **Local Search Layer (Memetic Operator) — 3 marks**
   - Triggered for **each offspring** (or top-k elite) with probability $p_{LS}$
   - Operators: **Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search, or Nelder-Mead**
   - Iterates for $L_{LS}$ steps within the neighborhood $\mathcal{N}(x)$

3. **Integration Flow — 2 marks**
   - Apply GA operators → generate offspring → apply local search → evaluate → replace via elitist strategy

**Part (b) — Efficiency Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Wall-clock time for pure GA**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{GA} &= G_{GA} \cdot N \cdot c_{GA} \\
&= 200 \times 50 \times 1 \\
&= 10000 \text{ ms} = 10 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Wall-clock time for HGA**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{HGA,per-gen} &= N \cdot c_{GA} + N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot L_{LS} \cdot c_{LS} \\
&= 50 \times 1 + 50 \times 0.2 \times 10 \times 4 \\
&= 50 + 400 \\
&= 450 \text{ ms per generation}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{HGA} &= G_{HGA} \cdot C_{HGA,per-gen} \\
&= 25 \times 450 \\
&= 11250 \text{ ms} = 11.25 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Quality-Time Trade-off**

Despite a **slightly higher wall-clock time** (11.25 s vs 10 s), the HGA achieves:
- **Solution quality improvement:** $\frac{1.42 - 0.001}{0.001} \times 100\% = 141900\%$ better
- **Generations reduced:** 200 → 25 (8× fewer)
- **Convergence efficiency ratio:** $\frac{\Delta f / T}{\text{ratio}}$ — HGA wins on accuracy/iteration basis

**Step 4: Conclusion**

For high-precision requirements ($f \leq 0.01$), HGA is **unavoidably more efficient** because the pure GA cannot reach the target quality even after 10 s. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Lamarckian and Baldwinian evolution models in Hybrid Genetic Algorithms. Discuss which is computationally superior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous optimization problem uses a Hybrid GA with $N = 80$, $p_c = 0.85$, $p_m = 0.02$, $p_{LS} = 0.15$. Each GA evaluation costs 2 ms, and each LS iteration costs 6 ms with $L_{LS} = 12$. Derive the formula for total cost per generation and compute its value. Compare the cost when $p_{LS}$ is changed to **0.05** and **0.30**. Comment on the optimal $p_{LS}$ value. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Lamarckian vs Baldwinian (7 marks):**

**Lamarckian Evolution (3.5 marks):**
- Local search **modifies the chromosome** directly.
- Improved solution becomes the new genotype: $x \leftarrow x'$.
- Inspired by **Lamarck's theory of inheritance of acquired characteristics**.
- **Pros:** Faster convergence, simpler implementation.
- **Cons:** Loses genetic diversity, may cause premature convergence.

**Baldwinian Evolution (3.5 marks):**
- Local search **only updates fitness**, not the chromosome.
- Genotype remains $x$, but $f(x) = f(x')$.
- Inspired by **Baldwin effect** in evolutionary biology.
- **Pros:** Preserves genetic diversity, biologically accurate.
- **Cons:** Slower convergence.

**Computational Superiority:** **Lamarckian** is computationally superior in convergence speed, but **Baldwinian** is superior in robustness and solution diversity. KTU examiners prefer Lamarckian for **smooth fitness landscapes** and Baldwinian for **rugged/multi-modal landscapes**.

**Part (b) — Cost Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Derive the cost formula**

$$
C_{HGA} = N \cdot c_{GA} + N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot L_{LS} \cdot c_{LS}
$$

**Step 2: Substituting given values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{HGA} &= 80 \times 2 + 80 \times p_{LS} \times 12 \times 6 \\
&= 160 + 5760 \cdot p_{LS}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute for three $p_{LS}$ values**

| $p_{LS}$ | $C_{HGA}$ (ms) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **0.05** | $160 + 5760 \times 0.05 = 448$ | Light hybridization |
| **0.15** | $160 + 5760 \times 0.15 = 1024$ | Standard balance |
| **0.30** | $160 + 5760 \times 0.30 = 1888$ | Heavy exploitation |

**Step 4: Optimal $p_{LS}$ Analysis**

- $p_{LS} = 0.05$: Low cost (448 ms), but limited refinement → poor local accuracy
- $p_{LS} = 0.15$: Balanced cost (1024 ms) → good trade-off, **recommended default**
- $p_{LS} = 0.30$: High cost (1888 ms) → risk of wasting compute on non-promising individuals

**Conclusion:** The optimal $p_{LS} \approx 0.15$ offers the **best exploitation–cost balance**. The function $C_{HGA}(p_{LS})$ is **linear and monotonically increasing**, so the designer's choice is governed by *available compute budget* and *required solution precision*.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing **adaptation rate** with **crossover rate** — they are independent parameters. Mixing them up costs 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting that in **Baldwinian HGA, the genotype is NOT changed**, only the fitness. Many students write "$x$ is replaced" — this is a Lamarckian mistake.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Omitting the **computational cost** when justifying hybridization. The HGA must be justified as **cost-effective**, not just "better" — KTU examiners require the trade-off analysis.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Writing Mermaid diagrams with reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) as node names — this **crashes the diagram** and the examiner will not award visualization marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — KTU Module 2: Hybrid Genetic Algorithms**

- **Definition (Must Memorize):** HGA = GA (exploration) + Local Search (exploitation), combined to overcome pure-GA weaknesses.
- **Memetic Algorithm:** Most popular HGA; coined by **Moscato (1989)**; "meme" = unit of cultural learning.
- **Two Learning Models:**
  - **Lamarckian:** Genotype *replaced* by improved version → fast but lossy.
  - **Baldwinian:** Only fitness updated, genotype *preserved* → slow but diverse.
- **Key Parameter:** $p_{LS}$ (local search probability) typically in **[0.1, 0.3]** range.
- **Local Search Operators Commonly Used:** Hill Climbing, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search, Nelder–Mead, BFGS gradient method.
- **Adaptation Strategies:** Random, Best-Trigger, Improvement-Trigger, Adaptive (dynamically tuned $p_{LS}$).
- **Theoretical Justification:** NFL Theorem → no single algorithm best on all problems → hybridization wins on specific problem classes.
- **Convergence:** HGA converges **strictly faster** than pure GA: rate $(1 - \alpha_{GA} - \alpha_{LS})^t$ vs $(1 - \alpha_{GA})^t$.
- **Cost Formula:** $C_{HGA} = N \cdot c_{GA} + N \cdot p_{LS} \cdot L_{LS} \cdot c_{LS}$.
- **Rule of Thumb:** HGA is cost-effective when $L_{LS} \cdot c_{LS} \leq 5\% \cdot c_{GA} \cdot T_{max}$.
- **Industrial Use Cases:** Aerospace design, VLSI placement, robotics path planning, bioinformatics, power dispatch.
- **Diagram Must-Knows:** (1) HGA flowchart, (2) Lamarckian vs Baldwinian branching, (3) Hybridization spectrum.
- **Common Exam Traps:** Lamarckian–Baldwinian confusion, forgetting cost analysis, reserved keywords in Mermaid diagrams, not citing NFL theorem.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** When answering 14-mark questions, always structure your answer as **Definition (2 marks) → Architecture/Diagram (4 marks) → Mathematical Analysis (5 marks) → Industrial Application (3 marks)**. This matches the official KTU 2024 marking scheme perfectly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parallel Genetic Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Parallel Genetic Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parallel Genetic Algorithm (PGA) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A **Parallel Genetic Algorithm (PGA)** is a computationally distributed variant of the classical Genetic Algorithm (GA) in which the evolutionary operations — *selection, crossover, mutation, and fitness evaluation* — are simultaneously executed across **multiple processing elements** (cores, threads, processors, or networked nodes) to reduce wall-clock execution time and/or to enhance solution quality through **cooperative sub-population evolution**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framing (PECNT869 — Module 2), the PGA is positioned as the canonical response to the **two principal bottlenecks** of serial GAs:

1. **Computational bottleneck** — Fitness evaluation of large populations over high-dimensional, expensive objective functions (e.g., CFD simulations, large-scale neural architecture search).
2. **Quality/convergence bottleneck** — Premature convergence in single-population GAs due to loss of *genetic diversity*.

The PGA addresses (1) by **functional decomposition** (parallelism over operations) and (2) by **spatial decomposition** (parallelism over the population). This yields a taxonomy of **three canonical parallel models** prescribed by the KTU 2024 module outcomes:

| KTU Designated Model | Granularity | Decomposition Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **Master–Slave PGA** | Global (Single Population) | Functional decomposition |
| **Island (Coarse-Grained) PGA** | Multiple Sub-populations | Spatial decomposition |
| **Cellular (Fine-Grained / Diffusion) PGA** | One-individual neighborhoods | Spatial decomposition |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Multiple Research Labs" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Solving a Maze with 100 Research Teams**
> Imagine a serial GA as a single research team of 100 scientists who all work in the same lab. They share a single white-board, argue over hypotheses, and slowly converge on one answer — often getting stuck in the same local optimum. Now imagine splitting those 100 scientists into **5 smaller teams of 20**, each placed in a **different country (an island)**. Each team evolves its own best hypotheses *independently* (parallelism), but occasionally they **email their best discoveries to each other (migration)**. The result? Faster wall-clock time *and* a much higher chance of finding the global best maze exit, because each team explores a different region of the solution space.

This intuitive mapping is exactly the **Island Model PGA** — and the analogy scales down to the **Cellular Model** (each scientist sits at their own desk and only shares notes with the *two nearest desks*).

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU-Mandated)

- **Deme / Island / Sub-population**: A semi-isolated sub-population in coarse-grained PGA.
- **Migration**: The exchange of individuals between demes.
- **Migration Rate ($m$)**: The fraction of the sub-population that migrates per exchange event.
- **Migration Interval / Frequency ($T_{\text{mig}}$)**: The number of generations between successive migrations.
- **Migration Topology**: The directed graph defining which demes communicate (ring, hypercube, torus, fully connected).
- **Speedup ($S_p$)**: The ratio of serial execution time to parallel execution time on $p$ processors.
- **Parallel Efficiency ($E_p$)**: The ratio $S_p / p$.
- **Communication Overhead ($\tau$)**: The latency cost imposed by inter-processor messaging.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Parallel Genetic Algorithms (Operational Logic)

### 2.1.1 Master–Slave Model (Global Parallelization)

- **Conceptual 'Why'**: The GA loop *itself* remains on a **master processor**; only the **fitness evaluation** is fanned-out to *N* slave worker nodes.
- **Operational 'How'**:
  1. Master holds the entire population $P_t$.
  2. For each generation, the master broadcasts candidate chromosomes to slaves.
  3. Each slave returns $\text{fit}(x_i)$ for its assigned subset.
  4. Master performs selection, crossover, mutation; broadcast new population.
- **Key Property**: **Algorithmically identical** to a serial GA. The only difference is a parallelized fitness oracle.
- **Limitation**: Synchronization barrier at every generation → performance degrades under high communication latency.
- **Best Use Case**: **Embarrassingly parallel** fitness functions (e.g., independent simulations, $N$-body evaluations, finite-element runs).

### 2.1.2 Island Model — Coarse-Grained PGA (Distributed Populations)

- **Conceptual 'Why'**: Multiple demes evolve in parallel and only **periodically migrate** individuals to inject genetic diversity.
- **Operational 'How'**:
  1. Initialize $k$ sub-populations $P_t^{(1)}, P_t^{(2)}, \dots, P_t^{(k)}$, each of size $n/k$.
  2. Each deme evolves independently for $T_{\text{mig}}$ generations.
  3. At migration intervals, select $m \cdot n/k$ individuals per deme.
  4. Replace these with immigrants from neighboring demes per the **migration topology**.
- **Migration Topologies** (KTU-mandated):
  - **Ring topology**: Deme $i$ ↔ Deme $(i+1) \mod k$
  - **Hyper-cube topology**: Deme $i$ ↔ Deme $j$ if Hamming distance of binary IDs $\leq d$
  - **Torus topology**: 2-D/3-D grid with wraparound neighbors
  - **Fully connected (panmictic)**: Every deme ↔ every other deme (degenerates toward island variants of MAs)
- **Migration Policies**:
  - **Best-worst replacement**: incoming best replaces local worst.
  - **Random replacement**: incoming replaces randomly chosen local individual.
  - **Elitist migration**: only the fittest $m\%$ migrate.

### 2.1.3 Cellular Model — Fine-Grained PGA (Diffusion PGA)

- **Conceptual 'Why'**: One individual per "cell" of a 2-D grid; selection and mating are restricted to a small **neighborhood** (e.g., 4 or 8 nearest neighbors).
- **Operational 'How'**:
  1. Place each individual on a unique cell of a $L \times L$ lattice.
  2. Define a **neighborhood structure** (von Neumann or Moore).
  3. In each generation, an individual mates with a neighbor; offspring occupies the cell.
  4. Spread of good genetic material occurs via **slow diffusion** across the grid.
- **Best Use Case**: Massive-scale fine-grained parallel hardware (GPUs, FPGAs, systolic arrays).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **exam-canonical equations** for Parallel GA performance analysis. All notations are KTU 2024 board-compliant.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Speedup** | $S_p = \dfrac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}(p)}$ | Ratio of serial time to parallel time on $p$ processors |
| 2 | **Parallel Efficiency** | $E_p = \dfrac{S_p}{p}$ | Normalized speedup per processor (ideal $E_p = 1$) |
| 3 | **Amdahl's Law Bound** | $S_p \le \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}}$ | Upper bound with $f$ = parallel fraction |
| 4 | **Gustafson's Scaled Speedup** | $S_p^{\text{(scaled)}} = p - (1-f)(p-1)$ | Speedup when problem size scales with $p$ |
| 5 | **Migration Loss** | $L_{\text{mig}} = k \cdot m \cdot n \cdot T_{\text{comm}}$ | Communication cost: $k$ demes, $m$ rate, $n$ pop, $T_{\text{comm}}$ latency |
| 6 | **Effective Deme Size** | $N_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{n}{k} \cdot (1 + m \cdot \rho)$ | $\rho$ = neighborhood connectivity |
| 7 | **Generational Computation** | $T_{\text{gen}} = T_{\text{eval}} + T_{\text{GA-op}} + \dfrac{\tau_{\text{comm}}}{T_{\text{mig}}}$ | Total wall time per generation |
| 8 | **Ideal Isoefficiency** | $W = \Theta(p)$ | Work $W$ for constant efficiency |
| 9 | **Communication-to-Computation Ratio** | $\gamma = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{comm}}}{\tau_{\text{comp}}}$ | Lower $\gamma$ ⇒ better parallelization |
| 10 | **Effective Selection Pressure** | $s_{\text{eff}} \approx s \cdot (1 + \alpha m)$ | $\alpha$ = topology coupling factor |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of PGA | Model Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace structural design** | Optimizing composite layups via expensive FEA fitness | Master–Slave |
| **Antenna array synthesis** | Multi-modal beam-pattern discovery | Island (Ring) |
| **Bioinformatics (MSA)** | Multiple sequence alignment with profile fitness | Island (Hypercube) |
| **Neural Architecture Search (NAS)** | Evolving deep network topologies | Cellular on GPU |
| **VLSI floor-planning** | Wire-length + area minimization | Island (Torus) |
| **Power-grid load dispatch** | Multi-objective economic dispatch | Island (Fully Connected) |
| **Robotic swarm control** | Distributed policy evolution | Cellular (Von Neumann) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Island Model dominates industry**: Because it requires **only loose synchronization** (every $T_{\text{mig}}$ generations) and naturally exploits **Beowulf clusters, Hadoop/Spark frameworks, and MPI grids**, the coarse-grained island PGA is the **de-facto standard** in industrial evolutionary optimization. The KTU 2024 module specifically tests its migration mathematics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Speedup of a Master–Slave PGA

We model the **wall-clock time per generation** under the master–slave decomposition. Let:

- $T_{\text{serial}}$ = time for one complete GA generation on **1 processor**.
- $f$ = fraction of $T_{\text{serial}}$ that is **parallelizable** (typically the fitness evaluation).
- $(1-f)$ = fraction that is **strictly serial** (selection, crossover, mutation overhead, master synchronization).
- $p$ = number of slave processors.
- $T_{\text{comm}}(p)$ = communication latency, modeled as $T_{\text{comm}} = \beta \cdot p$ where $\beta$ is a per-message constant.

**Step 1**: Decompose the serial time.

$$
T_{\text{serial}} = T_{\text{comp}}^{\text{(serial)}} + T_{\text{comp}}^{\text{(parallel)}}
$$

where

$$
T_{\text{comp}}^{\text{(serial)}} = (1-f) \cdot T_{\text{serial}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{comp}}^{\text{(parallel)}} = f \cdot T_{\text{serial}}
$$

**Step 2**: Parallel execution time of the parallelizable fraction on $p$ slaves.

$$
T_{\text{parallel}}^{\text{(parallel)}} = \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{serial}}}{p}
$$

**Step 3**: Add the serial fraction and the communication overhead.

$$
T_{\text{parallel}}(p) = (1-f) \cdot T_{\text{serial}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{serial}}}{p} + T_{\text{comm}}(p)
$$

**Step 4**: Substitute $T_{\text{comm}}(p) = \beta p$ (linear cost model).

$$
T_{\text{parallel}}(p) = T_{\text{serial}} \left[ (1-f) + \frac{f}{p} \right] + \beta p
$$

**Step 5**: Define the speedup $S_p$.

$$
S_p = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}(p)}
$$

**Step 6**: Solve for $S_p$.

$$
S_p = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{serial}} \left[ (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p} \right] + \beta p}
$$

**Step 7**: Divide numerator and denominator by $T_{\text{serial}}$.

$$
S_p = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p} + \dfrac{\beta p}{T_{\text{serial}}}}
$$

**Step 8**: Introduce the normalized communication parameter $\gamma = \beta / T_{\text{serial}}$.

$$
\boxed{\,S_p = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p} + \gamma p}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight**: Notice that for large $p$, the term $\gamma p$ **dominates**, causing $S_p$ to *decrease*. This is the classical **communication-saturation effect**. The optimal $p^*$ that maximizes $S_p$ is found by setting $dS_p/dp = 0$, yielding:
> $$p^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{f}{\gamma}}$$
> For $f = 0.9$ and $\gamma = 10^{-4}$, $p^* = \sqrt{9000} \approx 95$ processors — beyond this, adding more cores *hurts* performance.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel Efficiency and Isoefficiency

**Step 1**: Start from the definition of efficiency.

$$
E_p = \frac{S_p}{p}
$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the derived $S_p$ from §3.1.

$$
E_p = \frac{1}{p \left[ (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p} + \gamma p \right]}
$$

**Step 3**: Expand the denominator.

$$
E_p = \frac{1}{(1-f)p + f + \gamma p^2}
$$

**Step 4**: The dominant term for large $p$ is $\gamma p^2$. To keep $E_p$ **constant**, the serial workload $W$ must grow as:

$$
W \propto p^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\,W = \Theta(p^2)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **isoefficiency** of the master–slave PGA — the work-per-processor must grow **quadratically** with the number of processors to maintain constant efficiency. Compare with the island model, which achieves $W = \Theta(p)$ (linear isoefficiency) because migration cost is amortized over $T_{\text{mig}}$ generations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Migration Rate and Genetic Drift in the Island Model

**Step 1**: Consider $k$ demes, each of size $n/k$, with migration rate $m$ and interval $T_{\text{mig}}$.

The **effective gene-flow** per generation is:

$$
\Phi = \frac{m}{T_{\text{mig}}}
$$

**Step 2**: The **Wright–Fisher-like steady-state diversity** under island dynamics is:

$$
H_{\text{island}} = H_0 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2N_e}\right)^{g}
$$

where $N_e$ is the **effective deme size** accounting for migration.

**Step 3**: Under the island model, the effective population size is given by the **deme size plus immigrant contribution**:

$$
N_e = \frac{n}{k} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{k m T_{\text{mig}}}{2(k-1)} \right)
$$

**Step 4**: Therefore, the **diversity loss per generation** is:

$$
\Delta H = H_0 - H_{\text{island}} = H_0 \left[ 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{2 N_e}\right)^g \right]
$$

**Step 5**: Solving for $g$ (number of generations to lose a fraction $\delta$ of initial diversity):

$$
g = \frac{\ln(1-\delta)}{\ln\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{2 N_e}\right)}
$$

**Step 6**: Approximating $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$
\boxed{\,g \approx 2 N_e \cdot \delta = \frac{n}{k} \left(1 + \frac{k m T_{\text{mig}}}{2(k-1)}\right) \cdot \delta\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**: Increasing the migration rate $m$ or the migration interval $T_{\text{mig}}$ **inflates $N_e$**, thereby **slowing diversity loss**. This is the mathematical reason why island PGAs escape the premature-convergence problem of serial GAs. However, excessive migration degenerates the system into a single panmictic population and erodes the parallel speedup benefit.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Communication Cost of the Island Model

**Step 1**: Each migration event requires sending $m \cdot n / k$ individuals from each of $k$ demes.

**Step 2**: Total individuals transferred per migration event:

$$
N_{\text{trans}} = k \cdot \frac{m \cdot n}{k} = m \cdot n
$$

**Step 3**: Latency per message = $\alpha$ (startup), per-byte cost = $\beta$. For chromosome length $L$ bytes:

$$
T_{\text{comm, per-event}} = m \cdot n \cdot (\alpha + L \beta)
$$

**Step 4**: Per-generation amortized cost:

$$
T_{\text{comm, per-gen}} = \frac{m \cdot n \cdot (\alpha + L \beta)}{T_{\text{mig}}}
$$

**Step 5**: Therefore the **communication-to-computation ratio** is:

$$
\gamma_{\text{island}} = \frac{m n (\alpha + L \beta)}{T_{\text{mig}} \cdot T_{\text{comp, deme}}}
$$

**Step 6**: For the **ring topology** with $k$ demes, only $2m n / k$ individuals move (each deme sends/receives from 2 neighbors):

$$
\gamma_{\text{ring}} = \frac{2 m n (\alpha + L \beta)}{k \cdot T_{\text{mig}} \cdot T_{\text{comp, deme}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ring topology **scales as $1/k$** in communication cost, which is why it is preferred for large $k$ — this is a frequent 14-mark derivation target in KTU 2024.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Model Selection Decision Table

| Criterion | Master–Slave | Island (Coarse) | Cellular (Fine) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Synchronization | Tight (every gen) | Loose (every $T_{\text{mig}}$) | None (neighborhood) |
| Speedup scaling | $O(p)$ until comm. saturates | $O(k \cdot T_{\text{mig}})$ | $O(L^2)$ lattice cells |
| Communication pattern | Star (master) | Topology graph | Local (neighbors only) |
| Diversity preservation | Same as serial | High (parameter-controlled) | Highest (diffusion) |
| Hardware fit | Beowulf / cloud | MPI / Hadoop / Spark | GPU / FPGA / systolic |
| Isoefficiency | $\Theta(p^2)$ | $\Theta(p)$ | $\Theta(p)$ |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Best for expensive fitness | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Best for multimodal landscapes | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master–Slave PGA — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeM[Master Processor]
    nodeS1[Slave Worker 1]
    nodeS2[Slave Worker 2]
    nodeS3[Slave Worker P-1]
    nodeSP[Slave Worker P]
    nodeFit1[Fitness Eval Subset 1]
    nodeFit2[Fitness Eval Subset 2]
    nodeFitP1[Fitness Eval Subset P-1]
    nodeFitP2[Fitness Eval Subset P]
    nodePop[(Current Population P t)]
    nodeSel[Selection Operator]
    nodeXOver[Crossover Operator]
    nodeMut[Mutation Operator]
    nodeNew[(New Population P t+1)]

    nodePop --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeSel
    nodeSel --> nodeXOver
    nodeXOver --> nodeMut
    nodeMut --> nodeNew
    nodeNew -.Broadcast Chromosomes.-> nodeS1
    nodeNew -.Broadcast Chromosomes.-> nodeS2
    nodeNew -.Broadcast Chromosomes.-> nodeS3
    nodeNew -.Broadcast Chromosomes.-> nodeSP
    nodeS1 --> nodeFit1
    nodeS2 --> nodeFit2
    nodeS3 --> nodeFitP1
    nodeSP --> nodeFitP2
    nodeFit1 -.Return Fitness.-> nodeM
    nodeFit2 -.Return Fitness.-> nodeM
    nodeFitP1 -.Return Fitness.-> nodeM
    nodeFitP2 -.Return Fitness.-> nodeM
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The master holds the entire population and runs all GA operators *serially*. The only parallel action is the **fitness evaluation**, which is fanned out to $p$ slaves. The dashed arrows represent **network communication**.

## 4.2 Island Model — Ring Topology Migration Graph

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeD1[Deme 1\n n/k Individuals]
    nodeD2[Deme 2\n n/k Individuals]
    nodeD3[Deme 3\n n/k Individuals]
    nodeD4[Deme 4\n n/k Individuals]
    nodeD5[Deme 5\n n/k Individuals]
    nodeD6[Deme 6\n n/k Individuals]

    nodeD1 -- Migrate Best m percent --> nodeD2
    nodeD2 -- Migrate Best m percent --> nodeD3
    nodeD3 -- Migrate Best m percent --> nodeD4
    nodeD4 -- Migrate Best m percent --> nodeD5
    nodeD5 -- Migrate Best m percent --> nodeD6
    nodeD6 -- Migrate Best m percent --> nodeD1

    nodeD1 -.Immigrate Replace Worst.-> nodeD1
    nodeD2 -.Immigrate Replace Worst.-> nodeD2
    nodeD3 -.Immigrate Replace Worst.-> nodeD3
    nodeD4 -.Immigrate Replace Worst.-> nodeD4
    nodeD5 -.Immigrate Replace Worst.-> nodeD5
    nodeD6 -.Immigrate Replace Worst.-> nodeD6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Six demes arranged in a **ring**. Every $T_{\text{mig}}$ generations, the *best* $m$% of each deme migrates to its clockwise neighbor, where they replace the *worst* local individuals. The wraparound edge connects Deme 6 back to Deme 1, closing the ring.

## 4.3 Island Model — Hypercube Topology (3-D)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeH1[Deme 000]
    nodeH2[Deme 001]
    nodeH3[Deme 010]
    nodeH4[Deme 011]
    nodeH5[Deme 100]
    nodeH6[Deme 101]
    nodeH7[Deme 110]
    nodeH8[Deme 111]

    nodeH1 -- Bit 0 flips --> nodeH2
    nodeH1 -- Bit 1 flips --> nodeH3
    nodeH1 -- Bit 2 flips --> nodeH5
    nodeH2 -- Bit 1 flips --> nodeH4
    nodeH2 -- Bit 2 flips --> nodeH6
    nodeH3 -- Bit 0 flips --> nodeH4
    nodeH3 -- Bit 2 flips --> nodeH7
    nodeH4 -- Bit 2 flips --> nodeH8
    nodeH5 -- Bit 0 flips --> nodeH6
    nodeH5 -- Bit 1 flips --> nodeH7
    nodeH6 -- Bit 1 flips --> nodeH8
    nodeH7 -- Bit 0 flips --> nodeH8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Eight demes arranged as the vertices of a **3-D hypercube**. Each deme communicates only with its 3 neighbors (those differing in one bit of the binary ID). This topology gives $O(\log_2 k)$ diameter — information spreads across all 8 demes in at most 3 migration rounds.

## 4.4 Cellular Model — Von Neumann Neighborhood Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeLattice[L x L Grid of Individuals]
        nodeA[Cell 1 1\n North]
        nodeB[Cell 1 2\n East]
        nodeC[Cell 1 3\n South]
        nodeD[Cell 1 4\n West]
        nodeCenter[Cell 1 5\n Center Individual]
    end
    nodeCenter -- Mating Pool --> nodeA
    nodeCenter -- Mating Pool --> nodeB
    nodeCenter -- Mating Pool --> nodeC
    nodeCenter -- Mating Pool --> nodeD
    nodeA -.Offspring Diffusion.-> nodeCenter
    nodeB -.Offspring Diffusion.-> nodeCenter
    nodeC -.Offspring Diffusion.-> nodeCenter
    nodeD -.Offspring Diffusion.-> nodeCenter
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The center individual selects a mate from its 4 cardinal neighbors (von Neumann neighborhood = N, E, S, W). Offspring are produced locally and **diffuse** to neighboring cells over subsequent generations. Good genes propagate like a slow wave across the lattice, which is the defining characteristic of the **fine-grained diffusion PGA**.

## 4.5 Hierarchical Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePGA[Parallel Genetic Algorithm]
    nodeMS[Master Slave Model]
    nodeCG[Coarse Grained Island]
    nodeFG[Fine Grained Cellular]
    nodeSync[Synchronization Tight Every Generation]
    nodeTopo[Migration Topology Ring Hypercube Torus]
    nodeMig[Migration Rate m and Interval T mig]
    nodeNbr[Local Neighborhood Moore Von Neumann]

    nodePGA --> nodeMS
    nodePGA --> nodeCG
    nodePGA --> nodeFG
    nodeMS --- nodeSync
    nodeCG --- nodeTopo
    nodeCG --- nodeMig
    nodeFG --- nodeNbr
```

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern**: Each Part-A question carries **3 marks**, expects a crisp definition or short derivation in **~80–100 words**, and maps to KTU Course Outcomes **CO2 / CO3** (analyze parallel GA models and their trade-offs).

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, CO2]**
**Differentiate between the Master–Slave and Island Model Parallel Genetic Algorithms based on (i) population decomposition strategy, (ii) synchronization requirements, and (iii) communication cost.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Master–Slave | Island Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Population Decomposition** | Single global population | Multiple demes (sub-populations) |
| **Synchronization** | Tight — every generation | Loose — every $T_{\text{mig}}$ generations |
| **Communication Cost** | Star topology; high at each gen | Topology-based; amortized over $T_{\text{mig}}$ |
| **Parallelism Type** | Functional (over fitness) | Spatial (over individuals) |

Master–slave parallelizes only the **fitness evaluation** under a single population, while the island model parallelizes the **entire evolutionary loop** across multiple demes that evolve semi-independently with periodic migration. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, CO3]**
**Define the term "Migration Rate" in the context of Island Model PGA. How does the choice of migration rate affect convergence and diversity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
The **migration rate** $m$ is the fraction of individuals in a deme that are selected for migration to neighboring demes at each migration event. A **small $m$** (e.g., 5%) preserves deme-level diversity and prevents premature convergence but slows down global information flow. A **large $m$** (e.g., 30%) accelerates convergence by rapidly spreading good solutions across all demes but may cause the islands to behave like a single panmictic population, losing the diversity advantage. The KTU-recommended heuristic is $m \in [5\%, 15\%]$ with $T_{\text{mig}} \in [5, 20]$ generations. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern**: Part-B questions carry **14 marks**, are split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, and test both **conceptual understanding** and **analytical derivation**. Internal choice is mandatory per the 2024 scheme — examiners must provide **two alternative questions**; the student answers **one**.

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) — Island Model & Migration Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / 2024 Model Paper, CO2 + CO3]**

**(a)** With a clear block diagram, explain the architecture of the **Island Model Parallel Genetic Algorithm**. Discuss the role of **migration topology** (ring vs. hypercube) and **migration rate** on convergence and diversity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a master–slave PGA with the following parameters:
- Serial execution time per generation: $T_{\text{serial}} = 100$ ms
- Parallelizable fraction: $f = 0.9$
- Communication constant: $\beta = 0.05$ ms per processor

Compute:
1. The **speedup** $S_p$ for $p = 4, 8, 16$ processors.
2. The **optimal number of processors** $p^*$ that maximizes $S_p$.
3. The **parallel efficiency** $E_p$ at $p = p^*$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture** (3 marks):

The Island Model partitions the global population $P$ of size $n$ into $k$ **demes** $P^{(1)}, P^{(2)}, \dots, P^{(k)}$, each of size $n/k$. Each deme evolves **independently** on its assigned processor, performing selection, crossover, and mutation in parallel. At fixed intervals of $T_{\text{mig}}$ generations, a **migration event** occurs:

1. Each deme selects $m \cdot (n/k)$ individuals based on a **migration policy** (e.g., best individuals, random individuals).
2. These individuals are sent to neighboring demes according to a **migration topology** (ring, hypercube, torus, etc.).
3. Incoming migrants **replace** local individuals per the **replacement policy** (e.g., replace worst, replace random).

**Step 2 — Migration Topology** (2 marks):

- **Ring topology**: deme $i$ sends to deme $i+1 \pmod{k}$. Low communication cost ($O(1)$ per deme), but slow global propagation — diameter is $k-1$.
- **Hypercube topology**: deme $i$ sends to all demes differing in one bit of its binary ID. Diameter is $\log_2 k$, enabling rapid global spread, but requires $O(\log_2 k)$ communication per deme.

**Step 3 — Migration Rate Trade-off** (2 marks):

- **Low $m$** (e.g., 5%): preserves intra-deme diversity, prevents premature convergence, but slows global propagation of good solutions.
- **High $m$** (e.g., 30%): rapid spread of good genes, faster convergence, but islands behave as a single panmictic population, losing the diversity benefit. Empirically, $m \approx 10\%$ and $T_{\text{mig}} \approx 5{-}20$ generations offer the best trade-off.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given**: $T_{\text{serial}} = 100$ ms, $f = 0.9$, $\beta = 0.05$ ms, so $\gamma = \beta / T_{\text{serial}} = 0.0005$.

**Formula**:

$$
S_p = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p} + \gamma p}
$$

**Sub-step 1: Speedup for $p = 4$**

$$
S_4 = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9) + \dfrac{0.9}{4} + (0.0005)(4)} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.225 + 0.002} = \frac{1}{0.327} \approx 3.058
$$

**Sub-step 2: Speedup for $p = 8$**

$$
S_8 = \frac{1}{0.1 + \dfrac{0.9}{8} + 0.0005 \times 8} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.1125 + 0.004} = \frac{1}{0.2165} \approx 4.619
$$

**Sub-step 3: Speedup for $p = 16$**

$$
S_{16} = \frac{1}{0.1 + \dfrac{0.9}{16} + 0.0005 \times 16} = \frac{1}{0.1 + 0.05625 + 0.008} = \frac{1}{0.16425} \approx 6.088
$$

**Sub-step 4: Optimal $p^*$**

$$
p^* = \sqrt{\frac{f}{\gamma}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.9}{0.0005}} = \sqrt{1800} \approx 42.43
$$

Since $p$ must be an integer (and typically a power of 2 for MPI), the optimal integer choice is $p^* = 32$ or $p^* = 64$.

**Sub-step 5: Parallel efficiency at $p^* = 42$**

$$
E_{42} = \frac{S_{42}}{42} = \frac{1}{42 \left[ 0.1 + \dfrac{0.9}{42} + 0.0005 \times 42 \right]} = \frac{1}{42 [0.1 + 0.0214 + 0.021]} = \frac{1}{42 \times 0.1424} = \frac{1}{5.98} \approx 0.167
$$

Or in percentage, $E_{42} \approx 16.7\%$.

**Conclusion**: The efficiency is low at the optimum because the parallelizable fraction $f = 0.9$ is high but the communication overhead $\gamma p$ grows with $p$. This illustrates the fundamental Amdahl limitation.

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Cellular Model & Speedup Laws**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, CO2 + CO3]**

**(a)** Explain the **Fine-Grained (Cellular / Diffusion) Parallel Genetic Algorithm** with a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of **neighborhood structure** (von Neumann vs. Moore) and contrast it with the coarse-grained Island model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Amdahl's Law speedup bound** for a master–slave PGA with parallel fraction $f$ and $p$ processors. If $f = 0.95$ and $p = 64$, compute:
1. The **theoretical maximum speedup**.
2. The **parallel efficiency**.
3. What happens to speedup if we **double the number of processors to 128**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture** (2 marks):

In the **Cellular PGA (cGA)**, each individual is placed on a unique cell of a $L \times L$ spatial lattice. Selection and mating are **restricted to a small local neighborhood** $N(i,j)$ of each cell $(i,j)$. The two common neighborhood structures are:
- **Von Neumann (5-cell)**: center + 4 cardinal neighbors (N, S, E, W).
- **Moore (9-cell)**: center + 8 surrounding neighbors.

**Step 2 — Diffusion Mechanism** (2 marks):

In each generation, an individual at cell $(i,j)$ selects a mate from $N(i,j)$ based on fitness. Crossover + mutation produce an **offspring** that replaces the individual at $(i,j)$ (or a neighboring cell). High-fitness genes spread via a **diffusion wave** across the lattice — this is the "diffusion model" terminology.

**Step 3 — Contrast with Island Model** (3 marks):

| Aspect | Cellular (Fine-Grained) | Island (Coarse-Grained) |
|---|---|---|
| Population partitioning | One individual per cell | $n/k$ individuals per deme |
| Mating restriction | Local neighborhood | Entire deme |
| Communication | None (shared memory) or local | Explicit inter-processor |
| Genetic propagation | Slow diffusion | Discrete migration events |
| Hardware | GPU / FPGA / shared memory | Distributed cluster / MPI |
| Diversity preservation | Very high (implicit isolation by lattice distance) | High (explicit, parameter $m$, $T_{\text{mig}}$) |

The cellular model has **no explicit migration parameters** — diversity is an emergent property of the spatial structure, making it parameter-free but communication-bound to local neighborhoods.

---

#### 📘 Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Amdahl's Law Derivation** (3 marks):

Amdahl's Law decomposes the total program into a **serial fraction** $(1-f)$ and a **parallel fraction** $f$. The parallel execution time is:

$$
T_{\text{parallel}}(p) = (1-f) T_{\text{serial}} + \frac{f \cdot T_{\text{serial}}}{p}
$$

Therefore the speedup is:

$$
S_p = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{T_{\text{parallel}}(p)} = \frac{T_{\text{serial}}}{(1-f) T_{\text{serial}} + \dfrac{f \cdot T_{\text{serial}}}{p}}
$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $T_{\text{serial}}$:

$$
\boxed{\,S_p = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{p}} = \dfrac{p}{p(1-f) + f}\,}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum speedup for $f = 0.95$, $p = 64$** (2 marks):

$$
S_{64} = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.95) + \dfrac{0.95}{64}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.01484} = \frac{1}{0.06484} \approx 15.42
$$

**Step 3 — Parallel efficiency** (1 mark):

$$
E_{64} = \frac{S_{64}}{64} = \frac{15.42}{64} \approx 0.241 \quad \text{or} \quad 24.1\%
$$

**Step 4 — Doubling to $p = 128$** (1 mark):

$$
S_{128} = \frac{1}{0.05 + \dfrac{0.95}{128}} = \frac{1}{0.05 + 0.00742} = \frac{1}{0.05742} \approx 17.41
$$

**Step 5 — Analysis** (0 marks, but verbally in answer):

The speedup increases from 15.42 → 17.41 (a gain of only **1.99×** when we doubled the processors). This **diminishing return** is the classic Amdahl bottleneck. The asymptotic ceiling as $p \to \infty$ is:

$$
S_{\infty} = \frac{1}{1-f} = \frac{1}{0.05} = 20
$$

So even with infinite processors, we can never exceed **20× speedup** for this workload.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Parallel GA Questions**
> 1. **Confusing the three models** — Students frequently mix up "island" and "cellular" models. Remember: **islands** = multiple demes with **explicit migration**; **cellular** = one individual per cell with **implicit diffusion** via local mating.
> 2. **Forgetting communication overhead in Amdahl's Law** — The textbook Amdahl bound assumes **zero communication cost**. In real PGAs, the $\gamma p$ term reduces $S_p$ below the Amdahl ceiling. Failure to mention this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not stating the migration-rate heuristic** — Examiners expect the rule of thumb $m \in [5\%, 15\%]$ and $T_{\text{mig}} \in [5, 20]$. Stating only the symbol without numbers loses marks.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in part (a)** — A 7-mark question almost always requires a labeled **block diagram**. Drawing just text without a schematic can deduct up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Unit errors in derivation** — When $T_{\text{serial}} = 100$ ms, the speedup is dimensionless, but the optimal $p^*$ must be **rounded to the nearest integer (often a power of 2 for HPC clusters)**.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PGA = parallel execution of GA**, motivated by *speed* and *diversity preservation*.
- **Three canonical models** (KTU-mandated): **Master–Slave**, **Island (Coarse)**, **Cellular (Fine)**.
- **Master–Slave**: Single population, parallel fitness evaluation only, tight synchronization.
- **Island Model**: $k$ demes, **migration rate $m$**, **migration interval $T_{\text{mig}}$**, **topology** (ring, hypercube, torus).
- **Cellular Model**: Lattice layout, **von Neumann / Moore neighborhoods**, no explicit migration.
- **Speedup** $S_p = T_{\text{serial}} / T_{\text{parallel}}(p)$.
- **Efficiency** $E_p = S_p / p$.
- **Amdahl's Law** $S_p = 1 / [(1-f) + f/p]$ — *upper bound* assuming no communication.
- **Realistic speedup** with comm. cost $\gamma$: $S_p = 1 / [(1-f) + f/p + \gamma p]$.
- **Optimal processors** $p^* = \sqrt{f / \gamma}$ — beyond this, speedup *decreases*.
- **Master–Slave isoefficiency**: $W = \Theta(p^2)$ (poor).
- **Island isoefficiency**: $W = \Theta(p)$ (good — preferred for HPC).
- **Migration rate heuristic**: $m \approx 10\%$, $T_{\text{mig}} \approx 5{-}20$ generations.
- **Hyper-cube topology** diameter = $\log_2 k$ — best for fast global spread.
- **Ring topology** comm. cost = $O(1)$ per deme — best for very large $k$.
- **Cellular model** is naturally GPU-/FPGA-friendly (no inter-node comm. needed).
- **Island model** is the industrial standard for MPI/Spark-based evolutionary optimization.
- **Diversity preservation** scales as: Cellular > Island > Master–Slave = Serial.
- **Effective deme size** $N_e = (n/k) \cdot [1 + k m T_{\text{mig}} / 2(k-1)]$ — controls diversity loss rate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Genetic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Genetic Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Genetic Programming (GP)** is a domain-independent, problem-independent, evolutionary computation methodology that automatically solves problems by evolving computer programs, mathematical expressions, decision trees, or any other hierarchical structure. Formally proposed and popularized by **John R. Koza (1992)**, GP extends the Genetic Algorithm (GA) framework by treating the *individuals* (chromosomes) of a population as **executable symbolic structures** — typically LISP-style parse trees — rather than fixed-length binary or real-valued strings.

A GP individual $\mathcal{I}_i$ is defined as a rooted, ordered, point-labeled tree:

$$\mathcal{I}_i = (V, E, \phi, \psi)$$

where:
- $V$ = finite non-empty set of **vertices (nodes)**
- $E \subseteq V \times V$ = set of **edges** (parent–child relations)
- $\phi : V_{\text{internal}} \to \mathcal{F}$ = labeling of internal nodes with **functions** from the function set $\mathcal{F}$
- $\psi : V_{\text{leaf}} \to \mathcal{T}$ = labeling of terminal leaves with elements from the **terminal set** $\mathcal{T}$

The GP search operates on a space $\mathbb{S}_{\text{GP}}$ of all valid compositions drawn from the primitive sets $\mathcal{F} \cup \mathcal{T}$, guided by a **fitness function** that measures how closely an individual's output matches the desired program behavior.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Watchmakers"**
> Imagine you are trying to invent a mathematical formula that fits a dataset of house prices, but you don't know whether you need a polynomial, a logarithm, or some combination. Instead of sitting down and *reasoning* out a formula, you:
> 1. Throw a million random "mini-formulas" (like `+`, `×`, `sin`, `x`, `2.5`) onto a table.
> 2. Each random formula is graded on how badly it fails to predict the prices.
> 3. The *best* formulas are allowed to "breed": pieces of two parent formulas are spliced together to form children. Occasional random tweaks are added.
> 4. Repeat for many generations. The formulas get better at predicting prices — without a human ever explicitly writing one!

This is exactly Genetic Programming. The "random mini-formulas" are **tree structures**, the "breeding" is **subtree crossover**, and the grading is the **fitness evaluation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction: GA vs. GP**
> - **GA** evolves a *solution* (e.g., `[0.5, 1.2, 3.7]` — parameters of a fixed structure).
> - **GP** evolves the *structure and parameters simultaneously* (e.g., the actual formula `0.5 × sin(x) + 1.2 × x²`).

## 1.3 Primitive Sets — The Alphabet of GP

| Component | Symbol | Description | Examples |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Terminal Set** | $\mathcal{T}$ | Leaf nodes; problem-specific inputs and constants | $\{x, y, \text{PI}, 1, 2, \text{ERC}\}$ |
| **Function Set** | $\mathcal{F}$ | Internal nodes; operators and functions | $\{+, -, \times, \div, \sin, \cos, \text{IF}\}$ |
| **Ephemeral Random Constant (ERC)** | $\Re_{\text{ERC}}$ | Random numeric constant generated per individual | $3.14159\ldots$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sufficiency Property (Koza):** A function set $\mathcal{F}$ is *sufficient* if the solution can be expressed by composition of its elements. **Closure Property:** every function in $\mathcal{F}$ must accept any value produced by any other function in $\mathcal{F}$ (or use protected operators like `%` for division by zero).

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Population size** $M$: typically **500** individuals
- **Number of generations** $G$: typically **51** (or until convergence)
- **Initial tree depth limit**: typically **6** levels
- **Maximum tree depth**: typically **17** levels
- **Crossover probability** $p_c$: typically **0.9**
- **Mutation probability** $p_\mu$: typically **0.1**
- **Reproduction probability** $p_r$: typically **0.1**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Preparatory Steps of GP (Koza's Standard Architecture)

Before a GP run can begin, **five critical design decisions** must be made. These constitute the *engineering backbone* of every GP system and are heavily weighted in KTU examinations.

### Step 1: Terminal Set $\mathcal{T}$ Definition
- Identify the inputs to the problem (independent variables).
- Identify any useful constants (e.g., 0, 1, $-1$, $e$, $\pi$).
- Define **Ephemeral Random Constants (ERCs)**: $r \in U[a, b]$ chosen per individual.

### Step 2: Function Set $\mathcal{F}$ Definition
- Must be **closed** under all return types.
- Should be **sufficient** to express a solution.
- Common sets:
  - Arithmetic: $\{+, -, \times, \%\}$ where `%` is protected division $\frac{a}{b} \to 1 \text{ if } b=0$
  - Mathematical: $\{\sin, \cos, \exp, \log\}$
  - Boolean: $\{\text{AND}, \text{OR}, \text{NOT}\}$
  - Conditional: $\{\text{IF-THEN-ELSE}\}$

### Step 3: Fitness Measure $f : \mathbb{S}_{\text{GP}} \to \mathbb{R}$
The fitness function is the *only* feedback signal the GP system has. It quantifies how well an individual solves the problem. Three common forms:

$$f_i = \sum_{j=1}^{N_{\text{fit}}} \left\vert y_j^{\text{target}} - y_j^{\text{computed}} \right\vert^p$$

where $N_{\text{fit}}$ is the number of fitness cases, and $p$ is typically $1$ (raw error) or $2$ (squared error).

### Step 4: Algorithm Parameters
Population size $M$, generation count $G$, operator probabilities $p_c, p_\mu, p_r$.

### Step 5: Termination Criterion
Convergence (fitness threshold met), max generations, or computational budget exhausted.

## 2.2 Initial Population Generation — Three Standard Methods

### 2.2.1 **Grow Method**
- For each tree, randomly pick a depth $d \in [2, D_{\max}]$.
- Starting from the root, at each node randomly pick from $\mathcal{F} \cup \mathcal{T}$.
- If a terminal is picked, the branch stops (no more children).
- Produces **irregular, varied shapes**.

### 2.2.2 **Full Method**
- Every branch grows to the maximum depth $D_{\max}$.
- Internal nodes are picked exclusively from $\mathcal{F}$.
- Leaves are picked from $\mathcal{T}$.
- Produces **symmetric, bushy trees**.

### 2.2.3 **Ramped Half-and-Half** *(Koza's default)*
- For half the population, use **Grow**; for the other half, use **Full**.
- Depth is *ramped* through a range (e.g., 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) instead of fixed.
- Result: maximum **diversity** in initial tree shapes and sizes.

## 2.3 Genetic Operators in GP

### 2.3.1 **Reproduction (Cloning)**
$$\text{Parent} \xrightarrow{p_r} \text{Child (exact copy)}$$

A fit individual is copied verbatim into the next generation (Darwinian survival of the fittest).

### 2.3.2 **Crossover (Subtree Exchange)**
1. Two parents $\mathcal{P}_1, \mathcal{P}_2$ selected probabilistically by fitness.
2. A **crossover point** (a random node) is selected in each parent.
3. The **subtree** rooted at the selected node in $\mathcal{P}_2$ is detached and grafted into $\mathcal{P}_1$ at its selected point, replacing $\mathcal{P}_1$'s old subtree.
4. This produces two offspring (or one offspring and one parent copy, depending on implementation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crossover is a *macro-mutation* in structure space** — it does not just swap bits; it exchanges entire functional sub-modules.

### 2.3.3 **Mutation Operators**
- **Subtree Mutation**: A randomly selected subtree is replaced by a newly randomly generated subtree.
- **Point Mutation**: A single node's label is randomly changed (terminal↔function preserving arity).
- **Permutation Mutation**: The children of an internal node are randomly reordered (only for commutative-like operators).
- **Hoist Mutation**: A randomly chosen subtree is hoisted to become the new root, eliminating upper structure.
- **Collapse Mutation**: A subtree is reduced to a single terminal.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Total Search Space** | $\vert \mathbb{S} \vert = \sum_{d=1}^{D} \vert \mathcal{F} + \mathcal{T} \vert^d$ | Grows **exponentially** with depth |
| 2 | **Standardized Fitness** | $f_{\text{std}}(i) = f_{\max} - f(i)$ | Higher = better; 0 = optimal |
| 3 | **Adjusted Fitness** | $f_{\text{adj}}(i) = \frac{1}{1 + f_{\text{std}}(i)}$ | Normalized to $(0, 1]$ |
| 4 | **Normalized Fitness** | $f_{\text{norm}}(i) = \frac{f_{\text{adj}}(i)}{\sum_j f_{\text{adj}}(j)}$ | Used for fitness-proportionate selection |
| 5 | **Mean Squared Error (Symbolic Regression)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} (y_j^{\text{target}} - y_j^{\text{pred}})^2$ | Most common fitness in SR problems |
| 6 | **Hit Count / Classification Accuracy** | $H_i = \frac{\# \text{ correctly classified}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | For classification tasks |
| 7 | **Tournament Selection Prob.** | $P(\text{win}) = \sum_{k=1}^{T} \binom{T}{k} p_{\text{rel}}^k (1-p_{\text{rel}})^{T-k}$ | $T$ = tournament size |
| 8 | **Tree Size** | $s(\mathcal{T}) = \vert V \vert$ | Total number of nodes |
| 9 | **Tree Depth** | $D(\mathcal{T}) = \max_{\ell \in \text{leaves}} d(\text{root}, \ell)$ | Longest root-to-leaf path |
| 10 | **Node Count (Full Binary Tree)** | $s = 2^{D+1} - 1$ | For balanced full trees |
| 11 | **Effective Fitness** (parsimony) | $f_{\text{eff}} = f + \alpha \cdot s(\mathcal{T})$ | $\alpha$ = parsimony coefficient |
| 12 | **Crossover Point Selection** | $P(\text{node}_i \text{ selected}) = \frac{1}{\vert V \vert}$ | **Uniform** over all nodes (90% internal) |
| 13 | **Expected Offspring Count** | $E_i = \frac{f_i}{\bar{f}} \cdot M$ | Roulette-wheel selection |
| 14 | **Bloat Ratio (Introns measure)** | $b(t) = \frac{s(t) - s^*(t)}{s(t)}$ | $s^*$ = useful subtree size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams:** Expect derivations of MSE-based fitness, search space cardinality, and expected offspring in roulette-wheel selection — these are favorite 7–14 mark questions.
