# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]
**Define *stigmergy* and explain its role in ant foraging behavior.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Full Mark Allocation):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Stigmergy is a mechanism of *indirect coordination* between agents in which the *trace* left in the environment by one agent's action *stimulates* the subsequent actions of other agents in the same environment.

**Role in Ant Foraging (2 Marks):** In ant colonies, stigmergy is mediated by the **pheromone trail**. When an ant traverses a path, it deposits a chemical marker; this marker then *biases* the path selection of subsequent ants, who probabilistically follow stronger gradients. This creates a *self-reinforcing positive feedback loop* that allows the colony to collectively converge on the *shortest path* between nest and food, despite *no individual ant possessing global knowledge* of the optimal route.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Remark:** For full 3 marks, students must cite *pheromone* as the medium and explain *positive feedback*. A definition without the algorithmic/biological role earns only 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample Paper]
**Why is pheromone evaporation essential in the Ant Colony Optimization algorithm? What happens if $\rho = 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Function of Evaporation (2 Marks):** Pheromone evaporation serves as the *exploration safeguard* of the ACO algorithm. It enables the algorithm to *forget* historical trails, thereby preventing *premature convergence* to sub-optimal solutions. The evaporation rate $\rho$ governs the **exploration–exploitation tradeoff**: higher $\rho$ means faster forgetting (more exploration), while lower $\rho$ means slower forgetting (more exploitation).

**Consequence of $\rho = 0$ (1 Mark):** When $\rho = 0$, the pheromone **never decays**. The first good solution found by the colony is *frozen* indefinitely, and the algorithm loses its ability to explore the search space, leading to *stagnation* and *sub-optimal final solutions*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly state that $\rho = 0$ leads to *faster convergence* (it does, but to a *worse* solution). Examiners specifically test whether you understand that *convergence speed* and *solution quality* are *independent* metrics in ACO.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Describe the **biological inspiration** of ant foraging behavior in detail. Include the Deneubourg double-bridge experiment and explain how it validates the use of pheromone-based path selection. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **pheromone transition rule** $p_{ij}^{k}(t)$ used in Ant System. Clearly state the role of the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$, and discuss what happens when (i) $\alpha = 0$ and (ii) $\beta = 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

##### Part (a) — Biological Inspiration (7 Marks)

**1. Background of Real Ant Foraging (2 Marks):**
Many ant species (e.g., *Linepithema humile*, Argentine ants) are *blind* or have very poor vision. They locate food and navigate back to the nest using a chemical trail of **pheromones** (specifically, hydrocarbons) laid on the ground. As an ant walks, it continuously deposits a tiny amount of pheromone, creating a *gradient* that other ants can sense and follow via their antennae.

**2. The Double-Bridge Experiment (3 Marks):**
Deneubourg, Aron, Goss, and Pasteels (1990) designed a controlled experiment in which a nest of ants was connected to a food source by a *double bridge* with two arms of *unequal length* (one short, one long).

- *Initial phase:* Ants explore randomly; both arms receive approximately equal pheromone deposits.
- *Transition phase:* Ants using the **shorter arm** return to the nest *faster*. Since pheromone is deposited *continuously along the return journey*, the short arm accumulates pheromone *at a higher rate* per unit time.
- *Final phase:* Subsequent ants, sensing the *higher pheromone concentration* on the short arm, preferentially choose it. This *positive feedback* causes the colony to *converge* to the shorter path.

**3. Algorithmic Significance (2 Marks):**
This experiment provides *biological proof-of-concept* that:
- *No central control* is needed.
- *Simple local rules* (deposit + follow) yield *emergent global intelligence*.
- *Shorter paths are inherently favored* due to time-differential deposition.
- These insights directly justify the use of *pheromone update* and *probabilistic transition* rules in ACO.

---

##### Part (b) — Derivation of the Transition Rule (7 Marks)

**1. Setup (1 Mark):**
At iteration $t$, ant $k$ is at node $i$. It must choose the next node $j$ from its *feasible neighborhood* $N_i^k$ (set of unvisited nodes). Define the *attractiveness* of edge $(i,j)$ as:

$$
A_{ij}^{k}(t) = [\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}
$$

where:
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ is the *pheromone intensity* on edge $(i,j)$ at time $t$.
- $\eta_{ij} = 1 / d_{ij}$ is the *heuristic visibility* (inverse of distance).
- $\alpha \ge 0$ controls *pheromone influence*.
- $\beta \ge 0$ controls *heuristic influence*.

**2. Normalization Step (2 Marks):**
To convert attractiveness into a *valid probability*, we apply the *Kolmogorov normalization* (sum-to-one condition):

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in N_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}
$$

with $p_{ij}^{k}(t) = 0$ for $j \notin N_{i}^{k}$.

**3. Interpretation of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (2 Marks):**
- The exponent $\alpha$ amplifies the *collective memory* effect: if many prior ants have favored edge $(i,j)$, $\tau_{ij}$ will be high, and high $\alpha$ makes the algorithm *exploit* this knowledge strongly.
- The exponent $\beta$ amplifies the *greedy heuristic*: high $\beta$ makes the algorithm prefer *short* edges, even if their pheromone levels are low (a *myopic* but *fast-converging* behavior).

**4. Limiting Cases (2 Marks):**
- **Case (i): $\alpha = 0$.** The pheromone term vanishes. The transition rule reduces to $p_{ij}^{k} = \eta_{ij}^{\beta} / \sum \eta_{il}^{\beta}$, which is a *pure greedy heuristic* (nearest-neighbor). The algorithm performs *no learning* — every iteration behaves identically.
- **Case (ii): $\beta = 0$.** The heuristic term vanishes. The transition rule reduces to $p_{ij}^{k} = \tau_{ij}^{\alpha} / \sum \tau_{il}^{\alpha}$, which is *blind to distance*. The algorithm becomes a *pure pheromone follower*, leading to *degenerate, long tours* and *slow convergence*.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently fail to explicitly *derive* the normalization step from the sum-to-one axiom. Examiners specifically check whether you can show that the rule is a *probability distribution*. A "remembered" formula without derivation loses 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Derive the **continuous-time pheromone decay equation** $\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$ and show how it leads to the **discrete evaporation formula** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$ used in ACO. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Ant-Cycle pheromone update model** in detail. Compute the *final pheromone intensity* on edge $(1, 3)$ for a 4-city TSP instance after **one iteration**, given the following data:
- 3 ants in the colony.
- Evaporation rate $\rho = 0.1$.
- Initial pheromone $\tau_{13}(0) = 1.0$.
- Pheromone deposit constant $Q = 100$.
- Ant tours and lengths: Ant 1 traversed edge $(1, 3)$ with $L_1 = 250$; Ant 2 did not traverse $(1, 3)$, $L_2 = 300$; Ant 3 traversed edge $(1, 3)$ with $L_3 = 400$.
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

##### Part (a) — Derivation of Pheromone Decay (7 Marks)

**1. Continuous-Time Model Setup (2 Marks):**
Pheromone in nature is a *volatile chemical* that evaporates at a rate proportional to its *current concentration*. Let $\tau_{ij}(t)$ denote the pheromone intensity on edge $(i,j)$ at continuous time $t$. The law of *exponential decay* states:

$$
\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{dt} = -\lambda \cdot \tau_{ij}(t), \quad \lambda > 0
$$

where $\lambda$ is the *continuous evaporation constant*.

**2. Solving the ODE (3 Marks):**
This is a *separable* first-order ODE. Separating variables:

$$
\frac{d\tau_{ij}}{\tau_{ij}} = -\lambda \cdot dt
$$

Integrating both sides from $t$ to $t + \Delta t$:

$$
\int_{\tau_{ij}(t)}^{\tau_{ij}(t + \Delta t)} \frac{d\tau_{ij}}{\tau_{ij}} = -\lambda \int_{t}^{t + \Delta t} dt
$$

$$
\ln \tau_{ij}(t + \Delta t) - \ln \tau_{ij}(t) = -\lambda \cdot \Delta t
$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t + \Delta t) = \tau_{ij}(t) \cdot e^{-\lambda \cdot \Delta t}
$$

**3. Discretization to ACO Form (2 Marks):**
In the algorithmic setting, we discretize time into *integer iterations* with $\Delta t = 1$. Define $\rho = 1 - e^{-\lambda}$, so that $e^{-\lambda} = 1 - \rho$. Substituting:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t + 1) = \tau_{ij}(t) \cdot (1 - \rho)
$$

This is the **standard discrete evaporation equation** in Ant System, where $\rho \in (0, 1)$ is the *evaporation rate*. The complementary term $(1 - \rho)$ is the *pheromone persistence factor*.

---

##### Part (b) — Ant-Cycle Update Model with Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**1. The Ant-Cycle Model (3 Marks):**
In the *Ant-Cycle* variant (Dorigo, 1992), pheromone is deposited **only after an ant completes its full tour**, in an amount **inversely proportional to the total tour length**. The full update rule is:

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k = 1}^{m} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

where:

$$
\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ traversed edge } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Key Property:** Shorter tours cause *larger* deposits, reinforcing the *shortest-path preference* dynamically. This is in contrast to the *Ant-Density* (constant $Q$) and *Ant-Quantity* ($Q/d_{ij}$) models, which do not use the *global* tour length.

**2. Numerical Computation (4 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $\tau_{13}(0) = 1.0$
- $\rho = 0.1$, hence $(1 - \rho) = 0.9$
- $Q = 100$, $m = 3$

**Step 1: Compute each ant's pheromone deposit on edge $(1, 3)$:**

Ant 1 traversed $(1, 3)$ with $L_1 = 250$:

$$
\Delta \tau_{13}^{(1)} = \frac{Q}{L_1} = \frac{100}{250} = 0.4
$$

Ant 2 did *not* traverse $(1, 3)$:

$$
\Delta \tau_{13}^{(2)} = 0
$$

Ant 3 traversed $(1, 3)$ with $L_3 = 400$:

$$
\Delta \tau_{13}^{(3)} = \frac{Q}{L_3} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2: Sum the deposits:**

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{3} \Delta \tau_{13}^{k} = 0.4 + 0 + 0.25 = 0.65
$$

**Step 3: Apply the full update equation:**

$$
\tau_{13}(1) = (1 - 0.1) \cdot \tau_{13}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{3} \Delta \tau_{13}^{k}
$$

$$
\tau_{13}(1) = 0.9 \cdot 1.0 + 0.65
$$

$$
\tau_{13}(1) = 0.9 + 0.65 = 1.55
$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\tau_{13}(1) = 1.55}$ a.u. (arbitrary units)

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is to *omit* the contribution of ants that did *not* traverse the edge (i.e., forget to write $\Delta\tau = 0$ explicitly). Examiners check whether the *summation index* is correctly applied. Another pitfall is to use $\rho \cdot \tau_{ij}$ instead of $(1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}$ — this is the **most common arithmetic error** in ACO valuation.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Ant Foraging Behavior** is the *biological foundation* of Ant Colony Optimization, characterized by *stigmergy* (indirect communication via pheromone).
- ✅ The **Deneubourg double-bridge experiment (1990)** is the canonical biological proof that *shorter paths win* via *time-differential pheromone deposition*.
- ✅ The **transition rule** $p_{ij}^{k}(t) = [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta} / \sum [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}$ is a *normalized* probability distribution (softmax form).
- ✅ The **exponent $\alpha$** weights *pheromone experience* (collective learning); **$\beta$** weights *heuristic visibility* (greedy distance).
- ✅ Setting $\alpha = 0$ yields *pure greedy* search; setting $\beta = 0$ yields *blind exploration*.
- ✅ The **pheromone evaporation** equation $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$ is the *discretized* form of the continuous ODE $d\tau_{ij}/dt = -\lambda \tau_{ij}$, with $\rho = 1 - e^{-\lambda}$.
- ✅ Evaporation is the *exploration safeguard* — without it, the algorithm *stagnates* on the first good solution found.
- ✅ The **Ant-Cycle** model uses a *global* deposit $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_k$ (proportional to *inverse tour length*); the *Ant-Density* and *Ant-Quantity* models use *local* deposits.
- ✅ **Recommended parameters** (Dorigo & Stützle, 2004): $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$ to $5$, $\rho = 0.5$, $m = n$, $Q = 100$.
- ✅ ACO has a **probabilistic convergence guarantee**: $P(\text{best found}) \to 1$ as $t \to \infty$ (Stützle & Dorigo, 2002).
- ✅ **Time complexity** of one ACO iteration: $O(m \cdot n^2)$ for TSP.
- ✅ Real-world applications include **network routing (AntNet)**, **vehicle routing (VRP)**, **VLSI design**, and **swarm robotics**.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Mixing up the *biological* Ant Foraging Behavior (real ants) with the *algorithmic* Ant Colony Optimization (simulated ants in a graph) — they are related but *not identical*.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "PEP" Formula:** **P**heromone × **E**vaporation × **P**robability = the three pillars of every ACO question on ant foraging. If your answer covers all three, you score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theoretical Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Theoretical Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Theoretical Considerations** in Evolutionary Computing (EC) constitute the rigorous mathematical framework that explains *why* and *how* Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs) function. This domain investigates three fundamental pillars: (1) the **search dynamics** of populations evolving under stochastic operators, (2) the **convergence properties** that guarantee (or deny) discovery of optimal/optimal-approximate solutions, and (3) the **computational complexity** (runtime bounds) required to locate such solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Theoretical Considerations* encompasses the Schema Theorem, the Building Block Hypothesis, the No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem, Markov Chain convergence analysis, and runtime (computational complexity) analysis of Evolutionary Algorithms. These form the *mathematical backbone* that separates a purely heuristic tool from a rigorous computational science.

The four foundational theoretical results that every KTU 2024 examiner expects a student to articulate are:

| # | Theoretical Pillar | Originator / Year | Core Question Answered |
|---|--------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Schema Theorem** | Holland (1975) | *How do short, high-fitness bit patterns (schemata) propagate exponentially?* |
| 2 | **Building Block Hypothesis (BBH)** | Goldberg (1989) | *Does recombination of short, fit building blocks yield globally optimal solutions?* |
| 3 | **No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem** | Wolpert & Macready (1997) | *Is any single algorithm universally superior across all problem classes?* |
| 4 | **Runtime / Convergence Analysis** | Rudolph, He, Yao (1990s–2000s) | *How many fitness evaluations are needed to reach an optimum with probability ≥ ε?* |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant classroom of **10,000 students** attempting to guess a secret number between 0 and 10,000.

- Each student makes a random guess → a *population* of candidate *solutions*.
- The teacher reveals *how close* each guess is to the secret → the *fitness function*.
- The **best 20% of guessers** are allowed to "breed" — they tweak their guesses slightly, swap hints with each other, and a few try completely wild guesses → **selection, crossover, mutation**.
- After many generations, the population *clusters* around the secret number.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Intuitive Question:** *Why does this biological-mimicking guessing game reliably find the answer?* This is precisely the *theoretical consideration* we seek to mathematically formalize.

The **Schema Theorem** explains *why short winning patterns spread faster*. The **NFL Theorem** warns us that *this classroom-guessing strategy would be useless if the teacher changed the secret-number rule every minute*. The **Runtime Analysis** answers *how many guess-rounds the class needs*.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following are the universally accepted parameters in EC theoretical literature:

- **Schema order $o(H)$** = number of fixed (non-don't-care) positions in a schema $H$. Unit: *dimensionless count*.
- **Schema defining length $\delta(H)$** = distance between the first and last fixed positions. Unit: *bits*.
- **Fitness ratio $f(H)/\bar{f}$** = schema's average fitness ÷ population's mean fitness. Unit: *dimensionless ratio*.
- **Selection pressure** = expected number of copies an individual above-average solution receives. Unit: *dimensionless*.
- **Takeover time $T^*$** = generations required for selection alone to fill the population with the best individual. Unit: *generations*.
- **Convergence probability $P[\text{convergence}]$** = probability of reaching global optimum. Unit: *dimensionless ∈ [0,1]*.
- **Expected runtime $E[T]$** = expected number of fitness evaluations to find optimum. Unit: *function evaluations (FEs)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975) — Deep Dive

A **schema** $H$ is a similarity template over the alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$, where $*$ is a *don't-care* symbol. For example, the schema $H = 1\!*\!0\!*\!1$ matches any 5-bit string whose 1st, 4th, and 5th positions are 1, 0, and 1 respectively.

Let us dissect the operational steps of the theorem:

- **Step 1 — Schema Counting:** The number of schemata of order $o$ in a binary string of length $\ell$ is $\binom{\ell}{o} \cdot 2^{\ell - o}$. This is a *finite combinatorial space*.
- **Step 2 — Selection Effect:** Under **proportional (roulette-wheel) selection**, a schema $H$ with average fitness $f(H)$ has its instance count $m(H, t)$ evolve according to a multiplicative rule driven by the **fitness ratio** $f(H)/\bar{f}(t)$.
- **Step 3 — Crossover Disruption:** Crossover breaks long, fragile schemata. The probability that a schema of defining length $\delta$ survives single-point crossover is $p_s \geq 1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}$.
- **Step 4 — Mutation Disruption:** Each bit is flipped with probability $p_m$. A schema with $o(H)$ fixed bits survives mutation with probability $p_m^{o(H)} \approx (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$.
- **Step 5 — Combined Inequality:** Multiplying the three effects yields the Schema Theorem.

**Schema Theorem — The Fundamental Inequality:**

$$E[m(H, t+1)] \;\geq\; m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[ 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" in Plain English:** Above-average, short (small $\delta$), low-order (small $o$) schemata — called **building blocks** — *grow exponentially* in the population. This is the algebraic core of *implicit parallelism*.

### 2.2 Building Block Hypothesis (BBH) — Goldberg (1989)

**Hypothesis Statement:** *Genetic Algorithms discover high-quality solutions by combining short, low-order, high-fitness schemata (building blocks) via crossover.*

The BBH extends the Schema Theorem by asserting a constructive property: the *multiplicative growth* of fit building blocks eventually leads to their *assembly* into a globally optimal string. The BBH is **conditional** — it holds reliably only when **deception** (where low-order building blocks *mislead* the search) is absent. The famous **Royal Road** and **Royal Road Deceptive** functions (Mitchell, Forrest, Holland, 1991) were designed specifically to test BBH empirically.

### 2.3 No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem — Wolpert & Macready (1997)

**Theorem Statement:** *For any two optimization algorithms $A$ and $B$, averaged uniformly over all possible discrete problem instances $f : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$, the expected performance of $A$ equals the expected performance of $B$.*

$$\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} P(\text{offline-opt}_{A} = k \mid f) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} P(\text{offline-opt}_{B} = k \mid f)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for EAs:** NFL does *not* say EAs are useless — it says *no algorithm is universally best*. The design of problem-specific EAs (e.g., tailored crossovers, representations) is *theoretically justified* by the practical impossibility of universal superiority.

### 2.4 Markov Chain Convergence Analysis

A finite-state EA can be modeled as a **homogeneous Markov chain** on the state space $\mathcal{S} = \mathcal{P}$ of all possible populations. The transition kernel $P(x_{t+1} = y \mid x_t = x)$ is independent of $t$.

A chain is **ergodic** if it is irreducible and aperiodic. By the **Perron–Frobenius Theorem** for stochastic matrices, such a chain converges to a unique stationary distribution $\pi$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P(X_t = y) = \pi(y), \quad \sum_{y \in \mathcal{S}} \pi(y) = 1$$

A key result is **Rudolph's Convergence Guarantee (1994)**: A canonical GA *with elitism* (preserving the best individual) converges to the global optimum with probability 1. Without elitism, a non-elitist GA *does not* guarantee convergence to the global optimum.

### 2.5 Runtime Analysis & Computational Complexity

The **expected runtime** of an EA on problem $f$ is the random variable:

$$E[T] = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} t \cdot P(T = t) \quad \text{where } T = \min\{t \mid f(X_t^*) = \min_x f(x)\}$$

Common complexity classes encountered:

- **OneMax / LeadingOnes:** $E[T] = \Theta(n \log n)$ for the $(1+1)$-EA using standard bit-flip mutation with $p_m = 1/n$.
- **Needle-in-Haystack (deceptive):** $E[T] = \Theta(2^n)$ for standard mutation — exponential lower bound.
- **Royal Road functions:** Polynomial under specific crossover variants; exponential under pure mutation.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Interpretation | Standard Symbols | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $E[m(H,t+1)] \geq m(H,t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell-1} - p_m o(H) \right]$ | Schema Theorem (lower bound on growth) | $m$: instance count, $f$: fitness, $\bar{f}$: avg fitness, $\ell$: string length | Valid for proportional selection |
| 2 | $P_s \geq 1 - p_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{\ell-1}$ | Single-point crossover survival | $p_c$: crossover rate, $\delta$: defining length | For $\delta < \ell - 1$ |
| 3 | $P_{mut} \geq (1-p_m)^{o(H)}$ | Mutation survival of schema | $p_m$: mutation rate, $o(H)$: order | For $o(H) \geq 0$ |
| 4 | $T^* = \left\lceil \log_{\lambda}(\mu) \right\rceil$ for $(\mu,\lambda)$-selection | Selection takeover time (in generations) | $\mu$: parents, $\lambda$: offspring | Deterministic proportional selection |
| 5 | $P_{converge} = 1 - (1-\pi^*)^t$ | Convergence to optimum with elitism | $\pi^*$: stationary prob of optimum | For ergodic elitist EA |
| 6 | $E[T_{1+1,OneMax}] = \Theta(n \log n)$ | Runtime of (1+1)-EA on OneMax | $n$: problem dimension | For $p_m = 1/n$ |
| 7 | $\bar{f}(t+1) = \bar{f}(t) + \sigma_{sel} \cdot \sqrt{Var(f(t))}$ | Mean-fitness drift (selection-diffusion) | $\sigma_{sel}$: selection intensity | Gaussian fitness assumption |
| 8 | $\pi(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} \frac{f(x_i)^{1/T}}{\sum_{j} f(x_j)^{1/T}}$ | Boltzmann-selection stationary distribution | $T$: temperature | Simulated-annealing limit |
| 9 | $\Delta_{sel} = \int (f - \bar{f}) \cdot P_{sel}(f) \, df$ | Expected selection differential | $\Delta_{sel}$: response to selection | Quantitative-genetics form |
| 10 | $Cov_{HL} \leq n^2 \log n$ (upper bound) | Hypervolume-based runtime bound | $Cov_{HL}$: hypervolume | Multi-objective EA indicator |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Theoretical considerations are not academic luxuries; they directly enable:

- **Aerospace Trajectory Optimization:** Runtime bounds guarantee that an EA can plan an interplanetary mission trajectory in feasible time.
- **VLSI Floorplanning & Chip Layout:** Schema analysis guides the design of crossover operators that preserve critical wire-routing patterns.
- **Hyperparameter Tuning of Neural Networks:** Convergence proofs justify the use of evolutionary hyperparameter search over grid search.
- **Financial Portfolio Optimization:** NFL insights prevent blind trust in "the best algorithm" — risk-aware adaptation is essential.
- **Drug Discovery / Molecular Design:** Building-block-style EA representations efficiently explore combinatorial chemical spaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Schema Theorem

We derive Holland's Schema Theorem from first principles. Let a population at generation $t$ contain $N$ binary strings of length $\ell$. Let $m(H, t)$ denote the number of strings in $P(t)$ that are instances of schema $H$.

#### Step A — Selection Step Derivation

Under **proportional (roulette-wheel) selection**, the probability of selecting a string $x$ is:

$$P_{\text{select}}(x) = \frac{f(x)}{\sum_{y \in P(t)} f(y)} = \frac{f(x)}{N \cdot \bar{f}(t)}$$

The expected number of instances of $H$ after selection alone is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[m_{sel}(H, t)] &= \sum_{x \in P(t) \cap H} N \cdot P_{\text{select}}(x) \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{x \in P(t) \cap H} N \cdot \frac{f(x)}{N \cdot \bar{f}(t)} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{\bar{f}(t)} \sum_{x \in P(t) \cap H} f(x) \\[4pt]
&= m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The transition from line 2 to line 3 cancels $N$. The transition from line 3 to line 4 uses the **definition of schema average fitness**:

$$f(H) = \frac{1}{m(H, t)} \sum_{x \in P(t) \cap H} f(x)$$

> **Conversion Logic:** *Sum of fitnesses of schema instances = count × average schema fitness, divided by population mean fitness, yields the multiplicative growth factor.*

#### Step B — Crossover Disruption Step

For single-point crossover with rate $p_c$, a schema $H$ of defining length $\delta(H)$ is disrupted if the crossover point falls **between** the first and last fixed positions. There are $\delta(H)$ such dangerous cut points out of $\ell - 1$ total possible cut points. So:

$$P[\text{disruption by crossover}] \leq p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}$$

Therefore, the probability of **survival** through crossover satisfies:

$$P_s^{(c)} \geq 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}$$

#### Step C — Mutation Disruption Step

Each fixed bit of the schema is flipped with probability $p_m$. The schema survives mutation only if **none** of its $o(H)$ fixed bits are flipped. Assuming independence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s^{(m)} &= (1 - p_m)^{o(H)} \\[4pt]
&\geq 1 - p_m \cdot o(H) \quad \text{(Bernoulli's inequality)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step D — Combined Schema Theorem

Since selection, crossover, and mutation are applied independently in canonical GA, we multiply the three probabilities:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[m(H, t+1)] &= m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot P_s^{(c)} \cdot P_s^{(m)} \\[4pt]
&\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}\right] \cdot \left[1 - p_m \cdot o(H)\right] \\[4pt]
&\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The last inequality uses $(1-a)(1-b) \geq 1 - a - b$ for $a, b \in [0, 1]$. **Q.E.D.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Short, low-order, above-average schemata have $\delta, o$ small, so the bracket stays close to 1, and the multiplicative factor reduces to $\approx f(H)/\bar{f}$. This explains *implicit parallelism*: $\Theta(\ell^3)$ schemata are processed per generation with only $N$ evaluations.

### 3.2 Derivation of Selection Takeover Time

For a $(\mu, \lambda)$-EA with proportional selection, let $x^*(t)$ be the count of the best individual at generation $t$. The expected growth rate is:

$$E[x^*(t+1)] = x^*(t) \cdot \frac{\lambda}{\mu} \cdot \frac{f_{max}}{\bar{f}(t)}$$

When $x^*$ is rare, $\bar{f}(t) \approx f_{avg}$ (mean of all non-optimal), and $f_{max}/\bar{f} \approx (1 + s)$ where $s$ is the **selection coefficient** $(f_{max} - \bar{f})/\bar{f}$. In the continuous limit:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dx^*}{dt} &= x^* \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}(1+s) - 1\right) \approx x^* \cdot \frac{\lambda s}{\mu} \\[4pt]
x^*(t) &= x^*(0) \cdot \exp\left(\frac{\lambda s}{\mu} \cdot t\right) \\[4pt]
\text{Setting } x^*(T^*) &= N, \quad T^* = \frac{\mu}{\lambda s} \ln\left(\frac{N}{x^*(0)}\right) = \mathcal{O}(\log N) \text{ generations.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** *Exponential growth of the best individual until it fills the population — hence the logarithmic takeover time.*

### 3.3 Convergence via Markov Chain — Elitist GA

State space: $\mathcal{S} = $ all possible populations. Transition: $P(X_{t+1} = y \mid X_t = x)$. Define $X_t^* = $ best individual in population at time $t$. An **elitist** strategy copies $X_t^*$ to $X_{t+1}$ if no offspring beats it.

Let $Z = \{x \in \mathcal{S} : \text{best individual is a global optimum}\}$. Define indicator $I_t = \mathbf{1}_{X_t^* \in Z^*}$. We have:

$$P(I_{t+1} = 1 \mid I_t = 1) = 1 \quad \text{(elitist preservation)}$$

The set $Z$ is **absorbing** in the Markov chain. For a finite state space with a non-zero probability $p^* > 0$ of reaching $Z$ from any state, the chain converges:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P(X_t^* \in Z^*) = 1 \quad \text{(convergence in probability)}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** *Absorbing Markov chain states with non-zero entry probability guarantee asymptotic convergence to the optimum — a cornerstone result of Rudolph (1994).*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Theoretical Analysis Framework of Evolutionary Computing

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Population P of Candidate Solutions"] --> nodeB["Fitness Function Evaluation"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Selection Operator"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Crossover Operator"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Mutation Operator"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["New Population P t+1"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Convergence Check"]
    nodeG -- Not Converged --> nodeB
    nodeG -- Converged --> nodeH["Theoretical Analysis Layer"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Schema Theorem Analysis"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["No Free Lunch Theorem"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Markov Chain Convergence"]
    nodeH --> nodeL["Runtime Complexity Bounds"]
    nodeI --> nodeM["Implicit Parallelism Result"]
    nodeJ --> nodeN["Algorithm Specialization Justification"]
    nodeK --> nodeO["Absorbing Chain Guarantee"]
    nodeL --> nodeP["Polynomial vs Exponential Classification"]
```

### 4.2 Schema Theorem Propagation Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1["Schema H = 1**0* Defining Length delta=3 Order o=2"]
    nodeS2["Schema Instance Count m H t at Generation t"]
    nodeS3["Selection: Multiply by f H over f bar t"]
    nodeS4["Crossover: Survive with Prob 1 - pc times delta over L minus 1"]
    nodeS5["Mutation: Survive with Prob 1 - pm to the power o"]
    nodeS6["Expected Count E m H t+1 at Next Generation"]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 -- Exponentially Growing for Fit Short Low Order --> nodeS7["Building Block Assembly into Optimum"]
```

### 4.3 Convergence Dynamics State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialRandomPopulation
    InitialRandomPopulation --> SelectionPhase
    SelectionPhase --> CrossoverPhase
    CrossoverPhase --> MutationPhase
    MutationPhase --> EvaluateOffspring
    EvaluateOffspring --> ElitistCheck
    ElitistCheck --> PreserveBest : Best is preserved
    ElitistCheck --> ReplaceBest : Offspring beats parent
    PreserveBest --> ConvergenceTest
    ReplaceBest --> ConvergenceTest
    ConvergenceTest --> SelectionPhase : Not yet converged
    ConvergenceTest --> AbsorbingOptimumState : Global optimum reached
    AbsorbingOptimumState --> [*]
```

### 4.4 Runtime Complexity Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1["EA Class Selection"]
    nodeR2["OneMax or LeadingOnes"]
    nodeR3["Needle in Haystack"]
    nodeR4["Royal Road Constructive"]
    nodeR5["Deceptive Trap Functions"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR2
    nodeR1 --> nodeR3
    nodeR1 --> nodeR4
    nodeR1 --> nodeR5
    nodeR2 --> nodeR6["Polynomial Theta of n log n under pm = 1 over n"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeR7["Exponential Theta of 2 to the n under standard mutation"]
    nodeR4 --> nodeR8["Polynomial with crossover assisted BB assembly"]
    nodeR5 --> nodeR9["Exponential without diversity preserving mechanism"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
*State and explain the Schema Theorem of Holland with its significance in Evolutionary Algorithms.*

**Model Answer:**

The Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975) gives a lower bound on the expected number of instances of a schema $H$ at the next generation. The theorem states that:

$$E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1} - p_m \cdot o(H)\right]$$

**Significance:**
- It provides the *theoretical justification* for the exponentially growing propagation of short, low-order, high-fitness schemata (building blocks).
- It establishes the concept of **implicit parallelism**: a population of $N$ strings processes $\Theta(\ell^3)$ schemata simultaneously.
- It underpins the **Building Block Hypothesis** of Goldberg (1989).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
*Briefly explain the No Free Lunch Theorem and its implication on Evolutionary Algorithm design.*

**Model Answer:**

The **No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem** (Wolpert & Macready, 1997) states that for any two algorithms $A$ and $B$ whose performance is averaged uniformly over all possible discrete optimization problems, the expected performance of $A$ equals the expected performance of $B$. Formally:

$$\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f} P(\text{opt-cost}_A \mid f) = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f} P(\text{opt-cost}_B \mid f)$$

**Implication on EA Design:** NFL dictates that *no evolutionary algorithm is universally superior*. The practical corollary is that EA designers must exploit **problem-specific knowledge** — e.g., tailored representations, custom crossover operators, and domain-aware fitness functions. NFL does not contradict EA utility; it rationalizes specialization.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 3 Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - July 2023]

**(a) Derive Holland's Schema Theorem from first principles, clearly stating the assumptions made for selection, crossover, and mutation. Explain why the theorem is stated as a *lower bound* and not an exact equality.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the Building Block Hypothesis in detail. What is "deception" in this context? Describe a function (e.g., Royal Road) that was specifically designed to test the BBH, and explain its outcomes.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation of Holland's Schema Theorem** *(7 marks)*

**Assumptions:**
1. Binary alphabet $\{0, 1\}$, schema alphabet $\{0, 1, *\}$.
2. Proportional (roulette-wheel) selection.
3. Single-point crossover with rate $p_c$.
4. Bit-flip mutation with rate $p_m$, applied independently to each bit.

**Step 1 — Selection Operator:** Under proportional selection, the probability of selecting an individual $x$ with fitness $f(x)$ from a population of mean fitness $\bar{f}(t)$ is $f(x)/(N\bar{f}(t))$. The expected count of schema $H$ after selection is therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[m_{\text{sel}}(H, t)] &= m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Crossover Disruption:** A schema of defining length $\delta(H)$ is disrupted if the crossover point lies inside the schema's fixed-bit range. The probability of disruption is $p_c \cdot \delta(H)/(\ell - 1)$. Survival probability:

$$P_s^{(c)} \geq 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Mutation Disruption:** Each of the $o(H)$ fixed bits must remain unflipped. Independence gives $P_s^{(m)} = (1-p_m)^{o(H)} \geq 1 - p_m \cdot o(H)$ (Bernoulli's inequality).

**Step 4 — Combined Multiplicative Bound:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[m(H, t+1)] &\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell-1}\right) \cdot \left(1 - p_m o(H)\right) \\[4pt]
&\geq m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}(t)} \cdot \left[1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{\ell-1} - p_m o(H)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why a lower bound, not an equality:**
- Bernoulli's inequality $(1-a)(1-b) \geq 1 - a - b$ *underestimates* the true survival.
- The defining-length term $p_c \cdot \delta/(\ell-1)$ is an *upper bound* on disruption (some "disruptive" cuts may be repaired by the second parent).
- The fitness ratio uses $f(H)/\bar{f}(t)$, ignoring the variance of the fitness distribution.
- Hence the *exact* expected count is $\geq$ the algebraic lower bound.

**(b) Building Block Hypothesis, Deception, and Royal Road Functions** *(7 marks)*

**Building Block Hypothesis (BBH):** Proposed by Goldberg (1989), the BBH asserts that a Genetic Algorithm efficiently finds high-quality solutions by *combining short, low-order, high-fitness schemata* (building blocks) through crossover. It is a *constructive* generalization of the Schema Theorem: while the Schema Theorem guarantees that fit short schemata *grow*, the BBH claims they are *eventually assembled* into a globally optimal string.

**Deception:** A function is *deceptive* if its low-order building blocks *mislead* the search away from the global optimum. Formally, deception occurs when the schema with the highest *low-order* average fitness points *away from* the optimal bit at some position. For example, in a 3-bit deceptive trap:

- Order-1 schemata suggest bit 0 at every position (fitness $f(0) = 0.9$, $f(1) = 0.1$).
- However, the global optimum is $111$ (with fitness $1.0$), so the *low-order guidance is wrong*.

**Royal Road Functions (Mitchell, Forrest, Holland, 1991):** Royal Road functions were *constructive* testbeds designed to provide ideal conditions for the BBH. A Royal Road function rewards the discovery of a set of pre-defined "roadblocks" (e.g., 8-bit blocks that must all be correct) and gives high fitness for partial credit.

**Outcomes:** Empirical studies showed that *standard GA with uniform crossover did not efficiently solve Royal Road problems*, contradicting the BBH's strongest form. This led to the development of:
- **Royal Road Deceptive Functions** — adversarial variants.
- **Hitchhiking** — the phenomenon where suboptimal bits piggyback on fit schemata.
- **Revised crossover schemes** like **uniform crossover** and **Linkage Learning** algorithms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:**
> - Many students forget to justify *why the Schema Theorem is a lower bound*. Examiners specifically award marks for the "$\geq$" reasoning. State Bernoulli's inequality explicitly.
> - For part (b), students often describe deception abstractly without naming a concrete function. Always name the **deceptive trap** or **Royal Road** function with the precise bit pattern.
> - Do not confuse the Royal Road function with the Royal Road *Deceptive* function; the former is constructive, the latter is adversarial.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) State and prove the No Free Lunch Theorem. What are its practical implications for Evolutionary Algorithm design? Discuss with an example.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Model a canonical Genetic Algorithm as a Markov chain. Explain the conditions under which the chain is ergodic. State and prove Rudolph's convergence result for elitist GAs.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) No Free Lunch Theorem — Statement, Proof Sketch, and Implications** *(7 marks)*

**Statement (Wolpert & Macready, 1997):** Let $\mathcal{F}$ be the set of all functions $f : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ (with $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ finite). For any two algorithms $A$ and $B$ that do not revisit points, and for any performance measure $\Phi$:

$$\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \Phi(A, f) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \Phi(B, f)$$

**Proof Sketch:** Let $d^M_y$ denote the number of times algorithm $A$ visits value $y \in \mathcal{Y}$. The performance measure $\Phi$ depends only on these visit counts. The total number of *functions* consistent with a given observation sequence is $|\mathcal{Y}|^{|\mathcal{X}| - d^M}$ where $d^M = \sum_y d^M_y$. Summing $\Phi(A, f)$ over all $f$ and dividing by $|\mathcal{F}|$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert}\sum_{f} \Phi(A, f) &= \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert^{\vert \mathcal{X} \vert}} \sum_{d^M} \binom{\vert \mathcal{X} \vert}{d^M_0, d^M_1, \ldots} \prod_{y} \left(\frac{1}{|\mathcal{Y}|}\right)^{d^M_y} \cdot \Phi(d^M)
\end{aligned}
$$

The combinatorial sum depends **only on $|\mathcal{X}|$ and $|\mathcal{Y}|$**, not on the algorithm $A$. Hence the same expression holds for $B$, establishing $\bar{\Phi}(A) = \bar{\Phi}(B)$.

**Practical Implications & Example:**
- **No algorithm is universally best.** Averaged over *all* problems, every algorithm performs equally.
- **Specialization is essential.** If we restrict attention to a *class* of problems (e.g., continuous, differentiable, convex), then EAs can outperform random search — and this is *not* violated by NFL.
- **Example:** A Genetic Algorithm with bit-flip mutation may excel on binary combinatorial problems (e.g., MAX-SAT) but perform poorly on continuous, smooth problems where gradient methods dominate. NFL justifies this *empirical asymmetry*: the two problem classes are not equally represented in the uniform average.

**(b) Markov Chain Modeling of Canonical GA and Rudolph's Convergence Result** *(7 marks)*

**Markov Chain Model:** Let the state space be $\mathcal{S} = \mathcal{X}^N$ (all possible populations of $N$ strings). Define the transition kernel:

$$P(X_{t+1} = y \mid X_t = x) = P_{\text{mut}}(P_{\text{cross}}(P_{\text{sel}}(x)) = y)$$

Since selection, crossover, and mutation are stochastic and depend only on the current population $X_t$, the chain is **homogeneous Markovian** (memoryless).

**Ergodicity Conditions:** A Markov chain is **ergodic** if:
1. **Irreducible** — every state is reachable from every other state in a finite number of steps.
2. **Aperiodic** — $\gcd\{n : (P^n)_{ii} > 0\} = 1$ for all $i$.
3. **Positive recurrent** — expected return time to every state is finite.

For a canonical GA with non-zero mutation probability $p_m > 0$ and a non-zero probability of generating *any* string from *any* parent, the chain is **irreducible** (any population can reach any other population in at most $N$ generations via mutations alone). Aperiodicity follows from the fact that the diagonal transition probabilities $P_{ii} > 0$ (e.g., self-reproduction by selection followed by no crossover/mutation).

**Rudolph's Theorem (1994) — Convergence of Elitist GAs:**

**Theorem:** *A canonical Genetic Algorithm with elitist selection (preserving the best individual across generations) converges to the global optimum in probability, i.e.,*

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} P(f(X_t^*) = \min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} f(x)) = 1$$

**Proof Sketch:**
1. **Construction of absorbing set:** Define $Z = \{x \in \mathcal{S} : \text{best individual } x^* \text{ is a global optimum}\}$. Under elitism, once $X_t \in Z$, the chain stays in $Z$: $P(X_{t+1} \in Z \mid X_t \in Z) = 1$. Hence $Z$ is an **absorbing set**.
2. **Probability of entry into $Z$:** By ergodicity, the chain visits every state infinitely often. Let $p^* = \min_{x \notin Z} P(\text{reach } Z \mid X_0 = x) > 0$.
3. **Convergence in probability:** Let $T_Z = \min\{t \geq 0 : X_t \in Z\}$. The tail probability satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(T_Z > t) &\leq (1 - p^*)^t \\[4pt]
\lim_{t \to \infty} P(T_Z > t) &= 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P(\text{convergence to } Z) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

4. **Conclusion:** Elitist GAs converge to the global optimum with probability 1, regardless of the initial population.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:**
> - For part (a), students often present NFL as a *rebuttal* of EAs. This is **wrong** — NFL is a theorem about *averaging over all problems*, not a verdict on real-world utility. Examiners explicitly look for the distinction between *uniform average* and *problem-class-conditional* performance.
> - For part (b), students frequently forget the **positivity** condition (positive probability of reaching the optimum) when stating Rudolph's theorem. Without it, convergence is not guaranteed. Always pair ergodicity with a non-zero entry probability.
> - Do not state "all GAs converge" — the convergence result requires **elitism**. Non-elitist GAs can be shown to *fail* to converge to the global optimum.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Theoretical Considerations in EC**

- ✅ The **Schema Theorem** is a *lower bound* — never an equality — on schema instance growth under selection, crossover, and mutation.
- ✅ Three critical schema parameters: $f(H)$ (avg fitness), $\delta(H)$ (defining length), $o(H)$ (order). Short, low-order, fit schemata dominate.
- ✅ **Implicit parallelism:** $N$ binary strings of length $\ell$ process $\Theta(\ell^3)$ schemata simultaneously per generation.
- ✅ **Building Block Hypothesis (Goldberg 1989)** requires *non-deceptive* fitness landscapes. **Deception** occurs when low-order schemata misguide the search.
- ✅ **No Free Lunch Theorem (Wolpert & Macready 1997):** No algorithm is universally superior when averaged uniformly over *all* discrete problem instances. The uniform average is a theoretical artifact; in practice, problem-class specialization is essential.
- ✅ **Canonical GA as a Markov chain** is ergodic if (i) mutation is non-zero, (ii) the chain is irreducible, (iii) the chain is aperiodic. Ergodicity guarantees a unique stationary distribution.
- ✅ **Rudolph's Theorem (1994):** Elitist GA converges to the global optimum with probability 1. **Non-elitist GA does NOT guarantee global convergence.**
- ✅ **Takeover time** for $(\mu, \lambda)$-selection is $T^* = \mathcal{O}(\log N)$ generations — logarithmic in population size.
- ✅ **Runtime classification:** OneMax / LeadingOnes ⇒ $\Theta(n \log n)$ under $p_m = 1/n$. Needle-in-Haystack ⇒ $\Theta(2^n)$ exponential. Royal Road ⇒ polynomial with constructive crossover, exponential under pure mutation.
- ✅ **Bernoulli's inequality** $(1-a)(1-b) \geq 1 - a - b$ is the *algebraic origin* of the Schema Theorem's "$\geq$" sign.
- ✅ **Schema Theorem vs. NFL:** The Schema Theorem supports *why EAs work on certain problems*; NFL cautions that this *success does not transfer universally*.
- ✅ For KTU 14-mark questions, always pair a *formal statement* with a *derivation or proof sketch* and a *practical engineering example* — examiners reward all three.
- ✅ Memorize the **standard complexity classes** for OneMax, LeadingOnes, and trap functions — these appear repeatedly across KTU question papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convergence Proofs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Convergence Proofs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Convergence in Evolutionary Computing

In the context of Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs), **convergence** is a formal mathematical property describing the asymptotic behavior of the population's best fitness as the generation index $t \to \infty$. A canonical EA is said to *converge in probability* to the global optimum $f^{\star}$ if and only if:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \Pr\{F_{\mathrm{best}}(t) = f^{\star}\} \;=\; 1$$

where $F_{\mathrm{best}}(t) = \max_{x \in P(t)} f(x)$ is the maximum fitness in the population at generation $t$, and $P(t)$ denotes the population of candidate solutions at generation $t$.

A stronger notion is *convergence almost surely* (with probability 1):

$$\Pr\left\{\lim_{t \to \infty} F_{\mathrm{best}}(t) = f^{\star}\right\} \;=\; 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT869, Module 3):**
> Convergence proof techniques for canonical GAs, Evolution Strategies (ES), and Evolutionary Programming (EP) form the core assessment area. The four mandatory proofs are: **(i) Schema Theorem of Holland, (ii) Rudolph's elitist-GA convergence, (iii) (1+1)-ES convergence, and (iv) the No Free Lunch Theorem.**

## 1.2 Types of Convergence

| # | Type | Mathematical Statement | Practical Meaning |
|---|------|------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Convergence in probability | $\lim_{t\to\infty}\Pr(F_t = f^{\star}) = 1$ | With enough generations, algorithm finds optimum with high likelihood |
| 2 | Almost-sure convergence | $\Pr(\lim_{t\to\infty} F_t = f^{\star}) = 1$ | Optimum is *eventually* reached on every run of infinite length |
| 3 | Convergence of sequence | $F_t \to f^{\star}$ deterministically | Population stabilizes to an absorbing set |
| 4 | Convergence of distribution | $P_t \to P^{\star}$ (stationary) | Sampling distribution becomes stationary |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Climbing a Foggy Mountain"

Imagine a hiker trying to reach the highest peak of a mountain range in **dense fog**. The hiker cannot see the peak. At every step, the hiker takes a compass reading and moves a few meters in a direction that *appears* to go upward.

- The hiker is the **population of candidate solutions**.
- The compass reading corresponds to the **fitness function** (a noisy gradient signal).
- The fog represents **stochasticity** introduced by selection, crossover, and mutation operators.
- Reaching the peak is **convergence to the global optimum**.

If the hiker *eventually* stands on the highest peak with **probability 1** given infinite time, we have **almost-sure convergence**. If the hiker *eventually* reaches the peak on a randomly chosen infinite run, we have **convergence in probability**. The convergence proofs we study in this module mathematically formalize *when* and *how fast* this hiker reaches the summit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> Convergence proofs do *not* usually state that the algorithm "works well." They state that the algorithm *eventually* finds the global optimum, often without any time bound. Speed of convergence is a *separate* analysis (covered in convergence-rate theorems).

## 1.4 Historical Roadmap of Convergence Proofs

1. **1975 — Holland's Schema Theorem:** A *lower bound* on the propagation of short, high-fitness, low-order schemata. Although not a full convergence proof, it is the first rigorous mathematical statement about the dynamics of GAs.
2. **1989 — Goldberg:** Extended schema analysis and the *Building Block Hypothesis*.
3. **1994 — Rudolph:** First rigorous proof that an *elitist* canonical GA converges to the global optimum using **homogeneous finite Markov chains**.
4. **1990s — Bäck, Schwefel, Fogel:** Convergence proofs for **(1+1)-ES**, EP, and CMA-ES variants.
5. **1995 — Wolpert (No Free Lunch Theorem):** A meta-theorem stating that no optimizer is universally superior.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Convergence Proofs in EC

### Pillar 1 — Holland's Schema Theorem (1975)
The Schema Theorem provides a *lower bound* on the expected number of instances of a schema $H$ at generation $t+1$ given its count at generation $t$. It is the foundation for the *Building Block Hypothesis*.

### Pillar 2 — Rudolph's Elitist GA Convergence (1994)
Uses a **homogeneous finite Markov chain** of population states. With elitism (the best individual is always preserved), the GA converges in probability to the global optimum.

### Pillar 3 — (1+1)-Evolution Strategy Convergence
The simplest ES, with one parent and one offspring per generation, converges almost surely to the global optimum of strictly convex continuous functions under mild mutation conditions.

### Pillar 4 — No Free Lunch Theorem
For any two algorithms $A$ and $B$, averaged over *all possible fitness functions*, their expected performance is identical. Convergence results are therefore *function-class specific*.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Domain / Unit |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| $m(H,t)$ | Number of instances of schema $H$ in population at generation $t$ | Non-negative integer |
| $f(H)$ | Average fitness of strings in population matching schema $H$ | Real number |
| $\bar{f}$ | Mean fitness of the entire population | Real number |
| $\delta(H)$ | Defining length of schema $H$ (distance between outermost fixed positions) | Integer $[0, l-1]$ |
| $o(H)$ | Order of schema $H$ (number of fixed positions) | Integer $[0, l]$ |
| $l$ | Length of the binary string | Positive integer |
| $p_c$ | Crossover probability | $[0,1]$ |
| $p_m$ | Mutation probability per bit | $[0,1]$ |
| $f^{\star}$ | Global optimum fitness value | Real number |
| $P_t$ | Population state at generation $t$ | Set of strings |
| $q_{\min}$ | Minimum selection probability of an individual | $(0,1]$ |
| $\sigma_t$ | Mutation step-size at generation $t$ (ES) | Positive real |
| $\Phi(\cdot)$ | Standard normal CDF | Probability |
| $N(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | Gaussian mutation distribution | Continuous |

## 2.3 Core Mathematical Statement (Schema Theorem)

The canonical Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975) states:

$$m(H, t+1) \;\geq\; \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot m(H,t) \cdot \left(1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}\right) \cdot (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> This inequality underpins the design of competent GAs (e.g., **Compact GA, BOA, hBOA**). It tells designers that combining short, low-order, high-fitness schemata is the algorithmic "currency" of the search.

## 2.4 Canonical GA Convergence Condition (Rudolph)

A **canonical GA** (binary representation, fitness-proportionate selection, single-point crossover, bit-flip mutation, population size $\mu$, *no elitism*) is **not guaranteed** to converge to the global optimum. However, an **elitist canonical GA** converges in probability:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \Pr\{P(t) \cap S^{\star} \neq \emptyset\} \;=\; 1$$

where $S^{\star} = \{x : f(x) = f^{\star}\}$ is the set of global optima.

The bound on the probability of *not* finding the optimum in $t$ generations is:

$$\Pr\{F_{\mathrm{best}}(t) \neq f^{\star}\} \;\leq\; (1 - p_{\min})^{t}$$

where $p_{\min} > 0$ is the minimum probability of selecting an optimal individual in any single generation.

## 2.5 (1+1)-ES Convergence Equation

The progress of the (1+1)-ES on a strictly convex function $f$ with mutation step-size $\sigma$ satisfies the *progress expectation*:

$$E[X_{t+1} - X_t \mid X_t] \;\geq\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}} - c \cdot \sigma^2$$

where $c > 0$ is a function-dependent constant. The algorithm converges iff the positive drift dominates the variance-induced noise.

## 2.6 No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem — Wolpert (1995)

$$\sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \Pr\{A \text{ finds } x \mid f\} \;=\; \sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} \Pr\{B \text{ finds } x \mid f\}$$

for any pair of algorithms $A, B$ and *any* distribution over the function class $\mathcal{F}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** NFL does *not* say evolutionary algorithms are useless. It says performance gains on one problem class must be *paid for* by performance losses on another. Problem-specific knowledge is therefore the decisive factor in real engineering design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975)

We derive the lower-bound formula for the number of schema instances after one generation of selection, crossover, and mutation.

### Step 1 — Setup
Let $P(t) = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{\mu}\}$ be the population at generation $t$, with $\mu$ individuals. Let $H$ be a schema of length $l$. Define:

$$m(H, t) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{\mu} \mathbb{1}\{x_i \text{ is an instance of } H\}$$

The mean fitness of the population is:

$$\bar{f} \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{\mu} f(x_i)$$

The mean fitness of schema $H$ is:

$$f(H) \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i : x_i \in H} f(x_i)}{m(H, t)}$$

### Step 2 — Effect of Fitness-Proportionate Selection Alone
Under selection (no crossover, no mutation), the expected number of schema $H$ instances at $t+1$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s[m(H, t+1)] \;&=\; \mu \cdot \frac{m(H,t) \cdot f(H)}{\mu \cdot \bar{f}} \\
&=\; m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Each schema instance is copied with probability proportional to its fitness share, so the fraction of the new population that are instances of $H$ is the fitness ratio $f(H)/\bar{f}$.

### Step 3 — Effect of Single-Point Crossover
A schema $H$ with defining length $\delta(H)$ is destroyed by single-point crossover if:
- crossover occurs *between* its outermost fixed positions, AND
- the partner chromosome contributes the "wrong" allele at one of the fixed positions.

The probability that crossover *does not* fall inside the defining length is $1 - \delta(H)/(l-1)$. Thus the *survival probability* under crossover is:

$$p_{\mathrm{surv}}^{c} \;\geq\; 1 - p_c \cdot \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}$$

### Step 4 — Effect of Bit-Flip Mutation
Each fixed bit of the schema is independently flipped with probability $p_m$. There are $o(H)$ fixed bits, so the probability that *none* of them is flipped is:

$$p_{\mathrm{surv}}^{m} \;=\; (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$$

### Step 5 — Combine All Three Operators
Assuming the operators act independently (standard Holland assumption), the combined survival factor is multiplicative:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[m(H, t+1)] \;&\geq\; m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot p_{\mathrm{surv}}^{c} \cdot p_{\mathrm{surv}}^{m} \\
&=\; m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}\right) \cdot (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Schema Theorem**, valid as a *lower bound* because crossover between two identical schema instances can also *create* new instances (which are not counted in the lower bound but only in the exact form).

### Step 6 — Interpretation

$$m(H, t+1) \;\geq\; m(H,t) \cdot \underbrace{\frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}}_{\text{selection pressure}} \cdot \underbrace{\left(1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}\right)}_{\text{crossover disruption}} \cdot \underbrace{(1 - p_m)^{o(H)}}_{\text{mutation disruption}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the formula:** Schemata that are *short* (small $\delta$), *low-order* (small $o$), and *above-average* (large $f(H)/\bar{f}$) receive exponentially growing sampling rates across generations. These are called **Building Blocks**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Rudolph's Convergence Bound (Elitist Canonical GA)

We prove that an elitist canonical GA converges in probability to the global optimum.

### Step 1 — State Space
Let $\mathcal{S} = \{0,1\}^{l \times \mu}$ be the finite state space of all possible populations of $\mu$ binary strings of length $l$. The population sequence $\{P(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ is a **homogeneous finite Markov chain** because the next state depends only on the current state (operators are stationary).

### Step 2 — Absorbing States
A state is *absorbing* if the population contains at least one global optimum:

$$S^{\star} \;=\; \{P : \exists\, x \in P \text{ with } f(x) = f^{\star}\}$$

By the **elitism assumption**, if $P(t) \in S^{\star}$, then $P(t+1) \in S^{\star}$ (the optimal individual is never lost).

### Step 3 — Positive Reachability
From *any* non-optimal state, there is a positive probability of transitioning to an optimal state in one generation. Specifically:

$$p_{\min} \;=\; \min_{P \notin S^{\star}} \Pr\{P(t+1) \in S^{\star} \mid P(t) = P\} \;>\; 0$$

This positive probability arises because:
- Selection can copy the best individual to a parent slot (probability $\geq 1/\mu$),
- Crossover and mutation can produce the optimal string from a near-optimal parent (probability $\geq q > 0$ for finite $l$),
- The product of these is strictly positive.

### Step 4 — Geometric Decay of Failure Probability
The probability that the algorithm has *not* found the optimum by generation $t$ satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Pr\{P(t) \notin S^{\star}\} \;&\leq\; (1 - p_{\min}) \cdot \Pr\{P(t-1) \notin S^{\star}\} \\
&\leq\; (1 - p_{\min})^t \cdot \Pr\{P(0) \notin S^{\star}\} \\
&\leq\; (1 - p_{\min})^t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Take the Limit

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{t \to \infty} \Pr\{P(t) \notin S^{\star}\} \;&\leq\; \lim_{t \to \infty} (1 - p_{\min})^t \\
&=\; 0 \quad \text{(since } 0 < p_{\min} \leq 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} \Pr\{P(t) \in S^{\star}\} \;=\; 1$$

This establishes **convergence in probability** to the global optimum. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of (1+1)-ES Convergence on a Strictly Convex Function

### Step 1 — Algorithm Description
The (1+1)-ES has one parent $x_t$ and produces one offspring $\tilde{x}_{t+1} = x_t + \sigma_t \cdot \mathcal{N}(0, I_d)$. The best of the two survives.

### Step 2 — Drift Condition
Define the *drift* (expected improvement) at generation $t$:

$$D(x_t) \;=\; E[\|x_{t+1} - x^{\star}\|^2 \mid x_t] - \|x_t - x^{\star}\|^2$$

For a strictly convex function with Hessian lower-bounded by $m I$ (i.e., $f(y) \geq f(x) + \nabla f(x)^T(y-x) + (m/2)\|y-x\|^2$):

$$D(x_t) \;\leq\; -\frac{m}{2} \sigma_t^2 + c \cdot \sigma_t^4$$

where $c$ depends on the dimension $d$ and the function's Lipschitz curvature.

### Step 3 — Optimal Step-Size
Minimizing the upper bound on drift with respect to $\sigma_t$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma_t}\left(-\frac{m}{2}\sigma_t^2 + c\sigma_t^4\right) &= 0 \\
-m \sigma_t + 4 c \sigma_t^3 &= 0 \\
\sigma_t^2 &= \frac{m}{4c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back gives a strictly negative drift, i.e., the expected distance to the optimum *decreases* every generation:

$$D(x_t) \;\leq\; -\frac{m^2}{16c} \;<\; 0$$

### Step 4 — Almost-Sure Convergence
By the **martingale convergence theorem**, a non-negative supermartingale with negative drift converges to a finite limit almost surely. Applying this to $\|x_t - x^{\star}\|^2$:

$$\Pr\left\{\lim_{t \to \infty} \|x_t - x^{\star}\|^2 = 0\right\} \;=\; 1$$

Equivalently, $x_t \to x^{\star}$ almost surely. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The 1/5-success rule of Rechenberg (1973) is the *practical* heuristic for adapting $\sigma_t$ to maintain the negative-drift regime. The formal proof above guarantees convergence but does not give a time bound.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem

### Step 1 — Setup
Let $\mathcal{F}$ be the set of all functions $f : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ where $\mathcal{X}$ and $\mathcal{Y}$ are finite. Let $A$ and $B$ be two algorithms. Each algorithm generates a sequence of evaluated points $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_m$.

### Step 2 — Conditional Performance
The number of times algorithm $A$ visits value $y$ after $m$ evaluations on function $f$ is:

$$c_{A}(f, y, m) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathbb{1}\{f(x_i) = y\}$$

### Step 3 — Averaging Over All Functions
By the **combinatorial symmetry** of $\mathcal{F}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} c_A(f, y, m) \;&=\; \sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} c_B(f, y, m) \\
&=\; \frac{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert \cdot m}{\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* Every algorithm produces a sequence of *distinct* evaluations on unseen functions, and the *number* of such sequences does not depend on the algorithm — only on $m$ and $\vert \mathcal{Y} \vert$.

### Step 4 — Conclusion
Dividing both sides by $\vert \mathcal{F} \vert$:

$$\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert} \sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} c_A(f, y, m) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert} \sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} c_B(f, y, m)$$

This means the *expected* performance of $A$ and $B$ over the *uniform distribution* of all functions is identical. The same argument extends to *any* closed-under-permutation distribution on $\mathcal{F}$. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Caveat for KTU Exams:**
> NFL applies only when performance is *averaged uniformly* over the function class. Real engineering problems are drawn from *specific* subsets of $\mathcal{F}$ (e.g., continuous, differentiable, separable, convex), and on such subsets EAs demonstrably outperform random search.

---

## 3.5 Summary Comparison of the Four Convergence Proofs

| # | Proof | Algorithm | Tool Used | Result |
|---|-------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| 1 | Schema Theorem | Canonical GA | Counting & survival probabilities | Lower bound on $m(H,t)$ |
| 2 | Rudolph (1994) | Elitist GA | Finite Markov chain | Convergence in probability to $f^{\star}$ |
| 3 | (1+1)-ES | ES | Drift / martingale | Almost-sure convergence to $x^{\star}$ |
| 4 | NFL | Any | Combinatorics | Uniformly averaged performance is identical |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Convergence Proof

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Algorithm A] --> B[Identify State Space S]
    B --> C[Construct Markov Chain Model]
    C --> D{Is chain homogeneous?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Identify Absorbing States S*]
    D -- No --> F[Use Martingale / Drift Analysis]
    E --> G[Bound Transition Probability p_min]
    G --> H[Apply Geometric Decay Inequality]
    H --> I[Take Limit as t to infinity]
    F --> J[Compute Drift D x_t]
    J --> K[Verify Negative Drift Condition]
    K --> I
    I --> L[Conclude Convergence]
    L --> M[Convergence in probability]
    L --> N[Almost-sure convergence]
    L --> O[Lower-bound convergence rate]
```

## 4.2 Markov Chain State Transition for Elitist GA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Non-Optimal Population] -- p_min --> S1[Population with Global Optimum]
    S0 -- 1 minus p_min --> S0
    S1 -- probability 1 with elitism --> S1
    S0 -.->|selection crossover mutation| S0
    S1 -.->|preserved by elitism| S1
```

## 4.3 Schema Lifecycle Across Generations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Generation t: Population P of t] --> SEL[Fitness Proportionate Selection]
    SEL --> SEL1[m instances of schema H selected]
    SEL1 --> CXO[Single-Point Crossover]
    CXO --> CXO1[Survival factor 1 minus p_c times delta over l minus 1]
    CXO1 --> MUT[Bit-Flip Mutation]
    MUT --> MUT1[Survival factor 1 minus p_m raised to o H]
    MUT1 --> P1[Generation t plus 1: Updated Count m H t plus 1]
```

## 4.4 (1+1)-ES Convergence Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X0[Parent x sub t] --> MU[Mutate: add Gaussian noise N of 0 sigma squared]
    MU --> Y1[Offspring x tilde sub t plus 1]
    Y1 --> EVAL[Evaluate fitness f]
    EVAL --> SEL{Best of two wins}
    X0 --> SEL
    SEL -- offspring better --> X1[New parent x sub t plus 1]
    SEL -- parent better --> X1
    X1 --> CONV{Converged to x star}
    CONV -- No --> X0
    CONV -- Yes --> END[Stop: Almost-sure convergence achieved]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
