# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. State and explain Holland's Schema Theorem. Why is it expressed as a lower bound rather than an equality?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Holland's Schema Theorem gives a lower bound on the number of instances of a schema $H$ in generation $t+1$ as:

$$m(H, t+1) \;\geq\; m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}\right) \cdot (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$$

The inequality (not equality) arises because:
1. Crossover can *create* new instances of $H$ from two different parents that individually do not match $H$ — this constructive effect is not captured by the formula.
2. Mutation can also re-introduce fixed bits that were absent in the parent.
3. The "destruction" terms (those subtracted from 1) are over-estimated to give a *guaranteed lower bound*.

**Marking distribution:** 1 mark formula, 1 mark explanation, 1 mark reason for inequality.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. What is the No Free Lunch Theorem? State its significance in choosing an evolutionary algorithm for an engineering optimization problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wolpert's No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem (1995) states that the *average* performance of any two optimization algorithms $A$ and $B$, taken uniformly over the set of all possible objective functions $\mathcal{F}$, is identical:

$$\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert} \sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} c_A(f, y, m) \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{F} \vert} \sum_{f \in \mathcal{F}} c_B(f, y, m)$$

**Significance for engineering:** NFL applies only to the *uniform* average. Real engineering problems belong to *restricted* function classes (continuous, smooth, low-dimensional, convex or mildly non-convex). On such classes, EAs *with problem-specific encoding, mutation, and selection* can demonstrably outperform generic search. Hence NFL motivates the design of specialized EAs rather than universal ones.

**Marking distribution:** 1 mark statement, 1 mark equation, 1 mark engineering interpretation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — ESE Pattern, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

### Choice A (Option 1) — Schema Theorem and Building Blocks

**Q A(a)** [7 Marks] — Derive the Holland Schema Theorem from first principles. Clearly identify the role of selection, crossover, and mutation, and explain why the resulting expression is a *lower bound* rather than an exact count.

**Q A(b)** [7 Marks] — A binary GA uses $l = 20$, $p_c = 0.7$, $p_m = 0.01$. Consider two competing schemata:
- $H_1$: $\delta = 5$, $o = 3$, $f(H_1)/\bar{f} = 1.4$
- $H_2$: $\delta = 12$, $o = 8$, $f(H_2)/\bar{f} = 1.6$

Starting from $m(H_1, 0) = m(H_2, 0) = 10$, compute $m(H_1, 5)$ and $m(H_2, 5)$ using the Schema Theorem lower bound. Comment on which schema dominates and why.

#### Model Solution for Q A(a)

**Step 1 — Define population, fitness, schema:** (1 mark)
Let $P(t) = \{x_1, \dots, x_\mu\}$. Define $m(H, t)$, $f(H)$, $\bar{f}$ as in Section 3.1.

**Step 2 — Selection effect:** (2 marks)

$$E_s[m(H, t+1)] \;=\; m(H, t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}}$$

**Step 3 — Crossover disruption:** (2 marks)
A schema with defining length $\delta(H)$ survives single-point crossover with probability at least $1 - p_c \cdot \delta(H)/(l-1)$.

**Step 4 — Mutation disruption:** (1 mark)
Survival probability of fixed bits is $(1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$.

**Step 5 — Combined lower bound:** (1 mark)
$$m(H, t+1) \;\geq\; m(H,t) \cdot \frac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left(1 - p_c \frac{\delta(H)}{l-1}\right) \cdot (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$$

#### Model Solution for Q A(b)

**Step 1 — Compute survival factors for $H_1$:** (1 mark)

$$s_1 \;=\; \frac{f(H_1)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left(1 - 0.7 \cdot \frac{5}{19}\right) \cdot (1 - 0.01)^{3}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - 0.7 \cdot \frac{5}{19} \;&=\; 1 - 0.1842 \;=\; 0.8158 \\
(1 - 0.01)^3 \;&=\; 0.9703 \\
s_1 \;&=\; 1.4 \cdot 0.8158 \cdot 0.9703 \;=\; 1.1081
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute survival factors for $H_2$:** (1 mark)

$$s_2 \;=\; \frac{f(H_2)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left(1 - 0.7 \cdot \frac{12}{19}\right) \cdot (1 - 0.01)^{8}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 - 0.7 \cdot \frac{12}{19} \;&=\; 1 - 0.4421 \;=\; 0.5579 \\
(1 - 0.01)^8 \;&=\; 0.9227 \\
s_2 \;&=\; 1.6 \cdot 0.5579 \cdot 0.9227 \;=\; 0.8236
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply iteratively 5 times:** (3 marks)
Using $m(H, t+5) \geq m(H, 0) \cdot s^{5}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m(H_1, 5) \;&\geq\; 10 \cdot (1.1081)^5 \;=\; 10 \cdot 1.6729 \;=\; 16.73 \\
m(H_2, 5) \;&\geq\; 10 \cdot (0.8236)^5 \;=\; 10 \cdot 0.3793 \;=\; 3.79
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** (2 marks)
$H_1$ grows because, although its fitness ratio is smaller, its short defining length and low order give it a much higher survival factor. This is the **Building Block Hypothesis** in numerical form: short, low-order schemata dominate the search even when their fitness ratio is lower than that of long, high-order schemata.

---

### Choice B (Option 2) — Markov Chain Convergence of Elitist GA

**Q B(a)** [7 Marks] — Prove that an elitist canonical GA converges in probability to the global optimum. State clearly all assumptions used.

**Q B(b)** [7 Marks] — For an elitist GA with $p_{\min} = 0.02$, compute the probability that the algorithm has *not* found the global optimum after 50 generations. After how many generations is this failure probability below $10^{-6}$?

#### Model Solution for Q B(a)

**Step 1 — State-space assumption:** (1 mark)
Population of $\mu$ binary strings of length $l$ forms a finite state space $\mathcal{S} = \{0,1\}^{l \times \mu}$.

**Step 2 — Markov chain identification:** (1 mark)
The sequence $\{P(t)\}_{t \geq 0}$ is a homogeneous finite Markov chain (next state depends only on current state; operators are time-invariant).

**Step 3 — Absorbing states:** (1 mark)
$S^{\star} = \{P : P \cap S^{\star}_x \neq \emptyset\}$ where $S^{\star}_x = \{x : f(x) = f^{\star}\}$. Elitism guarantees $P \in S^{\star} \Rightarrow P' \in S^{\star}$.

**Step 4 — Positive reachability:** (2 marks)
Show $p_{\min} = \min_{P \notin S^{\star}} \Pr\{P(t+1) \in S^{\star} \mid P(t) = P\} > 0$ using the fact that selection, crossover, and mutation each have positive probability of producing an optimal string from any non-optimal population.

**Step 5 — Geometric decay & limit:** (2 marks)

$$\Pr\{P(t) \notin S^{\star}\} \;\leq\; (1 - p_{\min})^t \;\to\; 0 \;\text{ as }\; t \to \infty$$

Therefore $\lim_{t \to \infty} \Pr\{P(t) \in S^{\star}\} = 1$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for Q B(b)

**Step 1 — Apply the geometric bound:** (2 marks)

$$\Pr\{F_{\mathrm{best}}(50) \neq f^{\star}\} \;\leq\; (1 - 0.02)^{50}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical evaluation:** (2 marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.98)^{50} \;&=\; e^{50 \ln(0.98)} \;=\; e^{50 \cdot (-0.0202)} \;=\; e^{-1.010} \\
&\approx\; 0.3641
\end{aligned}
$$

So the failure probability is approximately **36.4 %** after 50 generations.

**Step 3 — Solve for the required $t$:** (2 marks)
We require $(1 - 0.02)^t < 10^{-6}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.98)^t \;&<\; 10^{-6} \\
t \cdot \ln(0.98) \;&<\; \ln(10^{-6}) \\
t \;&>\; \frac{-13.8155}{-0.0202} \;=\; 683.9
\end{aligned}
$$

So the algorithm needs **at least 684 generations** to guarantee failure probability below $10^{-6}$. (1 mark for the final interpretation.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "convergence" with "termination":** Convergence is a *property of the infinite sequence*; termination is a *finite stopping rule*. Do not use the terms interchangeably in answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the elitism hypothesis:** Rudolph's proof *requires* elitism. A non-elitist canonical GA is *not* guaranteed to converge to the global optimum — students often lose 2 marks by missing this.
> 3. **Mis-stating Schema Theorem as an equality:** The formula is a *lower bound*. Writing $=$ instead of $\geq$ is a 1-mark deduction in most valuation keys.
> 4. **Mixing up "in probability" and "almost surely":** These are *different* notions. Convergence in probability does NOT imply almost-sure convergence. State which type your proof establishes.
> 5. **Misapplying NFL:** The NFL theorem says nothing about performance on a *single* function or a *restricted* function class. Do not conclude "evolutionary algorithms are useless" from NFL.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Convergence in EC** = asymptotic discovery of the global optimum as $t \to \infty$.
- [ ] **Convergence in probability:** $\lim_{t\to\infty} \Pr(F_t = f^{\star}) = 1$.
- [ ] **Almost-sure convergence:** $\Pr(\lim_{t\to\infty} F_t = f^{\star}) = 1$.
- [ ] **Holland's Schema Theorem** gives a *lower bound* on the count of schema instances: $m(H, t+1) \geq m(H,t) \cdot (f(H)/\bar{f}) \cdot (1 - p_c \delta(H)/(l-1)) \cdot (1 - p_m)^{o(H)}$.
- [ ] **Building Blocks** = short, low-order, above-average-fitness schemata that grow exponentially.
- [ ] **Rudolph's 1994 result:** Elitist GA converges in probability; non-elitist canonical GA does *not*.
- [ ] **Geometric decay:** Failure probability $\leq (1 - p_{\min})^t$, hence reaches $10^{-6}$ in $O(\log(1/\epsilon)/p_{\min})$ generations.
- [ ] **(1+1)-ES convergence:** Negative drift on strictly convex $f$ with appropriately tuned $\sigma_t$; almost-sure convergence.
- [ ] **Rechenberg's 1/5 rule:** Adapt $\sigma_t$ so that *about 1/5* of mutations are successful.
- [ ] **No Free Lunch Theorem:** Averaged over *all* functions, no algorithm is better than random search; on *restricted* function classes, EAs can dominate.
- [ ] **Markov chain tool:** Population of $\mu$ binary strings of length $l$ forms a finite state space; elitist GA's optimal-state set is *absorbing*.
- [ ] **Drift analysis:** $\mathbb{E}[\|x_{t+1} - x^{\star}\|^2 \mid x_t] - \|x_t - x^{\star}\|^2 < 0 \Rightarrow$ convergence.
- [ ] **Schema survival factors** are multiplicative across operators (independence assumption).
- [ ] **Mutation disrupts** schemata in proportion to *order* $o(H)$.
- [ ] **Crossover disrupts** schemata in proportion to *defining length* $\delta(H)$.
- [ ] **NFL ≠ "EAs don't work":** NFL is a meta-theorem about averages; engineering design uses restricted problem classes.
- [ ] **Convergence proofs ≠ convergence rates:** A proof of "eventually converges" does not say "converges fast." Rate analysis uses different tools (e.g., drift bounds, hitting-time theorems).
- [ ] **EPT/EP convergence:** Fogel's evolutionary programming converges almost surely to the global optimum on bounded, continuous functions with positive-mutation support.
- [ ] **Stagnation ≠ non-convergence:** A population may stagnate for many generations and still find the optimum eventually (geometric tail).
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** when writing a convergence proof: binary/continuous, elitist/non-elitist, finite/infinite population, convex/non-convex.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ACO Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** ACO Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based metaheuristic probabilistic technique used for solving complex combinatorial optimization problems. It is a part of the **Swarm Intelligence** family of algorithms, inspired by the foraging behavior of real ant colonies. Formally introduced by **Marco Dorigo** in his 1992 Ph.D. thesis, ACO exploits the indirect communication mechanism among ants mediated by **pheromone trails** (a phenomenon called **stigmergy**) to discover high-quality solution paths through a search space.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, ACO is positioned as a **graph-based search algorithm** where artificial ants iteratively construct candidate solutions by traversing a fully connected weighted construction graph, depositing synthetic pheromones proportional to solution quality, and probabilistically biasing future searches toward regions of the search space that have previously yielded good solutions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECNT869 – Module 3):**
> ACO belongs to the broader umbrella of *Nature-Inspired Metaheuristics* alongside Genetic Algorithms, Particle Swarm Optimization, and Differential Evolution. The examiner frequently tests the relationship between **stigmergy**, **pheromone evaporation**, and the **exploration vs. exploitation** balance.

## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy: How Real Ants Inspire an Algorithm

Imagine you walk into a kitchen and find a trail of tiny black ants marching in a perfect single-file line from a tiny crack in the wall, across the counter, to a sugar bowl. They are following an invisible highway. But how did this highway form?

Let us trace back to the beginning. When the very first scout ant discovers the sugar, it has no idea how to get back. It wanders aimlessly. As it walks, it leaves a tiny chemical signal called a **pheromone** on the ground. Now imagine a second ant reaches the same junction where ant #1 took a left turn. Ant #2 smells the pheromone and is slightly more likely to follow the same path, also adding its own pheromone. A third ant arrives, smells an even stronger pheromone trail on the left path, and is *even more* likely to follow it.

**Key Insight:** The shorter the path, the faster ants complete a round trip, and the *faster* pheromone accumulates on the short path. Pheromone on the longer, slower path *evaporates* before it can build up. This positive feedback loop effectively reinforces the optimal (shortest) path — without any ant ever having a "map" or "knowing" it is the shortest.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stigmergy** = communication *through the environment* (rather than direct ant-to-ant contact). In algorithm terms, the pheromone matrix $\tau_{ij}$ acts as a **distributed, modifiable, shared memory** accessible to all artificial ants.

## 1.3 The Artificial Ant Translation

| Real Ant Behavior | Artificial Ant Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Walks along ground | Traverses a **construction graph** (nodes = cities, edges = decisions) |
| Deposits pheromone | Updates the **pheromone matrix** $\tau_{ij}$ |
| Pheromone evaporates | Multiplies trail by **$(1-\rho)$** decay factor |
| Chooses shorter path more often | Uses **probabilistic transition rule** biased by $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ |
| Multiple ants explore in parallel | Population of $m$ artificial ants construct solutions simultaneously |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Architecture — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The ACO algorithm operates in a cyclical pipeline. Below is a structured breakdown of the core operational logic:

### Step 1 — Initialization
- **What:** Set up pheromone matrix $\tau_{ij}$ to a small initial value $\tau_0$ for all edges $(i,j)$ in the graph.
- **Why:** Ensures no edge is unfairly favored at the start. Initializing uniformly allows *uniform random exploration* during the first iteration, which is critical for **exploration** in early phases.

### Step 2 — Solution Construction
- **What:** Each of the $m$ artificial ants independently builds a solution by sequentially selecting the next node to visit.
- **How:** At each decision point, ant $k$ at node $i$ selects the next node $j$ using a **probabilistic transition rule**.
- **Why:** Probabilistic (rather than greedy) selection preserves **diversity** and avoids premature convergence.

### Step 3 — Pheromone Evaporation
- **What:** Before new pheromone is deposited, all existing trails decay by a factor $(1-\rho)$.
- **Why:** Prevents pheromone accumulation from trapping the algorithm in **stagnation** (suboptimal convergence). Evaporation is the *forgetting mechanism* that permits the algorithm to escape local optima.

### Step 4 — Pheromone Deposition (Global Update)
- **What:** Ants (or only the best-so-far ant, depending on the variant) deposit additional pheromone on the edges they traversed.
- **Why:** Positive feedback reinforcement of high-quality solutions. This is the **exploitation** mechanism.

### Step 5 — Termination Check
- **What:** Stop when a maximum iteration count is reached, or when all ants follow the same path (stagnation behavior detected).

## 2.2 The Core Mathematical Engine

The ACO framework is governed by **three principal equations**. Mastery of these is non-negotiable for the KTU ESE.

### Equation 1 — The Transition (Decision) Rule

$$P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}, \quad \text{if } j \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}$$

Where:
- $P_{ij}^{k}(t)$ = probability that ant $k$ moves from node $i$ to node $j$ at iteration $t$
- $\tau_{ij}(t)$ = pheromone trail intensity on edge $(i,j)$ at iteration $t$
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ = **heuristic information** (visibility), inversely proportional to edge cost $d_{ij}$
- $\alpha$ = **pheromone influence exponent** (controls exploitation of learned information)
- $\beta$ = **heuristic influence exponent** (controls greedy/descent behavior)
- $\mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}$ = set of feasible/unvisited nodes for ant $k$ currently at node $i$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tuning Intuition:** $\alpha = 0$ → pure heuristic search (greedy nearest neighbor). $\beta = 0$ → pure pheromone-driven search (may stagnate). The KTU gold standard for balanced search is $\alpha \approx 1$, $\beta \in [2, 5]$.

### Equation 2 — Pheromone Evaporation + Global Update (Ant System variant)

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}(t)$$

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

### Equation 3 — Pheromone Deposit per Ant

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_{k}}, & \text{if ant } k \text{ used edge } (i,j) \text{ in its tour} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- $\rho \in (0,1)$ = **pheromone evaporation coefficient** (e.g., $\rho = 0.5$ means 50% of trail evaporates per cycle)
- $Q$ = constant controlling total pheromone deposited (problem-dependent)
- $L_k$ = total tour length of ant $k$ (shorter tour → larger deposit → stronger reinforcement)
- $m$ = number of ants in colony

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Range | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_{ij}(t)$ | Pheromone level on edge $(i,j)$ at time $t$ | Initial: $\tau_0 \approx 1/(n \cdot L_{nn})$ | Distributed long-term memory of past solutions |
| $\eta_{ij}$ | Heuristic visibility $= 1/d_{ij}$ | Static, precomputed | Encodes the *greedy* problem knowledge |
| $P_{ij}^{k}$ | Transition probability for ant $k$ | Sum over $j$ equals 1 | Stochastic next-move selector |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone weight | $\alpha \geq 0$ | Higher $\alpha \to$ more exploitation of trail |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic weight | $\beta \geq 0$ | Higher $\beta \to$ more greedy/short-sighted |
| $\rho$ | Evaporation rate | $0 < \rho < 1$ (often 0.1 to 0.5) | Higher $\rho \to$ faster forgetting of old info |
| $Q$ | Pheromone deposit constant | Problem-tuned (often $Q = 1$ or 100) | Global scaling of trail intensity |
| $L_k$ | Tour cost of ant $k$ | $L_k > 0$ | Fitness inversely proportional to deposit |
| $m$ | Number of ants | Typically $m = n$ (number of cities) | Population diversity parameter |
| $t_{max}$ | Maximum iterations | 100 to 5000 | Computational budget / stopping criterion |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

ACO has demonstrated industrial-grade success in:

- **Telecommunications:** Adaptive routing in packet-switched networks (AntNet).
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), last-mile delivery.
- **VLSI Design:** Wire routing on printed circuit boards.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein folding, DNA sequencing assembly.
- **Robotics:** Path planning in dynamic, partially known environments.
- **Power Systems:** Economic dispatch, unit commitment optimization.
- **Scheduling:** Job-shop and flow-shop scheduling in manufacturing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative Analysis: How the Transition Rule Emerges from Probability Logic

**Q: Where does the transition probability formula come from?**

The transition rule is a *weighted random sampling* rule, derived from the principle of combining two independent desirability signals:

1. **Pheromone trail** $\tau_{ij}$ — represents *learned* experience from the entire colony.
2. **Heuristic visibility** $\eta_{ij}$ — represents *a priori* knowledge of edge cost.

A rational decision-maker multiplies the two desirabilities. The power exponents $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are scaling weights to allow fine-tuned control. The denominator is a normalization constant so that $\sum_j P_{ij}^k = 1$ (a valid probability distribution).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Why is the transition rule raised to powers $\alpha$ and $\beta$ instead of being linear?"* The answer: *exponents amplify the bias — they make the algorithm more decisive (sharper probability distribution) when $\alpha, \beta$ are large, which speeds up convergence but risks premature convergence.*

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Variants of ACO

The original **Ant System (AS)** has spawned several improved variants. The KTU 2024 syllabus requires familiarity with at least the first three.

| Variant | Pheromone Update Strategy | Pheromone Bounds | Local Update? | Year | Key Innovation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ant System (AS)** | All ants deposit equally | None | No | 1991 | Original foundational algorithm |
| **Elitist AS (EAS)** | All ants + elite best-so-far (with weight $e$) | None | No | 1992 | Extra reinforcement of best tour |
| **Max-Min AS (MMAS)** | Only best-so-far or iteration-best deposits | $\tau_{min} \leq \tau_{ij} \leq \tau_{max}$ | No | 1996 | Bounded trails prevent stagnation |
| **Ant Colony System (ACS)** | Only global-best deposits; local update during construction | None | **Yes** | 1997 | Diversification via local decay |
| **Rank-based AS (AS$_{\text{rank}}$)** | Ants ranked; top-$w$ ants deposit weighted by rank | None | No | 1997 | Quality-proportional deposits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MMAS Mnemonic:** "Max-Min" = trails are clamped to a *maximum* (preventing one path from dominating) and a *minimum* (ensuring every edge retains some exploration chance). This is why MMAS is considered the most robust AS variant.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Solving a 4-City TSP with ACO

This is the **most important worked example** for KTU preparation. Walk through it step-by-step.

### Problem Setup
Consider 4 cities $\{A, B, C, D\}$ with the following symmetric distance matrix $d_{ij}$ (in km):

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\
\hline
A & 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\
B & 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\
C & 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\
D & 20 & 25 & 30 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Algorithm Parameters:** $m = 2$ ants (Ant 1 starts at A, Ant 2 starts at C), $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$, $\rho = 0.1$, $Q = 100$, initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 0.1$ on all edges.

### Step A — Compute Heuristic Matrix

The heuristic visibility $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & A & B & C & D \\
\hline
A & - & 0.100 & 0.0667 & 0.050 \\
B & 0.100 & - & 0.0286 & 0.040 \\
C & 0.0667 & 0.0286 & - & 0.0333 \\
D & 0.050 & 0.040 & 0.0333 & -
\end{array}
$$

### Step B — Construct Solutions (Iteration $t = 0$)

**Ant 1 (starts at A), with $\alpha = 1, \beta = 2$:**

The composite desirability for each candidate city from A is $\tau_{ij}^\alpha \cdot \eta_{ij}^\beta = 0.1^1 \cdot \eta_{ij}^2$:

- To B: $0.1 \times (0.100)^2 = 0.1 \times 0.01 = 0.00100$
- To C: $0.1 \times (0.0667)^2 = 0.1 \times 0.00445 = 0.000445$
- To D: $0.1 \times (0.050)^2 = 0.1 \times 0.0025 = 0.000250$

**Sum of desirabilities:** $S = 0.00100 + 0.000445 + 0.000250 = 0.001695$

**Probabilities:**

$$
P_{AB}^{1} = \frac{0.00100}{0.001695} \approx 0.590
$$

$$
P_{AC}^{1} = \frac{0.000445}{0.001695} \approx 0.263
$$

$$
P_{AD}^{1} = \frac{0.000250}{0.001695} \approx 0.147
$$

Suppose Ant 1 randomly selects **B** (highest probability). Visited set: $\{A, B\}$.

**Next decision (from B to unvisited {C, D}):**

- To C: $0.1 \times (0.0286)^2 = 0.1 \times 0.000818 = 0.0000818$
- To D: $0.1 \times (0.040)^2 = 0.1 \times 0.00160 = 0.000160$

Sum: $0.0000818 + 0.000160 = 0.000242$

$$P_{BC}^{1} = \frac{0.0000818}{0.000242} \approx 0.338, \quad P_{BD}^{1} = \frac{0.000160}{0.000242} \approx 0.662$$

Suppose Ant 1 selects **D**. Then last city must be **C** (forced). Path: A → B → D → C → A. Length:

$$L_1 = d_{AB} + d_{BD} + d_{DC} + d_{CA} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80$$

**Ant 2 (starts at C):** By similar calculation, suppose its path turns out to be C → A → B → D → C with length:

$$L_2 = d_{CA} + d_{AB} + d_{BD} + d_{DC} = 15 + 10 + 25 + 30 = 80$$

(For this example, both ants find tours of length 80. Ant 2 is selected arbitrarily as the "best" in case of tie.)

### Step C — Pheromone Evaporation

For all edges: $\tau_{ij}^{\text{evap}} = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij} = 0.9 \times 0.1 = 0.09$

### Step D — Pheromone Deposition (Ant System: all ants deposit)

Deposit by Ant 1 on edge $(i,j)$: $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{1} = Q/L_1 = 100/80 = 1.25$ on edges $AB, BD, DC, CA$.

Deposit by Ant 2 on edge $(i,j)$: $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{2} = 100/80 = 1.25$ on edges $CA, AB, BD, DC$.

(Note: the same edges are reinforced by both ants!)

### Step E — Updated Pheromone for Iteration 1

For edge $AB$ (traversed by both ants): $\tau_{AB}(1) = 0.9 \times 0.1 + 1.25 + 1.25 = 0.09 + 2.50 = 2.59$

For edge $AC$ (traversed by only Ant 2): $\tau_{AC}(1) = 0.9 \times 0.1 + 1.25 = 0.09 + 1.25 = 1.34$

For edge $AD$ (traversed by neither): $\tau_{AD}(1) = 0.9 \times 0.1 + 0 = 0.09$

The pheromone differential **reinforces the better-explored edges**, biasing future ants toward them — this is the positive feedback in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this question high-yield for KTU?** Examiners often give a small numerical problem like this (4-5 cities) and ask students to compute one iteration of ACO step-by-step. Mastery of *every arithmetic transition* above is essential.

## 3.4 The Exploration vs. Exploitation Trade-off — A Key Conceptual Derivative

The ACO algorithm inherently balances two competing objectives:

- **Exploitation:** Intensify search around the best-so-far solution (high $\alpha$ reinforces known trails).
- **Exploration:** Discover new regions of the search space (high $\rho$ evaporates old info; probabilistic rule allows detours).

The expected behavior over time $t$ can be loosely characterized:

$$
\text{Solution Diversity}(t) \propto \frac{1}{\alpha \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)} \quad \text{(diversity shrinks as trails grow)}
$$

$$
\text{Convergence Speed} \propto \frac{\beta}{\rho} \quad \text{(higher } \beta/\rho \text{ → faster but riskier convergence)}
$$

This is why parameter tuning ($\alpha, \beta, \rho$) is *not arbitrary* — it directly governs algorithm dynamics.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ACO Meta-Flowchart (Algorithm Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start ACO Algorithm] --> B[Initialize Pheromone Matrix tau with tau0]
    B --> C[Set Iteration Counter t = 0]
    C --> D[Place m Ants on Random Starting Nodes]
    D --> E[Each Ant Constructs Solution via Transition Rule]
    E --> F{All Ants Complete Tour?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute Tour Length Lk for each Ant]
    G --> H[Identify Best Ant and Best So Far Tour]
    H --> I[Apply Pheromone Evaporation tau = 1-rho times tau]
    I --> J[Apply Pheromone Deposition by Best Ants]
    J --> K[Update Pheromone Matrix tau t+1]
    K --> L{Termination Criterion Met?}
    L -->|No| M[Increment t = t + 1] --> E
    L -->|Yes| N[Return Best Solution Found]
    N --> O[End]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#ca8a04,color:#000000
    style L fill:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Pheromone Update Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Current Pheromone tau_ij of t] --> E[Evaporation Block 1 minus rho]
    E --> M[Multiply tau_ij with 1 minus rho]
    M --> S[Add Evaporated Trail to New Deposit]
    D[Pheromone Deposit Delta tau_ij = Q over Lk] --> S
    S --> U[Updated Pheromone tau_ij of t+1]
    U --> P2[Next Iteration Input]
    P2 --> P

    A1[Ant 1 Tour] --> D
    A2[Ant 2 Tour] --> D
    Am[Ant m Tour] --> D

    style P fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style S fill:#92400e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#991b1b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Transition Rule Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Ant k at node i] --> Visit[Get Set of Unvisited Neighbors N_k of i]
    Visit --> Loop[For each candidate node j in N_k]
    Loop --> Comp[Compute Numerator tau_ij to alpha times eta_ij to beta]
    Comp --> Sum[Sum All Numerators to get Denominator]
    Sum --> Prob[Compute Probability P_ij_k for each j]
    Prob --> Roll[Generate Random Number r in 0,1]
    Roll --> Select[Select node j where cumulative P exceeds r]
    Select --> Move[Move Ant k to node j]
    Move --> Check{All Nodes Visited?}
    Check -->|No| Visit
    Check -->|Yes| Done[Return to Nest and Compute Tour Length]

    style Start fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff
    style Done fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style Prob fill:#7e22ce,color:#ffffff
    style Select fill:#ca8a04,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — How Pheromone Memory Drives Learning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ExplorePhase[Exploration Phase Early Iterations]
        E1[High Pheromone Evaporation rho] --> E2[Low Trail Differentiation]
        E2 --> E3[Ants Explore Diverse Paths]
    end

    subgraph ExploitPhase[Exploitation Phase Later Iterations]
        X1[Low Evaporation or Bounded Trails MMAS] --> X2[Strong Trail Differentiation]
        X2 --> X3[Ants Converge Toward Best Path]
    end

    E3 --> X1
    X3 --> F[Final Solution Converges to High Quality Tour]

    style ExplorePhase fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style ExploitPhase fill:#dcfce7,color:#000000
    style F fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Ant Colony Optimization. What is stigmergy, and how does it enable indirect communication in ACO?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) is a population-based metaheuristic algorithm inspired by the foraging behavior of real ants, used to solve combinatorial optimization problems. It was proposed by Marco Dorigo in 1992.

**Stigmergy** is a form of indirect communication mediated by modifications to the environment. In ACO, artificial ants communicate by depositing a chemical substance called **pheromone** on the edges of a construction graph. Other ants sense the pheromone concentration and probabilistically bias their next-move decisions toward edges with higher pheromone levels. This produces a *positive feedback loop* in which high-quality solutions receive more pheromone and attract more ants in subsequent iterations, without any direct ant-to-ant interaction.
**(1 mark each for: definition of ACO, definition of stigmergy, explanation of indirect communication via pheromone).**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in the ACO transition rule. What happens when $\beta = 0$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In the transition probability formula $P_{ij}^{k} = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$:
- **$\alpha$ (pheromone influence):** Controls the relative importance of learned information from previous ants. Higher $\alpha$ makes the algorithm more *exploitative*, reinforcing well-traveled edges.
- **$\beta$ (heuristic influence):** Controls the greedy/short-sight bias. Higher $\beta$ makes the algorithm prefer edges with low cost (high $\eta_{ij}$), behaving more like a nearest-neighbor greedy heuristic.

**When $\beta = 0$:** The heuristic information $\eta_{ij}$ is completely ignored. The transition rule collapses to a probability proportional to pheromone only: $P_{ij}^{k} \propto [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}$. This is dangerous because initially all trails are equal, so the first few ants effectively choose *randomly*, and once a path gets even a tiny pheromone advantage, it rapidly monopolizes the search — leading to **premature convergence and stagnation**.
**(1 mark for role of $\alpha$, 1 mark for role of $\beta$, 1 mark for consequence of $\beta = 0$).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe the Ant Colony Optimization algorithm in detail. Explain the transition rule and the pheromone update rule with their mathematical formulations. Discuss the role of the evaporation parameter $\rho$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the three ACO variants: Ant System (AS), Ant Colony System (ACS), and Max-Min Ant System (MMAS). Highlight the key innovation in each. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Detailed ACO Description (7 marks)

**1. Algorithm Overview (2 marks):**
ACO is an iterative, population-based metaheuristic for combinatorial optimization. A colony of $m$ artificial ants constructs solutions in parallel. Solutions are represented as paths in a construction graph $G = (V, E)$. Each edge $(i,j)$ has a pheromone value $\tau_{ij}$ and a heuristic value $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$. Ants construct paths probabilistically, deposit pheromone proportional to solution quality, and pheromone evaporates over time.

**2. Transition Rule (2 marks):**
When ant $k$ is at node $i$, the probability of moving to node $j$ is:

$$P_{ij}^{k}(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$$

The denominator is the sum over all feasible (unvisited) neighbors, ensuring $P_{ij}^{k}$ is a valid probability.

**3. Pheromone Update Rule (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

where $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_{k}$ if ant $k$ traversed edge $(i,j)$, else 0.

**4. Role of Evaporation $\rho$ (1 mark):**
$\rho \in (0,1)$ controls the *forgetting* of old information. Higher $\rho$ → faster evaporation → more exploration but slower convergence. Lower $\rho$ → slower forgetting → faster convergence but risk of stagnation. The proper balance is essential for avoiding premature convergence to local optima.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparison of AS, ACS, MMAS (7 marks)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Ant System (AS) | Ant Colony System (ACS) | Max-Min Ant System (MMAS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pheromone deposit | All ants deposit | Only global-best deposits | Only iteration-best OR global-best |
| Pheromone bounds | Unbounded (can diverge) | Unbounded, but local update during construction caps growth | $\tau_{min} \leq \tau_{ij} \leq \tau_{max}$ (bounded) |
| Local update | None | Yes, during construction: $\tau_{ij} = (1-\xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi\tau_0$ | None |
| Exploitation level | Lowest (democratic) | Highest (focused) | Medium (regulated) |
| Stagnation risk | High | Medium | Lowest (bounded trails) |
| Convergence speed | Slow | Fast | Medium |

**Key Innovations (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **AS (1991):** The original foundational ACO. Established the template of probabilistic construction + pheromone reinforcement. Its simplicity makes it the pedagogical baseline but suffers from slow convergence.

2. **ACS (1997):** Introduced the *local pheromone update* during solution construction, which reduces pheromone on edges as they are traversed. This produces a *diversification pressure* — by the time later ants reach an edge, its pheromone has decayed, making them less likely to follow the exact same path. Result: ants explore different routes even when several ants start similarly.

3. **MMAS (1996):** Introduced **explicit bounds** $[\tau_{min}, \tau_{max}]$ on pheromone values. The bounds are dynamically updated based on the best-known solution. The $\tau_{max}$ upper bound prevents a single trail from dominating; the $\tau_{min}$ lower bound guarantees every edge retains some minimum exploration probability. This is the most robust variant in practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
**(a)** Consider a TSP with 4 cities A, B, C, D having the following distance matrix. With $\alpha = 1, \beta = 2, \rho = 0.1, Q = 100$, and initial pheromone $\tau_0 = 0.1$ on all edges, perform **one complete iteration** of ACO with 2 ants (Ant 1 at city A, Ant 2 at city C) and show the updated pheromone matrix. Distance matrix:

$$D = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major applications of ACO in engineering. Explain why ACO is particularly suited for the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Numerical ACO Iteration (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Heuristic Matrix $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ (1 mark):**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 0.1000 | 0.0667 | 0.0500 |
| B | 0.1000 | — | 0.0286 | 0.0400 |
| C | 0.0667 | 0.0286 | — | 0.0333 |
| D | 0.0500 | 0.0400 | 0.0333 | — |

**Step 2 — Ant 1 constructs tour from A (2 marks):**

- At A, desirabilities: $AB: 0.1 \times 0.01 = 0.00100$, $AC: 0.1 \times 0.00445 = 0.000445$, $AD: 0.1 \times 0.0025 = 0.000250$.
- Sum $S = 0.001695$. Probabilities: $P_{AB} = 0.590$, $P_{AC} = 0.263$, $P_{AD} = 0.147$.
- Ant 1 selects B (highest probability). Tour continues: Ant 1 picks D next (analogous computation yields $P_{BD} > P_{BC}$), then forced to C, then back to A.
- **Tour 1:** A → B → D → C → A, $L_1 = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = 80$.

**Step 3 — Ant 2 constructs tour from C (1 mark):**
By symmetric reasoning, assume Ant 2 builds tour **C → A → B → D → C** with $L_2 = 15 + 10 + 25 + 30 = 80$.

**Step 4 — Pheromone Evaporation (1 mark):**
$\tau_{ij}^{\text{evap}} = (1 - 0.1) \times 0.1 = 0.09$ for all edges.

**Step 5 — Pheromone Deposition (1 mark):**
$\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = 100/80 = 1.25$ for each edge used by an ant.

- Edge $AB$: used by both ants → deposit $1.25 + 1.25 = 2.50$
- Edge $BD$: used by both → deposit $2.50$
- Edge $DC$: used by both → deposit $2.50$
- Edge $CA$: used by both → deposit $2.50$
- Edge $AC$: used by 0 ants → deposit $0$
- Edge $AD$: used by 0 ants → deposit $0$

**Step 6 — Updated Pheromone Matrix (1 mark):**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | — | 0.09 + 2.50 = **2.59** | 0.09 + 0 = **0.09** | 0.09 + 0 = **0.09** |
| B | **2.59** | — | 0.09 + 0 = **0.09** | 0.09 + 2.50 = **2.59** |
| C | 0.09 + 2.50 = **2.59** | 0.09 + 0 = **0.09** | — | 0.09 + 2.50 = **2.59** |
| D | 0.09 + 0 = **0.09** | **2.59** | **2.59** | — |

The pheromone values on the four edges $AB, BD, DC, CA$ have grown to **2.59**, while unused edges remain at the evaporated level **0.09**. Future ants will now be strongly biased toward the discovered tour.

---

#### Part (b) — Applications and TSP Suitability (7 marks)

**Major Applications (4 marks — 1.5 each pair):**

1. **Network Routing (AntNet, ABC):** ACO is used in telecom and ad-hoc networks for adaptive packet routing. The dynamic pheromone update is naturally suited to fluctuating network topologies and congestion patterns.

2. **Vehicle Routing and Logistics:** Capacitated VRP, time-windowed VRP, multi-depot VRP. Companies like **Amazon** and **FedEx** use ACO-inspired heuristics for last-mile delivery optimization.

3. **Job-Shop Scheduling:** Optimal sequence of jobs on machines in manufacturing.

4. **Bioinformatics and Robotics:** Protein structure prediction, robot path planning in unknown terrain.

**Why ACO is Particularly Suited for TSP (3 marks):**

1. **Graph Structure Match:** TSP is naturally a *graph traversal* problem with cities as nodes and edges as possible transitions. ACO's construction graph maps perfectly onto TSP without modification.

2. **Symmetry and Constraint Handling:** TSP's "visit each city exactly once" constraint is handled by the *tabu list* (memory of visited nodes) that each artificial ant maintains. This is a clean, well-defined restriction.

3. **Iterative Improvement via Stigmergy:** The cumulative pheromone build-up mechanism in ACO mirrors the "good solution attracts more ants" intuition of real-world route discovery. For TSP, this has produced solutions within 1–3% of optimal for benchmarks like TSPLIB with thousands of cities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — ACO Numericals**
> 1. **Forgetting to square $\eta_{ij}$ when $\beta = 2$:** Many students compute $\tau_{ij} \cdot \eta_{ij}$ instead of $\tau_{ij}^{\alpha} \cdot \eta_{ij}^{\beta}$. This is the **#1 mark-loss** in numerical questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the denominator sum:** Students often compute only the numerator and forget to normalize. The sum must include *all unvisited candidates*, not just two.
> 3. **Confusing $L_k$ with $1/L_k$:** Some students invert $L_k$ in the deposit formula. The correct form is $Q/L_k$ — shorter tours deposit *more* pheromone.
> 4. **Mixing local and global updates:** In ACS, the local update uses a *different* formula (with $\tau_0$) than the global update (with $L_{\text{best}}$). Conflating them loses 1-2 marks.
> 5. **Not specifying the visited set:** The transition rule's denominator is over $\mathcal{N}_i^k$, the unvisited neighbors. Forgetting this constraint and summing over all neighbors is a subtle but penalized mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — ACO Algorithm Cheat Sheet**

- [x] **Origin:** Marco Dorigo, 1991-1992, Ph.D. thesis.
- [x] **Inspiration:** Real ant foraging via pheromone-mediated stigmergy.
- [x] **Class:** Population-based, graph-based, metaheuristic, probabilistic.
- [x] **Core Equations to Memorize:** Transition rule $P_{ij}^k$, pheromone update $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum \Delta\tau_{ij}^k$, deposit $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q/L_k$.
- [x] **Key Parameters:** $\alpha$ (pheromone weight), $\beta$ (heuristic weight), $\rho$ (evaporation), $Q$ (deposit constant), $m$ (number of ants).
- [x] **Tuning Rules:** $\alpha \in [1,2]$, $\beta \in [2,5]$, $\rho \in [0.1, 0.5]$ are typical. $\alpha = 0$ → greedy. $\beta = 0$ → stagnation.
- [x] **Five ACO Variants:** AS, EAS, MMAS, ACS, Rank-based AS. **MMAS is the most robust** (bounded trails).
- [x] **ACS Innovation:** Local pheromone update during construction (uses $\tau_0$).
- [x] **MMAS Innovation:** Bounded trails $\tau_{min} \leq \tau_{ij} \leq \tau_{max}$ prevent stagnation.
- [x] **Exploration vs. Exploitation:** Evaporation = exploration. Pheromone deposit = exploitation. $\alpha, \beta$ control the balance.
- [x] **Primary Application:** Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) — natural graph match.
- [x] **Industrial Applications:** Network routing (AntNet), vehicle routing, scheduling, VLSI routing, robotics.
- [x] **Stopping Criteria:** Max iterations, stagnation detection, or computational time budget.
- [x] **Advantage:** Positive feedback, distributed computation, easy to parallelize.
- [x] **Disadvantage:** Slow convergence in early iterations; sensitive to parameter tuning; can stagnate without bounds (use MMAS to mitigate).
- [x] **Numerical Tip:** Always compute $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ first, raise to the power $\beta$, multiply by $\tau_{ij}^{\alpha}$, then normalize over all unvisited candidates.
- [x] **Examiner's trap:** Do not confuse the *evaporation factor* $(1-\rho)$ with the *deposit amount* $\Delta\tau_{ij}$. The full update is BOTH applied together in a single equation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) is a population-based metaheuristic belonging to the class of Swarm Intelligence algorithms, introduced by Marco Dorigo in his PhD thesis (1992). It is used to solve combinatorial optimization problems by simulating the behavior of real ants searching for food, where artificial ants probabilistically construct solutions using a parameterized probabilistic model — the pheromone model — which is updated based on the quality of the previously generated solutions.*

In simple words, ACO is a clever way of solving hard routing problems (like the Travelling Salesman Problem) by mimicking how ants leave behind invisible "smell trails" to guide other ants. Artificial ants walk across a graph, dropping digital pheromones on good edges, and over time the colony collectively converges on the shortest path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Picnic Ant Colony":**
> Imagine a colony of ants trying to find the shortest path from their nest to a sugar cube placed on the ground. Initially, ants wander randomly. The first ant to reach sugar marks the path with a chemical called **pheromone**. Ants that follow a strong-pheromone path reach the food faster, so they return and reinforce the trail more often. Shorter paths get reinforced more frequently (because round-trips are quicker), so the trail becomes stronger on the short path and evaporates on longer paths. After some time, virtually every ant follows the optimal path — a beautiful **emergent intelligence** from simple local rules.

### 1.2 Model-Based Search (Estimation of Distribution Algorithms)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Model-Based Search algorithms (also known as Estimation of Distribution Algorithms, EDAs) replace the traditional variation operators (crossover and mutation) of Evolutionary Algorithms with the construction of an explicit probabilistic model of promising solutions. New candidate solutions are then sampled from this learned probability distribution, which is updated iteratively to bias the search toward better regions of the search space.*

In simple words, instead of "mating" parent solutions like in a Genetic Algorithm, a Model-Based Search algorithm **learns the patterns** of good solutions (e.g., "70% of good solutions have bit 3 = 1"), builds a probability model from those patterns, and then **samples new solutions directly from the model** — like a smart coin whose bias updates every generation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Hiring Manager":**
> Think of a hiring manager who interviews 100 candidates and notices a pattern: top performers almost always have a Computer Science degree, 3+ years of experience, and know Python. Instead of randomly interviewing more people, the manager builds a "model" of what a great candidate looks like and uses that model to filter and recruit new applicants. This is exactly what Model-Based Search does — it builds a statistical profile of good solutions and generates new candidates matching that profile.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Pheromone ($\tau_{ij}$)** | Artificial "scent" deposited on edge $(i,j)$ |
| **Heuristic Information ($\eta_{ij}$)** | Problem-specific greedy desirability (e.g., $1/d_{ij}$) |
| **Evaporation Rate ($\rho$)** | Fraction of pheromone that decays each iteration; $\rho \in (0, 1]$ |
| **Tabu List** | Memory of nodes already visited by an ant in current tour |
| **Probabilistic Model** | Joint/marginal distribution of promising solutions in EDAs |
| **Convergence** | Stable probability distribution in EDAs / uniform pheromone in ACO |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 ACO Algorithmic Mechanics — Step by Step

**Step 1: Initialization**
Place $m$ ants on $n$ nodes. Initialize pheromone trails uniformly:
$$\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0 \quad \text{(small positive constant)}$$

**Step 2: Solution Construction (Stochastic Local Decision)**
Each ant $k$ at node $i$ selects the next node $j$ using the **transition rule**:
$$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_k(s_i)} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}, \quad \text{if } j \in \mathcal{N}_k(s_i)$$

- $\alpha$ : importance of accumulated pheromone (**pheromone factor**)
- $\beta$ : importance of heuristic desirability (**heuristic factor**)
- $\mathcal{N}_k(s_i)$ : feasible neighborhood of ant $k$ from current state $s_i$
- $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ for TSP (Euclidean distance)

**Step 3: Pheromone Update (Global Update)**
After all ants complete their tours, pheromone evaporates and is deposited:
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)$$

The deposited amount depends on the **ant system variant**:

| Variant | Pheromone Deposit Formula |
|---------|---------------------------|
| **Ant-Density** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q$ (constant per edge) |
| **Ant-Quantity** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / d_{ij}$ (distance-weighted) |
| **Ant-Cycle** (most used) | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q / L_k$ (whole-tour quality) |

where $L_k$ is the total tour length of ant $k$ and $Q$ is a tuning constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pheromone evaporation?**
> Evaporation prevents the algorithm from getting stuck in **stagnation** — a state where all ants follow the same suboptimal path forever. Evaporation is a *forgetting mechanism* that allows exploration of new paths.

**Step 4: ACS Local Pheromone Update (Optional)**
In **Ant Colony System (ACS)**, a daemon applies a local update on traversed edges to encourage exploration:
$$\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1 - \xi) \cdot \tau_{ij} + \xi \cdot \tau_0$$
where $\xi \in (0,1]$ is the local evaporation coefficient.

### 2.2 Model-Based Search (EDA) — Algorithmic Mechanics

**Step 1: Initialize Population**
Generate $N$ candidate solutions uniformly at random in the search space.

**Step 2: Selection**
Select $M \le N$ elite/high-fitness solutions (e.g., top 30%).

**Step 3: Build Probabilistic Model $P^{(t)}(x)$**
Learn a probability distribution from selected solutions. The complexity of the model varies by algorithm:

| Algorithm | Model Complexity |
|-----------|------------------|
| **UMDA** (Univariate Marginal Distribution Algorithm) | Marginal probabilities of each variable independently |
| **PBIL** (Population-Based Incremental Learning) | Incremental update of univariate marginals |
| **MIMIC** | Bivariate (pairwise) dependency chain |
| **FDA** (Factorized Distribution Algorithm) | Fixed factorization |
| **BOA** (Bayesian Optimization Algorithm) | Bayesian network with learning + sampling |
| **ECGA** (Extended Compact GA) | Marginal Product Model (MPM) |

For **UMDA** on binary variables $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_n)$:
$$P^{(t)}(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i^{(t)}(x_i)$$

where
$$p_i^{(t)}(1) = \frac{\text{count of } x_i = 1 \text{ in selected set } S}{M}$$

**Step 4: Sampling**
Generate a new population by sampling $N$ solutions from $P^{(t)}(x)$.

**Step 5: Stopping Criterion**
Stop when convergence is reached, i.e., when $p_i(x_i) \in \{0, 1\}$ for all $i$ (premature convergence) or when a fitness threshold is met.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Range/Unit |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $p_{ij}^{k}(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}} [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ | ACO transition probability | $[0, 1]$ |
| 2 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$ | Global pheromone update | $\tau > 0$ |
| 3 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{Q}{L_k}$ | Ant-Cycle deposit | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| 4 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{Q}{d_{ij}}$ | Ant-Quantity deposit | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| 5 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q$ | Ant-Density deposit | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| 6 | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | TSP heuristic visibility | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| 7 | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi\tau_0$ | ACS local update | $\tau > 0$ |
| 8 | $P^{(t)}(x) = \displaystyle\prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i^{(t)}(x_i)$ | UMDA factorized model | probability |
| 9 | $p_i^{(t)}(1) = \dfrac{N_i^{S}}{M}$ | UMDA marginal estimation | $[0,1]$ |
| 10 | $f_{\text{selection}} = \dfrac{M}{N}$ | Selection pressure ratio | $(0, 1]$ |
| 11 | $L_{\text{GBest}}(t) = \min_k L_k(t)$ | Global-best tour length at iter $t$ | $\mathbb{R}^+$ |
| 12 | $\tau_{ij}^{\min} \le \tau_{ij} \le \tau_{ij}^{\max}$ | MMAS (MAX-MIN Ant System) bounds | clipping |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **ACO**: Vehicle routing (Amazon, FedEx delivery), network routing in telecommunications (AntNet), job-shop scheduling, robot path planning in warehouses, protein folding, DNA sequencing.
- **Model-Based Search / EDAs**: Feature selection in machine learning, hyper-parameter optimization, supply chain optimization, antenna design, bioinformatics, financial portfolio optimization.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Ant Foraging to ACO Transition Rule

**Goal**: Derive the ACO transition probability $p_{ij}^k(t)$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Empirical observation of real ants**
Real ants deposit pheromone at a constant rate $q$ per unit time, and the path-following probability is roughly proportional to pheromone concentration. If $P_A$ is the probability of choosing path A and $P_B$ for path B:
$$P_A = \frac{c + c_A}{c + c_A + c_B}, \quad P_B = \frac{c + c_B}{c + c_A + c_B}$$
where $c_A, c_B$ are pheromone concentrations and $c$ is a baseline attractiveness.

**Step 2 — Introduce exponential weighting**
To model the nonlinear amplification of "experience," we raise pheromone to power $\alpha$ and add a heuristic term:
$$\text{attractiveness of edge }(i,j) = [\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to obtain a probability**
Dividing by the sum of all attractive alternatives gives a valid probability distribution:
$$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_k(s_i)} [\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha} \cdot [\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$$

**Interpretation**: When $\alpha = 0$, only the heuristic matters (a greedy nearest-neighbor heuristic). When $\beta = 0$, only pheromone matters (could cause stagnation). Both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ must be positive for balanced exploration–exploitation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Convergence of Pheromone Trails in Ant-Cycle

**Setup**: Assume $m$ ants traverse the same shortest path of length $L^*$, and ignore evaporation temporarily.

**Step 1 — Single iteration deposit on optimal edge**
Each of the $m$ ants contributes $Q/L^*$, so total deposit per iteration is:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau^{k} = \frac{mQ}{L^*}$$

**Step 2 — With evaporation factor $(1-\rho)$**
The recurrence is:
$$\tau(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau(t) + \frac{mQ}{L^*}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear recurrence**
Let $\tau^*$ be the fixed point where $\tau(t+1) = \tau(t) = \tau^*$:
$$\tau^{*} = (1-\rho)\tau^{*} + \frac{mQ}{L^{*}}$$
$$\tau^{*} - (1-\rho)\tau^{*} = \frac{mQ}{L^{*}}$$
$$\rho \cdot \tau^{*} = \frac{mQ}{L^{*}}$$
$$\boxed{\tau^{*} = \frac{mQ}{\rho \cdot L^{*}}}$$

**Interpretation**: Higher evaporation $\rho$ lowers the steady-state pheromone; smaller problem (smaller $L^*$) yields higher concentration; this is why short paths dominate over time.

### 3.3 Derivation: UMDA Marginal Probability Update

**Goal**: Show how the marginal probability vector in UMDA converges under elitist selection.

**Step 1 — Define empirical frequency**
For a binary variable $x_i$ over $M$ selected solutions:
$$p_i^{(t)}(1) = \frac{1}{M} \sum_{s \in S^{(t)}} x_i^{(s)}, \quad p_i^{(t)}(0) = 1 - p_i^{(t)}(1)$$

**Step 2 — Update rule**
With learning rate $\lambda$ (PBIL-style extension):
$$p_i^{(t+1)}(1) = (1 - \lambda) p_i^{(t)}(1) + \lambda \cdot \frac{N_i^{S^{(t)}}}{M}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state analysis**
If $p_i^{S} = c$ is the asymptotic frequency in elite solutions, the fixed point satisfies:
$$c = (1-\lambda)c + \lambda c \implies c = c \;\; \text{(any value is consistent)}$$
Thus convergence speed depends on $\lambda$:
- Small $\lambda$ → slow but stable adaptation.
- Large $\lambda$ → fast but unstable (premature convergence to 0 or 1).

**Step 4 — Expected number of converged variables**
After $T$ iterations, the variance of $p_i$ shrinks as:
$$\text{Var}[p_i^{(T)}] = p_i(1 - p_i) \left( \frac{1}{M} + \lambda^2 \cdot \frac{1}{M} \cdot \text{(history terms)} \right)$$

Hence, **larger population $M$** reduces variance and prevents premature convergence.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ACO vs Model-Based Search

| Property | ACO | Model-Based Search (EDAs) |
|---------|-----|--------------------------|
| **Origin Metaphor** | Ant foraging behavior | Statistical learning |
| **Representation** | Solution = path/tour in graph | Probability distribution over variables |
| **Memory** | Pheromone matrix $\tau$ | Probabilistic model $P(x)$ |
| **Variation Operator** | Probabilistic path construction | Sampling from $P(x)$ |
| **Selection Pressure** | Implicit (pheromone bias) | Explicit (top-$M$ selection) |
| **Problem Domain** | Strong on routing/graph problems | Strong on discrete/binary optimization |
| **Convergence Signal** | Stagnation / uniform trails | Marginal probabilities $\to \{0,1\}$ |
| **Scalability Issue** | Pheromone matrix $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ | Model complexity $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}(n^k)$ |
| **Premature Convergence Risk** | High if $\rho$ too small | High if $M$ too small |
| **Theoretical Foundation** | Graph theory, Markov chains | Information theory, Bayesian learning |
| **Notable Variants** | ACS, MMAS, Elitist AS | UMDA, PBIL, BOA, MIMIC, ECGA |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: ACO on a 4-Node TSP

**Problem**: 4 cities with distances $d_{12}=2, d_{13}=5, d_{14}=4, d_{23}=3, d_{24}=6, d_{34}=1$. Start at node 1, $m=1$ ant, $\alpha=1, \beta=2, \rho=0.1, Q=10$. Initial $\tau=1$ on all edges.

**Step 1 — Heuristic values**
$\eta_{12}=1/2=0.5, \; \eta_{13}=0.2, \; \eta_{14}=0.25$

**Step 2 — From node 1: probabilities**
Denominator $D = (1)(0.5)^2 + (1)(0.2)^2 + (1)(0.25)^2 = 0.25 + 0.04 + 0.0625 = 0.3525$

- $p_{12} = 0.25 / 0.3525 \approx 0.7092$
- $p_{13} = 0.04 / 0.3525 \approx 0.1135$
- $p_{14} = 0.0625 / 0.3525 \approx 0.1773$

Suppose we pick node 2. From node 2, feasible = {3, 4}:
$\eta_{23} = 1/3, \; \eta_{24} = 1/6$
$D = 1/9 + 1/36 = 5/36 \approx 0.1389$
- $p_{23} = (1/9)/(5/36) = 4/5 = 0.8$
- $p_{24} = 0.2$

Pick node 3, then forced to 4, then return to 1. Tour: $1\to2\to3\to4\to1$.
Tour length: $L = 2 + 3 + 1 + 4 = 10$.

**Step 3 — Pheromone update**
$\Delta\tau_{12} = \Delta\tau_{23} = \Delta\tau_{34} = \Delta\tau_{41} = 10/10 = 1$
$\tau_{12}^{new} = 0.9(1) + 1 = 1.9$, similarly for tour edges.
Other edges: $0.9(1) = 0.9$.

**Step 4 — Next iteration, ant will favor edges 1–2, 2–3, 3–4, 4–1** (the optimal path).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ACO Metaheuristic Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize Parameters alpha beta rho Q] --> B[Set Initial Pheromone tau0 on all edges]
    B --> C[Place m ants on starting nodes]
    C --> D[For each ant: construct solution via transition rule]
    D --> E{All ants finished tour?}
    E -- No --> D
    E -- Yes --> F[Compute tour length Lk for each ant]
    F --> G[Apply Pheromone Evaporation tau = 1-rho times tau]
    G --> H[Deposit Pheromone on tour edges: delta tau = Q/Lk]
    H --> I{Termination criterion met?}
    I -- No --> C
    I -- Yes --> J[Output Best Tour Found]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**Define Ant Colony Optimization (ACO). Mention two real-world problems where ACO is applied.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*ACO is a population-based swarm intelligence metaheuristic proposed by Marco Dorigo, used to solve combinatorial optimization problems. It is inspired by the pheromone-laying behavior of real ants and uses a parameterized probabilistic model called the pheromone model. Solutions are constructed probabilistically by artificial ants, and the pheromone is updated based on solution quality.*

*Real-world applications (any two):* (1) **Travelling Salesman Problem and Vehicle Routing** in logistics; (2) **Network routing** in telecommunication systems (AntNet); (3) Job-shop scheduling; (4) Robot path planning.

**Mark Distribution**: Definition (2 marks) + Applications (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**What is a Model-Based Search algorithm? How does it differ from a Genetic Algorithm?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Model-Based Search algorithms, also known as Estimation of Distribution Algorithms (EDAs), are a class of evolutionary algorithms that replace traditional variation operators (crossover and mutation) with the construction and sampling of an explicit probabilistic model. The model captures statistical patterns from selected (elite) solutions, and new offspring are generated by sampling from this learned distribution.*

*Difference from GA:* In a GA, new solutions are produced by **combining** parent chromosomes (crossover) and random bit-flipping (mutation). In a Model-Based Search, there is no direct recombination — instead, a **joint probability distribution** over the search space is learned and sampled, which preserves the building blocks / linkage between variables automatically.

**Mark Distribution**: Definition (2 marks) + Difference from GA (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module 3 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** Explain the Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of pheromone update and evaporation with suitable equations. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe three variants of Ant System: Ant-Density, Ant-Quantity, and Ant-Cycle. Compare their performance characteristics. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks

1. **Introduction to ACO (1 mark):**
   ACO is a swarm-intelligence metaheuristic introduced by Marco Dorigo (1992) for solving combinatorial optimization problems. It mimics the foraging behavior of real ants using artificial ants that walk on a fully connected graph.

2. **Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
   - **Initialization**: Set pheromone $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_0$ for all edges, place $m$ ants on starting nodes.
   - **Solution Construction**: Each ant $k$ at node $i$ picks the next node $j$ with probability $p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_k}[\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$.
   - **Pheromone Update**: After all ants complete their tours, apply global update $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}$.

3. **Role of Pheromone Evaporation (2 marks):**
   The factor $(1-\rho)$ causes pheromone decay and prevents premature convergence / stagnation. Higher $\rho$ (e.g., 0.5) means faster forgetting; lower $\rho$ (e.g., 0.01) means longer memory. Evaporation enables exploration of new paths.

4. **Parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ (1 mark):**
   - $\alpha$ controls exploitation of accumulated experience.
   - $\beta$ controls the influence of greedy heuristic (e.g., $\eta_{ij}=1/d_{ij}$).

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks

| Variant | Pheromone Deposit | Key Property |
|---------|------------------|--------------|
| **Ant-Density** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q$ | Edge-local, independent of distance and tour length |
| **Ant-Quantity** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/d_{ij}$ | Edge-local, biased toward shorter edges |
| **Ant-Cycle** | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = Q/L_k$ | Global, deposited only after full tour; **best performance in practice** |

**Comparison (7 marks breakdown):**
- Ant-Density and Ant-Quantity use **step-by-step (local)** updates and depend on the edge's local properties; they are sensitive to $Q$ and $d_{ij}$ but ignore global tour quality. (2 marks)
- Ant-Cycle uses **global, delayed** update based on $L_k$, which makes it more robust and provides better quality solutions in practice — this is the recommended default in classical Ant System. (2 marks)
- Performance: Ant-Cycle > Ant-Quantity > Ant-Density for TSP (verified experimentally by Dorigo). (1 mark)
- Discussion of **ACS local update** as an additional variant using $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi\tau_0$. (1 mark)
- Mention of **MMAS** with bounds $\tau_{ij} \in [\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$. (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]`

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain the working of an Estimation of Distribution Algorithm (EDA). (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the Univariate Marginal Distribution Algorithm (UMDA) and the Population-Based Incremental Learning (PBIL) algorithm with their probability update equations. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks

1. **Flowchart (3 marks):** The flowchart should show the cyclic EDA process: **Initialize → Evaluate → Select elites → Build probabilistic model → Sample new population → Loop until convergence**.
2. **Initialization (1 mark):** Generate $N$ random candidate solutions uniformly.
3. **Selection (1 mark):** Select top $M$ solutions based on fitness.
4. **Model Building (1 mark):** Estimate $P^{(t)}(x)$ from the selected set.
5. **Sampling + Termination (1 mark):** Sample $N$ new individuals; iterate until convergence (e.g., marginals saturate at 0 or 1) or max generations.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**UMDA (3 marks):**
- Assumes variables are **independent**; the joint distribution factorizes as:
$$P^{(t)}(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i^{(t)}(x_i)$$
- Each marginal is estimated from selected solutions:
$$p_i^{(t)}(1) = \frac{\sum_{s \in S^{(t)}} x_i^{(s)}}{M}, \quad p_i^{(t)}(0) = 1 - p_i^{(t)}(1)$$
- Limitation: cannot capture variable interactions/linkage.

**PBIL (3 marks):**
- Originally designed for binary optimization by Baluja (1994).
- Uses a **probability vector** $P = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ instead of a population.
- Update rule (with learning rate $\lambda$):
$$p_i^{(t+1)} = (1 - \lambda) p_i^{(t)} + \lambda \cdot \frac{N_i^{1}}{M}$$
- Optionally applies **mutation**: with small probability, perturb a component $p_i \leftarrow p_i \pm \delta$ to maintain diversity.
- Limitation: also univariate; pairwise variants like **MIMIC** and **BMDA** exist.

**Comparison (1 mark):** PBIL is the incremental, single-vector version of UMDA — faster but more sensitive to premature convergence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the three Ant variants**: Students often write the same formula for all three. Remember — *Ant-Density* uses constant $Q$, *Ant-Quantity* uses $Q/d_{ij}$, *Ant-Cycle* uses $Q/L_k$. Writing the wrong formula costs 2–3 marks easily.
> 2. **Forgetting the denominator** in the transition probability $p_{ij}^k$ — the unnormalized product $[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}$ is **not** the probability. Always normalize over the feasible neighborhood.
> 3. **Mixing up ACO and GA**: ACO does **not** use crossover or mutation. It uses probabilistic path construction and pheromone updates. If you mention "crossover" in ACO, you lose a CO mark.
> 4. **Writing only one EDA algorithm**: KTU questions on "Model-Based Search" expect you to know **at least UMDA and PBIL** explicitly with the marginal update formula. Writing only a generic description without equations will cap you at 4/7 marks.
> 5. **Missing the role of evaporation**: A common error is to skip the $(1-\rho)$ term or to say "evaporation = pheromone deposition" — these are **opposite** processes.
> 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark questions**: KTU valuation keys explicitly award 1–2 marks for "neat labelled flowchart / block diagram". Always include one in part (a).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam**

- [ ] **ACO** is a **swarm-intelligence**, **population-based**, **constructive** metaheuristic introduced by **Marco Dorigo (1992)**.
- [ ] The **ACO transition rule** is $p_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\sum [\tau_{il}]^{\alpha}[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}}$ — never forget the denominator.
- [ ] $\alpha$ = pheromone weight, $\beta$ = heuristic weight, $\rho$ = evaporation rate.
- [ ] **Ant-Cycle** uses $\Delta\tau = Q/L_k$ and is the best-performing classical variant.
- [ ] **MMAS** bounds pheromone to $[\tau_{\min}, \tau_{\max}]$ to prevent stagnation.
- [ ] **ACS** adds a **local update** $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\xi)\tau_{ij} + \xi\tau_0$ to encourage exploration.
- [ ] Evaporation **prevents premature convergence** and allows exploration.
- [ ] **Model-Based Search (EDAs)** replace crossover/mutation with **explicit probabilistic model building** + **sampling**.
- [ ] **UMDA** uses **univariate marginals**: $P(x) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p_i(x_i)$ — **no variable interactions**.
- [ ] **PBIL** maintains a **single probability vector** with learning rate $\lambda$; faster but unstable.
- [ ] **MIMIC** uses a **chain of pairwise dependencies**; **BOA** uses a **Bayesian network**; **ECGA** uses a **Marginal Product Model (MPM)**.
- [ ] **Premature convergence** in EDAs occurs when all marginals $\to 0$ or $1$ before finding the optimum.
- [ ] For TSP, heuristic visibility is always $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$.
- [ ] ACO and EDAs are **complementary**: ACO excels on graph/TSP, EDAs excel on discrete/black-box optimization.
- [ ] Both algorithms are **population-based**, **stochastic**, and **iterative** — and share a common meta-framework of selection + variation + evaluation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line High-Yield Comparison to Write in Exam:**
> *"While ACO uses pheromone-guided constructive search inspired by ant foraging, Model-Based Search uses statistical model building and sampling inspired by information theory — both iteratively bias the search toward promising regions without explicit crossover or mutation."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variations of ACO: Elitist Ant System (EAS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Variations of ACO: Elitist Ant System (EAS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Ant Colony Optimization (ACO): Foundational Recap

**Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** is a population-based, nature-inspired metaheuristic introduced by **Marco Dorigo** in his 1992 Ph.D. thesis, used for solving combinatorial optimization problems. The algorithm mimics the foraging behavior of real ants, which deposit a chemical substance called **pheromone** on their trails to communicate indirect information about the quality of paths between a food source and their nest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECNT869 Module 3):**
> The 2024 Scheme Module 3 focuses on **Swarm Intelligence and ACO Variants**. Students must master the differences between *Ant System (AS)*, *Elitist Ant System (EAS)*, *Max-Min Ant System (MMAS)*, and *Ant Colony System (ACS)*. EAS is the most fundamental variant after vanilla AS.

## 1.2 Formal Definition: Elitist Ant System (EAS)

**Elitist Ant System (EAS)** is a refinement of the original Ant System introduced by **Dorigo, Maniezzo, and Colorni (1996)**. The defining feature of EAS is the **explicit reinforcement of the best-so-far global solution** by a single (or multiple) "**Elitist Ant(s)**" during the pheromone update phase.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Standard Wording):**
> *"The Elitist Ant System is a variant of the Ant System in which additional pheromone is deposited on the edges belonging to the best tour found since the start of the algorithm (the global-best tour), with the amount of additional pheromone being proportional to the length of the global-best tour and weighted by a user-defined parameter **e** (the number of elitist ants)."*

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy: The "Senior Forager" Concept

Imagine a **colony of ants** searching for the shortest path to a sugar source in a forest. The regular ants are like junior foragers — they explore randomly and leave small pheromone trails.

Now, introduce a **"Senior Elite Ant"** (the elitist ant). This elite ant does *not* re-explore. Instead, it confidently retraces the **shortest path discovered so far** by the entire colony, and lays down a **stronger, more concentrated pheromone trail** along that path.

**Why does this help?**
- Future junior ants are more likely to follow the elite ant's reinforced trail.
- This biases the search towards promising regions of the solution space.
- The colony converges **faster** to high-quality solutions.

The mathematical parameter **e** represents the *number of times the elite ant is allowed to "mark" the best path* per iteration — a stronger emphasis on exploitation over exploration.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:** EAS works exactly like a **feedback-based recommendation system**. When one product (the global-best solution) consistently gets 5-star reviews (high pheromone), the algorithm "boosts" it further, accelerating the algorithm's convergence to that high-quality answer.

## 1.4 Terminology Glossary (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| **τ_ij** | Pheromone trail intensity on edge (i,j) | "Tau" — Greek for trail |
| **ρ (rho)** | Pheromone evaporation rate, 0 < ρ < 1 | "Evaporation factor" |
| **e** | Elitist weight — number of elite ants | "Emphasis factor" |
| **T\*** | Global-best tour | "Best tour star" |
| **L\*** | Length of global-best tour | "Length of best" |
| **η_ij** | Heuristic information (typically 1/d_ij) | "Eta — visibility" |
| **α, β** | Pheromone/heuristic importance weights | "Alpha-beta balance" |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Elitist Ant System: Operational Pipeline

The EAS algorithm proceeds in **four explicit phases** per iteration:

### Phase 1: Solution Construction (Foraging)
Each of the **m** ants in the colony constructs a complete tour (or partial solution) by stochastically choosing the next node based on the **pseudo-random proportional rule**:

$$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{\left[\tau_{ij}(t)\right]^\alpha \cdot \left[\eta_{ij}\right]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} \left[\tau_{il}(t)\right]^\alpha \cdot \left[\eta_{il}\right]^\beta}, \quad \text{if } j \in N_i^k$$

Where:
- $p_{ij}^k(t)$ is the probability that ant **k** moves from city **i** to city **j** at iteration **t**.
- $N_i^k$ is the feasible neighborhood of ant **k** at city **i**.
- **α** controls the influence of pheromone (exploitation).
- **β** controls the influence of heuristic distance (exploration).

### Phase 2: Local Search (Optional, KTU-Optional)
An optional local optimizer (e.g., 2-opt, 3-opt) may improve each ant's tour. This is **not mandatory** in EAS but is commonly used in practice.

### Phase 3: Pheromone Evaporation (Decay)
To avoid unlimited pheromone accumulation and to forget poor decisions, all edges undergo **pheromone evaporation**:

$$\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **1 − ρ** is sometimes called the **persistence factor**. If **ρ = 0.1**, then **90%** of the previous pheromone is retained.

### Phase 4: Pheromone Deposit — The EAS Distinguishing Step
This is **where EAS differs from vanilla AS**. The pheromone update in EAS is:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t) + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t)$$

Where:
- $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t)$ is the pheromone deposited by ant **k** on edge (i,j) during iteration **t**.
- $\Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t)$ is the pheromone deposited by the **single global-best ant** on edge (i,j).
- **e** is the **elitist weight** (a positive integer or real number ≥ 1).

The individual contribution is:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L^k(t)} & \text{if edge (i,j) belongs to ant k's tour} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

And the elite contribution is:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L^*} & \text{if edge (i,j) belongs to the global-best tour } T^* \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

Where:
- **Q** is a constant (typically **Q = 1** in EAS).
- $L^k(t)$ is the length of the tour constructed by ant **k** in iteration **t**.
- $L^*$ is the length of the **best tour found since the start of the algorithm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of EAS:** By giving the global-best tour an extra pheromone boost of magnitude **e**, EAS **intensifies the search** around the best-known solution. This leads to **faster convergence** but introduces a trade-off: if **e** is too large, the algorithm may **prematurely converge** to a sub-optimal solution (a phenomenon called **stagnation**).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State Transition Probability | $p_{ij}^k = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$ | Determines ant's next move |
| 2 | Heuristic Information | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | Distance-based desirability |
| 3 | Pheromone Evaporation | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}$ | Forgetting mechanism |
| 4 | Ant's Pheromone Deposit | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q / L^k$ | Standard ant contribution |
| 5 | Elite Pheromone Deposit | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^* = Q / L^*$ | Global-best ant contribution |
| 6 | **EAS Master Update Rule** | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k + e\cdot\Delta\tau_{ij}^*$ | **Core EAS equation** |
| 7 | Convergence Condition | $p_{best} \to 1$ as $t \to \infty$ | All ants follow T* |
| 8 | Stagnation Threshold | $\tau_{ij} \to \tau_{max} \; \forall (i,j) \in T^*$ | Search collapses |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** When writing the EAS update rule, students often forget the **multiplier (1 − ρ)** before the existing $\tau_{ij}(t)$. This is the evaporation factor applied to the OLD pheromone, and it is **always present**. A common 2-mark error.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

EAS is deployed in industries where **graph-based combinatorial optimization** is critical:

- **Telecommunications:** Dynamic routing in **MPLS networks** and **5G/6G path selection**.
- **Logistics:** Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP) for **Amazon, FedEx, Flipkart delivery fleets**.
- **VLSI Design:** Wire routing on integrated circuit boards.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein folding and DNA sequencing.
- **Smart Grids:** Optimal power flow and load dispatch.

The **e** parameter is typically tuned between **1 and m** (the total number of ants), with **e = 1** being the safest default.
