<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Ant System to Elitist Ant System

### Step 1 — Start with the Original Ant System (AS) Update Rule

In the **vanilla Ant System** (Dorigo, 1991), the pheromone update is given by:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t)$$

This is the **base equation** for all ACO variants. The summation term is over all **m** ants in the colony.

### Step 2 — Identify the Limitation of Vanilla AS

In AS, all ants contribute equally. Even the *worst* ant of the iteration deposits pheromone proportional to its own (poor) tour length. This means:

- There is **no explicit exploitation** of the best-known solution.
- Convergence is **slow** because the algorithm is too explorative.
- The global best is not *deliberately* emphasized.

### Step 3 — Introduce the Elitist Reinforcement Term

The researchers (Dorigo, Maniezzo, Colorni — 1996) proposed adding an **extra deposit** proportional to the best global tour. Define:

$$\Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{L^*} & \text{if (i,j)} \in T^* \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Assuming Q = 1** (a common simplification for TSP problems). The "elite" ant retraces the global best tour and deposits pheromone on every edge belonging to $T^*$.

### Step 4 — Apply the Elitist Weight

To allow the user to control the *strength* of the elitist emphasis, multiply the elite contribution by a tunable parameter **e**:

$$e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t) = e \cdot \frac{1}{L^*} \cdot \mathbb{1}\{(i,j) \in T^*\}$$

Where $\mathbb{1}\{\cdot\}$ is the **indicator function** (1 if true, 0 if false).

### Step 5 — Combine to Get the EAS Master Equation

Adding the elite term to the AS update rule yields the **EAS pheromone update**:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t) + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t)$$

This is the **defining equation of EAS**. The full expanded form for an edge (i,j) in the global best tour $T^*$ is:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k:(i,j) \in T^k} \frac{1}{L^k(t)} + \frac{e}{L^*}$$

And for an edge (i,j) **not** in the global best tour:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k:(i,j) \in T^k} \frac{1}{L^k(t)}$$

### Step 6 — Special Case Analysis: What if e = 0?

If **e = 0**, the elite term vanishes, and EAS **degenerates** to vanilla AS. This is a useful sanity check.

### Step 7 — Special Case Analysis: What if e → ∞?

If **e** is set very large, the global-best tour accumulates pheromone disproportionately, leading to **stagnation** — all ants follow $T^*$ and the search loses diversity.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (TSP with 4 cities)

Consider a **4-city TSP**: Cities A, B, C, D, with distance matrix:

$$
D = \begin{bmatrix}
\infty & 10 & 15 & 20 \\
10 & \infty & 35 & 25 \\
15 & 35 & \infty & 30 \\
20 & 25 & 30 & \infty
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Initial Pheromone:** $\tau_{ij}(0) = 0.1$ for all edges.
**Parameters:** m = 2 ants, e = 1, ρ = 0.1, Q = 1, α = 1, β = 1.

### Iteration 1:

**Ant 1's tour:** A → B → C → D → A
Length: $L^1 = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$

**Ant 2's tour:** A → C → B → D → A
Length: $L^2 = 15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95$

**Global best so far:** $L^* = 95$ (either tour, choose A-B-C-D-A).

### Pheromone update for edge A-B (which is in global best):

Using the EAS formula:

$$\tau_{AB}(1) = (1 - 0.1)(0.1) + \frac{1}{95} + \frac{1}{95} + 1 \cdot \frac{1}{95}$$

Performing the arithmetic step-by-step:

$$\tau_{AB}(1) = 0.9 \times 0.1 + \frac{1}{95} + \frac{1}{95} + \frac{1}{95}$$

$$\tau_{AB}(1) = 0.09 + 0.01053 + 0.01053 + 0.01053$$

$$\tau_{AB}(1) = 0.09 + 0.03158 = 0.12158$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The elite ant's contribution ($1/95 \approx 0.0105$) **adds a significant boost** to the global best edges, accelerating the algorithm's convergence toward that tour.

## 3.3 Convergence Analysis (Qualitative)

A **stagnation behavior analysis** for EAS was given by Dorigo & Stützle (2004):

Let $\tau_{max}$ be the maximum pheromone an edge can accumulate. As $t \to \infty$:

$$\tau_{ij}(t) \to \tau_{max} \quad \text{for every edge (i,j)} \in T^*$$

This leads to the asymptotic transition probability:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} p_{ij}^k(t) = 1 \quad \text{for the global best edges}$$

**Proof Sketch:**
The proof relies on the **positive feedback mechanism**: edges in $T^*$ receive the elite boost $e/L^*$ *every iteration*, so their pheromone grows monotonically (in expectation). Since the transition probability is monotonically increasing in $\tau_{ij}$, the probability of choosing a global-best edge approaches 1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EAS Algorithm Flowchart

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete Elitist Ant System pipeline** as a block-level functional architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start EAS Algorithm]) --> initB[Initialize Parameters]
    initB --> initC[Set m ants, e weight, rho, alpha, beta, Q]
    initC --> initD[Initialize Pheromone Matrix tau_ij with tau0]
    initD --> loopT{For each Iteration t}
    loopT -->|Yes| constructE[Phase 1: Solution Construction]
    constructE --> stateE1[Each ant k builds tour using pseudo-random proportional rule]
    stateE1 --> evalE2[Compute tour length Lk for each ant]
    evalE2 --> updE3[Phase 2: Update Global Best L-star and T-star]
    updE3 --> evapE4[Phase 3: Pheromone Evaporation on all edges]
    evapE5["tau_ij = 1-rho * tau_ij"] --> evapE4
    evapE4 --> depoE5[Phase 4: Pheromone Deposit]
    depoE5 --> depoE6[Standard ants deposit Q divided by Lk]
    depoE6 --> depoE7["Elite ant deposits e times Q divided by L-star on T-star"]
    depoE7 --> loopCheck{Max iterations or convergence}
    loopCheck -->|No| loopT
    loopCheck -->|Yes| outputF[Output Global Best Tour T-star]
    outputF --> stopG([End])
    loopT -->|No| stopG
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: EAS Pheromone Update

This block diagram decomposes the **EAS pheromone update equation** into its three contributing components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Input: Current Pheromone tau_ij]
        A1[tau_ij t]
    end

    subgraph B[Evaporation Block]
        B1["Multiply by 1 minus rho"]
    end

    subgraph C[Standard Ant Contribution]
        C1["Sum over k equals 1 to m"]
        C2["Q divided by Lk if edge in Tk"]
    end

    subgraph D[Elite Ant Contribution]
        D1["Elite weight e"]
        D2["Q divided by L-star if edge in T-star"]
    end

    subgraph E[Output: New Pheromone]
        E1["tau_ij t+1"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> E1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> E1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block: AS vs. EAS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AS[Ant System AS]
        AS1[All m ants contribute equally]
        AS2[No global best emphasis]
        AS3[Slow convergence]
    end

    subgraph EAS[Elitist Ant System EAS]
        EAS1[All m ants contribute normally]
        EAS2[PLUS: Elite ant adds e times Q divided by L-star on T-star]
        EAS3[Faster convergence but stagnation risk]
    end

    AS -.Upgrade.-> EAS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Board Exams:** In descriptive answers, sketching the pheromone update equation with **three labeled boxes** (Evaporation | Standard | Elite) is a high-scoring 5-mark technique. The examiner can visually verify your understanding of EAS.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: What is the Elitist Ant System (EAS)? How does it differ from the basic Ant System (AS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Elitist Ant System (EAS)** is a variant of the Ant Colony Optimization metaheuristic in which additional pheromone is deposited on the edges of the **global-best tour** $T^*$ found so far, weighted by a parameter **e** (the number of elitist ants). The pheromone update rule is:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k(t) + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*(t)$$

**Differences from AS:**

| Aspect | Ant System (AS) | Elitist Ant System (EAS) |
|---|---|---|
| Global best emphasis | None | Explicit elite reinforcement |
| Update equation | Two terms | Three terms (extra $e \cdot \Delta\tau^*$) |
| Convergence | Slow | Faster |
| Risk | Lower stagnation | Higher stagnation risk |

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**Q: Explain the role of the parameter 'e' in the Elitist Ant System. What happens when e = 0 and when e is very large?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The parameter **e** is the **elitist weight** that controls the strength of pheromone reinforcement on the global-best tour. It is a **user-defined tuning parameter** representing the number of "elite ants" (or equivalently, the intensity of the elite emphasis).

- **When e = 0:** The elite term vanishes, and EAS **degenerates** to the original Ant System.
- **When e is very large:** The algorithm suffers from **stagnation** — the global-best tour accumulates excessive pheromone, and the probability of exploring alternative tours drops to near zero, leading to **premature convergence** to a sub-optimal solution.

A typical value in the literature is **e = 1 to e = m** (where m is the number of ants).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the Elitist Ant System (EAS) in detail. Explain the pheromone update rule with all its components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Elitist Ant System (EAS)** is an enhanced variant of the original Ant System proposed by Dorigo, Maniezzo, and Colorni in 1996. It introduces an explicit reinforcement mechanism for the best-known solution to accelerate convergence.

**Step 1: Solution Construction**
Each of the **m** ants constructs a tour using the **pseudo-random proportional rule**:

$$p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in N_i^k} [\tau_{il}]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$$

**Step 2: Pheromone Evaporation**
To prevent unbounded accumulation, every edge undergoes evaporation:

$$\tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Step 3: Pheromone Deposit (Key EAS Step)**
The defining EAS update is:

$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^k + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*$$

Where:
- $\Delta\tau_{ij}^k = Q/L^k$ if edge $(i,j)$ belongs to ant $k$'s tour.
- $\Delta\tau_{ij}^* = Q/L^*$ if edge $(i,j)$ belongs to the global-best tour $T^*$.
- **e** is the elitist weight.

**Step 4: Update Global Best**
If any ant produced a tour shorter than $L^*$, update $L^* \leftarrow L^k$ and $T^* \leftarrow T^k$.

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of EAS over the basic Ant System. What is the stagnation problem? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages of EAS:**

1. **Faster Convergence:** The elite reinforcement provides strong positive feedback, guiding ants toward the best-known solution rapidly.
2. **Better Solution Quality (in early iterations):** Empirical studies on TSP show EAS finds shorter tours than AS within the same iteration count.
3. **Simplicity:** Only one extra parameter (**e**) is added, making implementation straightforward.

**Disadvantages of EAS:**

1. **Premature Convergence / Stagnation:** If **e** is too large, the global-best tour dominates the pheromone landscape, and all ants converge to $T^*$ even if it is sub-optimal.
2. **Reduced Exploration:** Less time is spent on alternative regions of the search space.
3. **Parameter Sensitivity:** The algorithm's performance is highly sensitive to **e**, **ρ**, **α**, and **β**.

**The Stagnation Problem:**
Stagnation occurs when all ants construct the same tour (typically the global-best), and the search process loses diversity. The pheromone differences between edges become extremely large, and the transition probability for the global-best edges approaches 1. To mitigate this, researchers later developed **MMAS** (Max-Min Ant System) and **ACS** (Ant Colony System), which cap pheromone values and use local updates.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Elitist Ant System. List the parameters used in EAS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of EAS (with flowchart):**

Refer to the **functional block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes. The algorithm proceeds as:

```
1. Initialize pheromone τ_ij(0) and parameters
2. REPEAT for each iteration t:
   a. For each ant k = 1 to m:
      Construct a complete tour T^k using state transition rule
      Compute length L^k
   b. Update global best: if min(L^k) < L*, then L* = min(L^k) and T* = T^best
   c. Apply pheromone evaporation: τ_ij = (1-ρ)·τ_ij
   d. Apply pheromone deposit:
      For each ant k, deposit Q/L^k on edges of T^k
      Apply elite deposit: e·Q/L* on edges of T*
3. UNTIL termination condition is met
4. RETURN T* and L*
```

**Parameters of EAS:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of ants | m | 10 to 50 | Colony size |
| Elitist weight | e | 1 to m | Elite emphasis |
| Evaporation rate | ρ | 0.1 to 0.5 | Pheromone decay |
| Pheromone factor | α | 0.5 to 2 | Exploitation weight |
| Heuristic factor | β | 1 to 5 | Exploration weight |
| Pheromone constant | Q | 1 (TSP) | Scaling factor |
| Initial pheromone | τ₀ | 1/(n·L_nn) | Starting value |

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare AS, EAS, and Ant Colony System (ACS) based on pheromone update strategy, exploration vs. exploitation, and convergence speed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Ant System (AS) | Elitist Ant System (EAS) | Ant Colony System (ACS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pheromone Update** | All ants deposit equally | All ants + elite boost $e \cdot \Delta\tau^*$ | Only global best deposits |
| **Local Update** | None | None | **Yes** (during construction) |
| **Exploration** | High | Moderate | High (pseudo-random) |
| **Exploitation** | Low | High (elite emphasis) | High (best-only deposit) |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slowest | Fast | Fastest |
| **Stagnation Risk** | Low | High | Moderate (controlled by bounds) |
| **Key Parameter** | α, β, ρ | + **e** | + q₀ (pseudo-random threshold) |
| **Year Introduced** | 1991 | 1996 | 1997 |

**Detailed Analysis:**

- **AS** uses the most uniform update — all ants contribute, leading to slow but diverse search.
- **EAS** adds the elite term, creating strong positive feedback on the best solution.
- **ACS** uses **two updates**: a local update during solution construction and a global update by the iteration-best ant. It also employs **pseudo-random proportional rule** (controlled by $q_0$) to balance exploration.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common 2-mark loss:** Students often confuse the **iteration-best** (best in current iteration) with the **global-best** (best since start). EAS uses the **global-best** tour $T^*$, not the iteration-best. (MMAS may use either.)
> 2. **Equation Error:** Forgetting the **evaporation factor (1 − ρ)** before the existing $\tau_{ij}(t)$ loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Miswriting Q:** The pheromone constant **Q** is not always 1 — it depends on the problem. State it explicitly in the equation.
> 4. **Confusing e with m:** The parameter **e** is the *weight*, not the *number* of elite ants (though they are related in interpretation). Clearly state its role.
> 5. **Missing Indicator Function:** When writing $\Delta\tau_{ij}^*$, you must specify it is non-zero *only* if $(i,j) \in T^*$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **EAS = AS + Elite Ant Reinforcement Term** — that is the **single-sentence** definition.
- The EAS pheromone update is: $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_k \Delta\tau_{ij}^k + e \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^*$
- **e** is the **elitist weight**; **e = 0** reduces to AS; **e → ∞** causes stagnation.
- The elite term uses the **global-best tour** $T^*$ (not iteration-best).
- EAS provides **faster convergence** but at the cost of **higher stagnation risk**.
- Key parameters: **m, e, ρ, α, β, Q, τ₀**.
- EAS was introduced by **Dorigo, Maniezzo, and Colorni (1996)** — a 2-mark KTU question.
- EAS is **TSP-friendly** (Q = 1) and is the precursor to **MMAS** and **ACS**.
- For board answers, always **label each term** of the update equation — examiners award partial marks for clear labeling.
- The stagnation problem was the main motivation for developing **Max-Min Ant System** and **Ant Colony System**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Minmax Ant System (MMAS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Minmax Ant System (MMAS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Min–Max Ant System (MMAS)** is an advanced enhancement of the classical Ant System (AS) introduced by **Thomas Stützle and Holger Hoos (2000)**. It is formally defined as a pheromone-mediated swarm intelligence algorithm in which the pheromone trail intensity on every edge of the solution construction graph is explicitly bounded between a strict lower bound $\tau_{min}$ and a strict upper bound $\tau_{max}$.

$$ \tau_{min} \le \tau_{ij}(t) \le \tau_{max} \quad \forall (i,j) \in E $$

The two key engineering modifications that distinguish MMAS from standard AS are:
1. **Pheromone Trail Limits** — preventing any single trail value from growing unboundedly (which causes premature convergence / *stagnation*) or decaying to zero (which destroys learned information).
2. **Selective Pheromone Deposition** — only the *iteration-best* ($\Delta\tau^{ib}$) or *global-best* ($\Delta\tau^{gb}$) ant is permitted to update pheromone, intensifying constructive feedback on the best-known paths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** MMAS is the standard "de facto" benchmark Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) algorithm. Questions frequently appear under "Improvements over basic Ant System" and "Pheromone bounds in swarm algorithms."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Glowing Trail in a Forest

Imagine a colony of ants released in a dense forest searching for the nearest food source. In the basic Ant System, ants leave pheromone on **every** path they walk, and successful trails can become infinitely strong while forgotten trails can vanish completely — leading to all ants getting stuck following one mediocre trail forever.

In **MMAS**, the forest has a magical rule: **no single trail can glow brighter than a ceiling ($\tau_{max}$) or fade below a floor ($\tau_{min}$)**. Furthermore, only the *single fastest ant* of the day (or the *all-time record holder*) gets to refresh the trails. This has two powerful effects:
- **The ceiling** prevents the colony from becoming "addicted" to a suboptimal path — there is always a chance for a newly discovered shorter trail to compete.
- **The floor** guarantees that even abandoned paths remain slightly visible, giving them a non-zero probability of being re-explored.

> [!NOTE]
> Think of $\tau_{max}$ as the *maximum brightness* of a torch, and $\tau_{min}$ as the *residual glow* of a candle that never extinguishes completely.

### 1.3 Standard MMAS Parameters (with Units)

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Engineering Range |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Pheromone evaporation rate | **0.02** (recommended by Stützle & Hoos) |
| $\beta$ | Heuristic importance factor | **2.0** to **5.0** |
| $q_0$ | Pseudo-random proportional balance (ACS-mode) | **0.0** to **1.0** |
| $\alpha$ | Pheromone influence exponent | **1.0** (standard) |
| $L^*$ | Length of best-known global tour | measured in problem units (km, seconds, cost) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Six Structured Pillars

**Pillar 1 — Initialization at the Upper Bound.**
At $t = 0$, every pheromone trail is initialized to $\tau_{max}$ rather than an arbitrary value or zero. This causes *maximum initial exploration*, allowing ants to spread out and gather global information before any single trail dominates.

**Pillar 2 — Pheromone Evaporation (Global Mechanism).**
Before new pheromone is deposited, all existing trails decay uniformly:

$$ \tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) $$

This is **identical** to standard AS, but its effect is bounded by $\tau_{min}$ and $\tau_{max}$ below.

**Pillar 3 — Selective Best-Antenna Deposition.**
Only the *iteration-best* or *global-best* ant is allowed to deposit pheromone. This avoids the noisy, dilutive effect of allowing every ant (including those on poor tours) to contribute.

$$ \tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1 - \rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} $$

$$ \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{L^{best}} & \text{if edge } (i,j) \in T^{best} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

**Pillar 4 — Pheromone Trail Bounds (The Core Innovation).**
After every update, trails are **clamped**:

$$ \tau_{ij} \leftarrow \max(\tau_{ij}, \tau_{min}), \qquad \tau_{ij} \leftarrow \min(\tau_{ij}, \tau_{max}) $$

**Pillar 5 — The Upper Bound $\tau_{max}$.**
Theoretically derived as the asymptotic limit a trail converges to when only the global-best ant continually reinforces the same optimal edges:

$$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*}} $$

where $L^*$ is the length of the **best-known solution** found so far. As $L^*$ improves over time, $\tau_{max}$ dynamically **increases**, sharpening the bias toward the new optimum.

**Pillar 6 — The Lower Bound $\tau_{min}$.**
Stützle & Hoos derived a probabilistic lower bound ensuring that any edge has a non-zero probability $p_{best}$ of being selected by the transition rule:

$$ \boxed{\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \left(1 - p_{best}^{1/n}\right)}{\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) \cdot p_{best}^{1/n}}} $$

where $n$ is the number of nodes and $p_{best}$ is a user-defined confidence parameter (typically **0.05**).

> [!NOTE]
> A widely used simplified engineering approximation is $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}}{2n}$, but the rigorous formula above is the **expected KTU examination answer**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*}$ | Upper pheromone bound | Prevents stagnation, scales with solution quality |
| 2 | $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}(1 - p_{best}^{1/n})}{(n/2 - 1)\,p_{best}^{1/n}}$ | Lower pheromone bound | Preserves exploration, prevents early convergence |
| 3 | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta\tau_{ij}^{best}$ | Pheromone update | Reinforces only best-found edges |
| 4 | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{best} = \dfrac{1}{L^{best}}$ | Best-ant deposition amount | Inversely proportional to tour cost |
| 5 | $p_{ij}^k(t) = \dfrac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k}[\tau_{il}]^\alpha [\eta_{il}]^\beta}$ | Probabilistic transition rule | Stochastic exploitation of trail + heuristic |
| 6 | $s = \arg\max_{u \in J_i^k}\{[\tau_{iu}] [\eta_{iu}]^\beta\}$ if $q \le q_0$ | Deterministic pseudo-random-proportional rule (ACS mode) | Direct best-edge exploitation |
| 7 | $\eta_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{d_{ij}}$ | Heuristic visibility | Distance-based preference in TSP |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** In the cheat sheet, no vertical pipes `|x|` are used; absolute values are written with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

MMAS is widely deployed in:
- **Vehicle Routing Problems (VRP)** in logistics and Amazon-style delivery systems.
- **Telecommunications routing** (network packet switching).
- **Job-shop scheduling** in manufacturing.
- **Bioinformatics** (protein folding, DNA sequence alignment).
- **VLSI circuit partitioning** in chip design.

The bounded pheromone framework guarantees **provable convergence** to a near-optimal solution — a property the basic Ant System lacks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Upper Bound $\tau_{max}$

We begin by assuming that the global-best ant repeatedly traverses the **same optimal edges** for an infinite number of iterations, and that no other ant is depositing pheromone. We seek the steady-state value $\tau^*$ at which the update equation equilibrates.

**Step 1 — Write the MMAS update equation restricted to edges belonging to the global-best tour:**

$$ \tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{1}{L^*} $$

**Step 2 — Apply steady-state condition $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) = \tau^*$:**

$$ \tau^* = (1-\rho)\,\tau^* + \frac{1}{L^*} $$

**Step 3 — Expand and isolate $\tau^*$:**

$$ \tau^* - (1-\rho)\,\tau^* = \frac{1}{L^*} $$

$$ \tau^* \cdot [1 - (1-\rho)] = \frac{1}{L^*} $$

$$ \tau^* \cdot \rho = \frac{1}{L^*} $$

**Step 4 — Final closed-form:**

$$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*}} $$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This derivation assumes MMAS uses the *global-best* ant deposition rule. When using the *iteration-best* rule, the same closed form holds, but $L^*$ is replaced by $L^{ib}$ (iteration-best tour length).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Lower Bound $\tau_{min}$

The lower bound is derived from a probabilistic constraint: at steady state, the probability $p_{best}$ of selecting the *best* edge from a node with $n-1$ available edges must equal some small, user-defined value (typically $0.05$). We solve this constraint for $\tau_{min}/\tau_{max}$.

**Step 1 — State the selection probability for the best edge under the proportional pseudo-random rule:**

$$ p_{best} = \frac{\tau_{max}^\alpha \cdot \eta_{best}^\beta}{\tau_{max}^\alpha \cdot \eta_{best}^\beta + \sum_{j \neq best}\tau_{min}^\alpha \cdot \eta_j^\beta} $$

**Step 2 — Use the simplifying assumption $\alpha = 1$, and recognize that there are exactly $(n-1)$ remaining edges. Replace $\eta$ with an average inverse distance $\bar{\eta}$. The best edge's heuristic is also $\eta_{best}$. The simplification (Stützle & Hoos, 2000) yields:**

$$ p_{best} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{max} + (n-2)\,\tau_{min}} $$

because the best edge's term appears once in the numerator, while the remaining $n-2$ edges share $\tau_{min}$ in the denominator (one edge is "used", leaving $n-2$ competitors).

**Step 3 — Solve for $\tau_{min}$:**

$$ p_{best}\bigl[\tau_{max} + (n-2)\tau_{min}\bigr] = \tau_{max} $$

$$ p_{best}\,\tau_{max} + p_{best}(n-2)\tau_{min} = \tau_{max} $$

$$ p_{best}(n-2)\tau_{min} = \tau_{max}(1 - p_{best}) $$

$$ \tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}(1 - p_{best})}{(n-2)\,p_{best}} $$

**Step 4 — Generalize using the $p_{best}^{1/n}$ formulation which accounts for path length rather than single-edge selection. The rigorous expression becomes:**

$$ \boxed{\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max} \cdot \left(1 - p_{best}^{1/n}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{n}{2} - 1\right) \cdot p_{best}^{1/n}}} $$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** This more general form arises when one considers the selection of an entire tour segment rather than a single edge. For a tour of $n$ edges, the joint probability that $n$ "best choices" are made gives the $1/n$ exponent.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behavior Under Varying $\rho$

| Evaporation Rate $\rho$ | Effect on $\tau_{max}$ | Effect on $\tau_{min}$ | Algorithm Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\rho \to 0$ (very slow evaporation) | $\tau_{max} \to \infty$ | $\tau_{min} \to \infty$ | Trails accumulate forever; over-exploration persists |
| $\rho = 0.02$ (recommended) | Moderate bound | Moderate floor | Balanced exploration–exploitation |
| $\rho = 0.5$ (rapid evaporation) | $\tau_{max} \to 2/L^*$ | $\tau_{min}$ very small | Aggressive forgetting; high diversification |
| $\rho \to 1$ | $\tau_{max} \to 1/L^*$ | $\tau_{min} \to 0$ | Trails vanish instantly; no learning |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask students to justify **why $\rho = 0.02$ is empirically optimal**. The answer: it allows the algorithm to "remember" good solutions long enough to refine them while still permitting new discoveries.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MMAS Algorithm Flow — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initialize Graph G and n Ants] --> B[Set Initial Pheromone tau_ij = tau_max]
    B --> C[Compute tau_max = 1 / rho x L_star]
    B --> D[Compute tau_min from tau_max and p_best]
    C --> E[Loop: Iteration t = 1 to t_max]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Each Ant k Constructs Tour using Probabilistic Rule]
    F --> G{Is q less than q_zero?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Deterministic: Choose Best Edge by tau x eta to power beta]
    G -- No --> I[Stochastic: Sample Edge by Probabilities p_ij to power k]
    H --> J[Complete All Ant Tours and Evaluate Tour Lengths L_k]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Identify Best Ant: Global Best L_gb or Iteration Best L_ib]
    K --> L[Apply Pheromone Evaporation: tau_ij = 1 minus rho times tau_ij]
    L --> M[Deposit Pheromone by Best Ant Only on its Tour Edges]
    M --> N[Apply Trail Bounds: Clamp tau_ij between tau_min and tau_max]
    N --> O{Is L_best less than L_star?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Update L_star and Recompute tau_max and tau_min]
    O -- No --> Q[Keep Current L_star]
    P --> R{Termination Condition Met?}
    Q --> R
    R -- No --> E
    R -- Yes --> S[Output Global Best Tour L_star]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Pheromone Bounding Module

```mermaid
block-beta
    block:ID1["Pheromone Update Equation"]
    block:ID2["Clamping Module"]
    ID1 --> ID2
    block:ID3["Lower Bound tau_min"]
    block:ID4["Upper Bound tau_max"]
    ID3 --> ID2
    ID4 --> ID2
    block:ID5["Validated Pheromone Matrix for Next Iteration"]
    ID2 --> ID5
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block Diagram — AS vs. MMAS

```mermaid
block-beta
    block:GRP1["Basic Ant System AS"]
    block:GRP2["Min Max Ant System MMAS"]
    GRP1 --> GRP1a["All Ants Deposit Pheromone"]
    GRP1a --> GRP1b["No Trail Bounds"]
    GRP1b --> GRP1c["Risk of Stagnation"]
    GRP2 --> GRP2a["Only Best Ant Deposits"]
    GRP2a --> GRP2b["Bounded by tau_min and tau_max"]
    GRP2b --> GRP2c["Balanced Exploration"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `ID1`, `GRP1`), no reserved keywords are used as standalone IDs, and all labels contain clean alphanumeric text only.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the two main improvements introduced in Min–Max Ant System (MMAS) over the basic Ant System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two principal improvements are:
1. **Pheromone Trail Limits**: The pheromone value on every edge is restricted to lie between a fixed minimum bound $\tau_{min}$ and maximum bound $\tau_{max}$. This prevents any single trail from growing excessively large (causing stagnation) or decaying to zero (causing loss of useful information).
2. **Selective Deposition by Best Ant**: Only the iteration-best or global-best ant is allowed to deposit pheromone. This intensifies the reinforcement on the best-found solution edges and avoids dilution caused by poor ants.

*(Award 1.5 marks for each correctly stated improvement with brief explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define $\tau_{max}$ and write its mathematical expression as used in MMAS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$\tau_{max}$ is the **upper pheromone bound** enforced in MMAS. It is the asymptotic steady-state limit to which a pheromone trail would converge if the global-best ant continually reinforces the same edges in every iteration. Mathematically:

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*} $$

where $\rho$ is the pheromone evaporation rate and $L^*$ is the length of the best-known global tour. *(Award 2 marks for formula, 1 mark for explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Selected for examination**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]** 

**(a)** With a neat algorithmic structure, explain the **working of Min–Max Ant System (MMAS)** in detail. Highlight how pheromone bounds prevent stagnation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the upper bound $\tau_{max}$ and lower bound $\tau_{min}$ expressions used in MMAS, clearly stating the role of each parameter. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q3(a) — Working of MMAS (7 marks):**

The Min–Max Ant System operates through the following structured sequence of operations:

**Step 1 — Initialization.** A complete graph $G = (V, E)$ is defined. For TSP, $V$ represents $n$ cities and $E$ contains all pairwise connections. Each edge $(i,j)$ is initialized with pheromone $\tau_{ij}(0) = \tau_{max}$, where $\tau_{max}$ is computed using a preliminary estimate of $L^*$. This deliberate high initialization encourages maximum exploration in the initial iterations.

**Step 2 — Solution Construction.** For each ant $k$ placed on a starting city, the next city is selected using a probabilistic rule that balances exploration and exploitation:

$$ p_{ij}^k(t) = \frac{[\tau_{ij}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{ij}]^\beta}{\sum_{l \in J_i^k} [\tau_{il}]^\alpha \cdot [\eta_{il}]^\beta} $$

where $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ is the heuristic visibility, $\alpha$ controls pheromone influence, $\beta$ controls heuristic influence, and $J_i^k$ is the set of unvisited cities.

**Step 3 — Tour Evaluation.** Each completed tour is evaluated by computing its total length $L_k$. The iteration-best $L^{ib}$ and global-best $L^{gb}$ tours are identified.

**Step 4 — Pheromone Evaporation.** All trails decay uniformly:

$$ \tau_{ij}(t) \leftarrow (1-\rho) \cdot \tau_{ij}(t) $$

**Step 5 — Best-Ant Deposition.** Only the selected best ant (either $L^{ib}$ or $L^{gb}$ depending on configuration) deposits pheromone on its tour edges:

$$ \tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{1}{L^{best}} \quad \text{for } (i,j) \in T^{best} $$

**Step 6 — Pheromone Clamping (The Critical MMAS Step).** Every pheromone value is forcibly restricted:

$$ \tau_{ij} \leftarrow \min(\tau_{ij}, \tau_{max}), \qquad \tau_{ij} \leftarrow \max(\tau_{ij}, \tau_{min}) $$

**Stagnation Prevention:** The clamping ensures that no edge ever becomes infinitely more attractive than others. Even if the global-best ant keeps reinforcing the same edges, those trails saturate at $\tau_{max}$. This saturation leaves a non-zero probability that alternative edges (still visible via $\tau_{min}$) will be selected in future iterations, maintaining diversity and preventing premature convergence.

*(Award 1 mark per step, plus 1 mark for explaining stagnation prevention explicitly.)*

---

**Model Answer for Q3(b) — Derivation of $\tau_{max}$ and $\tau_{min}$ (7 marks):**

**Derivation of $\tau_{max}$ (3 marks):**

Consider the steady-state pheromone update on edges belonging to the global-best tour, assuming no other deposition:

$$ \tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \frac{1}{L^*} $$

At equilibrium, $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = \tau_{ij}(t) = \tau^*$:

$$ \tau^* = (1-\rho)\,\tau^* + \frac{1}{L^*} $$

$$ \tau^*\,\rho = \frac{1}{L^*} $$

$$ \boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*}} $$

**Role of Parameters:** $\rho$ controls how quickly trails decay (smaller $\rho$ yields larger $\tau_{max}$), and $L^*$ is the best-found tour length (better $L^*$ yields larger $\tau_{max}$, sharpening search around the optimum).

**Derivation of $\tau_{min}$ (3 marks):**

The lower bound is derived from the requirement that any non-best edge has a non-negligible probability $p_{best}$ of being selected. Using the probabilistic transition rule with $\alpha = 1$ and $n-2$ competitor edges at $\tau_{min}$:

$$ p_{best} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{max} + (n-2)\tau_{min}} $$

Solving:

$$ \tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}(1 - p_{best})}{(n-2)\,p_{best}} $$

Generalizing to whole-tour selection:

$$ \boxed{\tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}\left(1 - p_{best}^{1/n}\right)}{\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) p_{best}^{1/n}}} $$

**Role of Parameters:** $p_{best}$ is the desired selection probability of the best edge (typically $0.05$); $n$ is the number of cities. Smaller $p_{best}$ allows stronger exploration. *(1 mark for parameter role summary.)*

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the **basic Ant System (AS)** with **Min–Max Ant System (MMAS)** in terms of pheromone update, deposition policy, and convergence behavior. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TSP instance has $n = 20$ cities, evaporation rate $\rho = 0.05$, and best-known tour length $L^* = 100$. Compute the value of $\tau_{max}$. Assume $p_{best} = 0.05$ and compute $\tau_{min}$ using the simplified formula $\tau_{min} = \tau_{max}/(2n)$. Comment on the significance of these bounds. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for Q4(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\tau_{max}$:**

$$ \tau_{max} = \frac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*} = \frac{1}{0.05 \times 100} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\tau_{min}$:**

$$ \tau_{min} = \frac{\tau_{max}}{2n} = \frac{0.2}{2 \times 20} = \frac{0.2}{40} = 0.005 $$

**Step 3 — Significance Comment (3 marks):**

The ratio $\tau_{max}/\tau_{min} = 0.2/0.005 = 40$ indicates that the best edge can be at most **40 times more attractive** than a never-visited edge. This bounded ratio prevents the algorithm from locking onto a single solution prematurely. The very small $\tau_{min} = 0.005$ ensures that even abandoned paths retain a non-zero probability of being explored, preserving the swarm's ability to escape local optima — the central advantage of MMAS over the basic AS. *(Award 2 marks for numerical work, 3 marks for significant commentary and comparison.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in MMAS Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the denominator in $\tau_{max}$**: Many students write $\tau_{max} = 1/L^*$ and lose 2 marks. The correct form includes $\rho$: $\tau_{max} = 1/(\rho L^*)$.
> 2. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$**: $\alpha$ is the pheromone exponent; $\beta$ is the heuristic exponent. Swapping them is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Missing the $p_{best}^{1/n}$ exponent**: When asked for the *exact* $\tau_{min}$ formula, omitting the $1/n$ exponent or the $(n/2 - 1)$ factor loses 2 marks. The simplified $\tau_{min} = \tau_{max}/(2n)$ is only acceptable if explicitly permitted.
> 4. **Not explaining WHY MMAS is better than AS**: Simply listing the algorithm without justifying the stagnation prevention or bounded exploration loses the "analytical" component worth 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to update $L^*$ when a new best tour is found**: $\tau_{max}$ is *dynamic*; if $L^*$ improves, $\tau_{max}$ must be recomputed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **MMAS = Ant System + Trail Limits + Selective Best-Antenna Deposition**.
- **Upper bound:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\rho \cdot L^*}$ — dynamically increases as $L^*$ improves.
- **Lower bound:** $\tau_{min} = \dfrac{\tau_{max}(1 - p_{best}^{1/n})}{\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right)p_{best}^{1/n}}$ — ensures residual exploration.
- **Recommended settings:** $\rho = 0.02$, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 2$ to $5$, $p_{best} = 0.05$.
- **Only iteration-best OR global-best ant deposits pheromone** (alternating is also valid).
- **Initial pheromone = $\tau_{max}$** (not arbitrary, not zero).
- **Pheromone clamping** after every update is non-negotiable.
- **MMAS solves the stagnation problem** of basic AS by capping trail intensity.
- **MMAS is provably convergent** under certain conditions, unlike basic AS.
- **Real-world applications:** TSP, VRP, network routing, scheduling, bioinformatics, VLSI.
- **Examiners love:** derivations of $\tau_{max}$, $\tau_{min}$, comparison tables, numerical computations of bounds for given parameters.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rank Based Ant Colony System (RANKAS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rank Based Ant Colony System (RANKAS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Rank Based Ant Colony System (RANKAS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rank Based Ant System (RANKAS)** is an enhanced variant of the classical **Ant System (AS)** proposed by **Bullnheimer, Hartl, and Strauss (1999)**. It modifies the global pheromone update procedure of AS by ranking all ants in the current iteration according to the quality of their constructed solutions, and then allowing only a fixed number of the **best-ranked ants** (including the **best-so-far ant**) to deposit pheromone. Each depositing ant's contribution is weighted by its rank, so the elite ants of the current iteration and the historically best-known solution dominate the pheromone trail, thereby intensifying the search around high-quality regions of the solution space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — RANKAS**
> Belongs to the family of **Ant Colony Optimization (ACO)** metaheuristics. Listed as a direct extension of the basic Ant System under the evolutionary/swarm intelligence module. Mandatory learning outcome: *"Describe and apply rank-based variants of ACO."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a colony of **forager ants** searching for the shortest path from a food source back to the nest. Each ant lays a chemical scent (**pheromone**) along its path. In the basic **Ant System**, every ant — even those that wandered into long, useless paths — releases equal amounts of pheromone. The path therefore becomes noisy.

In **RANKAS**, a "supervisor ant" appears and **ranks** all the foragers from best (shortest path) to worst (longest detour). Only the **top few foragers** are allowed to release scent, and the **better the rank, the stronger the scent they release**. The best path so far in the entire colony's history is given the strongest reinforcement of all. Over time, the trail becomes concentrated on the genuinely best path — exploration is reduced, exploitation is sharpened, and the colony converges faster on the optimal route.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metaphor (KTU-style):** Think of Google Maps recommending routes. RANKAS behaves like an algorithm that only trusts the **top 5 fastest drivers from yesterday (rank)** and gives the **all-time best driver (best-so-far)** extra weight. Slower routes are ignored, regardless of how many drivers took them.

### 1.3 Key Boundary Symbols (Standard ACO Notation)

- **τ(i,j)** — Pheromone trail intensity on edge (i,j)
- **η(i,j)** — Heuristic desirability, usually **1/d(i,j)** for TSP
- **α** — Pheromone influence exponent
- **β** — Heuristic influence exponent
- **ρ** — Pheromone evaporation rate, **0 < ρ < 1**
- **w** — Number of elite ants allowed to deposit pheromone
- **σ** — Weight multiplier for the best-so-far ant (σ ≥ w)
- **r** — Rank position (1 = best of current iteration)
- **L_k** — Tour length of ant k
- **L_bsf** — Length of the best-so-far solution

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step by Step

RANKAS differs from the basic Ant System in **two principal modifications**:

### Modification A — Rank-Based Pheromone Deposition

In basic AS, *every* ant deposits pheromone:
$$
\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij} + \sum_{k=1}^{m}\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}
$$

In RANKAS, only the **best-ranked (w-1) ants** of the current iteration **plus** the **best-so-far ant** deposit pheromone. The amount deposited is **weighted by rank** so that high-quality solutions contribute more.

### Modification B — Rank-Weighted Contribution

The pheromone laid by an ant is **not** simply $1/L_k$, but rather:
$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = \frac{1}{L_k}
$$
**multiplied by a rank-based weight** $w_r$, where $r$ is the rank.

### Why This Works (Intuitive Justification)

- **Elitism bias:** Stronger pheromone reinforcement for elite solutions steers future ants toward promising regions.
- **Reduced noise:** Discarding the worst ants prevents bad solutions from polluting the trail.
- **Stagnation control:** The best-so-far (with the largest weight σ) prevents premature convergence because its constant reinforcement keeps the globally best path alive.
- **Faster convergence:** Empirically shown (Bullnheimer et al., 1999) to outperform basic AS on TSP benchmarks such as TSPLIB.

## 2.2 State Transition (Probabilistic Construction) Rule

This rule is **identical to basic AS**; the innovation of RANKAS lies in the update step only.

For an ant k at node i, the probability of choosing the next city j is:

$$
p_{ij}^{k}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{[\tau_{ij}(t)]^{\alpha}\,\,[\eta_{ij}]^{\beta}}{\displaystyle\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}}[\tau_{il}(t)]^{\alpha}\,\,[\eta_{il}]^{\beta}} & \text{if } j \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k} \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

where $\mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}$ is the set of unvisited neighbours of ant k at node i.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the boxed formulas below. They are the **most-frequently tested equations** for RANKAS in KTU board examinations.

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Formula / Definition** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|
| Pheromone update (RANKAS) | $\tau_{ij} \leftarrow (1-\rho)\tau_{ij} + \sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf} + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1} (w - r) \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{r}$ | Core RANKAS equation |
| Weight of best-so-far ant | $\sigma$ | $\sigma \geq w$ |
| Weight of rank-r ant | $w - r$ | Decreases linearly with rank |
| Number of elite ants per iteration | $w - 1$ | Plus the best-so-far ant |
| Rank-1 weight | $w - 1$ | Best ant of current iteration |
| Pheromone contribution of ant k | $\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = 1/L_k$ | Same as AS |
| Evaporation term | $(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}$ | $0 < \rho < 1$ |
| State transition (probabilistic) | $p_{ij}^{k} = \dfrac{\tau_{ij}^{\alpha}\eta_{ij}^{\beta}}{\sum_{l \in \mathcal{N}_{i}^{k}}\tau_{il}^{\alpha}\eta_{il}^{\beta}}$ | Same as AS |
| Heuristic value (TSP) | $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ | For Travelling Salesman Problem |
| Typical parameter values | $\rho \approx 0.5$, $w \approx 6$, $\sigma \approx 6$, $\alpha = 1$, $\beta = 5$ | Standard benchmark |

> **Engineering Utility:** RANKAS is deployed in real-world routing, scheduling, VLSI circuit partitioning, vehicle routing (VRP), network routing in telecom, supply chain optimisation, and bioinformatics sequence alignment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the RANKAS Pheromone Update Rule

### Starting Point — Basic Ant System (AS)

In classical AS, after all m ants complete their tours, the global update is:
$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t)
$$
where
$$
\Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t) = 
\begin{cases}
1/L_k & \text{if ant }k\text{ used edge}(i,j) \\
0 & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 1 — Sort the Ants by Tour Length

After all m ants complete a tour, the ants are **ranked** in increasing order of $L_k$:
$$
L_{(1)} \leq L_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq L_{(m)}
$$
Ant $(r)$ is the $r^{th}$ best, i.e. rank $r$.

### Step 2 — Restrict the Number of Depositing Ants

In RANKAS, only the **best (w-1)** ranked ants of the current iteration and the **best-so-far (bsf)** ant are allowed to deposit pheromone. Therefore, the contribution sum in the basic AS is replaced by:
$$
\sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf} + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1} (w-r) \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Rank Weighting

The weight of a rank-r ant is chosen as $w - r$ (linear decay). Hence:
- Rank 1 (best of current iteration) → weight = $w - 1$
- Rank 2 → weight = $w - 2$
- ...
- Rank $w-1$ → weight = $1$
- Best-so-far ant → weight = $\sigma$

### Step 4 — Final RANKAS Global Pheromone Update

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r) \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}(t)
$$

**Interpretation of each term:**

| **Term** | **Meaning** |
|---|---|
| $(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t)$ | Pheromone that survives evaporation |
| $\sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf}$ | Reinforcement from the best solution found in **all iterations so far** |
| $\sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r)\Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}$ | Reinforcement from the top $(w-1)$ solutions of the **current iteration**, weighted by rank |

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Illustration

> **Problem:** Suppose a RANKAS run with $m = 5$ ants, $w = 3$, $\sigma = 4$, and the following tour lengths in the current iteration:  $L = [120, 95, 140, 80, 110]$. The best-so-far length is $L_{bsf} = 75$. Edge $(i,j)$ is used by the best-so-far, the rank-1 ant (length 80), and the rank-2 ant (length 95). Find the new pheromone $\tau_{ij}(t+1)$ given $\tau_{ij}(t) = 1.5$ and $\rho = 0.5$.

**Sort the ants (ascending):** 80, 95, 110, 120, 140 → ranks 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

**Step 5a — Evaporation term:**
$$
(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) = (1-0.5)(1.5) = 0.75
$$

**Step 5b — Best-so-far contribution:**
$$
\sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{75} = 0.0533
$$

**Step 5c — Rank-1 ant (length 80) contribution:**
$$
(w-1)\cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{(1)} = (3-1)\cdot \frac{1}{80} = 2 \cdot 0.0125 = 0.025
$$

**Step 5d — Rank-2 ant (length 95) contribution:**
$$
(w-2)\cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{(2)} = (3-2)\cdot \frac{1}{95} = 1 \cdot 0.0105 = 0.0105
$$

**Step 5e — Combine:**
$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 0.75 + 0.0533 + 0.025 + 0.0105
$$

$$
\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 0.8388
$$

**Result:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 0.8388$.

> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always show the three-term decomposition (evaporation + best-so-far + rank-summation) explicitly. Marks are awarded for **term-by-term computation**, not just the final number.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — RANKAS Pipeline

The diagram below maps the **end-to-end operational pipeline** of a RANKAS iteration, including inputs, processes, and outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Start of Iteration t]) --> initPhero[Initialise Pheromone Matrix tau ij]
    initPhero --> setAnts[Place m Ants on Random Cities]
    setAnts --> construct[Tour Construction via State Transition Rule]
    construct --> eval[Evaluate Tour Length Lk for Each Ant]
    eval --> sortBlock[Sort Ants by Tour Length: L1 to Lm]
    sortBlock --> rankBlock{Is Ant Rank less than w?}
    rankBlock -- Yes --> eligible[Mark as Elite Depositor]
    rankBlock -- No --> skip[Skip This Ant]
    eligible --> combine[Combine with Best So Far Solution]
    skip --> combine
    combine --> update[Apply Rank Based Pheromone Update]
    update --> evap[Evaporate Trails: 1 minus rho times tau]
    evap --> deposit[Add Weighted Deposits: sigma times delta tau bsf plus sum rank weights]
    deposit --> newPhero[New Pheromone Matrix tau at t plus 1]
    newPhero --> bsfCheck{Better than Best So Far?}
    bsfCheck -- Yes --> updateBsf[Replace Best So Far Solution]
    bsfCheck -- No --> nextIter
    updateBsf --> nextIter[Increment t and Loop]
    nextIter --> stopCheck{Max Iterations Reached?}
    stopCheck -- No --> setAnts
    stopCheck -- Yes --> outputNode([Output Best So Far Solution])
```

## 4.2 Rank-Weighting Comparison Block (RANKAS vs. AS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph antSystem[Basic Ant System]
        asInput[All m Ants Deposit] --> asWeight[Equal Weight 1 per Ant] --> asTrail[Same Trail Reinforcement]
    end
    subgraph rankAS[Rank Based Ant System]
        rInput[Sort Ants by Lk] --> rFilter{Top w minus 1 plus BSF}
        rFilter -- Yes --> rWeight[Rank Weight w minus r and sigma] --> rTrail[Biased Trail Reinforcement]
        rFilter -- No --> rSkip[No Deposition] 
    end
    asTrail --> comparison[Convergence Behaviour]
    rTrail --> comparison
    comparison --> result[RANKAS: Faster Convergence and Better Solutions]
```

## 4.3 Parameter-Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1[rho Evaporation Rate] --> ctrl1[Controls Exploration versus Exploitation]
    p2[alpha Pheromone Weight] --> ctrl1
    p3[beta Heuristic Weight] --> ctrl1
    p4[w Number of Elite Ants] --> ctrl2[Controls Search Intensity]
    p5[sigma BSF Weight] --> ctrl2
    ctrl1 --> perf[Algorithm Performance]
    ctrl2 --> perf
    perf --> out[Optimal Tour for TSP or VRP]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, PECNT869]**
**Define Rank Based Ant System (RANKAS). How does it differ from the basic Ant System (AS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** RANKAS is an improved Ant System in which ants are ranked according to the quality of their solutions, and only the top-ranked ants together with the best-so-far (BSF) ant deposit pheromone, with deposits weighted by their rank.
- **Difference 1 (1 mark):** In basic AS, *all* m ants deposit pheromone equally, while in RANKAS only the (w-1) best ants of the current iteration and the BSF ant deposit pheromone.
- **Difference 2 (1 mark):** The pheromone deposit in RANKAS is multiplied by a rank-based weight ($w-r$ for rank $r$, and $\sigma$ for the BSF ant), whereas in AS the deposit is $1/L_k$ without any rank weighting. RANKAS therefore converges faster and produces better solutions.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PECNT869]**
**Write the global pheromone update rule of RANKAS and explain the role of the weight σ.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Equation (2 marks):**
  $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r)\,\Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}(t)$$
- **Role of σ (1 mark):** σ is the weight assigned to the best-so-far ant. Since σ ≥ w, the historically best solution contributes the most pheromone, ensuring that the global best path is never lost and providing long-term memory to the search.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECNT869]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Derive the rank-based pheromone update equation of RANKAS from the basic Ant System update. Clearly state the role of each term.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Start with basic AS update (1 mark):**
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k}(t), \quad \Delta\tau_{ij}^{k} = 1/L_k$$

**Step 2 — Rank the ants by tour length (1 mark):**
Sort ascending: $L_{(1)} \leq L_{(2)} \leq \ldots \leq L_{(m)}$.

**Step 3 — Restrict depositing ants to top (w-1) + BSF (1 mark):**
Drop contributions from ranks ≥ w; retain contributions from ranks 1 to (w-1) and from the BSF ant.

**Step 4 — Apply rank weighting (2 marks):**
Weight of rank-r ant = $w-r$; weight of BSF ant = $\sigma$.

**Step 5 — Final equation (1 mark):**
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\,\tau_{ij}(t) + \sigma\,\Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf}(t) + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r)\,\Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}(t)$$

**Step 6 — Role of each term (1 mark):**
- $(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}$ → Evaporation; prevents stagnation.
- $\sigma\,\Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf}$ → Reinforces the best-ever solution.
- Sum term → Reinforces current iteration's elite solutions with rank-biased weight.

**(b) [7 Marks] Consider a RANKAS algorithm with m = 6 ants, w = 4, σ = 5, ρ = 0.4, τ_{ij}(t) = 2.0. In the current iteration, the tour lengths are L = [150, 110, 200, 95, 130, 175]. The best-so-far tour length is L_{bsf} = 85. Suppose edge (i,j) is traversed by the BSF ant, the rank-1 ant, and the rank-2 ant. Compute the new pheromone value τ_{ij}(t+1).**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sort ascending (1 mark):**
$L = [95, 110, 130, 150, 175, 200]$ → ranks 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6.

**Step 2 — Identify the relevant ants (1 mark):**
- BSF ant: $L_{bsf} = 85$
- Rank 1: $L_{(1)} = 95$ (used edge)
- Rank 2: $L_{(2)} = 110$ (used edge)

**Step 3 — Evaporation term (1 mark):**
$$(1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) = (1-0.4)(2.0) = 1.2$$

**Step 4 — BSF contribution (1 mark):**
$$\sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf} = 5 \cdot \frac{1}{85} = 0.0588$$

**Step 5 — Rank-1 contribution (1 mark):**
$$(w-1) \cdot \frac{1}{L_{(1)}} = (4-1) \cdot \frac{1}{95} = 3 \cdot 0.0105 = 0.0316$$

**Step 6 — Rank-2 contribution (1 mark):**
$$(w-2) \cdot \frac{1}{L_{(2)}} = (4-2) \cdot \frac{1}{110} = 2 \cdot 0.00909 = 0.0182$$

**Step 7 — Sum (1 mark):**
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = 1.2 + 0.0588 + 0.0316 + 0.0182 = 1.3086$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau_{ij}(t+1) \approx 1.3086$

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PECNT869, Module 3 Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Compare Ant System (AS), Ant Colony System (ACS), and Rank Based Ant System (RANKAS) in terms of: (i) ants that deposit pheromone, (ii) weighting strategy, (iii) state transition rule, (iv) local pheromone update.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks — 1.75 marks per parameter):**

| **Parameter** | **AS** | **ACS** | **RANKAS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Depositing ants | All m ants | Only the global best (GB) ant | Top (w-1) ants + BSF ant |
| (ii) Weighting | Equal weight $1/L_k$ | GB ant weighted highest | Rank-based weight $(w-r)$; BSF weight $\sigma$ |
| (iii) State transition | Probabilistic (greedy-random) | Pseudo-random proportional with $q_0$ | Same as AS (probabilistic) |
| (iv) Local update | None | Yes — applied after each step | None |

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the algorithm of RANKAS with a step-by-step procedure. State its advantages over the basic Ant System.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Algorithm (4 marks — 0.5 mark per step):**
1. **Initialise** pheromone trails $\tau_{ij}$ and parameters ($\alpha, \beta, \rho, w, \sigma$).
2. **Place** m ants on randomly chosen starting cities.
3. **Construct** tours using the probabilistic state transition rule (same as AS).
4. **Evaluate** the tour length $L_k$ for every ant.
5. **Sort** the ants in ascending order of $L_k$.
6. **Apply** the global pheromone update using the rank-weighted formula:
   $$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf} + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r)\Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}$$
7. **Update** the best-so-far solution if any ant's tour improves it.
8. **Repeat** Steps 2–7 until the termination criterion (max iterations) is met.

**Advantages over basic AS (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Faster convergence** because only the best solutions contribute pheromone.
2. **Reduced stagnation** on poor trails, since the worst ants are filtered out.
3. **Better solution quality** as confirmed by Bullnheimer et al. (1999) on TSPLIB benchmarks such as **Eil101** and **KroA200**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Read Carefully)**
> 1. **Confusing BSF with GB ant:** RANKAS uses **best-so-far (BSF)** — the best across *all* iterations, **not** just the current iteration (GB ant is ACS's term). Mixing them up costs up to **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the $(1-\rho)$ evaporation term:** A frequent student error is to write only the deposit sum. The evaporation term is **compulsory** and worth **1 mark**.
> 3. **Wrong weight for rank-r ant:** It is $w - r$, **not** $r$. The best (rank 1) gets the *highest* weight, $w-1$.
> 4. **Forgetting the value of $\sigma$:** It must satisfy $\sigma \geq w$ to keep BSF trail dominant.
> 5. **Not showing the sort step:** RANKAS requires ranking — examiners expect a sorted list of tour lengths.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] RANKAS = **Rank Based Ant System**, by **Bullnheimer, Hartl, Strauss (1999)**.
- [x] Extension of **basic Ant System (AS)**; state transition rule is **identical** to AS.
- [x] After every iteration, **sort ants by tour length** $L_k$ in ascending order.
- [x] Only the **top (w − 1) ranked ants** of the current iteration deposit pheromone.
- [x] The **best-so-far (BSF)** ant **always** deposits pheromone, regardless of rank.
- [x] **Rank weight** for rank $r$ = $w - r$ (decreases linearly with rank).
- [x] **BSF weight** = $\sigma$, with $\sigma \geq w$.
- [x] Pheromone contribution of ant $k$ = $1/L_k$.
- [x] **Master equation**:
      $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sigma \cdot \Delta\tau_{ij}^{bsf} + \sum_{r=1}^{w-1}(w-r)\Delta\tau_{ij}^{(r)}$
- [x] Evaporation rate $0 < \rho < 1$ (typically 0.5).
- [x] Heuristic value $\eta_{ij} = 1/d_{ij}$ for TSP.
- [x] Advantages: **faster convergence, better solution quality, reduced stagnation** vs. AS.
- [x] Disadvantage: extra **computational cost** of ranking at every iteration.
- [x] Applications: **TSP, VRP, scheduling, network routing, VLSI design, bioinformatics**.
- [x] Tested KTU marks: typically 3 marks (definition/comparison) or 7–14 marks (derivation/numerical).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Behaviour of Real Bees
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Behaviour of Real Bees

# 🐝 Behaviour of Real Bees — KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Behaviour of Real Bees (Swarm Intelligence Perspective)**
> The *behaviour of real bees* refers to the collective foraging and decision-making dynamics exhibited by a honey-bee colony (*Apis mellifera*) in locating, evaluating, recruiting to, and abandoning nectar sources. From the standpoint of **Swarm Intelligence** and **Evolutionary Computing**, this behaviour is mathematically abstracted into a self-organising, decentralised search process in which a population of agents (bees) exchanges information about the quality of candidate solutions (food sources) through a symbolic "dance language," thereby enabling the colony to converge on optimal solutions with minimal central control.

The biological model was popularised in swarm intelligence literature by **Karaboga (2005)**, who formalised it into the **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Algorithm**, the computational counterpart of this natural behaviour.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a group project where the leader has no fixed desk.**
> Imagine **50 friends** dropped randomly across a city, each looking for the *best coffee shop*. Each friend (a **scout**) wanders until they find a shop, then they taste the coffee. They fly back to a central square (the **hive**) and perform a *little dance* whose **enthusiasm** (dance duration) tells other friends *how good* that shop is. Friends watching the dance (**onlookers**) decide probabilistically which shop to visit next — the better the dance, the more friends choose it. The most popular, tastiest shops get *more visitors*; the boring ones are *abandoned*. Over time, the entire crowd converges on the **best coffee shop in town** — and no single person was in charge.

In computing terms:
- **Food source** ⇔ A *candidate solution* (e.g., a set of neural-network weights).
- **Nectar amount** ⇔ The *fitness* of that solution.
- **Waggle dance** ⇔ A *probabilistic recruitment signal*.
- **Colony** ⇔ The *population* of candidate solutions being optimised.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Evolutionary Computing

The bee system is one of the **three classical swarm-intelligence paradigms** (alongside *Ant Colony Optimisation* and *Particle Swarm Optimisation*). It is uniquely valued because:
- It naturally balances **exploration** (scouts) and **exploitation** (employed/onlooker bees).
- It uses **only local information** and indirect (stigmergic-like) communication.
- It maps cleanly onto **continuous, combinatorial, and multi-modal** optimisation problems.
- The recruitment mechanism is *probabilistic* (not deterministic), giving the algorithm built-in **stochastic diversity**.

### 1.4 Key Entities in a Real Bee Colony

| Entity | Biological Role | Algorithmic Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Queen** | Reproduction of the colony | Not directly modelled |
| **Worker (Employed) Bee** | Currently exploiting a known food source | Exploitation phase of ABC |
| **Onlooker Bee** | Watches dances in the hive, decides where to forage | Probabilistic selection phase |
| **Scout Bee** | Searches the environment for new food sources | Exploration phase of ABC |
| **Waggle Dance** | Communicates direction, distance, and quality of source | Fitness-proportional recruitment |
| **Food Source** | A patch of flowers with nectar | A candidate solution with fitness |
| **Hive** | Central information-sharing hub | Global memory of the algorithm |
| **Abandoned Source** | A depleted food patch | A solution dropped after `limit` trials |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The lecturer will almost certainly frame the waggle dance as the *"biological equivalent of pheromone laying in ACO."* Memorise this analogy.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Phase Foraging Cycle

A real bee colony's foraging behaviour decomposes into three well-defined phases that are mirrored directly inside the ABC algorithm.

#### Phase 1 — **Scouting (Exploration)**
- A fraction of the colony (typically **5 %–10 %**) leaves the hive with *no prior information*.
- They perform **Levy-flight-like random walks** in the surrounding environment (radius ≈ 2 – 5 km).
- Upon discovering a food source, each scout returns and **becomes an employed bee**.

#### Phase 2 — **Recruitment (Information Exchange)**
- Back at the hive, employed bees perform the **waggle dance** on the comb.
- The **duration** of the dance is proportional to the **nectar quality** (sugar concentration × quantity).
- Direction encoding: the *angle of the waggle run* relative to vertical = the *angle of the food source* relative to the Sun.
- Distance encoding: the *length of the waggle run* (or duration) = the *distance to the food source*.
- Onlooker bees assess multiple dances and choose probabilistically.

#### Phase 3 — **Exploitation (Onlooker Foraging)**
- Onlookers follow dancers to chosen sources.
- They evaluate the nectar, refine their search *locally* (within a small radius).
- If a source is depleted (fails to improve after `limit` trials), it is **abandoned**, and its bee reverts to a **scout**.

### 2.2 The Waggle Dance — Mathematical Encoding

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The waggle dance is the **only symbolic communication** known in insects. For KTU, the key is the **fitness-proportional recruitment probability** it induces.

Let a colony have $N$ currently known food sources. For source $i$ with nectar amount $fit_i$, the probability that an onlooker bee selects source $i$ is given by:

$$
p_i \;=\; \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} fit_j}
$$

This is **roulette-wheel selection**, the same operator used in Genetic Algorithms, but here driven by a *biological* mechanism.

For minimisation problems, the fitness is transformed by:

$$
fit_i \;=\; \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}, & f_i \geq 0 \\[4pt] 1 + \vert f_i \vert, & f_i < 0 \end{cases}
$$

where $f_i$ is the raw objective function value (cost) at source $i$.

### 2.3 Local Search Around a Food Source

When an employed bee returns to a previously visited source $x_i$, it probes a *neighbour* in **only one dimension** $k$ (a key ABC feature):

$$
v_{i,k} \;=\; x_{i,k} \;+\; \phi_{i,k}\,(x_{i,k} - x_{j,k})
$$

where:
- $j \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$, $j \neq i$ (a randomly chosen *partner* food source).
- $k \in \{1, 2, \dots, D\}$ (a randomly chosen dimension).
- $\phi_{i,k} \in [-1,\, 1]$ is a *uniform random* scaling factor — the *biological equivalent of a step size inspired by visual-odometer noise*.

> [!NOTE]
> The fact that the perturbation uses a **partner solution** rather than the global best is what gives ABC its exploratory character — it does *not* suffer from premature convergence the way PSO sometimes does.

### 2.4 The "Abandonment" Rule

A food source $i$ is **scraped** (abandoned) when it cannot be improved over a fixed number of trials, called the **"limit"**:

$$
\text{limit} \;\approx\; N \cdot D
$$

where $N$ is the colony size and $D$ is the problem dimensionality. The associated employed bee becomes a **scout**, generating a fresh random source:

$$
x_{i,k} \;=\; x_{k}^{\min} \;+\; \mathrm{rand}(0,1)\,(x_{k}^{\max} - x_{k}^{\min})
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Range | Algorithmic Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| $N$ | Number of food sources (employed bees) | $20$ – $100$ | Population size |
| $D$ | Problem dimensionality | Problem-specific | Search-space dimension |
| $limit$ | Abandonment threshold | $N \cdot D$ | Exploration trigger |
| $fit_i$ | Nectar amount of source $i$ | $\geq 0$ | Fitness (selection) |
| $p_i$ | Probability source $i$ is chosen | $0 \le p_i \le 1$ | Onlooker recruitment |
| $\phi_{i,k}$ | Random perturbation coefficient | $[-1, 1]$ | Local-search step |
| $v_{i,k}$ | New candidate position (one dim) | Real-valued | Neighbour solution |
| $x_k^{\min}, x_k^{\max}$ | Bounds on dimension $k$ | User-defined | Feasibility region |
| $MCN$ | Maximum cycle number | $1000$ – $10000$ | Termination criterion |
| $c$ | Dance-duration constant | Empirical | Maps nectar → probability |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Bee-Based Optimisation |
|---|---|
| **Power Systems** | Economic dispatch, unit commitment |
| **Wireless Networks** | Clustering, routing, energy-efficient sleep scheduling |
| **Image Processing** | Multilevel thresholding, segmentation |
| **Mechanical Design** | Gear-train, spring, structural optimisation |
| **Data Mining** | Feature selection, neural-network training |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein structure prediction, motif discovery |
| **Control Systems** | PID tuning, model-predictive control |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU viva questions, you may be asked *"Why bees and not ants?"* — the answer is that **bees recruit probabilistically in a centralised location (the hive)**, whereas **ants recruit stigmergically (via pheromone trails)**. The hive acts as a *shared blackboard*, giving bees an additional layer of information sharing that ants do not have.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Onlooker Recruitment Probability

We derive Equation (2.1) from first principles using a **fitness-proportional** (roulette-wheel) selection model.

**Step 1 — Define the nectar vector.**
Let the colony have discovered $N$ food sources at the current cycle. Each source $i$ carries a nectar amount $n_i \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — Normalise to a probability mass function.**
For the onlooker to make a *probabilistic* choice, the nectar amounts must be converted into a discrete probability distribution that sums to one. The natural choice is the ratio of each source's contribution to the *total* available nectar:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \;=\; 1
$$

This is the standard normalisation axiom of any probability distribution.

**Step 3 — Impose proportionality.**
A direct proportionality between selection probability and nectar amount captures the biological observation that *longer, more vigorous dances recruit more onlookers*:

$$
p_i \;\propto\; n_i
$$

**Step 4 — Combine (3.1) and (3.2).**
Substituting proportionality into the normalisation:

$$
p_i \;=\; \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j}
$$

**Step 5 — Map nectar to fitness.**
In algorithmic terms, nectar is the **fitness** of the solution. So renaming $n_i \rightarrow fit_i$:

$$
\boxed{\,p_i \;=\; \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} fit_j}\,}
$$

This is the canonical ABC selection equation. **Every KTU question on bee-based optimisation begins here.**

### 3.2 Derivation of the Neighbourhood Production Rule

We derive the perturbation formula $v_{i,k} = x_{i,k} + \phi_{i,k}(x_{i,k} - x_{j,k})$ from the biological behaviour of a forager probing the vicinity of a known patch.

**Step 1 — Baseline position.**
A returning forager is at source $i$ with position vector $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$.

**Step 2 — Reference partner.**
The bee selects *another* known source $j$ at random. The biological rationale: bees have spatial memory of multiple patches and use the *difference* between patches to estimate local flower density gradients.

**Step 3 — Dimensional localisation.**
Rather than perturbing *all* dimensions (which would be costly and would mimic a global jump), the bee perturbs **only one dimension** $k$. This matches the *partial-update* biological strategy where the forager refocuses on a single scent gradient.

**Step 4 — Stochastic step.**
A uniformly random scalar $\phi_{i,k} \in [-1, 1]$ is applied. The sign decides direction (towards or away from partner $j$); the magnitude decides step size.

**Step 5 — Combine.**

$$
v_{i,k} \;=\; x_{i,k} \;+\; \phi_{i,k}\,\bigl(x_{i,k} - x_{j,k}\bigr)
$$

**Step 6 — Greedy selection (bees do not accept worse food).**
The newly produced candidate $v_{i,k}$ is compared to $x_{i,k}$ using a greedy mechanism:

$$
x_{i,k} \;\leftarrow\; \begin{cases} v_{i,k}, & f(v_{i,k}) \le f(x_{i,k}) \quad \text{(minimisation)} \\[4pt] x_{i,k}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This **greedy-on-one-dimension** rule is the single most important behavioural difference between real bees and real ants. In ACO, ants always reinforce a *complete* path; in ABC, bees update *one coordinate at a time*.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Behaviour-to-Algorithm Mapping

| Real-Bee Behaviour | Algorithmic Action | Parameter / Equation | Frequency per Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scout flies randomly | Generate new random source | $x_{i,k} = x_k^{\min} + \mathrm{rand}(0,1)(x_k^{\max}-x_k^{\min})$ | Once per `limit` trials |
| Employed bee returns & dances | Compute fitness $fit_i$ | $fit_i = \dfrac{1}{1+f_i}$ | Once per source |
| Waggle-dance duration | Selection probability | $p_i = fit_i / \sum_j fit_j$ | Once per cycle |
| Onlooker follows dancer | Probabilistic source selection | Roulette-wheel on $p_i$ | Once per onlooker |
| Bee probes a flower in same patch | Local search in one dim | $v_{i,k} = x_{i,k} + \phi_{i,k}(x_{i,k}-x_{j,k})$ | Once per employed bee |
| Bee accepts/rejects the new flower | Greedy replacement | $x_{i,k} \leftarrow v_{i,k}$ if better | Once per neighbour trial |
| Source exhausted | Abandon source, recruit scout | `trial[i] > limit` | Conditional |
| Colony reaches max foraging time | Terminate | $cycle \ge MCN$ | End |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 4-source ABC colony has the following cost values: $f_1=2$, $f_2=5$, $f_3=1$, $f_4=4$. Compute the onlooker recruitment probability for each source.

**Step 1 — Convert costs to fitness** (minimisation, all $f_i \ge 0$):

$$
fit_i \;=\; \frac{1}{1+f_i}
$$

$$
fit_1 = \tfrac{1}{3}, \quad fit_2 = \tfrac{1}{6}, \quad fit_3 = \tfrac{1}{2}, \quad fit_4 = \tfrac{1}{5}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum the fitnesses:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} fit_i \;=\; \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{5}
$$

Bring to common denominator $30$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} fit_i \;=\; \frac{10 + 5 + 15 + 6}{30} \;=\; \frac{36}{30} \;=\; \frac{6}{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute probabilities:**

$$
p_1 = \frac{1/3}{6/5} = \frac{5}{18} \approx 0.2778
$$

$$
p_2 = \frac{1/6}{6/5} = \frac{5}{36} \approx 0.1389
$$

$$
p_3 = \frac{1/2}{6/5} = \frac{5}{12} \approx 0.4167
$$

$$
p_4 = \frac{1/5}{6/5} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**

$$
p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + p_4 \;=\; \frac{5}{18} + \frac{5}{36} + \frac{5}{12} + \frac{1}{6}
$$

$$
= \frac{10 + 5 + 15 + 6}{36} \;=\; \frac{36}{36} \;=\; 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Source 3 (lowest cost) is selected with **41.67 %** probability — nearly *triple* the probability of source 2 (highest cost). This illustrates how the waggle-dance-equivalent concentrates onlookers on fitter sources without ever forbidding exploration.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Real-Bee Foraging Colony

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hive Central Blackboard] --> B[Scout Pool]
    A --> C[Employed Bee Pool]
    A --> D[Onlooker Bee Pool]
    B --> E[Random Search in Environment]
    E --> F{New Food Source Found?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Return to Hive with Nectar]
    F -->|No| E
    G --> H[Measure Nectar Quality]
    H --> I[Perform Waggle Dance]
    I --> J[Dance Duration proportional to Nectar]
    J --> D
    D --> K{Onlooker Selects Source by p_i}
    K -->|Probability p_i| L[Fly to Selected Source]
    L --> M[Local Search: Perturb One Dimension]
    M --> N{Better Nectar Found?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Update Memory of Source]
    N -->|No| P[Increment trial counter]
    O --> H
    P --> Q{trial exceeds limit?}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Abandon Source]
    Q -->|No| M
    R --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the **three closed feedback loops** in the diagram: the dance-recruitment loop, the local-improvement loop, and the abandonment-replacement loop. Each loop corresponds to a *distinct equilibrium* the colony maintains between exploration and exploitation.

### 4.2 Bee-Class State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Scout
    Scout --> Employed: Discovers new source
    Employed --> Employed: Local search succeeds
    Employed --> Scout: trial counter exceeds limit
    Employed --> Onlooker: Returns to hive dances
    Onlooker --> Employed: Selects source via p_i
    Onlooker --> Onlooker: Waiting at hive
    Employed --> [*]: Cycle termination MCN reached
    Onlooker --> [*]: Cycle termination MCN reached
    Scout --> [*]: Cycle termination MCN reached
```

### 4.3 Mapping Real-Bee Behaviour to ABC Algorithm Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BIO[Biological Layer]
        B1[Scout foraging]
        B2[Waggle dance]
        B3[Onlooker recruitment]
        B4[Local flower probing]
        B5[Source abandonment]
    end
    subgraph ALG[Algorithmic Layer]
        A1[Initialise random population]
        A2[Fitness evaluation]
        A3[Roulette-wheel selection]
        A4[Neighbour production in one dim]
        A5[Greedy selection and limit check]
    end
    B1 --> A1
    B2 --> A2
    B3 --> A3
    B4 --> A4
    B5 --> A5
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A1
```

> [!TIP]
