## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the role of the waggle dance in the foraging behaviour of real bees.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The waggle dance is the **communication mechanism** by which an *employed bee* informs onlooker bees in the hive about a discovered food source. The **direction** of the waggle run encodes the *direction* of the food source relative to the Sun, the **length** of the waggle run encodes the *distance* to the source, and the **duration / vigour** of the dance is **proportional to the nectar quality** (fitness). In algorithmic terms, it implements a *fitness-proportional recruitment* that channels onlooker bees towards the most promising food sources without central control. **(3 marks)**

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between scout bees, employed bees, and onlooker bees in a real honey-bee colony.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Bee Type | Function | Algorithmic Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Scout** | Performs random search in the environment with no prior knowledge | **Exploration** — generates new candidate solutions |
| **Employed** | Exploits a *known* food source; performs waggle dance on return | **Local search** — perturbs one dimension of current solution |
| **Onlooker** | Stays in hive, watches dances, and probabilistically picks a source to follow | **Selection / Recruitment** — roulette-wheel on fitness |

> The three roles together implement the explore–exploit balance that is the central strength of bee-based optimisation. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

> **Q.A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> (a) Describe the foraging behaviour of real honey bees in detail, highlighting the three phases of foraging. **(7 marks)**
> (b) Explain how the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm maps these biological behaviours into an optimisation procedure. Include the key equations. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

The foraging behaviour of a real honey-bee colony is a **self-organising, decentralised search process** that can be decomposed into three phases:

**1. Scouting Phase (Exploration) [2 marks]**
A small fraction of the colony (5 – 10 %) acts as scouts. They leave the hive and perform stochastic, *Levy-flight-like* trajectories over a foraging radius of several kilometres, searching for new food patches. Upon discovering a patch, a scout evaluates the nectar, loads up, and returns to the hive, transitioning into the *employed* role.

**2. Recruitment Phase (Information Exchange) [2 marks]**
Back in the hive, the employed bee performs the **waggle dance** on the vertical comb surface. The dance encodes three pieces of information:
- *Direction* of the food source relative to gravity (which bees interpret relative to the Sun).
- *Distance* to the food source.
- *Profitability* of the source, encoded as the **duration** of the dance — longer dances for richer nectar.

Onlooker bees in the hive observe multiple dances and choose one to follow, with probability proportional to the dance's duration.

**3. Exploitation Phase (Local Foraging) [2 marks]**
The chosen onlooker flies to the indicated source and performs **local search** within the patch — moving from flower to flower in a small radius. If the nectar improves, the bee updates its memory; if the patch is depleted (no improvement over `limit` trials), the source is **abandoned** and the bee reverts to a *scout*, ensuring continuous exploration.

**Conclusion [1 mark]**
The cycle repeats, with the colony gradually converging on the highest-quality, most accessible food sources in the environment, despite having no central controller.

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

The Artificial Bee Colony algorithm formalises the three-phase behaviour as follows:

**1. Initialisation [1 mark]**
A population of $N$ food sources is randomly initialised in the feasible region:

$$
x_{i,k} \;=\; x_k^{\min} + \mathrm{rand}(0,1)\,(x_k^{\max} - x_k^{\min})
$$

**2. Employed Bee Phase [2 marks]**
Each employed bee produces a neighbour by perturbing a single randomly chosen dimension $k$ of its current source $\mathbf{x}_i$ using a partner source $\mathbf{x}_j$:

$$
v_{i,k} \;=\; x_{i,k} + \phi_{i,k}\,(x_{i,k} - x_{j,k}), \quad \phi_{i,k} \in [-1, 1]
$$

A **greedy selection** is applied: $v_{i,k}$ replaces $x_{i,k}$ only if $f(v_{i,k}) \le f(x_{i,k})$.

**3. Onlooker Bee Phase [2 marks]**
Each food source's nectar is mapped to a fitness value:

$$
fit_i = \frac{1}{1 + f_i}
$$

The probability of being selected is:

$$
p_i = \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} fit_j}
$$

An onlooker performs the same neighbour production as in step 2, with greedy replacement.

**4. Scout Phase [1 mark]**
If a source $i$ cannot be improved after `limit` trials, the corresponding employed bee becomes a scout, and the source is reinitialised randomly:

$$
trial_i > limit \;\Rightarrow\; x_{i,k} = x_k^{\min} + \mathrm{rand}(0,1)(x_k^{\max}-x_k^{\min})
$$

**5. Termination [1 mark]**
The cycle repeats for a maximum of $MCN$ iterations, or until a convergence criterion is met.

This mapping preserves all three biological roles (scout, employed, onlooker), the waggle-dance recruitment, the local search, and the abandonment mechanism, producing a powerful, general-purpose metaheuristic.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

> **Q.B [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> (a) With neat equations, explain the fitness-proportional recruitment mechanism in the ABC algorithm. Show that the recruitment probabilities form a valid probability distribution. **(7 marks)**
> (b) An ABC colony with $N = 5$ food sources has the following cost values: $f_1 = 1$, $f_2 = 4$, $f_3 = 2$, $f_4 = 7$, $f_5 = 3$. Compute (i) the fitness of each source, and (ii) the probability of an onlooker selecting each source. Verify that the probabilities sum to one. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

The waggle dance of an employed bee is observed by onlooker bees inside the hive. The *longer and more vigorous* the dance, the *richer* the food source. Mathematically, this recruitment behaviour is modelled as a **fitness-proportional (roulette-wheel) selection.**

**Equation 1 — Selection probability [3 marks]**
For a source $i$ with fitness $fit_i$, the probability of an onlooker choosing it is:

$$
p_i = \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} fit_j}
$$

**Equation 2 — Fitness transform (minimisation) [1 mark]**

$$
fit_i = \frac{1}{1 + f_i} \quad \text{for } f_i \geq 0
$$

**Proof of valid probability distribution [3 marks]**

We need to show two properties:

**(i) Non-negativity:** Since $f_i \ge 0$ for all $i$, the fitness $fit_i = 1/(1+f_i)$ satisfies $0 < fit_i \le 1$. Therefore $p_i \ge 0$ for all $i$.

**(ii) Normalisation (sums to 1):**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} fit_j} \;=\; \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} fit_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} fit_j} \;=\; 1
$$

Hence $\{p_i\}_{i=1}^{N}$ is a valid probability mass function. ∎

##### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

Given $N = 5$ food sources with costs $f_1=1$, $f_2=4$, $f_3=2$, $f_4=7$, $f_5=3$.

**Part (i) — Fitness computation [3 marks]**
Using $fit_i = \dfrac{1}{1+f_i}$:

$$
fit_1 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5000
$$

$$
fit_2 = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2000
$$

$$
fit_3 = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333
$$

$$
fit_4 = \frac{1}{8} = 0.1250
$$

$$
fit_5 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.2500
$$

**Part (ii) — Sum of fitnesses [1 mark]**

$$
\sum fit_i = 0.5000 + 0.2000 + 0.3333 + 0.1250 + 0.2500 = 1.4083
$$

**Part (iii) — Recruitment probabilities [2 marks]**

$$
p_1 = \frac{0.5000}{1.4083} \approx 0.3550
$$

$$
p_2 = \frac{0.2000}{1.4083} \approx 0.1420
$$

$$
p_3 = \frac{0.3333}{1.4083} \approx 0.2367
$$

$$
p_4 = \frac{0.1250}{1.4083} \approx 0.0888
$$

$$
p_5 = \frac{0.2500}{1.4083} \approx 0.1775
$$

**Part (iv) — Verification [1 mark]**

$$
p_1 + p_2 + p_3 + p_4 + p_5 \approx 0.3550 + 0.1420 + 0.2367 + 0.0888 + 0.1775 = 1.0000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Interpretation:** Source 1 (lowest cost) is recruited with the highest probability, while source 4 (highest cost) is least likely — exactly mirroring the biological observation that *richer food sources attract more onlookers* through more vigorous waggle dances.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Bee-Based Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the waggle-dance duration with pheromone concentration** (an ant-colony concept). The dance is *not* deposited on the environment; it is *performed in the hive* on a comb. — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 2. **Perturbing all $D$ dimensions at once** instead of *only one*. The single-dimension perturbation is a *signature* feature of ABC. Writing a PSO-like update will be heavily penalised. — *Lose 2 marks.*
> 3. **Forgetting to verify $\sum p_i = 1$** in numerical problems. Examiners explicitly test this property. — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 4. **Stating $limit = N$ instead of $limit = N \cdot D$.** This is a frequently-asked valuation trap. — *Lose 1 mark.*
> 5. **Not distinguishing the employed-bee and onlooker-bee phases.** Both use the same neighbour formula, but the *selection mechanism* differs: employed bees act *deterministically* on their own source, onlookers act *probabilistically* on a chosen source. — *Lose 2 marks.*
> 6. **Using $\phi_{i,k} \in [0, 1]$ instead of $[-1, 1]$.** The negative range is essential for bidirectional exploration. — *Lose 1 mark.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Behaviour of Real Bees**

- ☐ A real honey-bee colony exhibits **self-organised, decentralised foraging** with three roles: **scout, employed, onlooker**.
- ☐ The **waggle dance** is the central communication mechanism; it encodes **direction, distance, and quality** of food sources.
- ☐ The **duration** of the waggle dance is proportional to **nectar amount** = fitness.
- ☐ Onlooker bees select sources via **roulette-wheel / fitness-proportional** selection: $p_i = fit_i / \sum fit_j$.
- ☐ Fitness transform for minimisation: $fit_i = 1/(1 + f_i)$.
- ☐ Local search uses a **single-dimension perturbation** with a random partner: $v_{i,k} = x_{i,k} + \phi_{i,k}(x_{i,k} - x_{j,k})$, with $\phi_{i,k} \in [-1, 1]$.
- ☐ A **greedy selection** is applied — better nectar always replaces worse.
- ☐ A food source is **abandoned** when `trial[i] > limit`, where **`limit ≈ N · D`**.
- ☐ A scout generates a fresh random source within the bounds $[x_k^{\min}, x_k^{\max}]$.
- ☐ The algorithm's three-way balance — **scout (exploration), employed (local), onlooker (recruitment)** — gives ABC its robustness.
- ☐ The cycle terminates after **MCN** iterations or upon convergence.
- ☐ **Compared to ACO:** bees recruit in a *centralised hive* via symbolic dance; ants recruit *stigmergically* via pheromone trails in the environment.
- ☐ **Compared to PSO:** ABC perturbs *one dimension* with a *random partner*; PSO uses *global best* and *all dimensions* — making ABC less prone to premature convergence.
- ☐ Real-world applications span **power systems, image processing, wireless networks, mechanical design, and bioinformatics**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute mnemonic — "S-E-O = S(earch)-E(xploit)-O(rchestrate)":**
> *S*couts **search** randomly,
> *E*mployed bees **exploit** locally,
> *O*nlookers **orchestrate** by recruiting from both.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ABC Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** ABC Algorithm ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) Algorithm** is a population-based, nature-inspired metaheuristic optimization technique proposed by **Derviş Karaboğa** in **2005**. It mathematically models the intelligent foraging behavior of a swarm of honey bees (*Apis mellifera*) and is used to locate near-optimal solutions in multi-dimensional, non-linear, and constrained search spaces. In the ABC paradigm, the **position of a food source** corresponds to a candidate solution of the optimization problem, while the **nectar amount** represents the *quality* (fitness) of that solution. The colony is partitioned into three functionally distinct agent classes: - **Employed Bees (EB):** Equal in number to the food sources; exploit a specific source and share information. - **Onlooker Bees (OB):** Remain in the hive; probabilistically select sources based on shared information. - **Scout Bees (SB):** Conduct random exploration when a source is exhausted (abandoned). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECNT869 Module 4):** > The ABC algorithm is a flagship Swarm Intelligence algorithm. It belongs to the broader class of **Swarm Intelligence (SI)** methods and is foundational for understanding modern hybrid optimizers. Under NEP 2020 and KTU 2024 OBE frameworks, students are expected to demonstrate **CO4 (Apply evolutionary computing techniques to solve real-world optimization problems)** and **CO5 (Analyze the convergence behavior of swarm-based algorithms)**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Honey Hunter" Metaphor Imagine a bee colony with **200 bees** tasked to find the richest flower patch in a vast valley. They cannot communicate by language; they communicate through a **waggle dance**. 1. **The Scouts (200 bees)** fly out in random directions, each landing on a random patch. They taste the nectar and return to the hive. 2. Half the bees (100) become **Employed Bees**, each becoming the "owner" of a patch. They return repeatedly, refining their search around the patch, and **dance** at the hive floor. The *longer and more vigorous* the dance, the richer the patch. 3. The other half, **Onlooker Bees (100)**, watch the dances. A patch with a long, energetic dance attracts more onlooker bees (more "investment"). Poor patches attract few followers. 4. If a patch becomes exhausted (no improvement for a threshold number of trials, say 20), the employed bee **abandons** it, becomes a **Scout**, and searches randomly again. **Mapping the Analogy to ABC Mathematics:** | Bee Colony Element | ABC Algorithm Equivalent | |---|---| | Valley of flowers | Search Space (Decision Variable Domain) | | A flower patch | A candidate solution (vector $\vec{x}_i$) | | Nectar amount of a patch | Fitness value $fit_i$ | | Waggle dance intensity | Probability $P_i$ of selection | | Forager bee | Search agent / Artificial bee | | Exhausted patch | Abandoned food source | | Limit threshold | `limit` (abandonment counter) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Unlike Genetic Algorithms (GAs) which use crossover/mutation, ABC uses **neighborhood-based perturbation** and **probabilistic greedy selection** — making it simple, derivative-free, and highly parallelizable. ### 1.3 Standard Algorithmic Parameters The following parameters are standard across KTU reference materials and Karaboğa's seminal work: - **SN (Food Source Number)** = number of employed bees = number of onlooker bees = **N/2** (where N is total colony size) - **D (Dimension)** = number of decision variables - **MCN (Maximum Cycle Number)** = termination criterion (max iterations) - **limit** = abandonment threshold, typically **SN × D** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Algorithmic Architecture — The Four Operational Phases The ABC algorithm executes iteratively through **four rigidly structured phases** per cycle: #### Phase 1: Initialization Phase - Generate **SN** food source positions randomly within the bounded search space. - Compute initial fitness of each source. - Set trial counter `trial[i] = 0` for all food sources. #### Phase 2: Employed Bee Phase - Each employed bee produces a **new candidate solution** $v_i$ by perturbing its currently assigned source $x_i$. - Apply **greedy selection**: if $fit(v_i) > fit(x_i)$, replace $x_i$ with $v_i$ and reset `trial[i] = 0`; otherwise increment `trial[i]`. - Share information (nectar info) with onlookers via waggle dance. #### Phase 3: Onlooker Bee Phase - Each onlooker chooses a food source using **roulette-wheel probabilistic selection** based on $P_i$. - Once a source is selected, the onlooker performs the same perturbation-and-greedy-update as an employed bee. #### Phase 4: Scout Bee Phase - If `trial[i] > limit` for any source $x_i$, it is **abandoned**. - The employed bee associated with it becomes a scout and generates a **new random food source**. - Set `trial[i] = 0` for the new source. ### 2.2 The Core 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step - **Why greedy selection?** To ensure monotonic non-degradation of the best-so-far solutions, mimicking survival-of-the-fittest. - **Why probability-based onlooker selection?** To allocate more computational effort to promising regions, balancing *exploitation* (employed bees refining locally) and *exploration* (scouts searching globally). - **Why the abandonment mechanism?** To escape local optima — the `limit` parameter acts as a *stagnation detector*. Without it, the algorithm would converge prematurely to a local minimum. - **How does the perturbation work?** It uses a *difference-vector* approach similar to DE (Differential Evolution), making ABC robust in continuous spaces. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Equation | Purpose / Application | |---|---|---| | **Initialization** | $x_{ij} = LB_j + \text{rand}(0,1) \cdot (UB_j - LB_j)$ | Randomly initialize food source $i$ in dimension $j$ within bounds | | **Neighbor Source Generation** | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ | Produce new candidate by perturbing $x_{ij}$ using partner $x_{kj}$ | | **Random Coefficient** | $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$ | Controls step size; uniform random perturbation | | **Fitness Conversion (for minimization)** | $fit_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}$ if $f_i \geq 0$, else $1 + \vert f_i \vert$ | Converts objective value to a *maximization* fitness (always positive) | | **Selection Probability** | $P_i = \dfrac{fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n}$ | Roulette-wheel probability for onlooker bees | | **Greedy Acceptance** | $x_i^{new} = v_i$ if $fit(v_i) > fit(x_i)$ else $x_i^{old}$ | Selection operator (1-to-1 comparison) | | **Abandonment Rule** | if $trial_i > limit$ then $x_i$ is replaced by a random scout | Escape local optima | | **Standard `limit` Value** | $limit = SN \cdot D$ | Recommended abandonment threshold (Karaboğa 2005) | | **Colony Sizing Rule** | $\vert \text{Employed} \vert = \vert \text{Onlooker} \vert = SN$ | Maintains balance between exploitation and exploration | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Notation Convention:** Throughout KTU materials, $f_i$ denotes the *objective function value* (to be minimized), while $fit_i$ denotes the *converted fitness* (to be maximized). Do NOT confuse the two in exam answers. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The ABC algorithm has been applied extensively in: - **Electrical Power Systems:** Economic load dispatch, optimal power flow. - **Mechanical Design:** Structural optimization, gear train design. - **Image Processing:** Feature selection, image segmentation, thresholding. - **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN):** Cluster head selection, energy-aware routing. - **Bioinformatics:** Protein structure prediction, gene selection. - **Machine Learning:** Training neural networks (ABC-ANN), hyperparameter tuning of SVMs. > [!IMPORTANT] > The ABC algorithm's strength lies in its **few control parameters** (only `SN`, `MCN`, `limit`), unlike GA which requires tuning crossover rate, mutation rate, selection pressure, and population size. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Fitness Conversion Formula The objective function $f(\vec{x})$ of a minimization problem returns values in the range $(-\infty, \infty)$, with the *best* value being the *smallest*. However, the **roulette-wheel selection** mechanism in the onlooker phase requires a *positive, maximizing* metric. Thus, a transformation is essential. **Case 1: $f_i \geq 0$ (non-negative objective)** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{We require } fit_i &> 0 \text{ and } fit_i \text{ to be monotonically increasing in quality.} \\ \text{Since lower } f_i & \text{ is better, we need } fit_i \text{ to be higher when } f_i \text{ is lower.} \\ \text{Hence, we invert: } fit_i &= \frac{1}{1 + f_i} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Verification of properties:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{If } f_i = 0 \text{ (best): } & \quad fit_i = \frac{1}{1+0} = 1 \quad \text{(maximum fitness)} \\ \text{If } f_i = 1: & \quad fit_i = \frac{1}{1+1} = 0.5 \\ \text{If } f_i \to \infty: & \quad fit_i \to 0 \quad \text{(worst fitness)} \\ \end{aligned} $$ This is valid only when $f_i \geq 0$ because $1 + f_i > 0$ in this domain. **Case 2: $f_i < 0$ (negative objective, e.g., profit maximization cast as minimization)** If $f_i = -2$, then $1 + f_i = -1$, which is invalid (fitness would be negative). Therefore, for $f_i < 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} fit_i &= 1 + \vert f_i \vert \\ \text{If } f_i = -1: & \quad fit_i = 1 + 1 = 2 \\ \text{If } f_i = -5: & \quad fit_i = 1 + 5 = 6 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Unified Fitness Function:** $$ \boxed{ fit_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}, & \text{if } f_i \geq 0 \\[10pt] 1 + \vert f_i \vert, & \text{if } f_i < 0 \end{cases} } $$ ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of the Selection Probability The onlooker bees employ **roulette-wheel selection**. Given $SN$ food sources with fitness values $fit_1, fit_2, \ldots, fit_{SN}$, the probability of selecting source $i$ must satisfy: **Requirement 1: All probabilities are non-negative.** $$ P_i \geq 0 \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, SN\} $$ Since $fit_i > 0$ by construction (per Section 3.1), this holds. **Requirement 2: Sum of probabilities equals 1 (normalization).** $$ \sum_{i=1}^{SN} P_i = 1 $$ The natural form is: $$ P_i = \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n} $$ **Verification of normalization:** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum_{i=1}^{SN} P_i &= \sum_{i=1}^{SN} \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n} \\ &= \frac{1}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n} \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{SN} fit_i \\ &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{SN} fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n} = 1 \quad \checkmark \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Cumulative Probability (for algorithmic implementation):** $$ CumP_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} P_j $$ A uniform random number $r \in [0,1]$ is drawn, and source $k$ is selected if $CumP_{k-1} < r \leq CumP_k$. ### 3.3 Worked Example: One Iteration of the ABC Algorithm **Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$ over $x_1, x_2 \in [-5, 5]$. **Given (hypothetical iteration data):** - $SN = 3$, $D = 2$, $limit = 10$. - Three current food sources: $$ \vec{x}_1 = (0.0,\ 1.0), \quad \vec{x}_2 = (1.0,\ 2.0), \quad \vec{x}_3 = (-1.0,\ 0.0) $$ - Trial counters: $trial_1 = 2,\ trial_2 = 5,\ trial_3 = 0$. **Step 1: Compute Objective Values $f_i$** $$ \begin{aligned} f_1 &= (0)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0 \\ f_2 &= (1)^2 + (2-1)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\ f_3 &= (-1)^2 + (0-1)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Convert to Fitness $fit_i$ (since all $f_i \geq 0$)** $$ \begin{aligned} fit_1 &= \frac{1}{1+0} = 1.000 \\ fit_2 &= \frac{1}{1+2} = 0.333 \\ fit_3 &= \frac{1}{1+2} = 0.333 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Selection Probabilities $P_i$** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum fit &= 1.000 + 0.333 + 0.333 = 1.666 \\ P_1 &= \frac{1.000}{1.666} = 0.6003 \\ P_2 &= \frac{0.333}{1.666} = 0.1999 \\ P_3 &= \frac{0.333}{1.666} = 0.1999 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Employed Bee Phase — Generate New Source for $\vec{x}_1$** Pick partner $k = 2$, dimension $j = 1$, random $\phi = -0.5$: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{1,1} &= x_{1,1} + \phi \cdot (x_{1,1} - x_{2,1}) \\ &= 0.0 + (-0.5) \cdot (0.0 - 1.0) \\ &= 0.0 + (-0.5)(-1.0) = 0.5 \\ \end{aligned} $$ Keep $v_{1,2} = x_{1,2} = 1.0$ (untouched dimension). So $\vec{v}_1 = (0.5,\ 1.0)$. **Evaluate:** $f(\vec{v}_1) = (0.5)^2 + (1-1)^2 = 0.25$ $$ \begin{aligned} fit(\vec{v}_1) &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.25} = 0.80 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Greedy selection:** $fit(\vec{v}_1) = 0.80 < fit(\vec{x}_1) = 1.00$. → **Reject** $\vec{v}_1$, retain $\vec{x}_1$, set $trial_1 = 3$. **Step 5: Onlooker Bee Phase — Roulette Selection** Draw $r = 0.42$. Cumulative: $CumP_1 = 0.60$, $CumP_2 = 0.80$, $CumP_3 = 1.00$. Since $0.42 \leq 0.60$, source **1** is selected. (Multiple onlookers may also select it; expected visits $\propto P_1$.) **Step 6: Scout Bee Phase** Check $trial_i > limit = 10$? No. No source is abandoned. **Result of this iteration:** Best solution is still $\vec{x}_1 = (0.0, 1.0)$ with $f = 0$ (the true global optimum!). Population diversity maintained. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ABC vs. Other Swarm Algorithms | Property | ABC (2005) | PSO (1995) | ACO (1991) | GA (1975) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Inspiration** | Honey bee foraging | Bird flocking | Ant pheromone trails | Biological evolution | | **Operators** | Perturbation + greedy + abandonment | Velocity update + position update | Pheromone deposit + evaporation | Selection + crossover + mutation | | **Information Sharing** | Probabilistic (onlooker) | Global best (gbest) or local (lbest) | Pheromone trail (stigmergy) | Population statistics (fitness) | | **Control Parameters** | **3** (SN, MCN, limit) | 4 (w, c1, c2, Vmax) | 5+ ($\alpha, \beta, \rho, Q, m$) | 4+ (Pc, Pm, pop, gen) | | **Premature Convergence Risk** | Low (due to scouts) | Medium-High | Medium | Medium | | **Constraint Handling** | Penalty / repair | Penalty / repair | Penalty | Penalty | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 ABC Algorithm — Top-Level Block Architecture Flow The following Mermaid block diagram maps the functional architecture and control flow of one complete iteration of the ABC algorithm. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start ABC Algorithm] --> B[Initialization Phase
Generate SN food sources randomly] B --> C[Compute fitness fit_i for each source] C --> D[Initialize trial counter
trial i = 0 for all i] D --> E{Iteration lt MCN?} E -- No --> Z[Return Best Solution and Best Fitness] E -- Yes --> F[Employed Bee Phase
Produce v_i from x_i using neighbor] F --> G{Greedy Selection
fit v_i gt fit x_i?} G -- Yes --> H[Accept v_i as new x_i
Reset trial i = 0] G -- No --> I[Reject v_i
Increment trial i] H --> J[Compute Probability P_i
P_i equals fit_i over sum fit_n] I --> J J --> K[Onlooker Bee Phase
Roulette wheel select source] K --> L[Onlooker Perturbs selected source
Produce new candidate] L --> M{Greedy Selection
for onlooker update} M -- Yes --> N[Update food source
Reset trial] M -- No --> O[Increment trial counter] N --> P[Scout Bee Phase
Find max trial i] O --> P P --> Q{trial max gt limit?} Q -- Yes --> R[Abandon worst source
Generate random new source
Reset trial to 0] Q -- No --> S[No abandonment
Proceed to next iteration] R --> S S --> E Z --> END[End of ABC Run] ``` ### 4.2 Bee Colony Functional Architecture — Agent Interactions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Random Initialization
Generate SN food sources] --> B[Employed Bees
SN agents] B --> C{Compare with old source} C -- Better --> D[Update Food Source] C -- Worse --> E[Increment trial counter] D --> F[Compute Probabilities P_i] E --> F F --> G[Onlooker Bees
SN agents] G --> H{Source trial i gt limit} H -- Yes --> I[Scout Bees
Random new source] H -- No --> J[Continue to next cycle] I --> J J --> B ``` ### 4.3 State Transition Diagram for a Food Source ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> ActiveSource : Initialization ActiveSource --> ActiveSource : Improvement found (trial = 0) ActiveSource --> StagnantSource : No improvement (trial++) StagnantSource --> ActiveSource : Late improvement (trial reset) StagnantSource --> AbandonedSource : trial > limit AbandonedSource --> ActiveSource : Scout creates new source ActiveSource --> [*] : Algorithm terminates StagnantSource --> [*] : Algorithm terminates AbandonedSource --> [*] : Algorithm terminates ``` ### 4.4 Neighbourhood Perturbation Schematic The new candidate solution $v_{ij}$ is generated by perturbing the current solution $x_{ij}$ along the difference vector to a randomly chosen partner $x_{kj}$: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CurrentFoodSource A[x_i,1] B[x_i,2] C[x_i,j] D[x_i,D] end subgraph PartnerFoodSource E[x_k,1] F[x_k,2] G[x_k,j] H[x_k,D] end A --> I[Dimension j selected] B --> I C --> I D --> I E --> I F --> I G --> I H --> I I --> J[v_ij equals x_ij plus phi_ij times x_ij minus x_kj] J --> K[New Candidate v_i] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** Only one dimension $j$ of the food source is perturbed per update (a common ABC variant). Some implementations perturb multiple dimensions. The partner $k$ is chosen uniformly at random from the other $SN-1$ sources. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *List and briefly explain the three types of bees in the Artificial Bee Colony algorithm.* **Model Answer:** The ABC algorithm divides the artificial bee colony into three functional groups: 1. **Employed Bees (EB):** They are equal in number to the food sources ($SN$). Each employed bee is associated with exactly one food source and exploits it by performing local search via the perturbation equation $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$. They share information about the source's nectar amount to onlookers through the waggle dance. 2. **Onlooker Bees (OB):** They remain in the hive and probabilistically select a food source to exploit based on the probability $P_i = fit_i / \sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n$. Sources with higher fitness attract more onlookers, implementing a fitness-proportional selection mechanism that allocates computational effort to promising regions. 3. **Scout Bees (SB):** When a food source cannot be improved for `limit` consecutive trials, it is abandoned. The employed bee associated with it becomes a scout and performs a random global search to discover a new food source, ensuring exploration of the search space and preventing premature convergence. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *What is the role of the `limit` parameter in ABC? What is its typical value?* **Model Answer:** The `limit` parameter is the **abandonment threshold** that controls when a food source is considered exhausted. If a food source $x_i$ cannot be improved after `limit` consecutive trials (i.e., $trial_i > limit$), it is **abandoned** and replaced by a new random source generated by a scout bee. **Role:** It acts as a *stagnation detector* that prevents the algorithm from being trapped in local optima. By forcing exploration when a source is exhausted, `limit` balances the trade-off between exploitation and exploration. **Typical Value:** As recommended by Karaboğa (2005) in his seminal paper, the standard value is: $$ limit = SN \times D $$ where $SN$ is the number of food sources and $D$ is the dimensionality of the problem. For example, for $SN = 50$ and $D = 30$, $limit = 1500$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Adaptation]** **(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm in detail. Describe each phase of the algorithm with the relevant equations.* **(b)** [7 Marks] *Consider the minimization problem $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2$ with bounds $-5 \leq x_j \leq 5$. Suppose at some iteration, two food sources are:* - $\vec{x}_1 = (0.5, 2.0)$ with $trial_1 = 3$ - $\vec{x}_2 = (1.0, 1.0)$ with $trial_2 = 5$ *Given $SN = 2$, $D = 2$, $limit = 10$, and assuming an employed bee for $\vec{x}_1$ uses partner $k = 2$, $j = 1$, $\phi_{11} = 0.3$, perform the Employed Bee Phase and determine whether the new solution is accepted. Also compute the selection probabilities for both sources.* --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Detailed Explanation of ABC Algorithm [7 Marks]** The ABC algorithm is a swarm intelligence-based optimization method proposed by Karaboğa (2005). It mimics the foraging behavior of honey bees. The algorithm has **four main phases**: **Phase 1: Initialization** Food sources are randomly initialized within the search space bounds: $$ x_{ij} = LB_j + \text{rand}(0,1) \cdot (UB_j - LB_j) $$ **Phase 2: Employed Bee Phase** Each employed bee produces a new candidate solution: $$ v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj}), \quad \phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1] $$ A **greedy selection** is applied: if $fit(v_i) > fit(x_i)$, the new solution replaces the old one, and $trial_i = 0$; else $trial_i$ is incremented. **Phase 3: Onlooker Bee Phase** Onlooker bees select a food source using **roulette-wheel probability**: $$ P_i = \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n} $$ The selected source is then perturbed using the same equation as in Phase 2. **Phase 4: Scout Bee Phase** If any $trial_i > limit$, the source is abandoned, and the employed bee becomes a scout that generates a new random source. The fitness of a source is computed as: $$ fit_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i}, & \text{if } f_i \geq 0 \\ 1 + \vert f_i \vert, & \text{if } f_i < 0 \end{cases} $$ The algorithm terminates after $MCN$ (Maximum Cycle Number) iterations. --- **(b) Worked Numerical Problem [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Compute objective values for current sources** $$ \begin{aligned} f(\vec{x}_1) &= (0.5)^2 + (2 - 2)^2 = 0.25 + 0 = 0.25 \\ f(\vec{x}_2) &= (1.0)^2 + (1 - 2)^2 = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.00 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Convert to fitness (since both $f_i \geq 0$)** $$ \begin{aligned} fit_1 &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.25} = 0.8000 \\ fit_2 &= \frac{1}{1 + 2.00} = 0.3333 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Employed Bee Phase for $\vec{x}_1$** Given $k = 2$, $j = 1$, $\phi_{11} = 0.3$: $$ \begin{aligned} v_{1,1} &= x_{1,1} + \phi_{11} \cdot (x_{1,1} - x_{2,1}) \\ &= 0.5 + 0.3 \cdot (0.5 - 1.0) \\ &= 0.5 + 0.3 \cdot (-0.5) \\ &= 0.5 - 0.15 = 0.35 \\ \end{aligned} $$ Other dimensions are kept: $v_{1,2} = x_{1,2} = 2.0$. So $\vec{v}_1 = (0.35,\ 2.0)$. **Step 4: Evaluate the new solution** $$ \begin{aligned} f(\vec{v}_1) &= (0.35)^2 + (2.0 - 2)^2 \\ &= 0.1225 + 0 = 0.1225 \\ fit(\vec{v}_1) &= \frac{1}{1 + 0.1225} = 0.8910 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Greedy Selection** $$ fit(\vec{v}_1) = 0.8910 > fit(\vec{x}_1) = 0.8000 $$ → **Accepted!** Update $\vec{x}_1 \leftarrow (0.35, 2.0)$, reset $trial_1 = 0$. **Step 6: Compute Selection Probabilities** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum fit &= 0.8000 + 0.3333 = 1.1333 \\ P_1 &= \frac{0.8000}{1.1333} = 0.7060 \quad (70.6\%) \\ P_2 &= \frac{0.3333}{1.1333} = 0.2940 \quad (29.4\%) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Source 1 has a higher probability of being selected by onlooker bees, consistent with its better fitness. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Sample Paper Adaptation]** **(a)** [7 Marks] *Discuss the role of employed bees, onlooker bees, and scout bees in the ABC algorithm. Compare ABC with the Genetic Algorithm (GA) in terms of operators, parameters, and convergence behavior.* **(b)** [7 Marks] *For a maximization problem, two food sources have objective values $f_1 = -3$ and $f_2 = 5$. Compute the fitness values $fit_1$ and $fit_2$ using the ABC fitness conversion rule. Then calculate the probabilities $P_1$ and $P_2$ for onlooker bee selection. If 100 onlooker bees are to be assigned, how many would approximately choose each source?* --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Role of Bees and ABC vs GA Comparison [7 Marks]** *(The detailed comparison is already presented in Section 3.4 above; here we summarize the key role descriptions.)* - **Employed Bees:** Local search agents; perform perturbation and greedy selection. - **Onlooker Bees:** Fitness-proportional global selectors; allocate computational effort. - **Scout Bees:** Global explorers; prevent stagnation. **ABC vs GA:** | Aspect | ABC | GA | |---|---|---| | Operators | Perturbation, greedy, abandonment | Crossover, mutation, selection | | Parameters | 3 (SN, MCN, limit) | 4+ (Pc, Pm, pop, gen) | | Solution Representation | Real-valued (continuous) | Binary or real | | Convergence | Slower but more thorough | Faster but may converge prematurely | | Memory | Stores trial counter | No memory mechanism | --- **(b) Worked Numerical Problem [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Apply Fitness Conversion Rule** For $f_1 = -3$ (negative case, $f_1 < 0$): $$ \begin{aligned} fit_1 &= 1 + \vert f_1 \vert = 1 + 3 = 4 \\ \end{aligned} $$ For $f_2 = 5$ (non-negative case, $f_2 \geq 0$): $$ \begin{aligned} fit_2 &= \frac{1}{1 + f_2} = \frac{1}{1 + 5} = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Probabilities** $$ \begin{aligned} \sum fit &= 4 + 0.1667 = 4.1667 \\ P_1 &= \frac{4}{4.1667} = 0.9600 \quad (96.0\%) \\ P_2 &= \frac{0.1667}{4.1667} = 0.0400 \quad (4.0\%) \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Expected Onlooker Bee Assignment (out of 100)** $$ \begin{aligned} N_1 &= 100 \times 0.9600 = 96 \text{ bees} \\ N_2 &= 100 \times 0.0400 = 4 \text{ bees} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Even though $f_2 = 5$ is positive and $f_1 = -3$ is negative, source 1 has a *much better* fitness (4 vs 0.167) because the fitness rule is designed so that the *better* solution gets the higher fitness. In this case, source 1 is the global optimum (since we are maximizing). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing $f_i$ and $fit_i$ (most common error!):** Students write $P_i = f_i / \sum f_n$ instead of $P_i = fit_i / \sum fit_n$. This is WRONG because the roulette-wheel requires positive, monotonically increasing values. Always convert using the fitness formula first. > > 2. **Forgetting the piecewise nature of fitness:** Using $1/(1+f_i)$ for negative $f_i$ produces negative denominators or invalid values. Always check the sign of $f_i$ first. > > 3. **Missing the abandonment condition:** Many students forget to mention that `limit` triggers the scout phase. In 14-mark questions, this omission costs 2-3 marks. > > 4. **Wrong indexing in $v_{ij}$:** The equation $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ requires $k \neq i$ and $j$ must be a randomly selected dimension. Forgetting $k \neq i$ is a common slip. > > 5. **Order of phases:** The correct order is: **Employed → Onlooker → Scout** within a single cycle. Reversing this leads to logical inconsistency in answers. > > 6. **Parameter `limit` value:** Always state $limit = SN \times D$ as per Karaboğa's original paper. Writing just "limit = 100" without justification is incomplete. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Originator:** Derviş Karaboğa, **2005** — frequently asked in 2-mark questions. - **Inspiration:** Foraging behavior of *Apis mellifera* (honey bees). - **Three Bee Types:** Employed (exploitation), Onlooker (selection), Scout (exploration). - **Equation of the Algorithm (must memorize):** $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})$ with $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$. - **Probability Formula (must memorize):** $P_i = fit_i / \sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n$. - **Fitness Rule (must memorize piecewise form):** $1/(1+f_i)$ for $f_i \geq 0$, $1+\vert f_i \vert$ for $f_i < 0$. - **Abandonment Threshold:** $limit = SN \times D$. - **Colony Composition:** $\vert EB \vert = \vert OB \vert = SN$, total colony $= 2 \cdot SN$. - **Phases Per Cycle:** Initialization → Employed → Onlooker → Scout (in that strict order). - **Termination:** After $MCN$ cycles (iterations). - **Greedy Selection:** $v_i$ replaces $x_i$ **only if** $fit(v_i) > fit(x_i)$. - **ABC is derivative-free, population-based, and uses probabilistic greedy selection.** - **Key Strengths:** Simple, only 3 control parameters, robust in continuous optimization. - **Key Weakness:** Slower convergence compared to PSO/GA in low-dimensional problems. - **Real-world apps:** WSN routing, power dispatch, image segmentation, neural network training. - **Variants to know:** GABC (Gbest-guided ABC), IABC (Improved ABC), MABC (Modified ABC), BABC (Binary ABC). - **Comparison anchor:** ABC uses *no crossover and no mutation* — it relies solely on **perturbation + greedy + abandonment**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** For a 14-mark question, the examiner expects: (1) the four phases with equations, (2) at least one numerical illustration, (3) justification of why greedy selection is used, and (4) the role of the `limit` parameter. Covering all four ensures full marks. Aim for **structured bullet points** with **boxed equations** — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.

topics.title: Variations of ABC: AbcGbest and AbcGbestDist
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECNT869 - EVOLUTIONARY COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variations of ABC: AbcGbest and AbcGbestDist

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Recap of Standard Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)

The **Artificial Bee Colony (ABC)** algorithm, proposed by **Karaboga (2005)**, is a swarm-intelligence metaheuristic inspired by the intelligent foraging behavior of honey bees. The colony is composed of three classes of artificial bees:

- **Employed Bees** (50% of colony): Each is associated with a specific food source and exploits its neighborhood.
- **Onlooker Bees** (50% of colony): Watch the "waggle dance" and probabilistically select food sources based on their nectar quality (fitness).
- **Scout Bees**: Replace abandoned (exploited) food sources via random reinitialization.

The classical **employed-bee / onlooker-bee search equation** is:

$$v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij} \cdot (x_{kj} - x_{ij})$$

where $k \neq i$, $j$ is a randomly chosen dimension index, and $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$ is a uniform random scalar.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ABC Limitation:** The search equation is purely *local* — it only uses information from a randomly chosen partner bee. There is **no global-best guidance**, which causes slow convergence and weak exploitation on unimodal/high-dimensional problems.

## 1.2 Definition of ABCgbest

**ABCgbest** (also called **GABC — Gbest-guided Artificial Bee Colony**) was proposed by **Zhu and Kwong (2010)**. It is a *hybridization* of ABC with the **global-best (gbest)** information from Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO). The defining idea is to inject the **best-so-far solution** of the colony into the onlooker-bee search equation, accelerating convergence toward the global optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *ABCgbest is a modified variant of the standard ABC algorithm in which the candidate solution generation step is augmented by a third term that pulls the new position toward the global best solution found by the colony so far, in addition to the standard neighbor-based perturbation.*

### Conceptual Analogy — ABCgbest

Imagine a flock of birds searching for food. In **standard ABC**, each bird only mimics one random neighbor's flight path. In **ABCgbest**, every bird **also glances at the highest-quality food patch the flock has ever seen** and adjusts its trajectory toward it. This is exactly what real bees do — they watch the most enthusiastic waggle dance (the best dancer wins more followers) and fly in that direction.

## 1.3 Definition of ABCgbestDist

**ABCgbestDist** is a further refinement of ABCgbest (proposed in the same line of research) that additionally incorporates an **Euclidean-distance-weighted perturbation**. By scaling the gbest term with the distance between the current solution and the global best, ABCgbestDist **adaptively balances exploration and exploitation**: the influence of gbest shrinks as the bee approaches the optimum, preventing premature convergence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *ABCgbestDist is a variant of ABCgbest in which the global-best guidance is modulated by a distance factor between the current candidate and the global best solution. The closer the bee is to the optimum, the smaller the gbest pull — preserving fine-tuning capability while retaining fast convergence.*

### Conceptual Analogy — ABCgbestDist

Think of a hiker descending a mountain in fog. In **ABCgbest**, the hiker always runs as hard as possible toward the lowest known point — overshooting the summit valley. In **ABCgbestDist**, the hiker **slows down** as the lowest known point gets closer (smaller step size), allowing careful, precise steps near the valley floor. The *distance* acts like a brake.

## 1.4 Key Metrics at a Glance

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Colony Size | $SN$ | $2 \times$ (number of food sources) |
| Employed bees | — | $SN / 2$ |
| Limit (abandonment) | $L$ | $SN \cdot D$ |
| Random coefficient (ABC) | $\phi_{ij}$ | $\mathcal{U}[-1, 1]$ |
| Random coefficient (ABCgbest) | $\psi_{ij}$ | $\mathcal{U}[0, 1.5]$ |
| Random coefficient (ABCgbestDist) | $\psi_{ij}$ | $\mathcal{U}[0, 1.5]$ with distance scaling |

> [!TIP]
> **D** denotes the **problem dimension**. All three algorithms use **greedy selection** between the old solution $x_i$ and the candidate $v_i$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Search Equations — Side-by-Side

| Variant | Search Equation |
|---|---|
| **Standard ABC** | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij} (x_{kj} - x_{ij})$ |
| **ABCgbest** | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij} (x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij} (y_j - x_{ij})$ |
| **ABCgbestDist** | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij} (x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij} (y_j - x_{ij}) \cdot w_{ij}$ |

with the **distance-based weight** $w_{ij}$ usually defined as:

$$w_{ij} = \frac{\vert x_{ij} - y_j \vert}{ \max_k \vert x_{kj} - y_j \vert + \varepsilon }$$

where $\varepsilon$ is a small positive constant (e.g., $10^{-6}$) to avoid division by zero.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Logic — Why Each Term Exists

**Term 1 — $x_{ij}$ (Base position):**
Preserves the current location of the bee. Acts as the "current reference point".

**Term 2 — $\phi_{ij} (x_{kj} - x_{ij})$ (Neighbor perturbation):**
Provides **local stochastic exploration**. The factor $\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$ controls step size and direction; the partner $k \neq i$ ensures diversity by avoiding self-comparison.

**Term 3 — $\psi_{ij} (y_j - x_{ij})$ (Global-best guidance):**
Provides **exploitation**. The vector $y$ is the global best, so this term always pulls the candidate **toward** the best-so-far solution (when $\psi_{ij} > 0$). Without it, bees wander.

**Term 4 — $w_{ij}$ (Distance scaling, ABCgbestDist only):**
Provides **adaptive exploitation strength**. Far from optimum → larger pull (broad search), close to optimum → smaller pull (fine tuning). Prevents premature convergence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Rule | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij})$ | Standard ABC search |
| 2 | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij})$ | ABCgbest search |
| 3 | $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij}) w_{ij}$ | ABCgbestDist search |
| 4 | $\phi_{ij} \sim \mathcal{U}[-1, 1]$ | Random uniform coefficient (ABC) |
| 5 | $\psi_{ij} \sim \mathcal{U}[0, 1.5]$ | Random coefficient for gbest term |
| 6 | $w_{ij} = \frac{\vert x_{ij} - y_j \vert}{\max_k \vert x_{kj} - y_j \vert + \varepsilon}$ | Distance weight for ABCgbestDist |
| 7 | $P_i = \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n}$ | Selection probability for onlooker bee |
| 8 | $fit_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i} & f_i \geq 0 \\ 1 + \vert f_i \vert & f_i < 0 \end{cases}$ | Fitness mapping (minimization) |
| 9 | Greedy selection | Keep $v_i$ iff $f(v_i) \leq f(x_i)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Exam Distinction:** Both $\phi_{ij}$ and $\psi_{ij}$ are **independent uniform random numbers regenerated for every dimension $j$** of every bee. They are **not constant** across iterations unless explicitly stated.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Power Systems** | Economic dispatch, unit commitment, optimal power flow |
| **Image Processing** | Feature selection, thresholding, segmentation |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks** | Node localization, energy-efficient clustering |
| **Mechanical Design** | Structural optimization, parameter tuning of controllers (PID gains) |
| **Bioinformatics** | Protein structure prediction, gene selection |
| **Robotics** | Path planning, inverse kinematics |

ABCgbest / ABCgbestDist are particularly favored for problems where **fast convergence to a good-enough solution** is required — e.g., real-time control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Example

## 3.1 Problem Setup (Illustrative)

Consider a 3-dimensional **minimization problem** $f(\mathbf{x})$ at one update step. Assume the following quantities are given:

- Current solution of bee $i$: $\mathbf{x}_i = (0.50, 0.30, 0.80)$
- Randomly chosen neighbor solution: $\mathbf{x}_k = (0.60, 0.40, 0.70)$
- Global best solution: $\mathbf{y} = (0.70, 0.50, 0.90)$
- Random coefficients for ABC/ABCgbest terms:

$$\phi_{ij} = (-0.50, \; +0.30, \; -0.20)$$

- Random coefficients for gbest terms:

$$\psi_{ij} = (+0.40, \; +0.60, \; +0.30)$$

- Small constant: $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$

## 3.2 Step 1 — Compute the Per-Dimension Differences

$$\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_i = (0.60 - 0.50, \; 0.40 - 0.30, \; 0.70 - 0.80)$$

$$= (+0.10, \; +0.10, \; -0.10)$$

$$\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_i = (0.70 - 0.50, \; 0.50 - 0.30, \; 0.90 - 0.80)$$

$$= (+0.20, \; +0.20, \; +0.10)$$

## 3.3 Step 2 — Standard ABC Candidate

Apply $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij} (x_{kj} - x_{ij})$ element-wise.

**Dimension 1:**
$$v_{i1} = 0.50 + (-0.50)(+0.10) = 0.50 - 0.05 = 0.45$$

**Dimension 2:**
$$v_{i2} = 0.30 + (+0.30)(+0.10) = 0.30 + 0.03 = 0.33$$

**Dimension 3:**
$$v_{i3} = 0.80 + (-0.20)(-0.10) = 0.80 + 0.02 = 0.82$$

So the **standard ABC candidate** is:

$$\mathbf{v}_i^{ABC} = (0.45, \; 0.33, \; 0.82)$$

## 3.4 Step 3 — ABCgbest Candidate

Apply $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij} (x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij})$ element-wise.

**Dimension 1:**
$$v_{i1} = 0.50 + (-0.50)(+0.10) + (+0.40)(+0.20) = 0.50 - 0.05 + 0.08 = 0.53$$

**Dimension 2:**
$$v_{i2} = 0.30 + (+0.30)(+0.10) + (+0.60)(+0.20) = 0.30 + 0.03 + 0.12 = 0.45$$

**Dimension 3:**
$$v_{i3} = 0.80 + (-0.20)(-0.10) + (+0.30)(+0.10) = 0.80 + 0.02 + 0.03 = 0.85$$

So the **ABCgbest candidate** is:

$$\mathbf{v}_i^{GABC} = (0.53, \; 0.45, \; 0.85)$$

**Observation:** Compared to standard ABC, every component of $v_i^{GABC}$ is **larger and closer to $\mathbf{y}$**, demonstrating that the gbest term indeed pulls the candidate toward the global best.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Distance Weights for ABCgbestDist

Compute $w_{ij} = \dfrac{\vert x_{ij} - y_j \vert}{\max_k \vert x_{kj} - y_j \vert + \varepsilon}$.

First, compute $\vert x_{ij} - y_j \vert$ for each dimension of bee $i$:

$$\vert x_{i1} - y_1 \vert = \vert 0.50 - 0.70 \vert = 0.20$$

$$\vert x_{i2} - y_2 \vert = \vert 0.30 - 0.50 \vert = 0.20$$

$$\vert x_{i3} - y_3 \vert = \vert 0.80 - 0.90 \vert = 0.10$$

Assuming the maximum across all bees is, say, $\max_k \vert x_{kj} - y_j \vert = (0.40, \; 0.30, \; 0.20)$, the weights become:

$$w_{i1} = \frac{0.20}{0.40 + 10^{-6}} \approx 0.5000$$

$$w_{i2} = \frac{0.20}{0.30 + 10^{-6}} \approx 0.6667$$

$$w_{i3} = \frac{0.10}{0.20 + 10^{-6}} \approx 0.5000$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — ABCgbestDist Candidate

Apply $v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij}) \cdot w_{ij}$.

**Dimension 1:**
$$v_{i1} = 0.50 + (-0.05) + (+0.40)(+0.20)(0.5000)$$
$$= 0.50 - 0.05 + 0.04 = 0.49$$

**Dimension 2:**
$$v_{i2} = 0.30 + (+0.03) + (+0.60)(+0.20)(0.6667)$$
$$= 0.30 + 0.03 + 0.08 = 0.41$$

**Dimension 3:**
$$v_{i3} = 0.80 + (+0.02) + (+0.30)(+0.10)(0.5000)$$
$$= 0.80 + 0.02 + 0.015 = 0.835$$

So the **ABCgbestDist candidate** is:

$$\mathbf{v}_i^{GABCDist} = (0.49, \; 0.41, \; 0.835)$$

## 3.7 Comparative Verification

| Variant | $v_{i1}$ | $v_{i2}$ | $v_{i3}$ | Pull-toward-$\mathbf{y}$ strength |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard ABC | 0.45 | 0.33 | 0.82 | None (pure neighbor) |
| ABCgbest | 0.53 | 0.45 | 0.85 | Strongest |
| ABCgbestDist | 0.49 | 0.41 | 0.835 | Adaptive (medium) |

**Conclusion of derivation:** The three equations form an ordered hierarchy — *Standard ABC < ABCgbestDist < ABCgbest* — in terms of global-best pull strength. ABCgbestDist smartly **interpolates** between the two extremes using the normalized distance.

## 3.8 Boundary Conditions & Constraint Handling

If a candidate dimension violates bounds $[x_j^{min}, x_j^{max}]$, the standard ABC fix is:

$$v_{ij} = \begin{cases} x_j^{max} & \text{if } v_{ij} > x_j^{max} \\ x_j^{min} & \text{if } v_{ij} < x_j^{min} \\ v_{ij} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This rule is **inherited unchanged** in both ABCgbest and ABCgbestDist.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of ABCgbest / ABCgbestDist

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START] --> A1[Initialize Food Sources xi]
    A1 --> A2[Evaluate Fitness fit_i]
    A2 --> A3[Identify Global Best y]
    A3 --> B1{Phase 1: Employed Bees}
    B1 --> B2[For each employed bee, choose partner k and dimension j]
    B2 --> B3[Generate candidate vi using variant equation]
    B3 --> B4[Apply Greedy Selection]
    B4 --> C1{Phase 2: Onlooker Bees}
    C1 --> C2[Compute selection probability Pi]
    C2 --> C3[Choose food source by Roulette Wheel]
    C3 --> C4[Generate candidate vi using variant equation]
    C4 --> C5[Apply Greedy Selection]
    C5 --> D1{Phase 3: Scout Bees}
    D1 --> D2[Abandon sources exceeding Limit L]
    D2 --> D3[Reinitialize abandoned source randomly]
    D3 --> E1[Update Global Best y]
    E1 --> F1{Max Iterations Reached?}
    F1 -- No --> B1
    F1 -- Yes --> G1[OUTPUT Best Solution]
```

## 4.2 Variant Comparison — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ABCS[Standard ABC]
        S1[xi] --> S2[+ phi times xk minus xi]
        S2 --> S3[vi ABC]
    end
    subgraph ABCG[ABCgbest]
        G1[xi] --> G2[+ phi times xk minus xi]
        G2 --> G3[+ psi times y minus xi]
        G3 --> G4[vi GABC]
    end
    subgraph ABCD[ABCgbestDist]
        D1[xi] --> D2[+ phi times xk minus xi]
        D2 --> D3[Compute wij distance weight]
        D3 --> D4[+ psi times y minus xi times wij]
        D4 --> D5[vi GABCDist]
    end
    ABCS --> COMP[Comparative Convergence Behaviour]
    ABCG --> COMP
    ABCD --> COMP
```

## 4.3 Per-Dimension Update Pipeline (ABCgbestDist)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Receive xi, xk, y, phi, psi] --> P1[Compute dx = xk minus xi]
    P1 --> P2[Compute dy = y minus xi]
    P2 --> P3[Compute wij = distance weight]
    P3 --> P4[Compute term1 = phi times dx]
    P4 --> P5[Compute term2 = psi times dy times wij]
    P5 --> P6[vi = xi + term1 + term2]
    P6 --> P7[Boundary clamp vi to xmin xmax]
    P7 --> P8[Return vi]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **distance-weighting step** (node P3) is the **only structural difference** between ABCgbestDist and ABCgbest. It is what gives ABCgbestDist its adaptive convergence behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**State the search equation of standard ABC and explain how ABCgbest modifies it. Mention the role of the global best vector $\mathbf{y}$.**

**Model Answer:**
The standard ABC employed/onlooker bee search equation is:

$$v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij})$$

where $\phi_{ij} \in [-1,1]$ and $k \neq i$.

In **ABCgbest**, this is augmented by an additional term involving the global best solution $\mathbf{y}$:

$$v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij})$$

where $\psi_{ij} \in [0, 1.5]$ is a uniform random coefficient.

**Role of $\mathbf{y}$:** It acts as an attractor that pulls the candidate solution toward the best-so-far solution found by the colony. This accelerates convergence and improves exploitation by leveraging collective memory.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Differentiate between ABCgbest and ABCgbestDist. Why is distance weighting introduced?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | ABCgbest | ABCgbestDist |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | Adds $\psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij})$ | Adds $\psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij}) \cdot w_{ij}$ |
| gbest influence | Constant | Distance-adaptive |
| Risk | Premature convergence | Balanced |

**Distance weighting is introduced** to control the strength of the global-best pull. When a bee is far from the optimum, $w_{ij}$ is large (encouraging rapid movement toward gbest). When it is near the optimum, $w_{ij}$ shrinks (allowing fine-grained local search). This adaptively balances **exploration** and **exploitation**, mitigating premature convergence.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Describe the standard Artificial Bee Colony (ABC) algorithm with its three phases. Explain the role of employed, onlooker, and scout bees. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the ABCgbest variant. State its search equation, derive the algorithmic steps, and discuss how the gbest term accelerates convergence compared to standard ABC. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution**

**(a) Standard ABC Algorithm — 7 Marks**

The standard ABC algorithm, proposed by Karaboga (2005), simulates the foraging behavior of a honey bee colony and consists of four key phases:

**Phase 1 — Initialization:**
- Generate $SN$ food sources randomly within bounds:

$$x_{ij} = x_j^{min} + \text{rand}(0,1) \cdot (x_j^{max} - x_j^{min})$$

- Evaluate fitness using:

$$fit_i = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{1 + f_i} & f_i \geq 0 \\ 1 + \vert f_i \vert & f_i < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Phase 2 — Employed Bee Phase:**
- Each employed bee produces a new candidate $v_i$ via:

$$v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij})$$

- Apply **greedy selection**: replace $x_i$ by $v_i$ iff $f(v_i) \leq f(x_i)$.

**Phase 3 — Onlooker Bee Phase:**
- Compute selection probability:

$$P_i = \frac{fit_i}{\sum_{n=1}^{SN} fit_n}$$

- Onlooker bees choose sources by **roulette-wheel** selection and produce new candidates using the same search equation.

**Phase 4 — Scout Bee Phase:**
- If a food source cannot be improved for $L$ trials (limit), it is **abandoned**.
- The associated employed bee becomes a **scout** and reinitializes the source randomly.

**Roles:**
- **Employed bees**: exploit known sources and provide information via waggle dance.
- **Onlooker bees**: probabilistic selection drives intensification toward good sources.
- **Scout bees**: prevent stagnation by introducing diversity.

**(b) ABCgbest Variant — 7 Marks**

**Motivation:** Standard ABC uses only neighbor information; convergence is slow. ABCgbest (Zhu & Kwong, 2010) integrates PSO-style gbest guidance.

**Search Equation:**

$$v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij})$$

with $\phi_{ij} \in [-1,1]$ and $\psi_{ij} \in [0, 1.5]$.

**Algorithmic Steps:**
1. Initialize $SN$ food sources; identify global best $\mathbf{y}$.
2. For each employed bee, produce $v_i$ using the **ABCgbest equation** above.
3. Apply greedy selection.
4. Compute $P_i$ and let onlooker bees select sources and apply the same equation.
5. Apply scout bee phase if source limit exceeded.
6. Update global best; iterate until convergence.

**Why it accelerates convergence:**
- The gbest term $(y_j - x_{ij})$ is a **direction vector pointing toward the best-so-far solution**.
- It adds a **positive bias** that pulls the candidate closer to $\mathbf{y}$, similar to the cognitive + social components of PSO.
- Combined with the random neighbor term, it yields **both diversity and directed exploitation**, yielding faster convergence on unimodal and moderately multimodal landscapes.

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Discuss the limitations of standard ABC that motivate hybrid variants. List three common hybrid ABC variants and their key modification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the ABCgbestDist algorithm in detail. Derive its distance-weight formula and show with a 3-D numerical example how a candidate is generated. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution**

**(a) Limitations of Standard ABC and Hybrid Variants — 7 Marks**

**Limitations:**
1. **Slow convergence** due to lack of global information.
2. **Weak exploitation** on unimodal problems (neighbor-only perturbation).
3. **Premature diversity loss** in late iterations without adaptive mechanisms.
4. **Sensitivity** to the limit parameter $L$.
5. **Poor performance** on high-dimensional, rotated, or non-separable problems.

**Three Common Hybrid ABC Variants:**

| Variant | Key Modification | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **ABCgbest (GABC)** | Adds global best term to search equation | Zhu & Kwong (2010) |
| **ABCgbestDist** | Adds distance-weighted global best term | Zhu & Kwong (2010) |
| **CABC (Chaotic ABC)** | Replaces $\phi_{ij}$ by chaotic maps for better diversity | Alatas (2010) |

Other variants include **IABC (Improved ABC)**, **MABC (Modified ABC)**, and **BABC (Binary ABC)**.

**(b) ABCgbestDist Algorithm with Numerical Example — 7 Marks**

**Equation:**

$$v_{ij} = x_{ij} + \phi_{ij}(x_{kj} - x_{ij}) + \psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij}) \cdot w_{ij}$$

**Distance Weight Formula:**

$$w_{ij} = \frac{\vert x_{ij} - y_j \vert}{\max_k \vert x_{kj} - y_j \vert + \varepsilon}$$

where $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ is a small constant.

**Derivation Logic:**
- The numerator measures the bee's distance from the gbest in dimension $j$.
- The denominator normalizes across the colony, producing $w_{ij} \in [0, 1]$.
- A bee close to gbest in a dimension → small $w_{ij}$ → small gbest pull → fine tuning.
- A bee far from gbest → large $w_{ij}$ → strong gbest pull → broad exploration toward the optimum.

**Numerical Example (3-D):**
Let $\mathbf{x}_i = (0.5, 0.3, 0.8)$, $\mathbf{x}_k = (0.6, 0.4, 0.7)$, $\mathbf{y} = (0.7, 0.5, 0.9)$, $\phi = (-0.5, 0.3, -0.2)$, $\psi = (0.4, 0.6, 0.3)$.

Compute differences:
- $\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_i = (0.1, 0.1, -0.1)$
- $\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}_i = (0.2, 0.2, 0.1)$

Compute weights (assuming $\max_k \vert x_{kj} - y_j \vert = (0.4, 0.3, 0.2)$):
- $w_{i1} = 0.20 / 0.40 = 0.50$
- $w_{i2} = 0.20 / 0.30 = 0.67$
- $w_{i3} = 0.10 / 0.20 = 0.50$

Apply equation:
- $v_{i1} = 0.5 + (-0.5)(0.1) + (0.4)(0.2)(0.50) = 0.5 - 0.05 + 0.04 = 0.49$
- $v_{i2} = 0.3 + (0.3)(0.1) + (0.6)(0.2)(0.67) = 0.3 + 0.03 + 0.08 = 0.41$
- $v_{i3} = 0.8 + (-0.2)(-0.1) + (0.3)(0.1)(0.50) = 0.8 + 0.02 + 0.015 = 0.835$

**Candidate solution:** $\mathbf{v}_i = (0.49, 0.41, 0.835)$.

**Comparison Insight:** The gbest pull is **smaller than in ABCgbest** because of $w_{ij} < 1$, demonstrating the adaptive softening effect of distance weighting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing $\phi_{ij}$ ranges:** Writing $\phi_{ij} \in [0,1]$ instead of $[-1,1]$ for standard ABC costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting boundary clamping:** ABCgbest candidates can exceed bounds; failure to mention clamping loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Misattributing the gbest term:** Writing the equation as $\psi_{ij}(x_{kj} - y_j)$ instead of $\psi_{ij}(y_j - x_{ij})$ reverses the direction of pull — major conceptual error, costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the $\varepsilon$ in $w_{ij}$:** Examiner penalises division-by-zero risk oversight.
> 5. **Missing the selection-probability update:** ABCgbest still uses $P_i$ — students who drop it lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong greedy selection sign:** Must be $f(v_i) \leq f(x_i)$ for minimization, not $\geq$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ABCgbest = Standard ABC + gbest term** in the search equation. The gbest term acts as a PSO-style attractor pulling every candidate toward the best-so-far solution.
- **ABCgbestDist = ABCgbest + distance weight $w_{ij}$** modulating the gbest term. The weight is computed as the **normalized absolute difference** between the current solution and the global best.
- **$\phi_{ij} \in [-1, 1]$** controls the neighbor-perturbation term in all variants.
- **$\psi_{ij} \in [0, 1.5]$** is the gbest coefficient in ABCgbest and ABCgbestDist.
- **Greedy selection** (keep better solution) is **mandatory** in all three variants.
- **Limit $L$** for scout phase is typically $SN \cdot D$.
- **Boundary clamping** is inherited unchanged from standard ABC.
- **Convergence ordering (typical):** ABCgbest < ABCgbestDist in stability, while ABC < both in speed. ABCgbestDist is the most balanced.
- **Best use case:** Problems needing **fast yet stable** convergence — e.g., real-time parameter tuning, engineering optimization.
- **Examiner favorite:** Be ready to (i) state the equations, (ii) explain the role of each term, (iii) compute a candidate numerically, and (iv) compare the three algorithms in a table.
